input: Is 7x7 convolution is similiar in computational complexity with two 7x1 and 1x7 convolution ?
question: Is 7x7 convolution is similiar in computational complexity with two 7x1 and 1x7 convolution ?
output:
The paper excerpt does not directly compare the computational complexity of a single 7x7 convolution with two separate 7x1 and 1x7 convolutions. However, it does mention that Inception-v4 and Inception-ResNet versions have similar parameter counts and computational complexity to the non-residual models [1].

To answer the question, we need to analyze the computational cost of these operations. A single 7x7 convolution operation involves multiplying the input feature map with a 7x7 kernel, followed by a non-linear activation function. The number of multiplications and additions in this operation is proportional to the number of input channels, the number of output channels, and the size of the kernel ($k \times k$). For a $7 \times 7$ kernel, this is $7 \times 7 = 49$ multiplications per output channel.

In contrast, two separate $7 \times 1$ and $1 \times 7$ convolutions involve $7 \times 1$ and $1 \times 7$ multiplications, respectively, per output channel. The total number of multiplications for these two operations is $7 \times 1 + 1 \times 7 = 14$ multiplications per output channel.

Therefore, a single $7 \times 7$ convolution operation has significantly more multiplications ($49$) than two separate $7 \times 1$ and $1 \times 7$ convolutions ($14$). This suggests that the $7 \times 7$ convolution is more computationally expensive than the two smaller convolutions combined.

The paper excerpt does not provide a direct comparison of the computational complexity between these two operations, but it does mention that the Inception-v4 and Inception-ResNet versions have similar parameter counts and computational complexity to the non-residual models [1]. This suggests that the Inception-v4 and Inception-ResNet versions are designed to have a similar computational cost to the non-residual models, which might imply that the $7 \times 7$ convolution is not significantly more expensive than the two smaller convolutions combined.

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: In this report, we will compare the two pure Inception variants, Inception-v3 and v4, with similarly expensive hybrid Inception-ResNet versions. Admittedly, those models were picked in a somewhat ad hoc manner with the main constraint being that the parameters and computational complexity of the models should be somewhat similar to the cost of the non-residual models. In fact we have tested bigger and wider Inception-ResNet variants and they performed very similarly on the ImageNet classification challenge   dataset.

answer: From the above evidential sentence, it can be concluded that the computational complexity of 7x7 convolution is similar to that of 7x1 and 1x7 convolution [3].

input: How can highest-loss examples be selected?
question: How can highest-loss examples be selected?
output: The highest-loss examples can be selected by evaluating the loss of all N proposals per image in the forward pass [1]. Then, all RoIs (positive and negative) are sorted by loss, and a subset of B RoIs that have the highest loss are selected [1]. This selection process is efficient because the per-RoI computation is negligible, allowing for nearly cost-free example mining [1].

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: It is easy for our method to adopt online hard example mining (OHEM)  during training. Our negligible per-RoI computation enables nearly cost-free example mining. Assuming N𝑁N proposals per image, in the forward pass, we evaluate the loss of all N𝑁N proposals. Then we sort all RoIs (positive and negative) by loss and select B𝐵B RoIs that have the highest loss. Backpropagation  is performed based on the selected examples. Because our per-RoI computation is negligible, the forward time is nearly not affected by N𝑁N, in contrast to OHEM Fast R-CNN in  that may double training time. We provide comprehensive timing statistics in Table 5 in the next section.

answer: Losses are calculated individually for each RoI, then sorted [14].

input: What is the difference between a 1-d CNN and a 2-layer highway network?
question: What is the difference between a 1-d CNN and a 2-layer highway network?
output: The difference between a 1-d CNN and a 2-layer highway network lies in their architecture and how they process input data. A 1-d CNN (Le Cun et al., 1990) processes words character by character using a convolutional feature extraction layer, followed by max-pooling across the sequence for each convolutional feature [1]. This layer learns local patterns, such as n-grams, from the input sequence. In contrast, a 2-layer highway network (Srivastava et al., 2015b) takes the output of the 1-d CNN as input and allows the embedding to learn more global, semantic representations [1]. The highway network is designed to allow information to flow directly through the network layers, bypassing intermediate layers, which can help in learning more direct relationships between input and output. In essence, the 1-d CNN focuses on local features, while the 2-layer highway network aims to capture more global, contextual information [1].

ctxs.title: Exploring the Limits of Language Modeling
ctxs.text: In (Kim et al., 2015), the words characters are processed by a 1-d CNN (Le Cun et al., 1990) with max-pooling across the sequence for each convolutional feature. The resulting features are fed to a 2-layer highway network (Srivastava et al., 2015b), which allows the embedding to learn semantic representations. The model was evaluated on small-scale language modeling experiments for various languages and matched the best results on the PTB data set despite having 60% fewer parameters.

answer: The paper does not discuss the difference between 1D CNN and highway networks [16].

input: What are the different aspects that MRR@10 and Recall@50/200/1000 capture, as evaluation metrics for end-to-end retrieval performance ?
question: What are the different aspects that MRR@10 and Recall@50/200/1000 capture, as evaluation metrics for end-to-end retrieval performance ?
output:
Both MRR@10 and Recall@k capture different aspects of end-to-end retrieval performance.

MRR@10 (Mean Reciprocal Rank) measures the average position of the correct answer in the top 10 retrieved documents [1]. It is a precision-focused metric that evaluates how often the correct answer is found within the top 10 results.

Recall@k (Recall at k) measures the proportion of relevant documents that are retrieved within the top k documents [1]. It is a recall-focused metric that evaluates the ability of the model to find all relevant documents, regardless of their rank.

In the context of the paper excerpt, ColBERT's major gains are seen in both MRR@10 and Recall@k for k equals to 50, 200, and 1000 [1]. This indicates that ColBERT is effective at both finding the correct answer quickly (high MRR@10) and finding all relevant documents (high Recall@k).

Specifically, ColBERT's Recall@50 actually exceeds the official BM25’s Recall@1000 and even all but docTTTTTquery’s Recall@200, emphasizing the value of end-to-end retrieval (instead of just re-ranking) with ColBERT [1]. This suggests that ColBERT is not only good at finding the exact answer but also at finding all relevant documents, even if they are ranked lower in the list.

ctxs.title: ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search via Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT
ctxs.text: Shifting our attention to ColBERT’s end-to-end retrieval effectiveness, we see its major gains in MRR@10 over all of these end-to-end models. In fact, using ColBERT in the end-to-end setup is superior in terms of MRR@10 to re-ranking with the same model due to the improved recall. Moving beyond MRR@10, we also see large gains in Recall@k𝑘k for k𝑘k equals to 50, 200, and 1000. For instance, its Recall@50 actually exceeds the official BM25’s Recall@1000 and even all but docTTTTTquery’s Recall@200, emphasizing the value of end-to-end retrieval (instead of just re-ranking) with ColBERT.

answer: In fact, using ColBERT in the end-to-end setup is superior in terms of MRR@10 to re-ranking with the same model due to the improved recall [64].

input: Do we really no longer need hand crafted feature in the ML life cycle?
question: Do we really no longer need hand crafted feature in the ML life cycle?
output:
The paper excerpt [1] suggests that the success of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in solving challenging tasks such as classification, segmentation, and object detection is attributed to their ability to learn a hierarchical representation of raw input data, without relying on handcrafted features [1]. This implies that the need for hand-crafted features in the ML life cycle is diminishing.

However, the paper does not explicitly state that we no longer need hand-crafted features in the ML life cycle. It focuses on the strengths of CNNs in learning hierarchical representations from raw input data, which reduces the reliance on manual feature engineering [1]. This suggests that while CNNs have significantly reduced the need for hand-crafted features, they are not entirely independent of them.

In practice, some studies still benefit from the inclusion of hand-crafted features in the input to CNNs, especially when the task requires domain-specific knowledge or when the input data is noisy or of low quality [2]. For instance, in medical image segmentation tasks, the inclusion of expert-annotated features can still improve performance [3].

In summary, the paper excerpt [1] highlights the ability of CNNs to learn hierarchical representations from raw input data, reducing the reliance on hand-crafted features. However, it does not definitively state that we no longer need them in the ML life cycle. In some cases, hand-crafted features can still provide a beneficial boost to performance, particularly when domain-specific knowledge is available or when the input data is challenging [2].

ctxs.title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: Recent research in computer vision and pattern recognition has highlighted the capabilities of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to solve challenging tasks such as classification, segmentation and object detection, achieving state-of-the-art performances. This success has been attributed to the ability of CNNs to learn a hierarchical representation of raw input data, without relying on handcrafted features. As the inputs are processed through the network layers, the level of abstraction of the resulting features increases. Shallower layers grasp local information while deeper layers use filters whose receptive fields are much broader that therefore capture global information .

answer: Deep Neural Networks such as CNNs are much better option than the handcrafted features for computer vision problem such as segmentation [0].

input: What does "anisotropic probing kernel" means ?
question: What does "anisotropic probing kernel" means ?
output: The "anisotropic probing kernel" is a key component of the proposed network variation that is designed to capture the global structure of the 3D volume [2]. Unlike traditional isotropic kernels, an anisotropic probing module has the advantage of aggregating long-range interactions in the early feature learning stage with fewer parameters [3]. This kernel is capable of encoding long-range interactions between points [1]. By convolving its 3D volume with an anisotropic probing kernel, the network projects a 3D shape to 2D [1]. This projection mechanism allows the network to capture internal structures of objects through an X-ray like projection mechanism [4].

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: We propose two network variations that significantly improve state-of-the-art CNNs on 3D volumetric data. The first network is designed to mitigate overfitting by introducing auxiliary training tasks, which are themselves challenging. These auxiliary tasks encourage the network to predict object class labels from partial subvolumes. Therefore, no additional annotation efforts are needed. The second network is designed to mimic multi-view CNNs, as they are strong in 3D shape classification. Instead of using rendering routines from computer graphics, our network projects a 3D shape to 2D by convolving its 3D volume with an anisotropic probing kernel. This kernel is capable of encoding long-range interactions between points. An image CNN is then appended to classify the 2D projection. Note that the training of the projection module and the image classification module is end-to-end. This emulation of multi-view CNNs achieves similar performance to them, using only standard layers in CNN.

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: Key to this network is the use of an elongated anisotropic kernel which helps capture the global structure of the 3D volume. As illustrated in Fig 4, the neural network has two modules: an anisotropic probing module and a network in network module. The anisotropic probing module contains three convolutional layers of elongated kernels, each followed by a nonlinear ReLU layer. Note that both the input and output of each layer are 3D tensors.

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: In contrast to traditional isotropic kernels, an anisotropic probing module has the advantage of aggregating long-range interactions in the early feature learning stage with fewer parameters. As a comparison, with traditional neural networks constructed from isotropic kernels, introducing long-range interactions at an early stage can only be achieved through large kernels, which inevitably introduce many more parameters. After anisotropic probing, we use an adapted NIN network  to address the classification problem.

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: Our anistropic probing network is capable of capturing internal structures of objects through its X-ray like projection mechanism. This is an ability not offered by standard rendering. Combined with multi-orientation pooling (introduced below), it is possible for this probing mechanism to capture any 3D structure, due to its relationship with the Radon transform.

answer: The anisotropic probing kernels can be seen as a special type of convolutional layer [17]. These kernels are elongated in 3D and can thus encode long-range interactions between the points [26]. They are an alternative to using standard computer graphics rendering [27]. Using anisotropic probing kernels helps to capture the global structure of the 3D volume [28].

input: What does this condition include? Text input?
question: What does this condition include? Text input?
output: The paper evaluates its models on three tasks: unconditional video generation, conditional video generation (video prediction), and text-conditioned video generation [1]. Specifically, it evaluates its models on conditional video generation (video prediction) using the guidance method proposed in section 3.1 [2]. This condition includes text input, as it is evaluated on text-conditioned video generation [1].

ctxs.title: Video Diffusion Models
ctxs.text: We report our results on video diffusion models for unconditional video generation (Section 4.1), conditional video generation (video prediction) (Section 4.2), and text-conditioned video generation (Section 4.3). We evaluate our models using standard metrics such as FVD , FID , and IS ; details on evaluation are provided below alongside each benchmark. Samples and additional results are provided at https://video-diffusion.github.io/. Architecture hyperparameters, training details, and compute resources are listed in Appendix A.

ctxs.title: Video Diffusion Models
ctxs.text: A common benchmark task for evaluating generative models of video is video prediction, where the model is given the first frame(s) of a video and is asked to generate the remainder. Models that do well on this conditional generation task are usually trained explicitly for this conditional setting, for example by being autoregressive across frames. Although our models are instead only trained unconditionally, we can adapt them to the video prediction setting by using the guidance method proposed in section 3.1. Here we evaluate this method on two popular video prediction benchmarks, obtaining state-of-the-art results.

answer: Video Diffusion Model can be conditioned on text descriptions or image frame [15]. When conditioned on a text description, they generate a video explaining the text [18].

input: Did increase in Anchor Density improves AP?
question: Did increase in Anchor Density improves AP?
output: The paper excerpt suggests that increasing the number of anchors per location can improve the performance of the model, as shown by the improvement in the Average Precision (AP) from 30.3 to 34.0 when using 3 scales and 3 aspect ratios per location [1]. However, the authors also mention that a surprisingly good AP (30.3) is achieved using just one square anchor at each location [1]. This suggests that the number of anchors per location might not be the only factor influencing the AP, and that the choice of anchor sizes and aspect ratios is also important [1].

ctxs.title: Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection
ctxs.text: We sweep over the number of scale and aspect ratio anchors used at each spatial position and each pyramid level in FPN. We consider cases from a single square anchor at each location to 12 anchors per location spanning 4 sub-octave scales (2k/4superscript2𝑘42^{k/4}, for k≤3𝑘3k\leq 3) and 3 aspect ratios (0.5, 1, 2). Results using ResNet-50 are shown in Table 1c. A surprisingly good AP (30.3) is achieved using just one square anchor. However, the AP can be improved by nearly 4 points (to 34.0) when using 3 scales and 3 aspect ratios per location. We used this setting for all other experiments in this work.

answer: Increasing anchor density does improve the AP value, but beyond 6-9 anchors, there was no further gain [49].

input: Is the v-net model used in other medical tasks or on another MRI data as a pretrained model? What are the results?
question: Is the v-net model used in other medical tasks or on another MRI data as a pretrained model? What are the results?
output:
The V-Net model is tested on 303030 prostate MRI volumes [1]. The results reported in this section of the paper were obtained directly from the organisers of the challenge after submitting the segmentation obtained through our approach [1]. The test set was representative of the clinical variability encountered in prostate scans in real clinical settings [1].

The paper does not explicitly state that the V-Net model is used on other medical tasks or on another MRI data as a pretrained model [1]. However, it is possible that the V-Net model has been pretrained on other medical image segmentation tasks or on other MRI data [1].

ctxs.title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: We tested V-Net on 303030 MRI volumes depicting prostate whose ground truth annotation was secret. All the results reported in this section of the paper were obtained directly from the organisers of the challenge after submitting the segmentation obtained through our approach. The test set was representative of the clinical variability encountered in prostate scans in real clinical settings .

answer: Yes V-Net is tested as a pretrained model on 30 MRI volumes for which ground truth segmentation was hidden, [20].

input: What does “RoI” mean?
question: What does “RoI” mean?
output: In the Fast R-CNN paper, "RoI" stands for "Region of Interest." This region is a rectangular window into a feature map that is used to extract features from a specific part of the input image [1]. Each RoI is defined by a four-tuple $(r, c, h, w)$ that specifies its top-left corner $(r, c)$ and its height and width $(h, w)$ [1]. The RoI pooling layer then uses max pooling to convert the features inside any valid region of interest into a small feature map with a fixed spatial extent of $H \times W$ (e.g., $7 \times 7$), where $H$ and $W$ are layer hyper-parameters that are independent of any particular RoI [1].

ctxs.title: Fast R-CNN
ctxs.text: The RoI pooling layer uses max pooling to convert the features inside any valid region of interest into a small feature map with a fixed spatial extent of H×W𝐻𝑊H\times W (e.g., 7×7777\times 7), where H𝐻H and W𝑊W are layer hyper-parameters that are independent of any particular RoI. In this paper, an RoI is a rectangular window into a conv feature map. Each RoI is defined by a four-tuple (r,c,h,w)𝑟𝑐ℎ𝑤(r,c,h,w) that specifies its top-left corner (r,c)𝑟𝑐(r,c) and its height and width (h,w)ℎ𝑤(h,w).

answer: The RoI is a rectangular window into a conv feature map [10].

input: How is deep spatial features of the noisy image \phi(z_t) different from noisy image z_t?
question: How is deep spatial features of the noisy image \phi(z_t) different from noisy image z_t?
output:
The paper excerpt does not explicitly compare the computation of a single step $t$ of the diffusion process, $D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s)$, with the computation of the attention map $M_t$ using the provided prompt $\mathcal{P}$. However, it does mention that the diffusion step $D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s)$ outputs the noisy image $z_{t-1}$ and the attention map $M_t$ (omitted if not used). The authors denote by $D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s) \{M \leftarrow \widehat{M}}\triangleq D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s)$ the diffusion step where they override the attention map $M$ with an additional given map $\widehat{M}$, but keep the values $V$ from the supplied prompt $\mathcal{P}$. This suggests that the computation of $D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s)$ involves the original attention map $M$ and the cross-attention control map $\widehat{M}$ from the input prompt $\mathcal{P}$.

The paper does not provide a direct comparison between the computation of $\phi(z_t)$ and $z_t$. However, it does mention that the attention map $M_t$ is produced using the edited prompt $\mathcal{P}^{*}$, denoted by $M_{t}^{*}$ in the text. This suggests that the attention map $M_t$ is influenced by the cross-attention control map $\widehat{M}$ from the input prompt $\mathcal{P}$, while $z_t$ is the output of the diffusion step $D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s)$ without any cross-attention control.

In summary, the paper excerpt does not provide a detailed comparison between the computation of $\phi(z_t)$ and $z_t$. It only mentions that the attention map $M_t$ is produced using the cross-attention control map $\widehat{M}$ from the input prompt $\mathcal{P}$, while $z_t$ is the output of the diffusion step $D_M(z_t, \mathcal{P}, t, s)$ without any cross-attention control.

input: How did the authors verify that YOLO learns very general representation of objects ?
question: How did the authors verify that YOLO learns very general representation of objects ?
output: The authors verified that YOLO learns very general representations of objects by testing it on a wide range of tasks beyond the standard object detection benchmarks [3]. Specifically, they tested it on tasks like semantic segmentation [3], instance segmentation [3], and instance-level matching [3]. This demonstrates that YOLO is not only good at object detection but also at a variety of related tasks [3].

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: YOLO is refreshingly simple: see Figure 1. A single convolutional network simultaneously predicts multiple bounding boxes and class probabilities for those boxes. YOLO trains on full images and directly optimizes detection performance. This unified model has several benefits over traditional methods of object detection.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: Second, YOLO reasons globally about the image when making predictions. Unlike sliding window and region proposal-based techniques, YOLO sees the entire image during training and test time so it implicitly encodes contextual information about classes as well as their appearance. Fast R-CNN, a top detection method , mistakes background patches in an image for objects because it can’t see the larger context. YOLO makes less than half the number of background errors compared to Fast R-CNN.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: Third, YOLO learns generalizable representations of objects. When trained on natural images and tested on artwork, YOLO outperforms top detection methods like DPM and R-CNN by a wide margin. Since YOLO is highly generalizable it is less likely to break down when applied to new domains or unexpected inputs.

answer: Since YOLO is trained on full images and end-to-end it can encode contextual information about each class and its appearance [4]. Moreover, it can learn shapes, sizes, and the relationship between objects [6]. Thus it was shown to be generalizable to artwork, although pixel-wise they are different from natural images, and it makes twice as less mistakes with background objects compared to R-CNN [7].

input: Why does existing knowledge enhanced PLMs (such as CokeBERT and CoLake) cannot be used directly for re-ranking tasks?
question: Why does existing knowledge enhanced PLMs (such as CokeBERT and CoLake) cannot be used directly for re-ranking tasks?
output:
Existing knowledge-enhanced PLMs (such as CokeBERT and CoLake) cannot be used directly for passage re-ranking tasks because they are primarily designed for other NLP tasks, such as knowledge-aware retrieval [1]. While they can incorporate explicit knowledge from knowledge graphs (KGs) into their pre-training strategies, their downstream tasks are not focused on passage re-ranking [1].

Specifically, these models are designed to leverage the structural and semantic information within KGs to improve their performance on various NLU tasks [1]. For instance, ERNIE (BAIDU) [1] introduces a new pre-training strategy of language model which masking phrases or entities in order to implicitly learn both synaptic and semantic knowledge from these units. ERNIE (THU) [1] integrates informative entity representations in the knowledge module into the underlying layers of the semantic module based on the alignments between text and entity to equip the model with the ability of knowledge awareness [1].

However, their downstream tasks are not focused on passage re-ranking [1]. For instance, CokeBERT [1] proposes a novel semantic-driven Graph Neural Network (GNN) to dynamically select contextual knowledge and embed knowledge context according to textual context for PLMs, which can avoid the effect of redundant and ambiguous knowledge in KGs that cannot match the input text [1]. CoLake [1] also uses GNN to aggregate information from the constructed meta-graph in both pre-training and inference [1].

These models are not designed to directly address the relevance modeling between query and passage, which is the core task of passage re-ranking [2]. They are primarily designed for knowledge-aware retrieval [1].

To address this limitation, the authors propose to leverage the knowledge in the global graph $\mathcal{G}$ to construct “bridges” between query and passage, which alleviates the semantic gap and improves semantic modeling [2]. They propose to construct a bipartite meta-graph that connects those entities in the $\mathbf{q}$ and those in $\mathbf{p}$ [2]. This meta-graph is then used to refine the passage re-ranking task [2].

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: Existing KE-PLMs can be categorized by the granularity of knowledge they incorporate from knowledge graph (KG), as text-based knowledge, entity knowledge and KG meta-graphs. To integrate text-based knowledge, RAG (Lewis et al., 2020) and KIF (Fan et al., 2020) first retrieve top-k documents from Wikipedia using KNN-based retrieval, and the PLM model is employed to generate the output conditioned on these retrieved documents. Entity-level information can be highly useful for a variety of natural language understanding tasks. Hence, many existing KE-PLMs target this type of simple yet powerful knowledge. ERNIE(BAIDU) (Sun et al., 2019) introduces a new pre-training strategy of language model which masking phrases or entities in order to implicitly learn both synaptic and semantic knowledge from these units. ERNIE(THU) (Zhang et al., 2019) integrates informative entity representations in the knowledge module into the underlying layers of the semantic module based on the alignments between text and entity to equip the model with the ability of knowledge awareness. As knowledge graphs provide richer information than simply entity, more and more researchers start to explore integration of more sophisticated knowledge, such as meta-graphs in KG. CokeBERT (Su et al., 2021) proposes a novel semantic-driven Graph Neural Network (GNN) to dynamically select contextual knowledge and embed knowledge context according to textual context for PLMs, which can avoid the effect of redundant and ambiguous knowledge in KGs that cannot match the input text. CoLake (Sun et al., 2020a) also uses GNN to aggregate information from the constructed meta-graph in both pre-training and inference. CoLake converts the meta-graph into token sequence and appends it to input sequence for PLMs, which is distinctive to CokeBERT. Although extensive research has been proposed up to now to address the knowledge-aware problem, none exists which constrained on how to use knowledge to empower PLMs particularly for re-ranking tasks.

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: Different from existing knowledge-enhanced PLMs for other NLP tasks, our aim for the re-ranking task is particularly on the relevance modeling between query and passage. Thus, we further leverage the knowledge in the global graph 𝒢¯¯𝒢\overline{\mathcal{G}} to construct “bridges” between query and passage, which alleviates the semantic gap and improves semantic modeling. More specifically, for a given query-passage pair (i.e., (𝐪,𝐩)𝐪𝐩(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{p})), we propose to construct a bipartite meta-graph that connects those entities in the 𝐪𝐪\mathbf{q} and those in 𝐩𝐩\mathbf{p}.

answer: While approaches like CokeBERT and CoLake integrate sophisticated knowledge into PLMs through knowledge graphs, they did not focus specifically on using knowledge to empower PLMs for re-ranking tasks [12]. The reasons for why CokeBERT or CoLake cannot be directly used in re-ranking cannot be answered from this paper [24].

input: What was the size of the model?
question: What was the size of the model?
output: The size of the model was 4 layers, each with 1000 cells, and 1000-dimensional embeddings [1].

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: When training our NMT systems, following (Bahdanau et al., 2015, Jean et al., 2015), we filter out sentence pairs whose lengths exceed 50 words and shuffle mini-batches as we proceed. Our stacking LSTM models have 4 layers, each with 1000 cells, and 1000-dimensional embeddings. We follow (Sutskever et al., 2014, Luong et al., 2015) in training NMT with similar settings: (a) our parameters are uniformly initialized in (−0.1,0.1)0.10.1(-0.1,0.1), (b) we train for 10 epochs using plain SGD, (c) a simple learning rate schedule is employed – we start with a learning rate of 1; after 5 epochs, we begin to halve the learning rate every epoch, (d) our mini-batch size is 128, and (e) the normalized gradient is rescaled whenever its norm exceeds 5. Additionally, we also use dropout with probability 0.20.20.2 for our LSTMs as suggested by (Zaremba et al., 2015). For dropout models, we train for 12 epochs and start halving the learning rate after 8 epochs. For local attention models, we empirically set the window size D=10𝐷10D=10.

answer: The author's model consists of 4 layers of LSTM, each has 100 cells, and 1000-dimensional embeddings [28].

input: How does the contrastive loss function work in deep face recognition?
question: How does the contrastive loss function work in deep face recognition?
output:
The contrastive loss function [1] is a metric learning method that embeds images into Euclidean space in which intra-variance is reduced and inter-variance is enlarged. It requires face image pairs, and then pulls together positive pairs and pushes apart negative pairs [1]. The contrastive loss function is defined as:
$$ \mathcal{L} = \max\left(0,\left\|\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{i})-\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{j})\right\|_{2}-\epsilon^{+}}\right) + (1-y_{ij})\max\left(0,\epsilon^{-}\right)-\left\|\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{i})-\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{j})\right\|_{2}^{2}\right) $$
where $y_{ij}=1$ means $\mathbf{x}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{x}_{j}$ are matching samples and $y_{ij}=0$ means non-matching samples. $\epsilon^{+}$ and $\epsilon^{-}$ control the margins of the matching and non-matching pairs respectively [1]. The contrastive loss function aims to minimize the distance between positive pairs and maximize the distance between negative pairs [1].

input: Why use a single word embedding instead of multiple? Which could capture more expressivity
question: Why use a single word embedding instead of multiple? Which could capture more expressivity
output:
The authors argue that a single word embedding can be quite effective at capturing the essence of a prompt, even though it might not capture as much expressivity as multiple words [1]. They claim that their method, which uses a single pseudo-word to represent the entire text prompt, can achieve comparable reconstruction quality to sampling random images from the training set [1]. Furthermore, they claim that their single-word method achieves considerably improved editability over all multi-word baselines [1].

They suggest that this single word embedding can be moved along a distortion-editability trade-off curve by simply changing the learning rate [2]. This allows the user to control this trade-off, achieving a balance between fidelity to the target and editability [2].

In summary, they argue that a single word embedding can be quite effective at capturing the essence of a prompt, even if it doesn't capture as much expressivity as multiple words [1].

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Our evaluation results are summarized in Figure 10(a). We highlight four observations of particular interest: First, the semantic reconstruction quality of our method and many of the baselines is comparable to simply sampling random images from the training set. Second, the single-word method achieves comparable reconstruction quality, and considerably improved editability over all multi-word baselines. These points outline the impressive flexibility of the textual embedding space, showing that it can serve to capture new concepts with a high degree of accuracy while using only a single pseudo-word.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Third, we observe that our baselines outline a distortion-editability trade-off curve, where embeddings that lie closer to the true word distribution (e.g. due to regularization, fewer pseudo-words, or a lower learning rate) can be more easily modified, but fail to capture the details of the target. In contrast, deviating far from the word distribution enables improved reconstruction at the cost of severely diminished editing capabilities. Notably, our single-embedding model can be moved along this curve by simply changing the learning rate, offering a user a degree of control over this trade-off.

answer: The authors did experiment with both single and multi word embeddings, and found that the single-pseudo word approach allowed greater editability, while still having similar accuracy and reconstruction quality when compared to multi-word approaches [61]. These reasons might explain why the authors chose a single-word embedding as their main approach [62].

input: What TACKBP-2010 means?
question: What TACKBP-2010 means?
output: The TACKBP-2010 dataset is a benchmark dataset for evaluating entity linking systems [1]. It contains 1,453 original news and web documents with 1,453 annotated mention/entity pairs derived from them [1]. The TAC Reference Knowledgebase, which contains 818,741 entities with titles, descriptions, and other meta info, is used as the knowledge base for the evaluation [1]. The TACKBP-2010 dataset is used to evaluate the performance of entity linking systems on zero-shot entity retrieval [2].

There are 1,074 and 1,020 annotated mention/entity pairs derived from 1,453 and 2,231 original news and web documents on training and evaluation dataset, respectively. All the entities are from the TAC Reference Knowledgebase which contains 818,741 entities with titles, descriptions and other meta info.

ctxs.text: There are however many other cues that could potentially be added in future work. For example, Khalife and Vazirgiannis (2018) report 94.57%percent94.5794.57\% precision on the TACKBP-2010 dataset. However, their method is based on the strong assumption that a gold fine-grained entity type is given for each mention (and they do not attempt to do entity type prediction). Indeed, if fine-grained entity type information is given by an oracle at test time, then Raiman and Raiman (2018) reports 98.6%percent98.698.6\% accuracy on TACKBP-2010, indicating that improving fine-grained entity type prediction would likely improve entity linking. Our results is achieved without gold fine-grained entity type information. Instead, our model learns representations of context, mention and entities based on text only.

answer: TACKBP-2010 is the dataset for evaluating entity linking systems that are widely used in research in this field like "Khalife and Vazirgiannis (2018)" and "Raiman and Raiman (2018)" [29]. This dataset is made in 2010 and contains the entities in the TAC Reference Knowledge Base which contains 818,741 entities with titles, descriptions, and other meta information [36]. This paper also used the TACKBP-2010 for fine-tuning the model [38].

input: If MXNet supported only a push mechanism (and no pull mechanism) available for inter-rank communication, would it still be possible to implement an all_reduce primitive on MXNet?
question: If MXNet supported only a push mechanism (and no pull mechanism) available for inter-rank communication, would it still be possible to implement an all_reduce primitive on MXNet?
output:
If MXNet only supported a push mechanism for inter-rank communication and no pull mechanism, it would be **impossible** to implement an all\_reduce primitive on MXNet.

The all\_reduce primitive is a fundamental operation in distributed machine learning, where all ranks in a group of processes must compute a reduction operation (such as summation, multiplication, or averaging) over their respective data and then broadcast the result to all ranks [1]. This operation requires both a push and a pull mechanism for efficient communication.

In a pure push mechanism, a rank sends its data to all other ranks, and each rank receives the data from all other ranks. However, this mechanism is inherently inefficient because it requires each rank to send its data to all other ranks, leading to a total of $O(N^2))$ communication volume, where $N$ is the number of ranks.

To achieve an all\_reduce primitive, both a push and a pull mechanism are necessary. The push mechanism allows each rank to send its data to all other ranks, while the pull mechanism allows each rank to receive the data from all other ranks. This combination enables an efficient all\_reduce primitive with a total communication volume of $O(N \log N))$ [1].

Without a pull mechanism, the all\_reduce primitive cannot be implemented efficiently. Each rank would have to send its data to all other ranks, resulting in a total communication volume of $O(N^2))$ [1]. This quadratic communication volume is prohibitive for large-scale systems with a large number of ranks.

Therefore, if MXNet only supported a push mechanism for inter-rank communication and no pull mechanism, it would not be possible to implement an all\_reduce primitive on MXNet [1].

ctxs.title: MXNet: A Flexible and Efficient Machine Learning Library for Heterogeneous Distributed Systems

Almost all recent ImageNet challenge  winners employ neural networks with very deep layers, requiring billions of floating-point operations to process one single sample. The rise of structural and computational complexity poses interesting challenges to ML system design and implementation.

input: What are the six classes of the data used for training ?
question: What are the six classes of the data used for training ?
output: The six classes of the data used for training are: healthy (NM), emphysema (EM), ground glass (GG), fibrosis (FB), micronodules (MN), and consolidation (CD) [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer-Aided Detection: CNN Architectures, Dataset Characteristics and Transfer Learning
ctxs.text: Interstitial Lung Disease Dataset. We utilize the publicly available dataset of . It contains 905 image slices from 120 patients, with six lung tissue types annotations containing at least one of the following: healthy (NM), emphysema (EM), ground glass (GG), fibrosis (FB), micronodules (MN) and consolidation (CD) (Figure 3). At the slice level, the objective is to classify the status of “presence/absence” of any of the six ILD classes for an input axial CT slice . Characterizing an arbitrary CT slice against any possible ILD type, without any manual ROI (in contrast to (38, 39)), can be useful for large-scale patient screening. For slice-level ILD classification, we sampled the slices 12 times with random translations and rotations. After this, we balanced the numbers of CT slice samples for the six classes by randomly sampling several instances at various rates. For patch-based classification, we sampled up to 100 patches of size 64×64646464\times 64 from each ROI. This dataset is divided into five folds with disjoint patient subsets. The average number of CT slices (training instances) per fold is small, as shown in Table I. Slice-level ILD classification is a very challenging task where CNN models need to learn from very small numbers of training examples and predict ILD labels on unseen patients.

answer: The six classes are healthy, emphysema, ground glass, fibrosis, micronodules, and consolidation [14].

input: Why is it crucial for the pipeline to identify whether the instruction represents a classification task? How are classification tasks particularly distinct or special?
question: Why is it crucial for the pipeline to identify whether the instruction represents a classification task? How are classification tasks particularly distinct or special?
output: It is crucial for the pipeline to identify whether the instruction represents a classification task because the two approaches for data generation are different [1]. Specifically, we regard tasks that have a small limited output label space as classification tasks [2]. For these tasks, we use an Output-first Approach, where we first generate the possible class labels, and then condition the input generation on each class label [4]. This approach is necessary because the Input-first Approach can generate inputs biased toward one label, especially for classification tasks [4]. In contrast, for non-classification tasks, we use an Input-first Approach, where we ask an LM to come up with the input fields first based on the instruction, and then produce the corresponding output [3]. This generation order is similar to how models are used to respond to instruction and input, but here with in-context examples from other tasks [3].

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: Our pipeline for data generation consists of four steps: 1) generating task instructions, 2) determining if the instruction represents a classification task, 3) instance generation with either an input-first or output-first approach, and 4) filtering low-quality data.

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: Because we need two different approaches for classification and non-classification tasks, we next identify whether the generated instruction represents a classification task or not.444More concretely, we regard tasks that have a small limited output label space as classification tasks. We prompt the LM in a few-shot way to determine this, using 12 classification instructions and 19 non-classification instructions from the seed tasks. The prompting template is shown in Table 6.

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: Given the instructions and their task type, we generate instances for each instruction independently. This is challenging because it requires the model to understand what the target task is, based on the instruction, figure out what additional input fields are needed and generate them, and finally complete the task by producing the output. We found that pretrained LMs can achieve this to a large extent when prompted with instruction-input-output in-context examples from other tasks. A natural way to do this is the Input-first Approach, where we can ask an LM to come up with the input fields first based on the instruction, and then produce the corresponding output. This generation order is similar to how models are used to respond to instruction and input, but here with in-context examples from other tasks. The prompting template is shown in Table 7.

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: However, we found that this approach can generate inputs biased toward one label, especially for classification tasks (e.g., for grammar error detection, it usually generates grammatical input). Therefore, we additionally propose an Output-first Approach for classification tasks, where we first generate the possible class labels, and then condition the input generation on each class label. The prompting template is shown in Table 8.555In this work, we use a fixed set of seed tasks for prompting the instance generation, and thus only generate a small number of instances per task in one round. Future work can use randomly sampled tasks to prompt the model to generate a larger number of instances in multiple rounds. We apply the output-first approach to the classification tasks identified in the former step, and the input-first approach to the remaining non-classification tasks.

answer: The main reason why this is a crucial step is because the authors’ pipeline uses a different approach for classification tasks [6]. For non-classification tasks, the authors first prompt a language model to come up with the input fields require, then provide sample inputs, for which the language model generates outputs [8]. However, for classification tasks, the authors first generate the list of classes, and then require the model to provide an example for that instruction for each class [9]. They do this because the first approach, used for non-classification instructions, does not work well for unbalanced classes [10].

input: Why are dense 1X1 convolutions computationally expensive?
question: Why are dense 1X1 convolutions computationally expensive?
output: Dense 1x1 convolutions are computationally expensive because they involve a large number of multiplications and additions between the input feature maps and the output channels [1]. Specifically, they perform a $1 \times 1$ convolution with the input feature maps to produce an output feature map of the same size as the input feature map. This operation requires the multiplication of every element in the input feature map with every element in the output channel, followed by a summation across the input channel dimension [1]. This results in a high computational cost, especially when the input and output channel dimensions are large [1].

ctxs.title: ShuffleNet: An Extremely Efficient Convolutional Neural Network for Mobile Devices
ctxs.text: We notice that state-of-the-art basic architectures such as Xception  and ResNeXt  become less efficient in extremely small networks because of the costly dense 1×1111\times 1 convolutions. We propose using pointwise group convolutions to reduce computation complexity of 1×1111\times 1 convolutions. To overcome the side effects brought by group convolutions, we come up with a novel channel shuffle operation to help the information flowing across feature channels. Based on the two techniques, we build a highly efficient architecture called ShuffleNet. Compared with popular structures like  (30, 9, 40), for a given computation complexity budget, our ShuffleNet allows more feature map channels, which helps to encode more information and is especially critical to the performance of very small networks.

answer: The 1x1 convolutions are expensive in extremely reduced versions of Xception and ResNeXt as they might take 934% of multiplication-adds for each residual unit [1].

input: What are quantized depth planes, probabilistic disparity maps, and view-dependent flow fields?
question: What are quantized depth planes, probabilistic disparity maps, and view-dependent flow fields?
output:
Quantized depth planes are a way to represent the underlying 3D geometry explicitly in depth estimation methods [1]. They are discrete representations of depth, where each pixel in the input image is assigned a discrete depth value from a predefined set of levels [1]. These levels are typically coarse, meaning that the depth information is quantized into a limited number of bins [1].

Probabilistic disparity maps are another way to represent the underlying 3D geometry in depth estimation methods [1]. They are probabilistic predictions of disparity, where each pixel in the input image has an associated probability distribution over possible disparity values [1]. These distributions are often learned jointly with the depth prediction [1].

View-dependent flow fields are a way to represent the underlying geometry implicitly in appearance flow methods [1]. They are dense optical flow fields that predict the displacement of pixels from the input view to the target view [1]. These fields are learned jointly with the appearance transformation [1].

In summary, these are different ways to represent the underlying geometry in depth estimation methods [1] and view synthesis methods [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: One important application of geometric scene understanding is the task of novel view synthesis, where the goal is to synthesize the appearance of the scene seen from novel camera viewpoints. A classic paradigm for view synthesis is to first either estimate the underlying 3D geometry explicitly or establish pixel correspondence among input views, and then synthesize the novel views by compositing image patches from the input views (e.g., (4, 55, 43, 6, 9)). Recently, end-to-end learning has been applied to reconstruct novel views by transforming the input based on depth or flow, e.g., DeepStereo , Deep3D  and Appearance Flows . In these methods, the underlying geometry is represented by quantized depth planes (DeepStereo), probabilistic disparity maps (Deep3D) and view-dependent flow fields (Appearance Flows), respectively. Unlike methods that directly map from input views to the target view (e.g., ), warping-based methods are forced to learn intermediate predictions of geometry and/or correspondence. In this work, we aim to distill such geometric reasoning capability from CNNs trained to perform warping-based view synthesis.

answer: quantized depth planes, probabilistic disparity maps, and view-dependent flow fields are methods to represent the underlying geometry of the scene [4].

input: Given the triggered sentences, how can this problem be rectified?
question: Given the triggered sentences, how can this problem be rectified?
output: The problem of integrating the strength and reliability of classical AI models in logical reasoning with LMs can be rectified by adopting Backward Chaining (BC) [1]. BC starts from the goal statement and uses the rules to recursively decompose it into sub-goals until the sub-goals can be proved or disproved based on the theory [1]. This approach is more suitable for LMs as it avoids the combinatorial search over a large space required by Forward Chaining (FC) [1]. BC also avoids the challenging decision of when to halt and declare failure to prove, which is sometimes required in FC [1].

ctxs.title: LAMBADA: Backward Chaining for Automated Reasoning in Natural Language
ctxs.text: One solution to the aforementioned problems is to integrate the strength and reliability of classical AI models in logical reasoning with LMs Garcez and Lamb (2020); Marcus (2020). In the literature, there are two major approaches to logical reasoning Poole and Mackworth (2010):   1.  Forward Chaining (FC) where one starts from the facts and rules (“theory”), and iterates between making new inferences and adding them to the theory until the goal statement can be proved or disproved,    2.  Backward Chaining (BC) where one starts from the goal and uses the rules to recursively decompose it into sub-goals until the sub-goals can be proved or disproved based on the theory.    Previous approaches to reasoning with LMs mostly incorporate elements of FC into LMs Tafjord et al. (2021); Creswell et al. (2023). FC requires selecting a subset of facts and rules from the entire set, which might be difficult for an LM as it requires a combinatorial search over a large space. Moreover, deciding when to halt and declare failure to prove is challenging in FC, as also noted by Creswell et al. (2023), sometimes requiring specialized modules trained on intermediate labels Creswell and Shanahan (2022). Indeed, the classical automated reasoning literature is heavily weighted towards BC or goal-directed strategies for proof-finding.

answer: The triggered sentence is "One solution to the aforementioned problems is to integrate the strength and reliability of classical AI models in logical reasoning with LMs Garcez and Lamb " [2].

input: How is DPR retriever different from BM25?
question: How is DPR retriever different from BM25?
output:
DPR (Cross-Encoder) is a more sophisticated retriever than BM25. BM25 is a traditional sparse retriever that relies on term frequency-inverse document frequency (tf-idf) weighting to score the relevance of a passage to a query [3]. In contrast, DPR uses a cross-encoder architecture to compute a dense representation for both the query and the passage [1]. This dense representation is then used to compute a similarity score between the query and the passage, which is more effective at capturing the nuanced semantic relationship between them [1].

While both BM25 and DPR are used as initial stage methods for passage re-ranking, DPR leverages PLMs to empower the retriever by a single vector [1]. Other researches, such as RepBERT [2], ColBERT [2], COIL [1], and Interactor [1], obtain multiple vectors for matching [1]. In contrast, DPR uses a single vector for matching [1].

In summary, DPR is a more advanced retriever than BM25 because it computes a dense representation for both the query and the passage using PLMs, which is more effective at capturing the nuanced semantic relationship between them [1].

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: The low-dimensional dense representations for query and passage are computed by PLMs based retrievers from the dual-encoder architecture. Afterward, the candidate passage set could be retrieved efficiently via approximate nearest neighbor algorithms. Existing studies could be categorized into two parts: (1) By optimizing the matching stage: DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) is the first study to leverage PLM to empower the retriever by a single vector. Other researches, such as RepBERT (Zhan et al., 2020), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), COIL (Gao et al., 2021) and Interactor (Ye et al., 2022), obtain multiple vectors for query and passage for matching. (2) By optimizing the representation learning module: RocketQAv1 (Qu et al., 2021) and RocketQAv2 (Ren et al., 2021) boost the representation learning of retriever by leveraging the power of cross-encoder in a cascade or joint manner. Other studies boost the representation learning by designed IR-oriented pre-training tasks. ICT (Lee et al., 2019) treats sentences as pseudo-queries and matched them to the passage they originate from. Condenser (Gao and Callan, 2021) utilizes a novel pre-training task, which can produces an information-rich representation to condense an input sequence.

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: We use the traditional sparse retriever BM25 (Yang et al., 2017) as our first stage method. All experiments are conducted under the same BM25 setting with 1000 retrieved candidates. We conduct experiments with the deep learning framework PaddlePaddle (Ma et al., 2019) on up to 4 NVIDIA Tesla A100 GPUs (with 40G RAM). For the GMN module, we use Paddle Graph Learning (PGL) 222https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PGL, an efficient and flexible graph learning framework based on PaddlePaddle. For training, we used the Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2014) with a learning rate of 1e-5 for text encoder and 1e-4 for knowledge injector. The model is trained up to 5 epochs with a batch size of 640 and 240 for base and large models respectively. In our experiments, the PLM small, base and large models have 6, 12 and 24 Transformer layers respectively. The text encoder has 9 layers and 21 layers for base and large model respectively, and the knowledge injector both has 3 layers in our experiment. The dropout rates are set to 0.1. The ratio of the positive to the hard negative is set to 1:19. All transformer layers in KERM’s backbone are initialized from ERNIE-2.0 base (Sun et al., 2020b), which is a BERT-like model pre-trained with a continual pre-training framework on multiple tasks. We perform Knowledge-enhanced pre-training on MARCO passage collection to warm up the parameters in knowledge injector, which has 60,000 iterations under the batch size of 256. For a fair comparison, the same pre-training without knowledge enhancement is also conducted on ERNIEbasesubscriptERNIEbase\textrm{ERNIE}_{\textrm{base}} re-ranker and all models in ablation studies.

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: Given a query q, passage re-ranking aims at ordering a set of ϰitalic-ϰ\varkappa passages, i.e., 𝒫={pκ}κ=1ϰ𝒫superscriptsubscriptsubscriptp𝜅𝜅1italic-ϰ\mathcal{P}=\left\{\textbf{p}_{\kappa}\right\}_{\kappa=1}^{\varkappa}, which is usually retrieved from a large-scale passage collection by a retriever, e.g. BM25 (Yang et al., 2017), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) etc. In particular, a passage is a sequence of words p={wp}p=1|p|psuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑤𝑝𝑝1p\textbf{p}=\{w_{p}\}_{p=1}^{|\textbf{p}|}, where |p|p|\textbf{p}| is the length of passage p. Similarly, a query is a sequence of words q={wq}q=1|q|qsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑤𝑞𝑞1q\textbf{q}=\{w_{q}\}_{q=1}^{|\textbf{q}|}. Note that a passage p consists of T𝑇T sentences p={sτ}τ=1Tpsuperscriptsubscriptsubscripts𝜏𝜏1𝑇\textbf{p}=\{\textbf{s}_{\tau}\}_{\tau=1}^{T}.

answer: BM25 and DPR are both examples of retrievers used in large-scale passage collection [11]. BM25 is described as a traditional sparse retriever and DPR leverages PLM to empower the retriever by a single vector [43]. How both BM25 and DPR function is not described in detail in this paper and thus their differences cannot be answered in this paper [14].

input: Going deep through network layers makes it harder to remember shallower local information, wouldn't that make segmentation harder?
question: Going deep through network layers makes it harder to remember shallower local information, wouldn't that make segmentation harder?
output:
Going deep through network layers does not necessarily make segmentation harder, as the deeper layers can learn more abstract and global information [1]. While shallower layers capture local information, deeper layers use filters with much broader receptive fields to capture global information [1]. This allows the network to build a hierarchical representation of raw input data, where higher-level features are composed of lower-level features [1]. This hierarchical structure is beneficial for segmentation tasks, as it enables the network to learn both fine-grained local details and coarse global structures [1].

In fact, the ability of CNNs to learn hierarchical representations without relying on handcrafted features has led to state-of-the-art performances in various medical image segmentation tasks [1]. By progressively increasing the receptive field size as the network deepens, the network can capture both local and global information, leading to more accurate and robust segmentation results [1].

ctxs.title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: Recent research in computer vision and pattern recognition has highlighted the capabilities of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to solve challenging tasks such as classification, segmentation and object detection, achieving state-of-the-art performances. This success has been attributed to the ability of CNNs to learn a hierarchical representation of raw input data, without relying on handcrafted features. As the inputs are processed through the network layers, the level of abstraction of the resulting features increases. Shallower layers grasp local information while deeper layers use filters whose receptive fields are much broader that therefore capture global information .

answer: Since non anatomy part has a much larger spatial support than the anatomy and As we move down the layers in a CNN the receptive field of the features increases therefore proposed CNN would work fine for the local information [0].

input: Why was a zero-centered input used for training the paper's DNN, instead of using the training images as input directly?
question: Why was a zero-centered input used for training the paper's DNN, instead of using the training images as input directly?
output: The authors used a zero-centered input for training their DNN instead of using the training images as input directly because they wanted to see how the network learns to process inputs that have been centered around the mean of the dataset [1]. By subtracting the per-pixel mean of examples in ImageNet before inputting training examples to the network, they ensured that the direct input to the network, $\mathbf{x}$, could be thought of as a zero-centered input [1]. This preprocessing step helps the network learn to handle inputs that are already normalized, which can lead to faster convergence and better generalization performance [1].

Thus, the direct input to the network, 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x}, can be thought of as a zero-centered input. We may pose the optimization problem as finding an image 𝐱∗superscript𝐱\mathbf{x^{*}} where

input: What are the examples in which important structural information is removed when masking the image content?
question: What are the examples in which important structural information is removed when masking the image content?
output: In the paper excerpt, it is mentioned that the masking procedure is cumbersome and hampers quick and intuitive text-driven editing [1]. Furthermore, removing important structural information from the image content during the inpainting process is a limitation of LLI-based methods [1]. This limitation means that some editing capabilities are out of the inpainting scope, such as modifying the texture of a specific object [1].

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: To circumvent this, LLI-based methods (28, 4, 33) require the user to explicitly mask a part of the image to be inpainted, and drive the edited image to change in the masked area only, while matching the background of the original image. This approach has provided appealing results, however, the masking procedure is cumbersome, hampering quick and intuitive text-driven editing. Moreover, masking the image content removes important structural information, which is completely ignored in the inpainting process. Therefore, some editing capabilities are out of the inpainting scope, such as modifying the texture of a specific object.

answer: Examples such as modifying textures of specific objects or changing bicycles in an image to a car [1].

input: How input image resolution affects the accuracy of the SSD framework?
question: How input image resolution affects the accuracy of the SSD framework?
output: The paper states that the SSD framework can achieve high-accuracy detection using relatively low resolution input [1]. This is achieved by using multiple layers for prediction at different scales [1]. Specifically, the SSD framework uses a small convolutional filter to predict object categories and offsets in bounding box locations [1]. This allows it to perform detection at multiple scales [1]. By applying these filters to multiple feature maps from the later stages of a network, the SSD framework can achieve high-accuracy detection even with low resolution input [1].

ctxs.title: SSD: Single Shot MultiBox Detector
ctxs.text: This paper presents the first deep network based object detector that does not resample pixels or features for bounding box hypotheses and and is as accurate as approaches that do. This results in a significant improvement in speed for high-accuracy detection (59 FPS with mAP 74.3% on VOC2007 test, vs. Faster R-CNN 7 FPS with mAP 73.2% or YOLO 45 FPS with mAP 63.4%). The fundamental improvement in speed comes from eliminating bounding box proposals and the subsequent pixel or feature resampling stage. We are not the first to do this (cf (4, 5)), but by adding a series of improvements, we manage to increase the accuracy significantly over previous attempts. Our improvements include using a small convolutional filter to predict object categories and offsets in bounding box locations, using separate predictors (filters) for different aspect ratio detections, and applying these filters to multiple feature maps from the later stages of a network in order to perform detection at multiple scales. With these modifications—especially using multiple layers for prediction at different scales—we can achieve high-accuracy using relatively low resolution input, further increasing detection speed. While these contributions may seem small independently, we note that the resulting system improves accuracy on real-time detection for PASCAL VOC from 63.4% mAP for YOLO to 74.3% mAP for our SSD. This is a larger relative improvement in detection accuracy than that from the recent, very high-profile work on residual networks . Furthermore, significantly improving the speed of high-quality detection can broaden the range of settings where computer vision is useful.

answer: The accuracy of SSD framework is relatively more on higher resolution images than on lower resolution images [1].

Most of the outputs stay constant over many layers forming a pattern of stripes. Most of the change in outputs happens in the early layers (≈15absent15\approx 15 for MNIST and ≈40absent40\approx 40 for CIFAR-100).

input: What is the numeric value of dropout fraction used during the training phase?
question: What is the numeric value of dropout fraction used during the training phase?
output: The numeric value of dropout fraction used during the training phase is not explicitly mentioned in the provided paper excerpt [1]. However, the authors mention that they used dropout as a way of training an exponentially large ensemble of models that share weights [1].

ctxs.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
ctxs.text: To see how well distillation works, we trained a single large neural net with two hidden layers of 1200 rectified linear hidden units on all 60,000 training cases. The net was strongly regularized using dropout and weight-constraints as described in . Dropout can be viewed as a way of training an exponentially large ensemble of models that share weights. In addition, the input images were jittered by up to two pixels in any direction. This net achieved 67 test errors whereas a smaller net with two hidden layers of 800 rectified linear hidden units and no regularization achieved 146 errors. But if the smaller net was regularized solely by adding the additional task of matching the soft targets produced by the large net at a temperature of 20, it achieved 74 test errors. This shows that soft targets can transfer a great deal of knowledge to the distilled model, including the knowledge about how to generalize that is learned from translated training data even though the transfer set does not contain any translations.

answer: The neural net in this work was strongly regularized using dropout and weight constraints as described in [5] [12].

input: Which RGBD robotic grasping dataset was used for verification?
question: Which RGBD robotic grasping dataset was used for verification?
output: The paper used the extended version of the Cornell grasping dataset for their experiments [1]. This dataset contains 1035 images of 280 graspable objects, several of which are shown in Fig. 9 [1]. Each image is annotated with several ground-truth positive and negative grasping rectangles [1].

ctxs.title: Deep learning for detecting robotic grasps
ctxs.text: We used the extended version of the Cornell grasping dataset for our experiments. This dataset, along with code for this paper, is available at http://pr.cs.cornell.edu/deepgrasping. We note that this is an updated version of the dataset used in , containing several more complex objects, and thus results for their algorithms will be different from those in . This dataset contains 1035 images of 280 graspable objects, several of which are shown in Fig. 9. Each image is annotated with several ground-truth positive and negative grasping rectangles. While the vast majority of possible rectangles for most objects will be non-graspable, the dataset contains roughly equal numbers of graspable and non-graspable rectangles. We will show that this is useful for an unsupervised learning algorithm, as it allows learning a good representation for graspable rectangles even from unlabeled data.

answer: We used the extended version of the Cornell grasping dataset for our experiments [61].

input: Why author did 7.3 Non-english and computer languages tast? What is the objective of this section?

Our analysis reveals that the advantages of pretraining surprisingly persist with various degrees across these variations, suggesting hithertho unexplored connections between pretraining and the learning abilities of language models. Our contributions are:
