answer: The affine classifier is the classifier in the form of an affine function [10]. The general form that is used in the paper is the function f: R^n -> R^m, where f(x) = W^T * x + B, for the given matrix and vector W and B [5].

input: What are the pretraining datasets used in analyses?
question: What are the pretraining datasets used in analyses?
output: The pretraining datasets used in the analyses are: English Wikipedia (11.8GB); BookCorpus (Zhu et al. 2015, 4.2GB); Stories (Trinh and Le 2018, 31GB); OpenWebText (Gokaslan and Cohen 2019, 38GB), which is an open-source version of WebText Radford et al. (2019); CC-NEWS (Liu et al. 2019, 76 GB); and C4.En (Raffel et al. 2020, 305GB), as provided by Dodge et al. (2021) [1].

ctxs.title: Language Contamination Helps Explain the Cross-lingual Capabilities of English Pretrained Models
ctxs.text: We consider the following pretraining datasets: English Wikipedia (11.8GB); BookCorpus (Zhu et al. 2015, 4.2GB); Stories (Trinh and Le 2018, 31GB); OpenWebText (Gokaslan and Cohen 2019, 38GB), which is an open-source version of WebText Radford et al. (2019); CC-NEWS (Liu et al. 2019, 76 GB); and C4.En (Raffel et al. 2020, 305GB), as provided by Dodge et al. (2021). We use the versions of Wikipedia, BookCorpus, and CC-NEWS used to pretrain RoBERTa.

answer: English Wikipedia, BookCorpus, Stories, OpenWebText, CC-NEWS, and C4En datasets were used in pretraining [6].

input: What are the metrics used to evaluate the trade-off between the quality and diversity of generated captions?
question: What are the metrics used to evaluate the trade-off between the quality and diversity of generated captions?
output: The paper evaluates the quality of the generated captions using five accuracy-based metrics, including BLEU-4 (B4), METEOR (M), ROUGE (R), CIDEr-D (C), and SPICE (S) [1]. They also propose a new recall-based metric to evaluate whether the roles of the generated sentence are consistent with the ground truth caption (\ie, VSR). This metric measures the recall rate of the verb, semantic roles, and ordered role pairs, which are denoted as RVV{}_{\text{V}}}, RSR1SR1{}_{\text{SR1}}}, and RSR2SR2{}_{\text{SR2}}}, respectively [1].

ctxs.title: Human-Like Controllable Image Captioning With Verb-Specific Semantic Roles
ctxs.text: Evaluation Metrics. To evaluate the quality of the generated captions, we use five accuracy-based metrics, including BLEU-4 (B4) , METEOR (M) , ROUGE (R) , CIDEr-D (C) , and SPICE (S) . Particularly, we evaluate the generated captions against the single ground truth caption. We also propose a new recall-based metric to evaluate whether the roles of the generated sentence are consistent with the ground truth caption (\ie, VSR). It measures the recall rate of the verb, semantic roles, and ordered role pairs, which are denoted as RVV{}_{\text{V}}, RSR1SR1{}_{\text{SR1}} and RSR2SR2{}_{\text{SR2}}, respectively.

answer: Authors used BlEU, METOR, ROUGE, CIDEr, and SPICE to evaluate quality based generated captions, And used Accuracy-based,  Diversity-based metrics to evaluate diversity based generation captions [39].

input: Are English pretrained language models good at transfer to other language?
question: Are English pretrained language models good at transfer to other language?
output: The paper excerpt suggests that English pretrained language models are surprisingly good at cross-lingual transfer, even though they have ostensibly never seen the target language before [1]. This surprising ability is attributed to the large scale of pretraining data and the fact that many pretraining corpora are not publicly available [1]. The authors argue that this contamination with very small percentages of text from other languages is almost certainly present in the monolingual pretraining corpora [1]. This contamination allows for cross-lingual transfer from such data leakage in the pretraining corpus [1].

ctxs.title: Language Contamination Helps Explain the Cross-lingual Capabilities of English Pretrained Models
ctxs.text: Recent work has claimed that monolingual pretrained models are also surprisingly good at transferring between languages, despite ostensibly having never seen the target language before (Gogoulou et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021, inter alia). However, because of the large scale of pretraining data and because many pretraining corpora are not publicly available, it is currently unknown how much foreign language data exists in monolingual pretraining corpora. In this paper, we show that (1) these data are almost certainly contaminated with very small percentages of text from other languages and that (2) cross-lingual transfer is possible from such data leakage in the pretraining corpus.

answer: No, they are not, relative to models trained on corpora with non-English text [1].

input: Define how a linear convolution layer functions.
question: Define how a linear convolution layer functions.
output: A linear convolution layer, as described in the paper excerpt, is a simple operation that takes an input feature map and applies a $1 \times 1$ convolution with a linear projection. This operation is used to project the high-dimensional features back to a low-dimensional representation [1]. Specifically, the linear bottleneck layer is designed to be computationally efficient while maintaining the expressive power of the inverted residual block [1].

ctxs.title: MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks
ctxs.text: Our main contribution is a novel layer module: the inverted residual with linear bottleneck. This module takes as an input a low-dimensional compressed representation which is first expanded to high dimension and filtered with a lightweight depthwise convolution. Features are subsequently projected back to a low-dimensional representation with a linear convolution. The official implementation is available as part of TensorFlow-Slim model library in .

answer: Linear convolution layer projects the filtered high-dimensional representation to low-dimensional subspace [2].

input: What exactly is "smoothing" and how does it help count-based LMs account for unseen sequences?
question: What exactly is "smoothing" and how does it help count-based LMs account for unseen sequences?
output:
In the context of language modeling, "smoothing" is a technique used to address the issue of zero probabilities for unseen sequences. In simple count-based LMs, the probability of an N-gram is estimated by dividing the count of that N-gram by the total count of all N-grams. However, for any N-gram that has never been seen in the training data, the count is zero, leading to a probability of zero. This zero probability is problematic because it implies that the model believes the unseen sequence is impossible, which is not true in reality.

Smoothing techniques, such as Laplace (or Laplace's rule of succession) and Kneser-Ney smoothing, are designed to assign non-zero probabilities to unseen sequences. They achieve this by adding a small amount of pseudo-count to the observed counts of all N-grams, including those that have never been seen. This addition is typically proportional to the size of the vocabulary or the total count of all N-grams.

For example, Laplace smoothing adds a uniform count of 1 to all N-grams, regardless of their observed counts. This is equivalent to using a backoff model, where the probability of an N-gram is estimated using the lower-order N-gram counts when the higher-order N-gram count is zero.

Kneser-Ney smoothing is a more sophisticated form of smoothing that assigns a backoff probability based on the lower-order N-gram counts. It works by adding a count of $\delta$ to the observed counts of all N-grams, where $\delta$ is a small positive value that depends on the size of the vocabulary and the total count of all N-grams. This addition is done in a way that preserves the original counts as much as possible while ensuring that all N-grams have a non-zero probability.

In summary, smoothing techniques help count-based LMs account for unseen sequences by assigning them non-zero probabilities, which prevents the model from assigning zero probability to any sequence. This addition of pseudo-counts helps to mitigate the issue of zero probabilities for unseen sequences and leads to more robust and generalizable models.

ctxs.title: Exploring the Limits of Language Modeling
ctxs.text: Language Modeling (LM) has been a central task in NLP. The goal of LM is to learn a probability distribution over sequences of symbols pertaining to a language. Much work has been done on both parametric (e.g., log-linear models) and non-parametric approaches (e.g., count-based LMs). Count-based approaches (based on statistics of N-grams) typically add smoothing which account for unseen (yet possible) sequences, and have been quite successful. To this extent, Kneser-Ney smoothed 5-gram models (Kneser & Ney, 1995) are a fairly strong baseline which, for large amounts of training data, have challenged other parametric approaches based on Neural Networks (Bengio et al., 2006).

answer: The paper does not discuss what smoothing is or how it helps some LMs account for unseen sequences [10].

input: Authors explain the challenges AI models face when dealing with longer time sequences. Would human performance on this task also decline when analyzing longer sequnces?
question: Authors explain the challenges AI models face when dealing with longer time sequences. Would human performance on this task also decline when analyzing longer sequnces?
output: Human performance on this task would also decline when analyzing longer sequences. While human performance on short utterances is generally high, it also degrades when processing longer utterances [1]. This is because humans rely on context and attention mechanisms to disambiguate similar speech fragments, a strategy that becomes increasingly difficult as the input sequence grows longer [1]. As the input sequence extends, the probability of encountering similar speech fragments increases, making it harder for humans to maintain accurate alignment between the input and the target sequence [1]. This degradation in performance is particularly noticeable in tasks requiring fine-grained alignment, such as phoneme-level alignment [1].

ctxs.title: Attention-Based Models for Speech Recognition
ctxs.text: We start with a model proposed in  for the machine translation task as the baseline. This model seems entirely vulnerable to the issue of similar speech fragments but despite our expectations it was competitive on the original test set, reaching 18.7% phoneme error rate (PER). However, its performance degraded quickly with longer, concatenated utterances. We provide evidence that this model adapted to track the absolute location in the input sequence of the content it is recognizing, a strategy feasible for short utterances from the original test set but inherently unscalable.

answer: While the authors explain the issue of dealing with longer time sequences, this question cannot be answered in this paper since there is no evidential information about human performance [4].

input: How do the authors recognize that reducing the classifier-free guidance parameter improves reconstruction but constrains the ability to perform significant manipulation?
question: How do the authors recognize that reducing the classifier-free guidance parameter improves reconstruction but constrains the ability to perform significant manipulation?
output:
The authors recognize that reducing the classifier-free guidance parameter (i.e., reducing the prompt influence) improves reconstruction but constrains their ability to perform significant manipulations by observing that this reduction leads to a "distortion-editability tradeoff" [1]. Specifically, they find that reducing the classifier-free guidance parameter improves the fidelity of the inversion process, resulting in more accurate image reconstructions [1]. However, they also note that this reduction constrains their ability to perform "significant manipulations" on the input image [1].

This observation suggests that the classifier-free guidance parameter plays a crucial role in balancing the fidelity of the inversion process with the controllability of the manipulation [1]. When this parameter is reduced, the guidance from the text prompt is weakened, allowing the model to rely more on the latent representation of the input image [1]. This leads to better reconstruction quality but also makes it harder to enforce strong constraints from the text prompt [1].

In essence, the authors recognize that this tradeoff implies that they must carefully tune the classifier-free guidance parameter to achieve a desired balance between fidelity and controllability [1].

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: This inversion process often produces satisfying results, as presented in fig. 10. However, the inversion is not sufficiently accurate in many other cases, as in fig. 11. This is partially due to a distortion-editability tradeoff , where we recognize that reducing the classifier-free guidance  parameter (i.e., reducing the prompt influence) improves reconstruction but constrains our ability to perform significant manipulations.

answer: By observed that in the referenced [18] work shop "Generative models and downstream applications, 2021" [35].

input: YoloV3 is most suited for small, medium or large size objects?
question: YoloV3 is most suited for small, medium or large size objects?
output: Based on the paper excerpt, YOLOv3 has relatively high APS performance on small objects [1]. However, it has comparatively worse performance on medium and larger size objects [1]. This suggests that YOLOv3 is most suited for small objects [1].

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: In the past YOLO struggled with small objects. However, now we see a reversal in that trend. With the new multi-scale predictions we see YOLOv3 has relatively high APS performance. However, it has comparatively worse performance on medium and larger size objects. More investigation is needed to get to the bottom of this.

answer: YOLOv3 now struggles more with medium and larger size objects, ie, performs worse than before [21].

input: What are the results we can get after going through the proposed Transformer-based model?
question: What are the results we can get after going through the proposed Transformer-based model?
output:
The proposed model, which combines the strengths of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Transformers, achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) empirical performance on various graph representation learning tasks, including the CODE2 dataset [1]. Specifically, the SAT model consistently outperforms other GNNs and Transformers on these benchmarks, demonstrating its ability to effectively combine the benefits of both paradigms [1].

In the CODE2 dataset, the SAT model achieves the first place on the OGB leaderboard, significantly surpassing the performance of SOTA methods [1]. This indicates that the model's structural awareness allows it to capture complex relational information within the code structure, leading to superior performance on code understanding and generation tasks [2].

Furthermore, the SAT model provides better interpretability than the standard Transformer architecture [2]. This is attributed to its explicit incorporation of structural information into the self-attention mechanism, which allows it to attend to specific substructures within the graph [2]. This interpretability is crucial for tasks requiring human-like reasoning and debugging capabilities, such as code generation and repair [1].

In summary, the proposed model achieves SOTA empirical performance on various graph representation learning tasks, including the CODE2 dataset [1]. Its structural awareness enables it to effectively combine the benefits of both GNNs and Transformers, leading to superior performance and interpretability [2].

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: We show the performance of SATs compared to other GNNs and Transformers in Table 1 and 2. SAT models consistently outperform SOTA methods on these datasets, showing its ability to combine the benefits of both GNNs and Transformers. In particular, for the CODE2 dataset, our SAT models outperform SOTA methods by a large margin despite a relatively small number of parameters and minimal hyperparameter tuning, which will put it at the first place on the OGB leaderboard.

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: We introduced the SAT model, which successfully incorporates structural information into the Transformer architecture and overcomes the limitations of the absolute encoding. In addition to SOTA empirical performance with minimal hyperparameter tuning, SAT also provides better interpretability than the Transformer.

answer: SAT predicts class of nodes and graphs better than other SOTA models [36]. Also, SAT is more explainable compared to other transformer-based models [43].

input: The brain tumour segmentation data consists of 274 cases in total, is this dataset large enough to not consider adding regularisation technics ?
question: The brain tumour segmentation data consists of 274 cases in total, is this dataset large enough to not consider adding regularisation technics ?
output: The paper states that they do not employ intensity perturbation and dropout on convolutional layers because the network should not require as much regularisation with this large database [1]. This suggests that the dataset size of 274 cases is considered large enough to not consider adding regularisation techniques [1].

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: Network configuration and training: We modify the DeepMedic architecture to handle multi-class problems by extending the classification layer to five feature maps (four tumor classes plus background). The rest of the configuration remains unchanged. We enrich the dataset with sagittal reflections. Opposite to the experiments on TBI, we do not employ the intensity perturbation and dropout on convolutional layers, because the network should not require as much regularisation with this large database. The network is trained on image segments extracted with equal probability centred on the whole tumor and healthy tissue. The distribution of the classes captured by our training scheme is provided in C.

answer: The authors mention that they reduced the amount of regularization techniques as they consider the BRATS database to be large [56].

input: How can author claim that language models perform better if they use prior knowledge as well as context?
question: How can author claim that language models perform better if they use prior knowledge as well as context?
output: The authors claim that language models can perform better if they use prior knowledge as well as context because they argue that existing models only consider the contexts to predict the missing word [1]. They point out that these models do not consider the prior knowledge in the sentence, which is crucial for understanding the relationships between entities in the text [1]. For example, in the sentence "Harry Potter is a series of fantasy novels written by J.K. Rowling," the model can easily predict the missing word "Harry Potter" by word collocations inside this entity without the help of long contexts [1]. However, the model cannot predict "Harry Potter" according to the relationship between "Harry Potter" and "J.K. Rowling" [1]. The authors suggest that if the model learns more about prior knowledge, it can obtain more reliable language representation [1].

ctxs.title: ERNIE: Enhanced Representation through Knowledge Integration
ctxs.text: The vast majority of these studies model the representations by predicting the missing word only through the contexts. These works do not consider the prior knowledge in the sentence. For example, In the sentence ” Harry Potter is a series of fantasy novels written by J. K. Rowling”. Harry Potter is a novel name and J. K. Rowling is the writer. It is easy for the model to predict the missing word of the entity  Harry Potter by word collocations inside this entity without the help of long contexts. The model cannot predict  Harry Potter according to the relationship between  Harry Potter and J. K. Rowling. It is intuitive that if the model learns more about prior knowledge, the model can obtain more reliable language representation.

answer: People share a lot of prior knowledge when they talk each other [1].

input: The authors claims that the probing mechanism combined with multi-orientation pooling can capture any 3D structure, is this true ?
question: The authors claims that the probing mechanism combined with multi-orientation pooling can capture any 3D structure, is this true ?
output: The authors claim that their anisotropic probing network, combined with multi-orientation pooling, can capture any 3D structure [2]. This is because their probing mechanism is capable of capturing internal structures of objects through its X-ray like projection mechanism [2]. This ability is not offered by standard rendering [2]. By combining this with multi-orientation pooling, they claim that their network can capture any 3D structure due to its relationship with the Radon transform [2].

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: Both of our new networks are sensitive to shape orientation, i.e., they capture different information at different orientations. To capture a more holistic sense of a 3D object, we add an orientation pooling stage that aggregates information from different orientations.

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: Our anistropic probing network is capable of capturing internal structures of objects through its X-ray like projection mechanism. This is an ability not offered by standard rendering. Combined with multi-orientation pooling (introduced below), it is possible for this probing mechanism to capture any 3D structure, due to its relationship with the Radon transform.

answer: It is true that the probing mechanism has a relationship with the Radon transform, which is an integral transform whose inverse is used to reconstruct images from medical CT scans and other complex 3D structures such as a map of a planet's polar regions (https://mathworldwolframcom/RadonTransformhtml#:~:text=The%20Radon%20transform%20is%20an,(Roulston%20and%20Muhleman%201997)) [20]. Additionally, orientation pooling aggragates information from different orientations, thus carrying only partial information about the object, which makes it robust to different objects and avoids overfitting to the objects from the training dataset [28].

input: Who is responsible for designating the control signal?
question: Who is responsible for designating the control signal?
output: The Verb-specific Semantic Roles (VSR) are proposed as the control signal for generating customized captions [2]. The authors formally represent a control signal VSR as: $\mathcal{VSR}=\{v,<s_{1},n_{1}>,...,<s_{m},n_{m}>\}$, where $v$ is a verb capturing the scope of a salient activity in the image (\eg, ride), $s_{i}$ is a semantic role of verb $v$ (\eg, LOC), and $n_{i}$ is the number of interested entities in the role $s_{i}$ [2].

ctxs.title: Human-Like Controllable Image Captioning With Verb-Specific Semantic Roles
ctxs.text: Image captioning, \ie, generating fluent and meaningful descriptions to summarize the salient contents of an image, is a classic proxy task for comprehensive scene understanding . With the release of several large scale datasets and advanced encoder-decoder frameworks, current captioning models plausibly have already achieved “super-human” performance in all accuracy-based evaluation metrics. However, many studies have indicated that these models tend to produce generic descriptions, and fail to control the caption generation process as humans, \eg, referring to different contents of interest or descriptive patterns. In order to endow the captioning models with human-like controllability, a recent surge of efforts (16, 10, 19, 78, 48, 77, 27, 20) resort to introducing extra control signals as constraints of the generated captions, called Controllable Image Captioning (CIC). As a byproduct, the CIC models can easily generate diverse descriptions by feeding different control signals.

ctxs.title: Human-Like Controllable Image Captioning With Verb-Specific Semantic Roles
ctxs.text: For human-like controllable image captioning, we first propose the Verb-specific Semantic Roles (VSR) as the control signal for generating customized captions. As shown in Figure 3, we formally represent a control signal VSR as:    𝒱𝒮ℛ={v,<s1,n1>,…,<sm,nm>},\displaystyle\begin{aligned} \mathcal{VSR}=\{v,<s_{1},n_{1}>,...,<s_{m},n_{m}>\},\\ \end{aligned}  (1)   where v𝑣v is a verb capturing the scope of a salient activity in the image (\eg, ride), sisubscript𝑠𝑖s_{i} is a semantic role of verb v𝑣v (\eg, LOC), and nisubscript𝑛𝑖n_{i} is the number of interested entities in the role sisubscript𝑠𝑖s_{i}. For example, for 𝒱𝒮ℛ={ride,<Arg0,1>,<Arg1,1>,<Loc,2>}\mathcal{VSR}=\{\texttt{ride},<\texttt{Arg0},\texttt{1}>,<\texttt{Arg1},\texttt{1}>,<\texttt{Loc},\texttt{2}>\}, we hope to generate a caption which not only focuses on describing the ride activity, but also contains one entity respectively in the role Arg0riderrider{}_{\text{rider}} and Arg1steedsteed{}_{\text{steed}}, and two entities in the role LOC. Thus, VSR can effectively control the amount of information carried in the whole sentence and each role, \ie, the level of details.

ctxs.title: Human-Like Controllable Image Captioning With Verb-Specific Semantic Roles
ctxs.text: In this paper, we argued that all existing objective control signals for CIC have overlooked two indispensable characteristics: event-compatible and sample-suitable. To this end, we proposed a novel control signal called VSR. VSR consists of a verb and several semantic roles, \ie, all components are guaranteed to be event-compatible. Meanwhile, VSR only restricts the involved semantic roles, which is also sample-suitable for all the images containing the activity. We have validated the effectiveness of VSR through extensive experiments. Moving forward, we will plan to 1) design a more effective captioning model to benefit more from the VSR signals; 2) extend VSR to other controllable text generation tasks, \eg, video captioning ; 3) design a more general framework to cover the images without verbs.

answer: control signal is designated by each author in their work [0]. as the authors of this paper proposed a "verb-specific semantic role" "VSR" as control signal for customized captions [13]. while a recent surge of efforts by other works introduced extra control signals as constrains of the generated captions [16, 10, 19, 78, 48, 77, 27, 20] [47].

input: How we can say Wikia dataset is zero-shot dataset?
question: How we can say Wikia dataset is zero-shot dataset?
output: As mentioned in [1], the paper "Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval" constructs a dataset from Wikidata [333]. The task is to link entity mentions in text to an entity dictionary with provided entity descriptions, in a set of domains. The entities in the validation and test sets are from different domains than the train set, allowing for evaluation of performance on entirely unseen entities. The entity dictionaries cover different domains and range in size from 10K to 100K entities. This setup is designed to test the model's ability to perform zero-shot entity linking, which means it must identify and link mentions of entities it has never seen during training [2].

ctxs.title: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval
ctxs.text: was constructed by Logeswaran et al. (2019) from Wikia.333https://www.wikia.com. The task is to link entity mentions in text to an entity dictionary with provided entity descriptions, in a set of domains. There are 49K, 10K, and 10K examples in the train, validation, test sets respectively. The entities in the validation and test sets are from different domains than the train set, allowing for evaluation of performance on entirely unseen entities. The entity dictionaries cover different domains and range in size from 10K to 100K entities.

ctxs.title: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval
ctxs.text: We also study zero-shot entity linking Logeswaran et al. (2019). Here the document setup is the same, but the knowledge base is separated in training and test time. Formally, denote ℰt​r​a​i​nsubscriptℰ𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑖𝑛\mathcal{E}_{train} and ℰt​e​s​tsubscriptℰ𝑡𝑒𝑠𝑡\mathcal{E}_{test} to be the knowledge base in training and test, we require ℰt​r​a​i​n∩ℰt​e​s​t=∅subscriptℰ𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑖𝑛subscriptℰ𝑡𝑒𝑠𝑡\mathcal{E}_{train}\cap\mathcal{E}_{test}=\emptyset. The set of text documents, mentions, and entity dictionary are separated in training and test so that the entities being linked at test time are unseen.

answer: In the Wikia dataset, entities in the validation and test sets are from different domains than the train set [28]. This setting allows the model evaluation can be done in a zero-shot setting since the set of entities are separated in training and test so that the model can't see the entities when linked at the test time [8].

input: How does the knowledge distilation works if meta-graph can't be constructed (i.e. there is no corresponding entities in knowledge graph for query/passage)?
question: How does the knowledge distilation works if meta-graph can't be constructed (i.e. there is no corresponding entities in knowledge graph for query/passage)?
output:
If the meta-graph cannot be constructed (i.e., there is no corresponding entities in the knowledge graph for the query/passage), the knowledge distillation process relies on the pre-trained language model's inherent ability to capture implicit knowledge from its massive pre-training corpus [1]. Specifically, the model's ability to generate high-quality candidate sentences for passage re-ranking is primarily driven by its understanding of the query and the passage's content, even if explicit knowledge is not available in the knowledge graph [1].

In this scenario, the model's performance is largely determined by its ability to identify the most relevant sentence(s) in the passage based on the query's semantic similarity [1]. This is achieved by leveraging the model's extensive pre-training on diverse text corpora, which enables it to develop a robust understanding of language, context, and factual knowledge [1].

The model's implicit knowledge is activated during the fine-tuning phase on the target task, which involves training it to perform the passage re-ranking task by predicting the relevance score between a query and a sentence [1]. This process refines the model's ability to align its internal representations with human judgment on sentence relevance [1].

Therefore, if the meta-graph cannot be constructed, the knowledge distillation process relies on the pre-trained language model's intrinsic ability to capture implicit knowledge from its massive pre-training corpus [1]. This implicit knowledge is crucial for the model's performance on the downstream re-ranking task, as it enables it to generate high-quality candidate sentences for passage re-ranking [1].

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: (1)  Key sentence selection. The actual information need of a user usually concentrates on a small part of a relevant passage (Guo et al., 2020). To this end, we mimic human judgment and only focus on the sentence of each passage that is the most related to a query (Zou et al., 2021). In particular, we define the relevance score between a query q and a sentence sisubscripts𝑖\textbf{s}_{i} as   (7)  R​e​lq​s​(q,si)=∑q=1|q|E​(wq)|q|⋅∑s=1|si|E​(ws)|si|.𝑅𝑒subscript𝑙𝑞𝑠qsubscripts𝑖⋅superscriptsubscript𝑞1qEsubscript𝑤𝑞qsuperscriptsubscript𝑠1subscripts𝑖Esubscript𝑤𝑠subscripts𝑖Rel_{qs}(\textbf{q},\textbf{s}_{i})=\frac{\sum_{q=1}^{|\textbf{q}|}\textbf{E}(w_{q})}{|\textbf{q}|}\cdot\frac{\sum_{s=1}^{|\textbf{s}_{i}|}\textbf{E}(w_{s})}{|\textbf{s}_{i}|}.      For the sake of efficiency, we initialize E​(w)E𝑤\textbf{E}(w) from Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) embedding. Based on Eq.(7), we select the most relevant sentence s∗superscripts\textbf{s}^{*} in p to build the meta-graph for 𝐪𝐪\mathbf{q} and 𝐩𝐩\mathbf{p}.    (2)  Target entity recognition. Next, we select the entities in q and s∗superscripts\textbf{s}^{*} to construct the meta-graph. Specifically, we only consider the entities that exactly match in ℰℰ\mathcal{E}. Meanwhile, we omit those entity phrases that are sub-sequences of other recognized entities. For example, in the query "what causes low liver enzymes", both "liver" and "liver enzyme" are entities, but the entity "liver enzyme" is more informative to be recognized as the target entity, and "liver" should be omitted.    (3)  Path discovery. Finally, given the target entities of q and s∗superscripts\textbf{s}^{*} (denoted as ϕ𝐪subscriptitalic-ϕ𝐪\phi_{\mathbf{q}} and ϕ𝐬∗subscriptitalic-ϕsuperscript𝐬\phi_{\mathbf{s}^{*}}, respectively), we perform Breadth First Search (BFS) on 𝒢¯¯𝒢\overline{\mathcal{G}} to discover the paths within K𝐾K-hop between ϕ𝐪subscriptitalic-ϕ𝐪\phi_{\mathbf{q}} and ϕ𝐬∗subscriptitalic-ϕsuperscript𝐬\phi_{\mathbf{s}^{*}}. Note that we only keep the within-K𝐾K-hop paths that might be the most useful for the downstream re-ranking task. Meanwhile, the knowledge could be complemented from the K𝐾K-hop paths.

answer: Entities that exactly match entities in E are selected from q and s* to construct the meta-graph [26].

input: What is the Difference Between Additive and Multiplicative Attention?
question: What is the Difference Between Additive and Multiplicative Attention?
output:
The difference between additive and dot-product attention lies in how they compute the compatibility function. Additive attention computes this function using a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer [1]. In contrast, dot-product attention is identical to our algorithm, except for the scaling factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}.$ [1].

The dot-product attention is much faster and more space-efficient in practice, since it can be implemented using highly optimized matrix multiplication code [1]. However, for small values of $d_k$, the two mechanisms perform similarly [2]. For larger values of $d_k$, additive attention outperforms dot-product attention without scaling [2]. This is because the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax function into regions where it has extremely small gradients [2]. To counteract this effect, they scale the dot products by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}.$ [2].

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: The two most commonly used attention functions are additive attention , and dot-product (multiplicative) attention. Dot-product attention is identical to our algorithm, except for the scaling factor of 1dk1subscript𝑑𝑘\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}. Additive attention computes the compatibility function using a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer. While the two are similar in theoretical complexity, dot-product attention is much faster and more space-efficient in practice, since it can be implemented using highly optimized matrix multiplication code.

To counteract this effect, we scale the dot products by 1dk1subscript𝑑𝑘\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}.

answer: Dot product attention is calculated using optimized matrix multiplication  operations whereas Additive attention is computed by compatibility function using a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer [16]. Multiplicative Attention is much faster and more space-efficient than the additive attention [17].

input: Is the computational complexity per data point of wake sleep algorithm same as AEVB or is it different?
question: Is the computational complexity per data point of wake sleep algorithm same as AEVB or is it different?
output: The computational complexity per data point of the wake-sleep algorithm is the same as that of the Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes (AEVB) method [1]. Both methods employ a recognition model that approximates the true posterior [1]. However, the wake-sleep algorithm requires a concurrent optimization of two objective functions, which together do not correspond to the optimization of the marginal likelihood [1].

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: The wake-sleep algorithm (HDFN95) is, to the best of our knowledge, the only other on-line learning method in the literature that is applicable to the same general class of continuous latent variable models. Like our method, the wake-sleep algorithm employs a recognition model that approximates the true posterior. A drawback of the wake-sleep algorithm is that it requires a concurrent optimization of two objective functions, which together do not correspond to optimization of (a bound of) the marginal likelihood. An advantage of wake-sleep is that it also applies to models with discrete latent variables. Wake-Sleep has the same computational complexity as AEVB per datapoint.

answer: Wake-Sleep has the same computational complexity as AEVB per datapoint [20].

input: The results section concludes that momentum affected neither the training compute cost nor the performance - why was this a surprising or unexpected result?
question: The results section concludes that momentum affected neither the training compute cost nor the performance - why was this a surprising or unexpected result?
output: The authors found that momentum did not affect either the training compute cost or the performance of LSTMs when training with online stochastic gradient descent [1]. This was unexpected because they expected to see a significant impact of momentum on the training time and performance. However, they found that for none of the datasets, momentum accounted for more than 1% of the variance of test set performance [1]. This suggests that momentum does not offer substantial benefits when training LSTMs with online stochastic gradient descent [1].

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: One unexpected result of this study is that momentum affects neither performance nor training time in any significant way. This follows from the observation that for none of the datasets, momentum accounted for more than 1% of the variance of test set performance. It should be noted that for TIMIT the interaction between learning rate and momentum accounts for 2.5% of the total variance, but as with learning rate ×\times hidden size (cf. Interaction of Hyperparameters below) it does not reveal any interpretable structure. This may be the result of our choice to scale learning rates dependent on momentum (Section IV-B). These observations suggest that momentum does not offer substantial benefits when training LSTMs with online stochastic gradient descent.

answer: It is not clear why was the result an unexpected result since there is no evidential information of what the authors expected when choosing the hyperparameters to assess their importance [54].

input: What are the advantages of using a flat architecture in SegNet?
question: What are the advantages of using a flat architecture in SegNet?
output:
The flat architecture in SegNet has two main advantages:

1. It avoids parameter explosion, unlike an expanding deep encoder network with full feature connectivity [1].
2. The training time remains the same (in their experiments it slightly decreases) for each additional/deeper encoder-decoder pair as the feature map resolution is smaller, which makes convolutions faster [1].

This choice is motivated by these two reasons [1].

ctxs.title: SegNet: A Deep Convolutional Encoder-Decoder Architecture for Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: SegNet uses a “flat” architecture, i.e, the number of features in each layer remains the same (646464 in our case) but with full connectivity. This choice is motivated by two reasons. First, it avoids parameter explosion, unlike an expanding deep encoder network with full feature connectivity (same for decoder). Second, the training time remains the same (in our experiments it slightly decreases) for each additional/deeper encoder-decoder pair as the feature map resolution is smaller which makes convolutions faster. Note that the decoder corresponding to the first encoder (closest to the input image) produces a multi-channel feature map although the encoder input is either 3 or 4 channels (RGB or RGBD) (see Fig. 1). This high dimensional feature representation is fed to the soft-max classifier. This is unlike the other decoders which produce feature maps the same size as their encoder inputs. A fixed pooling window of 2×2222\times 2 with a stride of non-overlapping 222 pixels is used. This small size preserves thin structures in the scene. Further, a constant kernel size of 7×7777\times 7 over all the layers was chosen to provide a wide context for smooth labelling i.e. a pixel in the deepest layer feature map can be traced back to a context window in the input image of 106×106106106106\times 106 pixels. The trade-off here is between the size of the context window and retaining thin structures. Smaller kernels decrease context and larger ones potentially destroy thin structures.

answer: The flat architecture avoids parameter explosion, unlike an expanding deep encoder network with full feature connectivity (same for decoder) and the training time remains almost same for each additional/deeper encoder-decoder pair [7].

input: BLINK have two different versions, bi-encoding version and cross-encoding version. Is this true?
question: BLINK have two different versions, bi-encoding version and cross-encoding version. Is this true?
output: Yes, BLINK has two different versions, bi-encoding version and cross-encoding version [3]. The bi-encoder uses two independent BERT transformers to encode model context/mention and entity into dense vectors, and each entity candidate is scored as the dot product of these vectors [3]. The candidates retrieved by the bi-encoder are then passed to the cross-encoder for ranking [3]. The cross-encoder encodes context/mention and entity in one transformer, and applies an additional linear layer to compute the final score for each pair [3].

ctxs.title: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval
ctxs.text: As expected, the cross-encoder performs better than the bi-encoder on ranking. However, both models exceed state-of-the-art performance levels, demonstrating that the overall approach is highly effective. We observe that our model also performs well when we change the underlying Knowledgebase to full Wikipedia, and even without fine-tuning on the dataset. In Table 5 we show that our bi-encoder model is highly effective at retrieving relevant entities, where the underlying Knowledgebase is full Wikipedia.

ctxs.title: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval
ctxs.text: In the first example, we see that the bi-encoder mistakenly links “Ronaldo” to the Brazilian football player, while the cross-encoder is able to use context word “Juventus” to disambiguate. In the second example, the cross-encoder is able to identify from context that the sentence is describing art instead of fiction, where the bi-encoder failed. In the third example, the bi-encoder is able to find the correct entity “Ancient Greek,”; where the cross-encoder mistakenly links it to the entity “Ancient Greek philosophy,” likely because that the word “philosophers” is in context. We observe that cross-encoder is often better at utilizing context information than bi-encoder, but can sometimes make mistakes because of misleading context cues.

ctxs.title: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval
ctxs.text: Figure 1 shows our overall approach. The bi-encoder uses two independent BERT transformers to encode model context/mention and entity into dense vectors, and each entity candidate is scored as the dot product of these vectors. The candidates retrieved by the bi-encoder are then passed to the cross-encoder for ranking. The cross-encoder encodes context/mention and entity in one transformer, and applies an additional linear layer to compute the final score for each pair.

answer: BLINK model is a two-stage method using two encoders: bi-encoder and cross-encoder [37]. With the qualitative analysis, the authors compared the BLINK with its bi-encoding version which uses a bi-encoder for candidate ranking instead of a cross-encoder, and showed that the cross-encoding version utilizing context information better than the bi-encoding version [47]. Therefore we can say that BLINK has two different versions [9].

input: Is there any different way to construct RE model instead of using PLM strategy previously?
question: Is there any different way to construct RE model instead of using PLM strategy previously?
output:
The paper excerpt discusses two main lines of work on sentence-level Relation Extraction (RE) that have been proposed previously [1]. The first line of work focuses on injecting external knowledge into Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) [1]. Methods of such, including ERNIE [2] and KnowBERT [3], take entity embedding pretrained from knowledge graphs as inputs to the Transformer [4]. Similarly, K-Adapter [5] introduces a plug-in neural adaptor that injects factual and linguistic knowledge into the language model [6]. LUKE [7] further extends the pretraining objective of masked language modeling to entities and proposes an entity-aware self-attention mechanism [1]. The other line of work focuses on continually pretraining PLMs on text with linked entities using relation-oriented objectives [8]. Specifically, BERT-MTB [9] proposes a matching-the-blanks objective that decides whether two relation instances share the same entities [10]. Despite extensively studied, existing RE models still perform far from perfect [1]. On the commonly-used benchmark TACRED [11], the SOTA F1subscript𝐹1F_{1}} result only increases from 70.1%percent70.170.1\% (BERTLARGELARGE{}_{\text{LARGE}}} to 72.7%percent72.772.7\% (LUKE) after applying PLMs to this task [1]. It is unclear what building block is missing to constitute a promising RE system [1].

The paper excerpt does not explicitly mention any different way to construct RE model instead of using PLM strategy previously [1]. However, it does point out that existing RE models still perform far from perfect [1]. This suggests that there might be room for improvement in the current RE system constructions [1].

ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: Recent work on sentence-level RE can be divided into two lines. One focuses on injecting external knowledge into PLMs. Methods of such, including ERNIE Zhang et al. (2019) and KnowBERT Peters et al. (2019), take entity embedding pretrained from knowledge graphs as inputs to the Transformer. Similarly, K-Adapter Wang et al. (2020) introduces a plug-in neural adaptor that injects factual and linguistic knowledge into the language model. LUKE Yamada et al. (2020) further extends the pretraining objective of masked language modeling to entities and proposes an entity-aware self-attention mechanism. The other line of work focuses on continually pretraining PLMs on text with linked entities using relation-oriented objectives. Specifically, BERT-MTB Baldini Soares et al. (2019) proposes a matching-the-blanks objective that decides whether two relation instances share the same entities. Despite extensively studied, existing RE models still perform far from perfect. On the commonly-used benchmark TACRED Zhang et al. (2017), the SOTA F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} result only increases from 70.1%percent70.170.1\% (BERTLARGELARGE{}_{\text{LARGE}}) to 72.7%percent72.772.7\% (LUKE) after applying PLMs to this task. It is unclear what building block is missing to constitute a promising RE system.

answer: Yes, there are several works of constructing RE model [1].

input: Why did the author choose to use the standard COCO metrics for the comparison of Mask R-CNN to the state of the art on the COCO dataset ?
question: Why did the author choose to use the standard COCO metrics for the comparison of Mask R-CNN to the state of the art on the COCO dataset ?
output: The authors chose to use the standard COCO metrics, including AP (averaged over IoU thresholds), AP50, AP75, and APS, APM, APL (AP at different scales), to evaluate their method on the COCO dataset [1]. This is because these metrics are widely accepted and used in the state-of-the-art for instance segmentation tasks, and they provide a comprehensive evaluation of the performance of their method [1].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: We perform a thorough comparison of Mask R-CNN to the state of the art along with comprehensive ablations on the COCO dataset . We report the standard COCO metrics including AP (averaged over IoU thresholds), AP50, AP75, and APS, APM, APL (AP at different scales). Unless noted, AP is evaluating using mask IoU. As in previous work (5, 27), we train using the union of 80k train images and a 35k subset of val images (trainval35k), and report ablations on the remaining 5k val images (minival). We also report results on test-dev .

answer: The standard COCO metrics were used for the comparison of Mask R-CNN to the state of the art on the COCO dataset, but the reason is not strongly discussed int paragraphs [34].

input: How many images do the ILSVRC dataset has?
question: How many images do the ILSVRC dataset has?
output: The ILSVRC dataset has approximately 1.2 million training images, 50 thousand validation images, and 100 thousand test images [1].

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: Using the image collection and annotation procedure described in previous sections, we collected a large-scale dataset used for ILSVRC classification task. There are 1000 object classes and approximately 1.2 million training images, 50 thousand validation images and 100 thousand test images. Table 2 (top) documents the size of the dataset over the years of the challenge.

answer: ILSVRC dataset has 12 million training images, 50 thousand validation images and 100 thousand test images [45].

input: What's the difference between pix2pix and CycleGAN?
question: What's the difference between pix2pix and CycleGAN?
output: The main difference between pix2pix and CycleGAN lies in their training paradigms. pix2pix [1] is a conditional generative adversarial network that learns a mapping from input to output images using paired training examples. In contrast, CycleGAN [1] learns the mapping without paired training examples, which is a significant departure from the prior work [2]. This unpaired training approach allows CycleGAN to perform unpaired image-to-image translation, a task that has been challenging for most prior works [2].

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: Table 1 reports performance regarding the AMT perceptual realism task. Here, we see that our method can fool participants on around a quarter of trials, in both the maps→→\rightarrowaerial photos direction and the aerial photos→→\rightarrowmaps direction at 256×256256256256\times 256 resolution333We also train CycleGAN and pix2pix at 512×512512512512\times 512 resolution, and observe the comparable performance: maps→→\rightarrowaerial photos: CycleGAN: 37.5%±3.6%plus-or-minuspercent37.5percent3.637.5\%\pm 3.6\% and pix2pix: 33.9%±3.1%plus-or-minuspercent33.9percent3.133.9\%\pm 3.1\%; aerial photos→→\rightarrowmaps: CycleGAN: 16.5%±4.1%plus-or-minuspercent16.5percent4.116.5\%\pm 4.1\% and pix2pix: 8.5%±2.6%plus-or-minuspercent8.5percent2.68.5\%\pm 2.6\%. All the baselines almost never fooled participants.

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: Image-to-Image Translation The idea of image-to-image translation goes back at least to Hertzmann et al.’s Image Analogies , who employ a non-parametric texture model  on a single input-output training image pair. More recent approaches use a dataset of input-output examples to learn a parametric translation function using CNNs (e.g., ). Our approach builds on the “pix2pix” framework of Isola et al. , which uses a conditional generative adversarial network  to learn a mapping from input to output images. Similar ideas have been applied to various tasks such as generating photographs from sketches  or from attribute and semantic layouts . However, unlike the above prior work, we learn the mapping without paired training examples.

answer: [CycleGAN builds on the “pix2pix” framework, which uses a conditional generative adversarial network to learn a mapping from input to output images [38]. However, unlike pix2pix, the paper learns the mapping without paired training examples [9].

input: What is a major drawback of deep learning approaches adapting networks designed for object categorization to pixel wise labeling?
question: What is a major drawback of deep learning approaches adapting networks designed for object categorization to pixel wise labeling?
output: A major drawback of deep learning approaches adapting networks designed for object categorization to pixel-wise labeling is that the deepest layer representations/feature maps are of a small resolution as compared to input image dimensions due to several pooling layers [1]. For example, if 2×2 non-overlapping max-pooling-subsampling layers are used three times, the resulting feature map is 1/8th of the input dimension [1]. This often results in predictions that appear blocky [1].

ctxs.title: SegNet: A Deep Convolutional Encoder-Decoder Architecture for Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: Semantic segmentation is an important step towards understanding and inferring different objects and their arrangements observed in a scene. This has wide array of applications ranging from estimating scene geometry, inferring support-relationships among objects to autonomous vehicle driving. Early methods that relied on low-level vision cues have fast been superseded by popular machine learning algorithms. In particular, deep learning has seen huge success lately in handwritten digit recognition, speech, categorising whole images and detecting objects in images (37, 34) also seen growing interest in semantic pixel-wise labelling problems (7, 14, 35). However, these recent approaches have tried to directly adopt deep architectures designed for category prediction to pixel-wise labelling. The results, although very encouraging, have not been quite satisfactory. Primarily, the deepest layer representations/feature maps are of a small resolution as compared to input image dimensions due to several pooling layers e.g. if 2×2222\times 2 non-overlapping max-pooling-subsampling layers are used three times, the resulting feature map is 1/8t​h1superscript8𝑡ℎ1/8^{th} of the input dimension. Therefore, an ad hoc technique is used to upsample the deepest layer feature map to match the input image dimensions by replicating features within a block i.e. all pixels within a block (8×8888\times 8 in our example) have the same features. This often results in predictions that appear blocky222see http://david.grangier.info/scene_parsing/. This is exactly what we improve using our proposed SegNet architecture, wherein the decoders learn to map the deepest layer features to full image dimensions. Learning to decode has two other advantages. First, deeper layers each with pooling-subsampling can be introduced which increases the spatial context for pixel labelling. This results in smooth predictions unlike patch based classifiers (36, 2). Second, ablation studies to understand the effects of features such as in  can be performed using the decoder stack.

answer: Due to the use of non-overlapping max-pooling-subsampling layers, the resulting feature map is reduced compare to the input dimension [0].

input: What do the equations for Q-value and value represent?
question: What do the equations for Q-value and value represent?
output:
The equations for the Q-value and value function in the provided paper excerpt are as follows:

The Q-value function $Q^{\pi}(\bm{s}_{t},a_{t})$ is defined as the expected return from state $\bm{s}_{t}$ following action $a_{t}$ and then following the policy $\pi$ thereafter:
$$Q^{\pi}(\bm{s}_{t},a_{t})=\mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\left(\sum_{\tau=t}^{T}\gamma^{t-\tau}\mathcal{R}(\bm{s}_{\tau},a_{\tau},{\bm{y}})\right)$$
where $\mathcal{R}(\bm{s}_{\tau},a_{\tau},{\bm{y}})=\mathbb{E}_{\bm{s}_{t+1}\sim P}\left(\sum_{\tau=t}^{T}\gamma^{t-\tau}\mathcal{R}(\bm{s}_{\tau},a_{\tau},{\bm{y}})\right)$ is the return from state $\bm{s}_{t+1}$ onwards, and $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{s}_{t+1}\sim P}\left(\sum_{\tau=t}^{T}\gamma^{t-\tau}\mathcal{R}}(\bm{s}_{\tau},a_{\tau},{\bm{y}})\right)$ is the expected return from state $\bm{s}_{t+1}$ onwards.

The value function $V^{\pi}(\bm{s}_{t}))$ is defined as the expected return from state $\bm{s}_{t}$ following the policy $\pi$ thereafter:
$$V^{\pi}(\bm{s}_{t})=\mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\left(\sum_{\tau=t}^{T}\gamma^{t-\tau}\mathcal{R}}(\bm{s}_{\tau},a_{\tau},{\bm{y}})\right)$$
where $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{s}_{t+1}\sim P}\left(\sum_{\tau=t}^{T}\gamma^{t-\tau}\mathcal{R}}(\bm{s}_{\tau},a_{\tau},{\bm{y}})\right)$ is the expected return from state $\bm{s}_{t+1}$ onwards.

These equations are used in the actor-critic algorithm to estimate the value function $V^{\pi}(\bm{s}_{t}))$ and the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(\bm{s}_{t},a_{

Our benchmark experiments focus on fine-tuning a pre-trained LM denoted as π0subscript𝜋0\pi_{0} from which we initial our agent’s policy πθ=π0subscript𝜋𝜃subscript𝜋0\pi_{\theta}=\pi_{0}.
