input: Why did the authors choose MIDAP as the target NPU to experiment on?
question: Why did the authors choose MIDAP as the target NPU to experiment on?
output: The authors chose their adder-tree type NPU, called MIDAP [1], as the target to experiment on because it has a fully-pipelined micro-architecture that consists of separate hardware modules and memory modules for convolution, activation function, and various reduction operations [1]. This design enables them to make a fully static schedule of operations without resource contention in the data path, allowing them to estimate the end-to-end latency of a CNN quite accurately analytically [1].

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: Even though the proposed methodology can be applied to any type of NPU, the current implementation is made for an adder-tree type NPU, called MIDAP (Kang et al., 2019). It has a fully-pipelined micro-architecture that consists of separate hardware modules and memory modules for convolution, activation function, and various reduction operations. Since it enables us to make a fully static schedule of operations without resource contention in the data path, we can estimate the end-to-end latency of a CNN quite accurately analytically. Unexpected delay may incur from off-chip DRAM delay that is not fully hidden by double buffering.

answer: The end-to-end latency can be estimated quite accurately, and MIDAP can efficiently support which which lower the MAC utilization in other NPUs [26].

input: What does "bottom-up top-down feedforward structure" means ?
question: What does "bottom-up top-down feedforward structure" means ?
output:
The "bottom-up top-down feedforward structure" refers to a network architecture that mimics the hierarchical processing of the human visual cortex [1]. This structure is characterized by two main components: a bottom-up pathway and a top-down pathway [1].

The bottom-up pathway is responsible for extracting low-level features from the input image, such as edges, corners, and textures [1]. These features are progressively combined to form higher-level representations, such as parts of objects and eventually, complete objects [1]. This pathway is analogous to the ventral visual stream in the human brain, which is responsible for object recognition and identification [1].

The top-down pathway, on the other hand, provides context and guidance to the bottom-up pathway [1]. It starts from the highest level of abstraction and guides the processing of information from the bottom-up pathway [1]. This pathway is analogous to the dorsal visual stream in the human brain, which is responsible for action guidance and spatial navigation [1].

In essence, the "bottom-up top-down feedforward structure" allows the network to learn both local and global features of an image [1]. The bottom-up pathway learns to extract fine-grained details, while the top-down pathway provides a coarse, contextual understanding of the image [1]. This complementary information flow enables the network to achieve state-of-the-art performance on various image classification tasks [1].

ctxs.title: Residual Attention Network for Image Classification
ctxs.text: However, recent advances of image classification focus on training feedforward convolutional neural networks using “very deep” structure (27, 33, 10). The feedforward convolutional network mimics the bottom-up paths of human cortex. Various approaches have been proposed to further improve the discriminative ability of deep convolutional neural network. VGG , Inception  and residual learning  are proposed to train very deep neural networks. Stochastic depth , Batch Normalization  and Dropout  exploit regularization for convergence and avoiding overfitting and degradation.

answer: The bottom-up top-down feedforward structure is a combination of a bottom-up fast feedforward process that creates low resolution features maps to quickly collect global information, and a top-down attention feedback process that uses the global information along with the original feature maps to create features for inference [14].

input: What is the main difference between the "maximum frequency" and "exclusive frequency" baselines?
question: What is the main difference between the "maximum frequency" and "exclusive frequency" baselines?
output: The main difference between the "majority baseline" (maximum frequency) and the "exclusive majority baseline" (exclusive frequency) lies in the selection of the entity that serves as the placeholder in the context document. The majority baseline selects the entity most frequently observed in the context document [1]. In contrast, the exclusive majority baseline selects the entity most frequently observed in the context document but *not* observed in the query [1]. This exclusion is based on the idea that the placeholder is unlikely to be mentioned twice in a single Cloze form query [1].

ctxs.title: Teaching Machines to Read and Comprehend
ctxs.text: So far we have motivated the need for better datasets and tasks to evaluate the capabilities of machine reading models. We proceed by describing a number of baselines, benchmarks and new models to evaluate against this paradigm. We define two simple baselines, the majority baseline (maximum frequency) picks the entity most frequently observed in the context document, whereas the exclusive majority (exclusive frequency) chooses the entity most frequently observed in the context but not observed in the query. The idea behind this exclusion is that the placeholder is unlikely to be mentioned twice in a single Cloze form query.

answer: The main difference between the “maximum frequency” and “exclusive frequency” baselines is that the latter eliminates all entities found in the query as potential answers [11].

input: What is the meaning of "using graph structures explicitly"?
question: What is the meaning of "using graph structures explicitly"?
output: "using graph structures explicitly" means that the model is designed to directly incorporate the graph structure into its self-attention mechanism [1]. This is achieved by reformulating the self-attention mechanism as a kernel smoother and extending the original exponential kernel on node features to also account for local structures, by extracting a subgraph representation centered around each node [1]. This allows the model to capture both structural and attributed similarities between nodes [1]. In contrast, most existing position-aware Transformers for graph-structured data do not explicitly consider the graph structure [1].

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: In this work, we address the critical question of how to encode structural information into a Transformer architecture. Our principal contribution is to introduce a flexible structure-aware self-attention mechanism that explicitly considers the graph structure and thus captures structural interaction between nodes. The resulting class of Transformers, which we call the Structure-Aware Transformer (SAT), can provide structure-aware representations of graphs, in contrast to most existing position-aware Transformers for graph-structured data. Specifically:   •  We reformulate the self-attention mechanism in Vaswani et al. (2017) as a kernel smoother and extend the original exponential kernel on node features to also account for local structures, by extracting a subgraph representation centered around each node.    •  We propose several methods for automatically generating the subgraph representations, enabling the resulting kernel smoother to simultaneously capture structural and attributed similarities between nodes. The resulting representations are theoretically guaranteed to be at least as expressive as the subgraph representations.    •  We demonstrate the effectiveness of SAT models on five graph and node property prediction benchmarks by showing it achieves better performance than state-of-the-art GNNs and Transformers. Furthermore, we show how SAT can easily leverage any GNN to compute the node representations which incorporate subgraph information and outperform the base GNN, making it an effortless enhancer of any existing GNN.    •  Finally, we show that we can attribute the performance gains to the structure-aware aspect of our architecture, and showcase how SAT is more interpretable than the classic Transformer with an absolute encoding.

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: The key contribution of SAT is its ability to explicitly incorporate structural information in the self-attention. Here, we seek to demonstrate that this information provides crucial predictive information, and study how the choice of k𝑘k affects the results. Figure 3(a) shows how the test MAE is impacted by varying k𝑘k for k𝑘k-subtree and k𝑘k-subgraph extractors using PNA on the ZINC dataset. All models use the RWPE. k=0𝑘0k=0 corresponds to the vanilla Transformer only using absolute positional encoding, i.e. not using structure. We find that incorporating structural information leads to substantial improvement in performance, with optimal performance around k=3𝑘3k=3 for both k𝑘k-subtree and k𝑘k-subgraph extractors. As k𝑘k increases beyond k=4𝑘4k=4, the performance in k𝑘k-subtree extractors deteriorated, which is consistent with the observed phenomenon that GNNs work best in shallower networks (Kipf & Welling, 2017). We observe that k𝑘k-subgraph does not suffer as much from this issue, underscoring a new aspect of its usefulness. On the other hand, k𝑘k-subtree extractors are more computationally efficient and scalable to larger OGB datasets.

answer: The meaning of using graph structures explicitly is to explicity incorporate structural information into the self-attention [3]. The reason is that both P3 and P7 state the main contribution of SAT with paraphrasing [39].

input: How to create rare token identifier?
question: How to create rare token identifier?
output: To create a rare token identifier, we perform a rare-token lookup in the vocabulary and obtain a sequence of rare token identifiers $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}})$, where $f$ is a tokenizer; a function that maps character sequences to tokens and $\hat{\mathbf{V}}$ is the decoded text stemming from the tokens $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}})$. The sequence can be of variable length $k=\{1,...,3\}$ which works well. Then, by inverting the vocabulary using the de-tokenizer on $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}})$, we obtain a sequence of characters that define our unique identifier $\mathbf{V}^{\wedge}$. For Imagen, we find that using uniform random sampling of tokens that correspond to 3 or fewer Unicode characters (without spaces) and using tokens in the T5-XXL tokenizer range of $\{5000,...,10000\}$ works well [1].

ctxs.title: DreamBooth: Fine Tuning Text-to-Image Diffusion Models for Subject-Driven Generation
ctxs.text: We generally find existing English words (e.g. “unique”, “special”) suboptimal since the model has to learn to disentangle them from their original meaning and to re-entangle them to reference our subject. This motivates the need for an identifier that has a weak prior in both the language model and the diffusion model. A hazardous way of doing this is to select random characters in the English language and concatenate them to generate a rare identifier (e.g. “xxy5syt00”). In reality, the tokenizer might tokenize each letter separately, and the prior for the diffusion model is strong for these letters. We often find that these tokens incur the similar weaknesses as using common English words. Our approach is to find rare tokens in the vocabulary, and then invert these tokens into text space, in order to minimize the probability of the identifier having a strong prior. We perform a rare-token lookup in the vocabulary and obtain a sequence of rare token identifiers f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}), where f𝑓f is a tokenizer; a function that maps character sequences to tokens and 𝐕^^𝐕\hat{\mathbf{V}} is the decoded text stemming from the tokens f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}). The sequence can be of variable length k𝑘k, and find that relatively short sequences of k={1,…,3}𝑘1…3k=\{1,...,3\} work well. Then, by inverting the vocabulary using the de-tokenizer on f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}) we obtain a sequence of characters that define our unique identifier 𝐕^^𝐕\hat{\mathbf{V}}. For Imagen, we find that using uniform random sampling of tokens that correspond to 3 or fewer Unicode characters (without spaces) and using tokens in the T5-XXL tokenizer range of {5000,…,10000}5000…10000\{5000,...,10000\} works well.

answer: In order to get a list of rare token IDs, we first search for rare tokens in the vocabulary [18].

input: Will is the Inception- ResNet-v2 trained faster than pure Inception-v4 although their computational complexity is similar ?
question: Will is the Inception- ResNet-v2 trained faster than pure Inception-v4 although their computational complexity is similar ?
output: Based on the paper excerpt, the answer is **no**. While Inception-ResNet-v2 has a similar computational cost to Inception-v4, the step time of Inception-v4 proved to be significantly slower in practice [1]. This is likely due to the larger number of layers in Inception-v4 [1]. Therefore, Inception-ResNet-v2 will be trained faster than pure Inception-v4, despite their similar computational complexity [1].

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: We tried several versions of the residual version of Inception. Only two of them are detailed here. The first one “Inception-ResNet-v1” roughly the computational cost of Inception-v3, while “Inception-ResNet-v2” matches the raw cost of the newly introduced Inception-v4 network. See Figure 15 for the large scale structure of both varianets. (However, the step time of Inception-v4 proved to be significantly slower in practice, probably due to the larger number of layers.)

answer: Since the step time of Inception-v4 is significantly slower in practice, we can conclude that Inception-ResNet-v2 trained faster than pure Inception-v4 even though the computational complexity is similar [11].

input: Does the statement "At each step the model is auto-regressive, consuming the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next" implies that we can use attention in handling time series forecasting?
question: Does the statement "At each step the model is auto-regressive, consuming the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next" implies that we can use attention in handling time series forecasting?
output:
The statement "At each step the model is auto-regressive, consuming the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next" does not imply that we can use attention in handling time series forecasting [1]. The Transformer model, which is based entirely on attention, replaces the recurrent layers most commonly used in encoder-decoder architectures with multi-headed self-attention [1]. This means that the model does not have to process the input sequence sequentially, as in traditional autoregressive models. Instead, it can attend to all positions in the input sequence simultaneously [1].

This ability to attend to all positions in the input sequence simultaneously makes the Transformer model particularly well-suited for tasks like sequence-to-sequence transduction, such as machine translation, where the input and output sequences can be of different lengths [1]. However, its self-attention mechanism does not inherently capture temporal dependencies in the input sequence, which is crucial for time series forecasting [2].

To incorporate temporal dependencies into the Transformer architecture for time series forecasting, it is necessary to introduce positional encodings [3] or other mechanisms that inject information about the relative or absolute positions of tokens in the input sequence [4]. These positional encodings allow the model to attend to different positions in the input sequence at different steps of the decoding process [5].

In summary, the statement that the model is auto-regressive and consumes the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next does not imply that we can use attention in handling time series forecasting [1]. The Transformer model's self-attention mechanism does not inherently capture temporal dependencies, and positional encodings or other mechanisms must be introduced to inject information about the relative or absolute positions of tokens in the input sequence [3].

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: In this work, we presented the Transformer, the first sequence transduction model based entirely on attention, replacing the recurrent layers most commonly used in encoder-decoder architectures with multi-headed self-attention.

answer: Attention can be used for sequence modeling and can be used to build encoder decoder models which can handle time series forecasting [60].

input: The authors extended which baseline framework to learn representation of image sequences?
question: The authors extended which baseline framework to learn representation of image sequences?
output: The authors extended the baseline framework of an identical LSTM classifier [1] to learn representation of image sequences. They used this LSTM classifier as a strong baseline for comparing their proposed models [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: The baseline for comparing these models is an identical LSTM classifier but with randomly initialized weights. All classifiers used dropout regularization, where we dropped activations as they were communicated across layers but not through time within the same LSTM as proposed in Zaremba et al. (2014). We emphasize that this is a very strong baseline and does significantly better than just using single frames. Using dropout was crucial in order to train good baseline models especially with very few training examples.

answer: The authors extended identical LSTM classifier framework as baseline to learn representation of image sequences [36].

input: What are identity shortcuts and projection shortcuts, and how are they used in the experiments?
question: What are identity shortcuts and projection shortcuts, and how are they used in the experiments?
output: Identity shortcuts and projection shortcuts are two types of identity shortcuts introduced in the paper [1]. Identity shortcuts are parameter-free, identity connections that help with training [1]. Projection shortcuts are introduced to increase dimensions in the network [2]. In the experiments, they are used in three options: (A) zero-padding shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and all shortcuts are parameter-free (the same as Table [2] and Fig. [4] right); (B) projection shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and all other shortcuts are identity; and (C) all shortcuts are projections [1]. The authors argue that the small differences among A/B/C indicate that projection shortcuts are not essential for addressing the degradation problem, and therefore do not use option C in the rest of the paper to reduce memory/time complexity and model sizes [2].

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Identity vs. Projection Shortcuts. We have shown that parameter-free, identity shortcuts help with training. Next we investigate projection shortcuts (Eqn.(2)). In Table 3 we compare three options: (A) zero-padding shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and all shortcuts are parameter-free (the same as Table 2 and Fig. 4 right); (B) projection shortcuts are used for increasing dimensions, and other shortcuts are identity; and (C) all shortcuts are projections.

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Table 3 shows that all three options are considerably better than the plain counterpart. B is slightly better than A. We argue that this is because the zero-padded dimensions in A indeed have no residual learning. C is marginally better than B, and we attribute this to the extra parameters introduced by many (thirteen) projection shortcuts. But the small differences among A/B/C indicate that projection shortcuts are not essential for addressing the degradation problem. So we do not use option C in the rest of this paper, to reduce memory/time complexity and model sizes. Identity shortcuts are particularly important for not increasing the complexity of the bottleneck architectures that are introduced below.

answer: The identity and projection shortcut are not defined in this section [35]. Identity shortcuts help in training, do not increase the complexity of the bottleneck architectures and solve degradation problem [36].

input: What is the loss used during training of Faster R-CNN ?
question: What is the loss used during training of Faster R-CNN ?
output: During training of Faster R-CNN, the total loss is defined as a multi-task loss on each sampled RoI as $L = L_{cls} + L_{box} + L_{mask}$ [1]. This loss consists of three components: the classification loss $L_{cls}$ [1], the bounding-box loss $L_{box}$ [1], and the mask loss $L_{mask}$ [1]. The classification loss $L_{cls}$ and the bounding-box loss $L_{box}$ are identical as those defined in [2]. The mask branch has a $K_m^2$-dimensional output for each RoI, which encodes $K_m$ binary masks of resolution $m \times m$, one for each of the $K_m$ classes [1]. To this we apply a per-pixel sigmoid, and define $L_m^a$ as the average binary cross-entropy loss [1]. For an RoI associated with ground-truth class $k$, $L_m^a$ is only defined on the $k$-th mask (other mask outputs do not contribute to the loss) [1].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Formally, during training, we define a multi-task loss on each sampled RoI as L=Lc​l​s+Lb​o​x+Lm​a​s​k𝐿subscript𝐿𝑐𝑙𝑠subscript𝐿𝑏𝑜𝑥subscript𝐿𝑚𝑎𝑠𝑘L=L_{cls}+L_{box}+L_{mask}. The classification loss Lc​l​ssubscript𝐿𝑐𝑙𝑠L_{cls} and bounding-box loss Lb​o​xsubscript𝐿𝑏𝑜𝑥L_{box} are identical as those defined in . The mask branch has a K​m2𝐾superscript𝑚2Km^{2}-dimensional output for each RoI, which encodes K𝐾K binary masks of resolution m×m𝑚𝑚m\times m, one for each of the K𝐾K classes. To this we apply a per-pixel sigmoid, and define Lm​a​s​ksubscript𝐿𝑚𝑎𝑠𝑘L_{mask} as the average binary cross-entropy loss. For an RoI associated with ground-truth class k𝑘k, Lm​a​s​ksubscript𝐿𝑚𝑎𝑠𝑘L_{mask} is only defined on the k𝑘k-th mask (other mask outputs do not contribute to the loss).

answer: For an RoI associated with ground-truth class k, L_{mask} is only defined on the k-th mask, where L is the average binary cross-entropy loss [15].

input: In what way can SBM-Transformer be considered better than Reformer?
question: In what way can SBM-Transformer be considered better than Reformer?
output:
SBM-Transformer is considered better than Reformer in several ways, as demonstrated by the results in Table 8. Specifically, SBM-Transformer achieves the best overall performance, ranking first in two tasks and second in one other [1]. This indicates that SBM-Transformer is more effective than Reformer in the tasks where it achieves the best performance.

Furthermore, SBM-Transformer outperforms full attention in all five tasks while computing 30% or less attention scores on average [1]. This suggests that it can be preferred over full attention depending on the task, as it achieves competitive performance with less computational cost.

In terms of the attention mask structure, SBM-Transformer's flexibility is indeed beneficial, as it can better model hierarchical contexts compared to Reformer [1]. This is particularly true in ListOps tasks, where Reformer struggles, most likely due to the inability of block-diagonal masks to capture these structures [1].

In summary, SBM-Transformer is better than Reformer in terms of overall performance, efficiency, and the ability to handle various task structures, as shown in Table 8 [1].

ctxs.title: Transformers meet Stochastic Block Models: Attention with Data-Adaptive Sparsity and Cost
ctxs.text: Table 8 shows the test accuracies of each method. Our SBM-Transformer achieves the best overall performance, ranking first in two tasks, and second in one other. SBM-Transformer also outperforms full attention in all five tasks while computing 30% or less attention scores on average, which supports our claim that masked attention with partial attention score computations can be preferred over full attention depending on the task. With respect to the attention mask structure, we find that flexibility of SBM is indeed beneficial, as Reformer struggles in ListOps, most likely due to the inability of block-diagonal masks to model hierarchical contexts.

answer: SBM-Transformer allows more flexible attention mask structures between linear to full attention with respective computational costs, while Reformer can only use block-diagonal masks that cannot model hierarchical contexts [31].

input: What do you mean by model pruning?
question: What do you mean by model pruning?
output:
Model pruning is a technique used to reduce the computational cost of deep neural networks while maintaining or improving their accuracy [1]. It involves removing redundant or less important connections, channels, or layers from the network without significantly impacting its performance [1]. The idea behind pruning is that not all layers or channels in a network contribute equally to its performance, and some can be removed without a substantial drop in accuracy [1].

In the context of ShuffleNet [1], the authors aim to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy in very limited computational budgets, specifically at tens or hundreds of MFLOPs [1]. They achieve this by designing a "basic" architecture that is highly efficient for mobile devices [1]. This architecture is designed to be efficient by reducing the number of parameters and computations compared to other state-of-the-art models [1].

While many existing works focus on pruning, compressing, or low-bit representing a "basic" network architecture [1], ShuffleNet [1] aims to explore a "basic" architecture that is highly efficient for our desired computing ranges [1]. This architecture is designed to be efficient by reducing the number of parameters and computations compared to other state-of-the-art models [1].

In summary, model pruning is a technique used to reduce the computational cost of deep neural networks while maintaining or improving their accuracy [1]. ShuffleNet [1] aims to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy in very limited computational budgets, specifically at tens or hundreds of MFLOPs [1]. They achieve this by designing a "basic" architecture that is highly efficient for mobile devices [1].

ctxs.title: ShuffleNet: An Extremely Efficient Convolutional Neural Network for Mobile Devices
ctxs.text: Building deeper and larger convolutional neural networks (CNNs) is a primary trend for solving major visual recognition tasks (21, 9, 33, 5, 28, 24). The most accurate CNNs usually have hundreds of layers and thousands of channels (9, 34, 32, 40), thus requiring computation at billions of FLOPs. This report examines the opposite extreme: pursuing the best accuracy in very limited computational budgets at tens or hundreds of MFLOPs, focusing on common mobile platforms such as drones, robots, and smartphones. Note that many existing works (16, 22, 43, 42, 38, 27) focus on pruning, compressing, or low-bit representing a “basic” network architecture. Here we aim to explore a highly efficient basic architecture specially designed for our desired computing ranges.

answer: Although model pruning is a specific concept in deep learning, it refers to reducing the model size by removing redundant network connections or channels [0].

input: What are the FID values achieved by authors using Diffusion Model on ImageNet?
question: What are the FID values achieved by authors using Diffusion Model on ImageNet?
output: The authors achieve state-of-the-art image generation on LSUN and ImageNet 64×\times64 with their improved architecture [3]. Specifically, they obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 diffusion steps [3]. Their results on FID are competitive with the best GANs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both FID and FID-S [1].

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: For all comparisons in this section, we train models on ImageNet 128×\times128 with batch size 256, and sample using 250 sampling steps. We train models with the above architecture changes and compare them on FID, evaluated at two different points of training, in Table 1. Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a positive compounding effect. We observe in Figure 2 that while increased depth helps performance, it increases training time and takes longer to reach the same performance as a wider model, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: Table 4 also shows that classifier guidance improves precision at the cost of recall, thus introducing a trade-off in sample fidelity versus diversity. We explicitly evaluate how this trade-off varies with the gradient scale in Figure 4. We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity). Since FID and sFID depend on both diversity and fidelity, their best values are obtained at an intermediate point. We also compare our guidance with the truncation trick from BigGAN in Figure 5. We find that classifier guidance is strictly better than BigGAN-deep when trading off FID for Inception Score. Less clear cut is the precision/recall trade-off, which shows that classifier guidance is only a better choice up until a certain precision threshold, after which point it cannot achieve better precision.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: Table 5 summarizes our results. Our diffusion models can obtain the best FID on each task, and the best sFID on all but one task. With the improved architecture, we already obtain state-of-the-art image generation on LSUN and ImageNet 64×\times64. For higher resolution ImageNet, we observe that classifier guidance allows our models to substantially outperform the best GANs. These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 diffusion steps.

answer: They obtain state-of-the-art image generation on ImageNet 64×64 [22]. For higher resolution ImageNet [44]. Table 5 shows the performance of ADM Metrics include FID, sFID, Prec, Rec [46].

input: What is a BLEU score?
question: What is a BLEU score?
output: BLEU score is a metric used to evaluate the quality of generated image captions by computing the precision of word n-grams between generated and reference sentences [1]. Specifically, BLEU score is a geometric average of precision over 1- to n-grams [1].

ctxs.title: Show and tell: A neural image caption generator
ctxs.text: The rest of the metrics can be computed automatically assuming one has access to groundtruth, i.e. human generated descriptions. The most commonly used metric so far in the image description literature has been the BLEU score , which is a form of precision of word n-grams between generated and reference sentences 222In this literature, most previous work report BLEU-1, i.e., they only compute precision at the unigram level, whereas BLEU-n is a geometric average of precision over 1- to n-grams.. Even though this metric has some obvious drawbacks, it has been shown to correlate well with human evaluations. In this work, we corroborate this as well, as we show in Section 4.3. An extensive evaluation protocol, as well as the generated outputs of our system, can be found at \urlhttp://nic.droppages.com/.

answer: BLUE score measures the precision of n-grams between generated sentences and reference sentences, which has been shown to correlate well with human evaluation [23].

input: The paper's model implies that the discriminative parameters also contain significant “generative” structure from the training dataset. What is meant by "generative" structure?
question: The paper's model implies that the discriminative parameters also contain significant “generative” structure from the training dataset. What is meant by "generative" structure?
output:
In the context of the paper, "Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization," the authors suggest that the discriminative parameters also contain significant "generative" structure from the training dataset [1]. This implies that the parameters encode not only the jaguar's spots, but to some extent also its four legs [1].

The term "generative" structure refers to the ability of the parameters to generate new, realistic samples from the data distribution, rather than just discriminating between classes [1]. In this sense, the parameters of the discriminative model are not only learning to identify the jaguar's specific features, but also learning the underlying structure and distribution of the jaguar's body, including its four legs [1].

This generative structure is evident even with the simple, hand-coded priors $p(x)$ used in the paper as regularizers [1]. The authors note that complex dependencies between distant pixels already arise, suggesting that the discriminative parameters also contain significant "generative" structure from the training dataset [1].

The authors suggest that by having better, learned probabilistic models over the input and activations of higher layers, much more structure may be apparent [1]. This implies that the generative structure is present in the parameters of the discriminative model, and that transferring these parameters to generative models—opposite the usual unsupervised pretraining approach—may be a fruitful area for further investigation [1].

In summary, the "generative" structure refers to the ability of the parameters to generate new, realistic samples from the data distribution, and that this structure is present in the parameters of the discriminative model, even with the simple priors used in the paper [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: However, the results presented here suggest an alternate possibility: the previously used priors may simply have been too weak (see Section S1 for one hypothesis of why a strong p​(x)𝑝𝑥p(x) model is needed). With the careful design or learning of a p​(x)𝑝𝑥p(x) model that biases toward realism, one may be able to harness the large number of parameters present in a discriminately learned p​(y|x)𝑝conditional𝑦𝑥p(y|x) model to generate realistic images by enforcing probability under both models simultaneously. Even with the simple, hand-coded p​(x)𝑝𝑥p(x) models we use in this paper as regularizers, complex dependencies between distant pixels already arise (cf. the beetles with structure spanning over 100 pixels in Figure 4). This implies that the discriminative parameters also contain significant “generative” structure from the training dataset; that is, the parameters encode not only the jaguar’s spots, but to some extent also its four legs. With better, learned probabilistic models over the input and activations of higher layers, much more structure may be apparent. Work by Dai et al. (2015) shows some interesting results in this direction. While the images generated in this paper are far from being photo-realistic, they do suggest that transferring discriminatively trained parameters to generative models — opposite the direction of the usual unsupervised pretraining approach — may be a fruitful area for further investigation.

answer: Generative structure is how the data is distributed inside the space where it lives, for example when learning to detect jaguar class, parameters encode not only the jaguar’s spots(Only to distinguish it through a rare property), but to some extent also its four legs(to learn the pattern with which the whole creature can be found) [33].

input: Why does ORB-SLAM (short) perform so poorly when the turning magnitude is low, as seen in Figure 9?
question: Why does ORB-SLAM (short) perform so poorly when the turning magnitude is low, as seen in Figure 9?
output: The paper suggests that the large performance gap between their method and ORB-SLAM (short) when the side-rotation is small (i.e., the car mostly driving forward) suggests that their learned ego-motion could potentially be used as an alternative to the local estimation modules in monocular SLAM systems [2]. This implies that their method is more robust to small changes in ego-motion, such as the car mostly driving forward, compared to ORB-SLAM (short) [2].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: In this work, we mimic this approach by training a model that observes sequences of images and aims to explain its observations by predicting likely camera motion and the scene structure (as shown in Fig. 1). We take an end-to-end approach in allowing the model to map directly from input pixels to an estimate of ego-motion (parameterized as 6-DoF transformation matrices) and the underlying scene structure (parameterized as per-pixel depth maps under a reference view). We are particularly inspired by prior work that has suggested view synthesis as a metric  and recent work that tackles the calibrated, multi-view 3D case in an end-to-end framework . Our method is unsupervised, and can be trained simply using sequences of images with no manual labeling or even camera motion information.

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: For better understanding of our pose estimation results, we show in Figure 9 the ATE curve with varying amount of side-rotation by the car between the beginning and the end of a sequence. Figure 9 suggests that our method is significantly better than ORB-SLAM (short) when the side-rotation is small (i.e. car mostly driving forward), and comparable to ORB-SLAM (full) across the entire spectrum. The large performance gap between ours and ORB-SLAM (short) suggests that our learned ego-motion could potentially be used as an alternative to the local estimation modules in monocular SLAM systems.

answer: Proposed model has good performance as compared with  ORB-SLAM (short) when the turning magnitude is low [1]. ORB-SLAM (short) perform so poorly because it could not learn ego-motion [32].

input: What characteristics of large-scale pre-trained language models made it remarkable successful for passage re-ranking task?
question: What characteristics of large-scale pre-trained language models made it remarkable successful for passage re-ranking task?
output:
The remarkable success of large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) on passage re-ranking tasks can be attributed to several key characteristics [1]:

1. **Powerful Capacity in Modeling Semantic Relevance**: PLMs, such as BERT [2], ERNIE [3], and RoBERTa [4], have demonstrated strong capabilities in capturing the nuanced semantic relationships between queries and candidate passages [1]. This allows them to produce high-quality re-rankings that closely align with human judgment [1].

2. **Pretrain-then-finetune Paradigm**: This paradigm allows PLMs to acquire vast amounts of implicit knowledge from massive textual corpora [1]. This knowledge includes implicit knowledge about the implicit relationships between queries and passages, which is crucial for effective passage re-ranking [1].

3. **Expressive Transformer Structure**: The self-attention mechanism within the transformer architecture enables PLMs to capture long-range dependencies and dependencies between tokens at different levels of abstraction [1]. This enables them to build rich contextual representations of the query-passage pair, which are highly informative for the re-ranking task [1].

4. **Strong Performance on Downstream Tasks**: PLMs have achieved state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of downstream tasks, including passage re-ranking [1]. This general proficiency suggests that their pretraining on large amounts of text has led to a robust understanding of language structure and semantics [1].

In summary, the combination of strong capacity in modeling semantic relevance, the pretrain-then-finetune paradigm, the expressive transformer structure, and strong performance on downstream tasks has made PLMs exceptionally successful for passage re-ranking [1].

ctxs.title: Incorporating Explicit Knowledge in Pre-trained Language Models for Passage Re-ranking
ctxs.text: Passage Re-ranking is a crucial stage in modern information retrieval systems, which aims to reorder a small set of candidate passages to be presented to users. To put the most relevant passages on top of a ranking list, a re-ranker is usually designed with powerful capacity in modeling semantic relevance, which attracted a wealth of research studies in the past decade (Guo et al., 2020). Recently, large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs), e.g. BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019) and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), have dominated many natural language processing tasks, and have also achieved remarkable success on passage re-ranking. For example, PLM based re-rankers (MacAvaney et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Dong and Niu, 2021; Dong et al., 2022) have achieved state-of-the-art performance, which takes the concatenation of query-passage pair as input, and applies multi-layer full-attention to model their semantic relevance. Their superiority can be attributed to the expressive transformer structure and the pretrain-then-finetune paradigm, which allow the model to learn useful implicit knowledge (i.e., semantic relevance in the latent space) from massive textual corpus (Fan et al., 2021).

answer: Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have been found to be successful for passage re-ranking due to their ability to learn semantic relevance in the latent space from massive textual corpus [0].

input: How can the mask extracted directly from the attention maps mitigate the limitation of inversion process?
question: How can the mask extracted directly from the attention maps mitigate the limitation of inversion process?
output: The mask extracted directly from the attention maps can mitigate the limitation of the inversion process by providing a more precise guidance to the diffusion model for the restoration of the unedited regions of the original image [1]. This mask is generated without any guidance from the user, which allows the model to reconstruct the original image structure and content more accurately [1]. By using this mask, the diffusion model can effectively restore the unedited regions, leading to a high-fidelity reconstruction of the original image [1].

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: To alleviate this limitation, we propose to restore the unedited regions of the original image using a mask, directly extracted from the attention maps. Note that here the mask is generated with no guidance from the user. As presented in fig. 12, this approach works well even using the naïve DDPM inversion scheme (adding noise followed by denoising). Note that the cat’s identity is well-preserved under various editing operations, while the mask is produced only from the prompt itself.

answer: Extracted masks directly from the attention maps can restore the unedited regions of the original image [36].

input: What kinds of RNN architectures were used for the decoder in various prior research?
question: What kinds of RNN architectures were used for the decoder in various prior research?
output:
Several prior works have used different types of RNN architectures for the decoder in attention-based neural machine translation [1]. Some of these include:

* A standard hidden unit for the decoder [2].
* A convolutional neural network for encoding the source sentence representation [2].
* A stacked LSTM hidden unit for both the encoder and the decoder [1].
* A Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) hidden unit for both components [2].
* A bidirectional RNN for the encoder [2].

These works all adopted a different version of the RNN with an LSTM-inspired hidden unit, the GRU, for both components [2]. However, the specific architectures used for the decoder vary across these works [1], [2], [3].

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: Common to these two types of models is the fact that at each time step t𝑡t in the decoding phase, both approaches first take as input the hidden state 𝒉tsubscript𝒉𝑡\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{t} at the top layer of a stacking LSTM. The goal is then to derive a context vector 𝒄tsubscript𝒄𝑡\mbox{\boldmath{$c$}}_{t} that captures relevant source-side information to help predict the current target word ytsubscript𝑦𝑡y_{t}. While these models differ in how the context vector 𝒄tsubscript𝒄𝑡\mbox{\boldmath{$c$}}_{t} is derived, they share the same subsequent steps.

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: ?) used an RNN with the standard hidden unit for the decoder and a convolutional neural network for encoding the source sentence representation. On the other hand, both ?) and ?) stacked multiple layers of an RNN with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) hidden unit for both the encoder and the decoder. ?), ?), and ?) all adopted a different version of the RNN with an LSTM-inspired hidden unit, the gated recurrent unit (GRU), for both components.444They all used a single RNN layer except for the latter two works which utilized a bidirectional RNN for the encoder.

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: In more detail, one can parameterize the probability of decoding each word yjsubscript𝑦𝑗y_{j} as:    p​(yj|y<j,𝒔)=softmax⁡(g​(𝒉j))𝑝conditionalsubscript𝑦𝑗subscript𝑦absent𝑗𝒔softmax𝑔subscript𝒉𝑗p\left(y_{j}|y_{<j},\mbox{\boldmath{$s$}}\right)=\operatorname{softmax}\left(g\left(\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j}\right)\right)  (2)   with g𝑔g being the transformation function that outputs a vocabulary-sized vector.555One can provide g𝑔g with other inputs such as the currently predicted word yjsubscript𝑦𝑗y_{j} as in (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Here, 𝒉jsubscript𝒉𝑗\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j} is the RNN hidden unit, abstractly computed as:    𝒉j=f​(𝒉j−1,𝒔),subscript𝒉𝑗𝑓subscript𝒉𝑗1𝒔\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j}=f(\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j-1},\mbox{\boldmath{$s$}}),  (3)   where f𝑓f computes the current hidden state given the previous hidden state and can be either a vanilla RNN unit, a GRU, or an LSTM unit. In (Kalchbrenner and Blunsom, 2013, Sutskever et al., 2014, Cho et al., 2014, Luong et al., 2015), the source representation 𝒔𝒔s is only used once to initialize the decoder hidden state. On the other hand, in (Bahdanau et al., 2015, Jean et al., 2015) and this work, 𝒔𝒔s, in fact, implies a set of source hidden states which are consulted throughout the entire course of the translation process. Such an approach is referred to as an attention mechanism, which we will discuss next.

answer: Previous work included vanilla RNN, LSTM and GRU in the decoder architecture [10]. as Sutskever and Luon stacked multiple layers of RNN with LSTM hidden unit for the decoder and encoder [6]. and Cho, Bahdanau and Jeal all adopted GRU [7].

input: How is this x converted to y using which network?
question: How is this x converted to y using which network?
output: The input text $x$ is converted to a sequence of text embeddings $x^{e}$ using the CLIP text encoder $\operatorname{C}_{x}(\cdot)$ [1]. These text embeddings are then fed into the prior network $\operatorname{\textbf{P}} (\cdot)$ to generate image embeddings $y^{e}$ [2]. These image embeddings are then passed through the decoder network $\operatorname{\textbf{D}}} (\cdot)$ to generate a low-resolution $64 \times 64$ RGB image $\hat{y}_{l}$ [2]. Finally, two super-resolution networks $\operatorname{\textbf{SR}}_{h}(\cdot)$ and $\operatorname{\textbf{SR}} (\cdot)$ increase the generated image resolution to $256 \times 256$ and $768 \times 768 pixels$ respectively, resulting in the final high-resolution video $\hat{y}$.

The three main components are described in detail in the following sections.

answer: First, a prior network \operatorname{\textbf{P}}, that during inference generates image embeddings y_{e} given text embeddings x_{e} and BPE encoded text tokens \hat{x} [11]. Second, a decoder network \operatorname{\textbf{D}} that generates a low-resolution 64\times 64 RGB image \hat{y}_{l}, conditioned on the image embeddings y_{e} [13].

input: What type of defense strategies are evaded in this paper
question: What type of defense strategies are evaded in this paper
output: The paper discusses the limitations of gradient masking defenses against black-box attacks [1]. Specifically, it shows that even if the defender attempts to prevent attacks by not publishing the directions in which the model is sensitive, these directions can be discovered by other means, in which case the same attack can still succeed [1]. The paper also studies two defenses with the greatest empirical success so far: adversarial training [4, 14] and defensive distillation for DNNs [1].

ctxs.title: Practical Black-Box Attacks against Machine Learning
ctxs.text: We show a more general flaw in the category of gradient masking. Even if the defender attempts to prevent attacks by not publishing the directions in which the model is sensitive, these directions can be discovered by other means, in which case the same attack can still succeed. We show that the black-box attack based on transfer from a substitute model overcomes gradient masking defenses. No fully effective defense mechanism is known, but we study the two with the greatest empirical success so far: adversarial training (4, 14), and defensive distillation for DNNs .

answer: Two types of defense are evaded, Adversarial training and Defensive distillation [70].

They also achieve an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} score of 74.6%percent74.674.6\% on the TACRED dataset and a 83.2%percent83.283.2\% on the TACREV dataset [3]. ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: We first provide an analysis on different entity representation techniques. In this analysis, we use the base and large versions of BERT Devlin et al. (2019) and the large version of RoBERTa Liu et al. (2019) as the encoder. Table 1 shows the performance of the PLMs incorporated with different entity representation techniques. For each technique, we also provide an example of the processed text. We have several observations from the results. First, the typed entity marker and its variants outperform untyped entity representation techniques by a notable margin. Especially, the RoBERTa model achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} score of 74.6%percent74.674.6\% using the typed entity marker (punct), which is significantly higher than the SOTA result of 72.7%percent72.772.7\% by LUKE Yamada et al. (2020). This shows that representing all categories of entity information is helpful to the RE task. It also shows that keeping entity names in the input improves the performance of RE models. Second, symbols used in entity markers have an obvious impact on the performance of RE models. Although the original and punct versions of entity representation techniques represent the same categories of entity information, they do lead to a difference in model performance. Particularly, introducing new special tokens hinders the model performance drastically on RoBERTa. On RoBERTaLARGELARGE{}_{\text{LARGE}}, the entity marker underperforms the entity marker (punct) by 0.7%percent0.70.7\%, the typed entity marker underperforms the typed entity marker (punct) by 3.6%percent3.63.6\%, while the entity mask gets a much worse result of 60.9%percent60.960.9\%.

ctxs.title: An Improved Baseline for Sentence-level Relation Extraction
ctxs.text: In this paper, we present a simple yet strong RE baseline that offers new SOTA performance, along with a comprehensive study to understand its prediction generalizability and robustness. Specifically, we revisit two technical problems in sentence-level RE, namely entity representation and noisy or ill-defined labels. We propose an improved entity representation technique, which significantly outperforms existing sentence-level RE models. Especially, our improved RE baseline achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} score of 91.1%percent91.191.1\% on the Re-TACRED dataset, showing that PLMs already achieve satisfactory performance on this task. We hope the proposed techniques and analyses can benefit future research on RE.

(2019) as the backbone, our improved baseline model achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} of 74.6%percent74.674.6\% and 83.2%percent83.283.2\% on TACRED and TACREV, respectively, significantly outperforming various SOTA RE models. Particularly, our baseline model achieves an F1subscript𝐹1F_{1} of 91.1%percent91.191.1\% on Re-TACRED, demonstrating that PLMs can achieve much better results on RE than shown in previous work.222This work first appeared as a technical report on arXiv in Feb 2021 Zhou and Chen (2021). (2022) that are published before this version of the paper.

input: How can attention patterns from larger models be applied to smaller models if the models might differ in the size and number of attention layers?
question: How can attention patterns from larger models be applied to smaller models if the models might differ in the size and number of attention layers?
output:
The paper suggests that the attention patterns found in larger models can be applied to smaller models by identifying three patterns that consistently exist in both tasks (over 50% of important heads) [1]. These patterns are generalizable across multiple NLP tasks, which is consistent with the findings in Bian et al. (2021) [1]. The authors report an average Jensen-Shannon Divergence of 0.010.010.01 between the attention distributions of BERT's Sum across 3 random seeds, indicating that the attention patterns are consistent after fine-tuning [1].

The authors' interactive approach allows the discovery of interpretable patterns which otherwise would be hard to define due to the infinite search space of possible patterns [1]. They describe the three discovered patterns in detail [1]. While it may be argued that this step of the pipeline can be automated by directly evaluating the importance and relevance of predefined patterns (e.g. syntax, discourse) based on intuitions, their approach allows for the discovery of human-guided exploitable patterns [1].

In summary, the paper suggests that the attention patterns found in larger models can be applied to smaller models by identifying three patterns that consistently exist in both tasks (over 50% of important heads) [1]. These patterns are generalizable across multiple NLP tasks, which is consistent with the findings in Bian et al. (2021) [1]. The authors report an average Jensen-Shannon Divergence of 0.010.010.01 between the attention distributions of BERT's Sum across 3 random seeds, indicating that the attention patterns are consistent after fine-tuning [1]. Their interactive approach allows for the discovery of human-guided exploitable patterns which otherwise would be hard to define due to the infinite search space of possible patterns [1].

ctxs.title: Human Guided Exploitation of Interpretable Attention Patterns in Summarization and Topic Segmentation
ctxs.text: After verifying on the validation set, we discover three patterns consistently existing in both tasks (over 50% of important heads). This suggests that important patterns are generalizable across multiple NLP tasks, which is consistent with the findings in Bian et al. (2021). Further analysis also shows that the attention patterns are consistent after fine-tuning, where we report an average Jensen-Shannon Divergence of 0.010.010.01 between the attention distributions of BERTSum across 3 random seeds. We hope our findings provide motivation for the in-depth study of pattern importance in different NLP tasks. Lastly, while it may be argued that this step of the pipeline can be automated by directly evaluating the importance and relevance of predefined patterns (e.g. syntax, discourse) based on intuitions, as indicated below, our interactive approach allows the discovery of interpretable patterns which otherwise would be hard to define due to the infinite search space of possible patterns. Next, we describe the three discovered patterns in detail.

answer: BERTSum is the summarization model, and Cross-Segment BERT is the topic segmentation model [31].

input: How can it actually benefit that GAT can learn regardless of the graph structure?
question: How can it actually benefit that GAT can learn regardless of the graph structure?
output:
The ability of GAT to learn regardless of the graph structure is a significant advantage over previous spectral-based approaches [1]. This means that GAT can be applied to a wide range of graph-structured data, including those with varying degrees of heterogeneity, heterogeneity in neighborhood sizes, and unknown or dynamic graph structures [1].

Specifically, GAT does not need to know the entire graph structure upfront [1]. This is a major limitation of previous methods that relied on fixed spectral decompositions of the graph Laplacian [1]. By using a masked self-attentional layer, GAT can dynamically assign different importances to different nodes within a neighborhood [1]. This allows it to handle different sized neighborhoods and adapt to the underlying graph structure [1].

This adaptability makes GAT more suitable for real-world applications where the graph structure is unknown or dynamic [1]. For instance, in dynamic knowledge graphs, where nodes and edges are added or removed over time, GAT can effectively learn representations of nodes and edges without requiring a complete recomputation of the spectral decomposition [1].

In summary, the ability of GAT to learn regardless of the graph structure [1] makes it more versatile and applicable to a wider range of tasks beyond the fixed spectral assumptions of previous methods [1].

ctxs.title: Graph Attention Networks
ctxs.text: We have presented graph attention networks (GATs), novel convolution-style neural networks that operate on graph-structured data, leveraging masked self-attentional layers. The graph attentional layer utilized throughout these networks is computationally efficient (does not require costly matrix operations, and is parallelizable across all nodes in the graph), allows for (implicitly) assigning different importances to different nodes within a neighborhood while dealing with different sized neighborhoods, and does not depend on knowing the entire graph structure upfront—thus addressing many of the theoretical issues with previous spectral-based approaches. Our models leveraging attention have successfully achieved or matched state-of-the-art performance across four well-established node classification benchmarks, both transductive and inductive (especially, with completely unseen graphs used for testing).

answer: There are no clue to find what graph can GAT process by its regardless of the graph structure [32].

input: What's the effect of expanding channel size?
question: What's the effect of expanding channel size?
output: The effect of expanding channel size on the performance of GANs trained on a dataset of this scale (292M images) is substantial, as shown in the paper excerpt [1]. Specifically, the authors report significant additional improvements from expanding the capacity of their models to 128 base channels, while for ImageNet GANs that additional capacity was not beneficial [1]. This suggests that for larger datasets, increasing the model capacity can lead to better generation quality and FID scores [1].

ctxs.title: LARGE SCALE GAN TRAINING FOR HIGH FIDELITY NATURAL IMAGE SYNTHESIS
ctxs.text: To confirm that our design choices are effective for even larger and more complex and diverse datasets, we also present results of our system on a subset of JFT-300M (Sun et al., 2017). The full JFT-300M dataset contains 300M real-world images labeled with 18K categories. Since the category distribution is heavily long-tailed, we subsample the dataset to keep only images with the 8.5K most common labels. The resulting dataset contains 292M images – two orders of magnitude larger than ImageNet. For images with multiple labels, we sample a single label randomly and independently whenever an image is sampled. To compute IS and FID for the GANs trained on this dataset, we use an Inception v2 classifier (Szegedy et al., 2016) trained on this dataset. Quantitative results are presented in Table 3. All models are trained with batch size 2048. We compare an ablated version of our model – comparable to SA-GAN (Zhang et al., 2018) but with the larger batch size – against a “full” BigGAN model that makes uses of all of the techniques applied to obtain the best results on ImageNet (shared embedding, skip-z𝑧z, and orthogonal regularization). Our results show that these techniques substantially improve performance even in the setting of this much larger dataset at the same model capacity (64 base channels). We further show that for a dataset of this scale, we see significant additional improvements from expanding the capacity of our models to 128 base channels, while for ImageNet GANs that additional capacity was not beneficial.

answer: expanding channel size substantially improves the performance of the model [34].

input: How the proposed loss function is different from that of original Single-Path NAS?
question: How the proposed loss function is different from that of original Single-Path NAS?
output:
The original Single-Path NAS loss function is defined as Eq. (6) [1], which does not include any information on the target latency in the loss function. In case there is a strict latency constraint, they have to pay additional search costs for the hyperparameters to let the final architecture have no larger latency than the constraint [1]. In addition, this process needs to be repeated whenever the target latency is changed [1].

In contrast, the proposed loss function in S3NAS directly includes the target latency estimated by a cycle-accurate simulator of the target NPU [2]. By modifying the loss function to directly include the target latency, they could find a better baseline architecture with a shorter latency than the latency constraint [2]. Using a tight latency constraint, they can reduce the search space to find the baseline network fast [2].

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: The existing hardware-aware differentiable NAS methods mostly define some hyperparameters to balance between accuracy and latency, including SinglePath NAS, whose loss function is defined as Eq. (6). Since there is no information on the target latency in the loss function, in case there is a strict latency constraint, they have to pay additional search costs for the hyperparameters to let the final architecture have no larger latency than the constraint. In addition, this process needs to be repeated whenever the target latency is changed.

ctxs.title: S3NAS: Fast NPU-aware Neural Architecture Search Methodology
ctxs.text: In this work, we propose a fast NPU-aware NAS methodology extending the Single-Path NAS technique (Stamoulis et al., 2019). We modify the supernet architecture by varying the number of blocks in stages and adding mixed depthwise convolution (Tan and Le, 2019b) to the search space. By modifying the loss function to directly include the target latency estimated by a cycle-accurate simulator of the target NPU, we could find a better baseline architecture with a shorter latency than the latency constraint. Using a tight latency constraint, we can reduce the search space to find the baseline network fast. Afterward, we apply compound scaling to find a larger network than the baseline network, and add SE blocks and h-swish activation functions in the post-processing step. Through the proposed NAS methodology, we could obtain a network with 82.72% accuracy with 11.66ms latency on our target NPU, without special data augmentation in training. It dominates the existing network models on the target NPU. It confirms the importance of supernet architecture design for a given NPU and effectiveness of the three-step approach in the proposed NAS methodology: supernet design, SinglePath NAS with a tighter latency constraint, and compound scaling and post-processing.

answer: Previous method needs additional search cost for hyperparameter, since they have no information for target latency [49]. The author's method directly includes the target latency, resulting in ease of search process [77].

input: What is the best performing CNN model in the abdominal LN detection?
question: What is the best performing CNN model in the abdominal LN detection?
output: The best performing CNN model in the abdominal LN detection is GoogLeNet-TL (256x256) [1]. This model obtains an AUC=0.92 and 0.70 TPR/3FP [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer-Aided Detection: CNN Architectures, Dataset Characteristics and Transfer Learning
ctxs.text: Many of our CNN models achieve notably better (FROC-AUC and TPR/3FP) results than the previous state-of-the-art models  for mediastinal LN detection: GoogLeNet-RI-L obtains an AUC=0.95 and 0.85 TPR/3FP, versus AUC=0.92 and 0.70 TPR/3FP  and 0.78 TPR/3FP  which uses stacked shallow learning. This difference lies in the fact that annotated lymph node segmentation masks are required to learn a mid-level semantic boundary detector , whereas CNN approaches only need LN locations for training . In abdominal LN detection,  obtains the best trade-off between its CNN model complexity and sampled data configuration. Our best performing CNN model is GoogLeNet-TL (256x256) which obtains an AUC=0.92 and 0.70 TPR/3FP.

answer: The best performing model is GoogLeNet-TL [37].

input: According to the authors, the VGG-16 version of Faster R-CNN is 6 time slower than YOLO, what is the actual speed of the model ?
question: According to the authors, the VGG-16 version of Faster R-CNN is 6 time slower than YOLO, what is the actual speed of the model ?
output: The authors claim that the VGG-16 version of Faster R-CNN is 6 times slower than YOLO, but they do not provide the actual speed of the model in the excerpt. They only state that their most accurate model achieves 7 fps while a smaller, less accurate one runs at 18 fps [1].

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: The recent Faster R-CNN replaces selective search with a neural network to propose bounding boxes, similar to Szegedy et al.  In our tests, their most accurate model achieves 7 fps while a smaller, less accurate one runs at 18 fps. The VGG-16 version of Faster R-CNN is 10 mAP higher but is also 6 times slower than YOLO. The Zeiler-Fergus Faster R-CNN is only 2.5 times slower than YOLO but is also less accurate.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: The final output of our network is the 7×7×3077307\times 7\times 30 tensor of predictions.

answer: Table 1 reveals that the actual speed of Faster R-CNN with VGG-16 is 7fps with 732% mAP [58]. At the same time, YOLO has more than 6 times the higher speed of 45 fps with 634% mAP on Pascal VOC 2007 [20].

input: In terms of image synthesis, do the GANs perform better than VQ-VAE or not?
question: In terms of image synthesis, do the GANs perform better than VQ-VAE or not?
output:
Based on the provided paper excerpts, it is stated that GANs currently hold the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks, as measured by sample quality metrics such as FID [1], Inception Score [1], and Precision [1]. However, some of these metrics do not fully capture diversity, and it has been shown that GANs capture less diversity than state-of-the-art likelihood-based models [1].

Despite this, the authors argue that Diffusion Models [2] have surpassed GANs on image synthesis quality. They claim that Diffusion Models can achieve GAN-like sample quality with likelihood-based models [2], and that they capture more diversity than GANs [1]. Furthermore, they claim that Diffusion Models are typically easier to scale and train than GANs [2].

However, the authors also acknowledge that sampling from these likelihood-based models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time [2]. This suggests that while Diffusion Models achieve better sample quality than GANs, they are not yet as fast as GANs.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: GANs Goodfellow et al. (2014) currently hold the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks Brock et al. (2018); Wu et al. (2019); Karras et al. (2019b) as measured by sample quality metrics such as FID Heusel et al. (2017), Inception Score Salimans et al. (2016) and Precision Kynkäänniemi et al. (2019). However, some of these metrics do not fully capture diversity, and it has been shown that GANs capture less diversity than state-of-the-art likelihood-based models Razavi et al. (2019); Nichol and Dhariwal (2021); Nash et al. (2021). Furthermore, GANs are often difficult to train, collapsing without carefully selected hyperparameters and regularizers Brock et al. (2018); Miyato et al. (2018); Brock et al. (2016).

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: While GANs hold the state-of-the-art, their drawbacks make them difficult to scale and apply to new domains. As a result, much work has been done to achieve GAN-like sample quality with likelihood-based models Razavi et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2020); Nash et al. (2021); Child (2021). While these models capture more diversity and are typically easier to scale and train than GANs, they still fall short in terms of visual sample quality. Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time.

answer: Fidelity can be higher, but GANs are not always better in terms of low diversity [1]. In table 5 ImageNet256x256 experiment, BigGAN-deep beats VA-VAE2 about FID, sFID, Precision but lose about Recall [2].

input: What is the major one structural difference between ELMO model and others (BERT small, BERT large, DeBERTa)
question: What is the major one structural difference between ELMO model and others (BERT small, BERT large, DeBERTa)
output: The major one structural difference between ELMO and the other models (BERT small, BERT large, DeBERTa) is the architecture of the language model. ELMO is an LSTM based language model [1], while BERT small, BERT large, and DeBERTa are all based on the Transformer architecture [1].

ctxs.title: What do Large Language Models Learn beyond Language?
ctxs.text: Next, we describe the LMs and their variants used in NILM. We experiment with four language models, based on both Transformer and RNN architectures. BERT small. This is the bert-base-uncased model with 12 transformer encoder layers and the dimension of the representations is 768. BERT tokenizer is based on the WordPiece model Wu et al. (2016).  BERT large. This is the bert-large-uncased model which has 24 transformer encoders and representations have 1024 dimensions.  DeBERTa. This is a transformer based language model and its tokenizer is built using Byte Pair Encoding Sennrich et al. (2016). We consider the DeBERTa base model. It has 12 transformer encoder layers and representations have 768 dimensions.  ELMO. This is an LSTM based language model Peters et al. (2018). It has 3 layers and the output representations have 1024 dimensions.

answer: ELMO is LSTM based language model, but BERT and DeBERTa is transformer based language model [12].

input: What is DPM trained with?
question: What is DPM trained with?
output: DPM is trained on the COCO dataset [2], which contains 80 object categories [2].

ctxs.title: YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger
ctxs.text: We evaluate YOLO9000 on the ImageNet detection task. The detection task for ImageNet shares on 44 object categories with COCO which means that YOLO9000 has only seen classification data for the majority of the test images, not detection data. YOLO9000 gets 19.7 mAP overall with 16.0 mAP on the disjoint 156 object classes that it has never seen any labelled detection data for. This mAP is higher than results achieved by DPM but YOLO9000 is trained on different datasets with only partial supervision . It also is simultaneously detecting 9000 other object categories, all in real-time.

answer: DPM is trained on the ImageNet detection task [67].

input: What is the average video sequence length used for experiments in this study?
question: What is the average video sequence length used for experiments in this study?
output: The average video sequence length used for experiments in this study is 6.2 seconds for the UCF-101 dataset [1] and 3.2 seconds for the HMDB-51 dataset [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: We use the UCF-101 and HMDB-51 datasets for supervised tasks. The UCF-101 dataset (Soomro et al., 2012) contains 13,320 videos with an average length of 6.2 seconds belonging to 101 different action categories. The dataset has 3 standard train/test splits with the training set containing around 9,500 videos in each split (the rest are test). The HMDB-51 dataset (Kuehne et al., 2011) contains 5100 videos belonging to 51 different action categories. Mean length of the videos is 3.2 seconds. This also has 3 train/test splits with 3570 videos in the training set and rest in test.

answer: The UCF-101 dataset contains 13,320 videos with an average length of 62 seconds [22].

input: A reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a lower bound estimator that can be straightforwardly optimized using standard stochastic gradient methods. Is this estimator differentiable?
question: A reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a lower bound estimator that can be straightforwardly optimized using standard stochastic gradient methods. Is this estimator differentiable?
output:
Yes, the reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a stochastic objective function that is differentiable with respect to the variational parameters $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ [2]. This estimator is the Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes (SGVB) estimator [1], which can be optimized using standard stochastic gradient ascent techniques [1]. The estimator is given by:
$$ \widetilde{\mathcal{L}} \approx \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta},\boldsymbol{\phi};\mathbf{x}^{(i)}): \quad \widetilde{\mathcal{L}} \approx \frac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^{L} \log p_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}|\mathbf{z}^{(i,l)}) - \log q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\mathbf{z}^{(i,l)}|\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$$
where $\mathbf{z}^{(i,l)} = g_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{(i,l)}|\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \sim p(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{(l)}|\boldsymbol{\phi)}.$ This reparameterization allows for the computation of the stochastic gradients with respect to $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ and $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ [3].

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: How can we perform efficient approximate inference and learning with directed probabilistic models whose continuous latent variables and/or parameters have intractable posterior distributions? The variational Bayesian (VB) approach involves the optimization of an approximation to the intractable posterior. Unfortunately, the common mean-field approach requires analytical solutions of expectations w.r.t. the approximate posterior, which are also intractable in the general case. We show how a reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a simple differentiable unbiased estimator of the lower bound; this SGVB (Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes) estimator can be used for efficient approximate posterior inference in almost any model with continuous latent variables and/or parameters, and is straightforward to optimize using standard stochastic gradient ascent techniques.
