input: Does each channel maps the a response from a different position of the image ?
question: Does each channel maps the a response from a different position of the image ?
output:
Yes, each channel in the R-FCN is designed to respond to a specific relative position of an object within the input image [1]. This is achieved by designing the receptive field of each channel to be small, typically $k \times k = 3 \times 3$ [1]. This small receptive field allows each channel to focus on a small region of the input image and produce a response that is highly sensitive to the presence of an object at that specific relative position [1].

For example, the "top-center-sensitive" score map is expected to be strongly activated at a specific relative position near the top-center of an object [1]. Similarly, other channels are designed to respond to other relative positions of an object, such as the "bottom-left-sensitive" score map [1].

This design choice allows the R-FCN to learn a set of specialized maps, where each map is responsible for detecting an object at a particular relative position [1]. By aggregating the votes from all these channels, the final prediction for the object's presence and location is made [1].

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: Visualization. In Figure 4 and 4 we visualize the position-sensitive score maps learned by R-FCN when k×k=3×3𝑘𝑘33k\times k=3\times 3. These specialized maps are expected to be strongly activated at a specific relative position of an object. For example, the “top-center-sensitive” score map exhibits high scores roughly near the top-center position of an object. If a candidate box precisely overlaps with a true object (Figure 4), most of the k2superscript𝑘2k^{2} bins in the RoI are strongly activated, and their voting leads to a high score. On the contrary, if a candidate box does not correctly overlaps with a true object (Figure 4), some of the k2superscript𝑘2k^{2} bins in the RoI are not activated, and the voting score is low.

answer: The k^2 channels for each category C of the final convolutional layer each map to cells within a spatial grid that correspond to a position relative to an object [18].

input: How is using an encoder-decoder structure as a mask different than local convolutions soft masks, a part from the test error ?
question: How is using an encoder-decoder structure as a mask different than local convolutions soft masks, a part from the test error ?
output: The paper compares the performance of a Residual Attention Network with a local convolution soft mask [1]. The local convolutions soft mask consists of three Residual Units using the same number of FLOPs [1]. The Attention-56 is used to construct Attention-Encoder-Decoder-56 and Attention-Local-Conv-56 respectively [1]. The Attention-Encoder-Decoder-56 network achieves lower test error of 5.52% compared with Attention-Local-Conv-56 network 6.48% [1]. This suggests that the soft attention optimization process will benefit from multi-scale information [1].

ctxs.title: Residual Attention Network for Image Classification
ctxs.text: We conduct experiments to validate the effectiveness of encoder-decoder structure by comparing with local convolutions without any down sampling or up sampling. The local convolutions soft mask consists of three Residual Units using the same number of FLOPs. The Attention-56 is used to construct Attention-Encoder-Decoder-56 and Attention-Local-Conv-56 respectively. Results are shown in Table 4. The Attention-Encoder-Decoder-56 network achieves lower test error 5.52%percent5.525.52\% compared with Attention-Local-Conv-56 network 6.48%percent6.486.48\% with a considerable margin 0.94%percent0.940.94\%. The result suggests that the soft attention optimization process will benefit from multi-scale information.

answer: The local convolutions' soft mask only consists of three Residual units, which remain the same size [34].

input: What is the goal behind constructing individual single scale SR system?
question: What is the goal behind constructing individual single scale SR system?
output: The goal behind constructing individual single scale SR system is to cope with multiple scale SR (possibly including fractional factors) [1]. This is because existing SR methods, like SRCNN, are trained for a single scale factor and are supposed to work only with the specified scale [1]. Therefore, if a new scale is on demand, a new model has to be trained [1].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: Scale As in most existing SR methods, SRCNN is trained for a single scale factor and is supposed to work only with the specified scale. Thus, if a new scale is on demand, a new model has to be trained. To cope with multiple scale SR (possibly including fractional factors), we need to construct individual single scale SR system for each scale of interest.

answer: Since SRCNN is only trained for a single scale, we need to train individual single scale SRCNNs to deal with multiple scales [15].

input: What is IS as a measure of fidelity?
question: What is IS as a measure of fidelity?
output: Inception Score (IS) is a metric that measures how well a model captures the full ImageNet class distribution while still producing individual samples that are convincing examples of a single class [1]. It is proposed by Salimans et al. [1]. One drawback of this metric is that it does not reward covering the whole distribution or capturing diversity within a class, and models which memorize a small subset of the full dataset will still have high IS [1].

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: Inception Score (IS) was proposed by Salimans et al. , and it measures how well a model captures the full ImageNet class distribution while still producing individual samples that are convincing examples of a single class. One drawback of this metric is that it does not reward covering the whole distribution or capturing diversity within a class, and models which memorize a small subset of the full dataset will still have high IS Barratt and Sharma (2018). To better capture diversity than IS, Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) was proposed by Heusel et al. , who argued that it is more consistent with human judgement than Inception Score. FID provides a symmetric measure of the distance between two image distributions in the Inception-V3 Szegedy et al. (2015) latent space. Recently, sFID was proposed by Nash et al.  as a version of FID that uses spatial features rather than the standard pooled features. They find that this metric better captures spatial relationships, rewarding image distributions with coherent high-level structure. Finally, Kynkäänniemi et al.  proposed Improved Precision and Recall metrics to separately measure sample fidelity as the fraction of model samples which fall into the data manifold (precision), and diversity as the fraction of data samples which fall into the sample manifold (recall).

answer: IS measures of fidelity but it has a drawback that it does not reward covering the whole distribution or capturing diversity within a class, and models which memorize a small subset of the full dataset will still have high IS [16].

input: What are benefits of using learnable parameters for capturing positional information rather than using sines and cosines to capture these positions?
question: What are benefits of using learnable parameters for capturing positional information rather than using sines and cosines to capture these positions?
output:
The sinusoidal positional encodings are fixed and do not require any additional parameters to be learned during training [1]. This means that they do not increase the total number of parameters in the model, which can lead to a more efficient training process [2].

In contrast, learnable positional embeddings require additional parameters to be learned during training, which can increase the total number of parameters in the model [2]. While these learned positional embeddings can potentially capture more complex positional information than the fixed sinusoidal ones, they may require more data to be trained effectively [1].

The authors chose the sinusoidal version because they believed it might allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training [2]. This is because the fixed sinusoidal positional encodings have a fixed relationship with the input positions, which can be extrapolated to longer sequences without requiring the model to learn new positional embeddings.

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: Since our model contains no recurrence and no convolution, in order for the model to make use of the order of the sequence, we must inject some information about the relative or absolute position of the tokens in the sequence. To this end, we add "positional encodings" to the input embeddings at the bottoms of the encoder and decoder stacks. The positional encodings have the same dimension dmodelsubscript𝑑modeld_{\text{model}} as the embeddings, so that the two can be summed. There are many choices of positional encodings, learned and fixed .

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: We also experimented with using learned positional embeddings  instead, and found that the two versions produced nearly identical results (see Table 3 row (E)). We chose the sinusoidal version because it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training.

answer: The two choices of Positional encoding are learned and fixed [28]. In the experiments the two versions produced nearly identical results [32].

input: How the proposed LSTM future predictor model is different from the  Ranzato model.
question: How the proposed LSTM future predictor model is different from the  Ranzato model.
output: The proposed LSTM future predictor model is different from the Ranzato model in the way it predicts the future video frames. While Ranzato et al. (2014) predict only the next frame at each time step [1], the proposed model predicts a long sequence into the future [1]. Specifically, the Future Predictor Model uses a decoder LSTM to predict the future video frames that come after the input sequence [1]. This makes it a more powerful model for long-term prediction compared to Ranzato et al.'s model [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: Another natural unsupervised learning task for sequences is predicting the future. This is the approach used in language models for modeling sequences of words. The design of the Future Predictor Model is same as that of the Autoencoder Model, except that the decoder LSTM in this case predicts frames of the video that come after the input sequence (Fig. 3). Ranzato et al. (2014) use a similar model but predict only the next frame at each time step. This model, on the other hand, predicts a long sequence into the future. Here again we can consider two variants of the decoder – conditional and unconditioned.

answer: Ranzato model predict only the next frame but LSTM future predictor model predicts a long sequence into the future [15].

input: Is there any limitation of this technique?
question: Is there any limitation of this technique?
output: The paper excerpt does not explicitly mention any limitations of the One-Shot Video Tuning technique. However, it does suggest that the technique is designed to be intuitive and learn from a single video example, which implies that it might be sensitive to the quality and diversity of the input video [1].

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: Humans possess the ability to create new concepts, ideas, or things by utilizing their existing knowledge and the information provided to them. For example, when presented a video with a textual description of “a man skiing on snow”, we can imagine how a panda would ski on snow, drawing upon our knowledge of what a panda looks like. As T2I models pretrained with large-scale image-text data already capture knowledge of open-domain concepts, a intuitive question arises: can they infer other novel videos from a single video example, like humans? A new T2V generation setting is therefore introduced, namely, One-Shot Video Tuning, where only a single text-video pair is used to train a T2V generator. The generator is expected to capture essential motion information from the input video and synthesize novel videos with edited prompts.

answer: Training with opendomain data, it is hard to capture necessary motion knowledge from the input video and synthesize novel videos guided by edited prompts [1].

input: What do authors mean by saying that RoI layer was added "unnaturally" in the ResNet  ?
question: What do authors mean by saying that RoI layer was added "unnaturally" in the ResNet  ?
output: The authors mean that the RoI layer was added "unnaturally" in the ResNet [2] because it was inserted between two sets of convolutional layers, which was not the original design of the network. This insertion created a deeper RoI-wise subnetwork that improved accuracy, but at the cost of lower speed due to the unshared per-RoI computation [2]. This modification was necessary to address the issue of inferior detection accuracy when using all convolutional layers to construct the shared, fully convolutional subnetwork in the object detection architecture [1].

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: A prevalent family (8, 6, 18) of deep networks for object detection can be divided into two subnetworks by the Region-of-Interest (RoI) pooling layer : (i) a shared, “fully convolutional” subnetwork independent of RoIs, and (ii) an RoI-wise subnetwork that does not share computation. This decomposition  was historically resulted from the pioneering classification architectures, such as AlexNet  and VGG Nets , that consist of two subnetworks by design — a convolutional subnetwork ending with a spatial pooling layer, followed by several fully-connected (fc) layers. Thus the (last) spatial pooling layer in image classification networks is naturally turned into the RoI pooling layer in object detection networks (8, 6, 18).

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: But recent state-of-the-art image classification networks such as Residual Nets (ResNets)  and GoogLeNets (24, 26) are by design fully convolutional111Only the last layer is fully-connected, which is removed and replaced when fine-tuning for object detection.. By analogy, it appears natural to use all convolutional layers to construct the shared, convolutional subnetwork in the object detection architecture, leaving the RoI-wise subnetwork no hidden layer. However, as empirically investigated in this work, this naïve solution turns out to have considerably inferior detection accuracy that does not match the network’s superior classification accuracy. To remedy this issue, in the ResNet paper  the RoI pooling layer of the Faster R-CNN detector  is unnaturally inserted between two sets of convolutional layers — this creates a deeper RoI-wise subnetwork that improves accuracy, at the cost of lower speed due to the unshared per-RoI computation.

answer: Old object detection networks used two subnetworks, one being a convolutional subnetwork with a pooling layer, and another being fully connected layers [0]. The pooling layer served as a RoI pooling layer [1].

input: For the paper's pretrained DNN, if the input does not contain a training set class, why does the probability vector show sensitivity towards the noise in input?
question: For the paper's pretrained DNN, if the input does not contain a training set class, why does the probability vector show sensitivity towards the noise in input?
output: When using direct file input to classify photos from Flickr or Google Images, classifications are often correct and highly confident (softmax probability for correct class near 1). On the other hand, when using input from a webcam, predictions often cannot be correct because no items from the training set are shown in the image. The training set’s 1000 classes, though numerous, do not cover most common household objects. Thus, when shown a typical webcam view of a person with no ImageNet classes present, the output has no single high probability, as is expected. Surprisingly, however, this probability vector is noisy and varies significantly in response to tiny changes in the input, often changing merely in response to the noise from the webcam. We might have instead expected unchanging and low confidence predictions for a given scene when no object the network has been trained to classify is present. Plotting the fully connected layers ($\mathsf{fc6}$ and $\mathsf{fc7}$) also reveals a similar sensitivity to small input changes [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: •  One of the most interesting conclusions so far has been that representations on some layers seem to be surprisingly local. Instead of finding distributed representations on all layers, we see, for example, detectors for text, flowers, fruit, and faces on 𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟦𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟦\mathsf{conv4} and 𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧\mathsf{conv5}. These conclusions can be drawn either from the live visualization or the optimized images (or, best, by using both in concert) and suggest several directions for future research (discussed in Section 4).    •  When using direct file input to classify photos from Flickr or Google Images, classifications are often correct and highly confident (softmax probability for correct class near 1). On the other hand, when using input from a webcam, predictions often cannot be correct because no items from the training set are shown in the image. The training set’s 1000 classes, though numerous, do not cover most common household objects. Thus, when shown a typical webcam view of a person with no ImageNet classes present, the output has no single high probability, as is expected. Surprisingly, however, this probability vector is noisy and varies significantly in response to tiny changes in the input, often changing merely in response to the noise from the webcam. We might have instead expected unchanging and low confidence predictions for a given scene when no object the network has been trained to classify is present. Plotting the fully connected layers (𝖿𝖼𝟨𝖿𝖼𝟨\mathsf{fc6} and 𝖿𝖼𝟩𝖿𝖼𝟩\mathsf{fc7}) also reveals a similar sensitivity to small input changes.    •  Although the last three layers are sensitive to small input changes, much of the lower layer computation is more robust. For example, when visualizing the 𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧\mathsf{conv5} layer, one can find many invariant detectors for faces, shoulders, text, etc. by moving oneself or objects in front of the camera. Even though the 1000 classes contain no explicitly labeled faces or text, the network learns to identify these concepts simply because they represent useful partial information for making a later classification decision. One face detector, denoted 𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧𝟣𝟧𝟣subscript𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧151\mathsf{conv5_{151}} (channel number 151 on 𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧𝖼𝗈𝗇𝗏𝟧\mathsf{conv5}), is shown in Figure 2 activating for human and lion faces and in Figure 1 activating for a cat face. Zhou et al. (2014) recently observed a similar effect where convnets trained only to recognize different scene types — playgrounds, restaurant patios, living rooms, etc. — learn object detectors (e.g. for chairs, books, and sofas) on intermediate layers.

answer: The reason is that convolution layers learn parameters that can extract useful information and relations from the feature map that can help it afterwards to judge and give suitable responses of what this category is [16].

input: How does the proposed method address the issue of cluster collapse?
question: How does the proposed method address the issue of cluster collapse?
output:
The proposed method, which is Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Mutual Information Regularized Assignment [1], addresses the issue of cluster collapse by penalizing assignment that exhibits low Mutual Information (MI) [1]. Specifically, it naturally avoids collapsed solution via the MI term in Eq. 4 [1].

The MI term in Eq. 4 takes a minimum value when collapsing happens [1]. MIRA naturally avoids collapsed solution via penalizing assignment that exhibits low MI [1]. Unless starting from the collapsed state, MIRA finds MI-maximizing points around the model prediction; it will not choose collapsed pseudo-labels [1]. Hence, the iterative training to predict such labels will not collapse whenever the prediction of pseudo-labels is achievable [1].

The authors claim that their empirical results verify that MIRA does not require extra training techniques or artificial constraints to address collapsing [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Mutual Information Regularized Assignment
ctxs.text: The MI term in Eq. 4 takes a minimum value when collapsing happens. MIRA naturally avoids collapsed solution via penalizing assignment that exhibits low MI. Specifically, unless starting from the collapsed state, MIRA finds MI-maximizing points around the model prediction; it will not choose collapsed pseudo-labels. Hence, the iterative training to predict such labels will not collapse whenever the prediction of pseudo-labels is achievable. Our empirical results verify that MIRA does not require extra training techniques or artificial constraints to address collapsing.

answer: Mutual information regularizer unfavors collapsed representation [19].

input: What is meant by "hacks"?
question: What is meant by "hacks"?
output:
In the context of the paper excerpt, "hacks" refer to specific types of manipulations or modifications to input images that cause neural networks to produce high or low activations, even when the input images are correctly classified [1]. These "hacks" are not inherent properties of the network architecture but rather artifacts of the optimization process [1]. They include extreme pixel values, structured high-frequency patterns, and copies of common motifs without global structure [1].

The authors argue that these "hacks" are a consequence of the locally linear behavior of neural nets [1]. This means that even small, imperceptible changes to an input image can cause large changes in the network's activations [1]. This sensitivity to input perturbations is what allows these "hacks" to be applied to correctly classified images to cause them to be misclassified [1].

The paper highlights that these "hacks" can be found even without the gradient information [1], suggesting that they are not solely dependent on the optimization process [1]. Furthermore, the authors point out that the abundance of non-natural looking images that cause extreme activations can be explained by this local linearity [1].

In summary, "hacks" in this context are adversarial examples or perturbations that exploit the local linearity of neural nets to cause them to make incorrect predictions [1]. They are not inherent properties of the network architecture but rather artifacts of the optimization process [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: These gradient-based approaches are attractive in their simplicity, but the optimization process tends to produce images that do not greatly resemble natural images. Instead, they are composed of a collection of “hacks” that happen to cause high (or low) activations: extreme pixel values, structured high frequency patterns, and copies of common motifs without global structure (Simonyan et al., 2013; Nguyen et al., 2014; Szegedy et al., 2013; Goodfellow et al., 2014). The fact that activations may be effected by such hacks is better understood thanks to several recent studies. Specifically, it has been shown that such hacks may be applied to correctly classified images to cause them to be misclassified even via imperceptibly small changes (Szegedy et al., 2013), that such hacks can be found even without the gradient information to produce unrecognizable “fooling examples” (Nguyen et al., 2014), and that the abundance of non-natural looking images that cause extreme activations can be explained by the locally linear behavior of neural nets (Goodfellow et al., 2014).

answer: 'Hacks' means that they are not likely to naturally exist (non-natural looking images) [8].

input: What is the difference between five-fold cross validation and leave-one-patient out?
question: What is the difference between five-fold cross validation and leave-one-patient out?
output:
The difference between five-fold cross-validation (CV) and leave-one-patient-out (LOO) validation lies in how the dataset is split into training and testing sets. In standard 5-fold CV, the dataset is randomly split into 5 subsets, and the model is trained and tested on each subset sequentially. This means that each subset is used as the validation set exactly once, and the model is trained on the remaining 4 subsets. In LOO, the dataset is split into $N$ training sets and $N$ testing sets, where $N$ is the number of patients. For each patient, the model is trained on all other patients' data and tested on the held-out patient's data. This ensures that the model is evaluated on data it has never seen during training.

The paper excerpt suggests that LOO validation yields quantitatively better performances than standard 5-fold CV, particularly in ILD slice-level classification [1]. For instance, using AlexNet-TL, the accuracy level increases from 0.46 to 0.867 when using LOO validation compared to standard 5-fold CV [1]. Similarly, using GoogLeNet-TL, the accuracy level improves from 0.57 to 0.902 when using LOO validation [1]. This suggests that LOO validation provides a more robust and less optimistic estimate of the model's generalization ability on unseen patient data.

ctxs.title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer-Aided Detection: CNN Architectures, Dataset Characteristics and Transfer Learning
ctxs.text: To investigate the performance difference between five-fold cross-validation (CV) in Sec. IV-B and leave-one-patient-out (LOO) validation, this experiment is performed under the LOO protocol. By comparing results in Table III (CV-5) to those in Table VI (LOO), one can see that LOO’s quantitative performances are remarkably better than CV-5’s. For example, in ILD slice-level classification, the accuracy level drastically increases from 0.46 to 0.867 using AlexNet-TL, and from 0.57 to 0.902 for GoogLeNet-TL.

answer: LOO performs better than five-fold cross validation [51].

input: What are the reasons behind the guidelines of choosing values of {-1,-2 and -3} for the initial bias for convolutional highway network of depth {10, 20 and 30} ?
question: What are the reasons behind the guidelines of choosing values of {-1,-2 and -3} for the initial bias for convolutional highway network of depth {10, 20 and 30} ?
output:
The guidelines for choosing values of $\{-1,-2, \text{ and } -3\}$ for the initial bias of the highway network are not explicitly mentioned in the provided paper excerpt. The authors performed a random search of 100 runs for both plain and highway networks to find good settings for the following hyperparameters: initial learning rate, momentum, learning rate exponential decay factor & activation function (either rectified linear or tanh). For highway networks, an additional hyperparameter was the initial value for the transform gate bias (between $-1$ and $-10$). Other weights were initialized using the same normalized initialization as plain networks [1].

The authors did not specify the reasons behind choosing these values for the initial bias of the highway network. However, the choice of these values is likely related to the initialization of the weights and biases in the highway network. In the paper, they used the same normalized initialization as plain networks for all weights except the transform gate bias, which was initialized between $-1$ and $-10$ [1]. This initialization range suggests that the authors were exploring different values for this bias to see which ones worked best for their highway network.

ctxs.title: Training Very Deep Networks
ctxs.text: We trained both plain and highway networks of varying varying depths on the MNIST digit classification dataset. All networks are thin: each layer has 50 blocks for highway networks and 71 units for plain networks, yielding roughly identical numbers of parameters (≈\approx5000) per layer. In all networks, the first layer is a fully connected plain layer followed by 9, 19, 49, or 99 fully connected plain or highway layers. Finally, the network output is produced by a softmax layer. We performed a random search of 100 runs for both plain and highway networks to find good settings for the following hyperparameters: initial learning rate, momentum, learning rate exponential decay factor & activation function (either rectified linear or tanh). For highway networks, an additional hyperparameter was the initial value for the transform gate bias (between -1 and -10). Other weights were initialized using the same normalized initialization as plain networks.

answer: Although not explicitly stated, given that the authors selected the best hyperparameters out of 100 experiments, it is highly likely that the initial bias was also selected by these experiments [24].

input: What does the authors means by reframing object detection as a "single regression problem" ?
question: What does the authors means by reframing object detection as a "single regression problem" ?
output: The authors mean by reframing object detection as a "single regression problem" that they propose to solve the task of object detection in a unified manner [3]. Instead of using separate components for classification and localization, they propose a single neural network that takes an image as input and outputs all bounding boxes across all classes simultaneously [2]. This means that the network reasons globally about the full image and all the objects in it [2]. The network directly predicts the bounding box coordinates and class probabilities for every object in the image from the input image pixels [3]. This end-to-end training enables real-time performance while maintaining high average precision [2].

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: Current detection systems repurpose classifiers to perform detection. To detect an object, these systems take a classifier for that object and evaluate it at various locations and scales in a test image. Systems like deformable parts models (DPM) use a sliding window approach where the classifier is run at evenly spaced locations over the entire image .

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: We unify the separate components of object detection into a single neural network. Our network uses features from the entire image to predict each bounding box. It also predicts all bounding boxes across all classes for an image simultaneously. This means our network reasons globally about the full image and all the objects in the image. The YOLO design enables end-to-end training and real-time speeds while maintaining high average precision.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: We reframe object detection as a single regression problem, straight from image pixels to bounding box coordinates and class probabilities. Using our system, you only look once (YOLO) at an image to predict what objects are present and where they are.

answer: Reframing object detection as a simple regression problem means predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities directly from image pixels avoiding complex pipelines and steps which most of the existing (classifier-based) methods do [1]. YOLO can be trained end-to-end and can predict bounding boxes and respective class probabilities directly from an entire image [10]. Also, its loss function directly corresponds to detection performance, which makes optimizing it more intuitive and easier [3].

input: Why does the author use a rectangular shaped RoI?
question: Why does the author use a rectangular shaped RoI?
output: The author uses a rectangular shaped RoI (Region of Interest) because it is a standard and effective way to define a region in a feature map for RoI pooling [1]. This fixed spatial extent (H×W) is independent of any particular RoI, which allows the layer to handle different input sizes and aspect ratios [1].

ctxs.title: Fast R-CNN
ctxs.text: The RoI pooling layer uses max pooling to convert the features inside any valid region of interest into a small feature map with a fixed spatial extent of H×W𝐻𝑊H\times W (e.g., 7×7777\times 7), where H𝐻H and W𝑊W are layer hyper-parameters that are independent of any particular RoI. In this paper, an RoI is a rectangular window into a conv feature map. Each RoI is defined by a four-tuple (r,c,h,w)𝑟𝑐ℎ𝑤(r,c,h,w) that specifies its top-left corner (r,c)𝑟𝑐(r,c) and its height and width (h,w)ℎ𝑤(h,w).

answer: in order to make a fixed feature map [10].

input: Does a zero-shot scenario in this context refer to cases where relevance annotations are not available? Or are you referring to the case where the query set is also unavailable?
question: Does a zero-shot scenario in this context refer to cases where relevance annotations are not available? Or are you referring to the case where the query set is also unavailable?
output:
In the context of the paper excerpt, a zero-shot scenario refers to cases where no training data is available to train the system [1]. This means that the system is being evaluated on a task or domain where it has not been trained. In this setting, the system is expected to perform well on unseen data, which is a strong generalization capability.

The paper specifically points out that many retrieval systems are applied in a zero-shot setup, with no available training data to train the system [1]. This implies that the system is being evaluated on a task or domain where it has not been trained.

Therefore, in the context of the paper excerpt, a zero-shot scenario refers to cases where no training data is available to train the system [1]. It does not necessarily mean that relevance annotations are not available, nor does it mean that the query set is also unavailable. Instead, it means that the system is being evaluated on a task or domain where it has not been trained.

ctxs.title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
ctxs.text: However, creating a large training corpus is often time-consuming and expensive and hence many retrieval systems are applied in a zero-shot setup, with no available training data to train the system. So far, it is unclear how well existing trained neural models will perform for other text domains or textual retrieval tasks. Even more important, it is unclear how well different approaches, like sparse embeddings vs. dense embeddings, generalize to out-of-distribution data.

answer: creating a large training corpus is often time-consuming and expensive and hence many retrieval systems are applied in a zero-shot setup, with no available training data to train the system [2].

input: What are some examples of the SR model that uses deep neural network to encode user behavior sequences?
question: What are some examples of the SR model that uses deep neural network to encode user behavior sequences?
output:
Several models have used deep neural networks to encode user behavior sequences for the task of next-item prediction [1]. These models include:

*   **SASRec** [1] is a Transformer-based baseline for SR that uses a self-attention mechanism to capture the relationships between items in a user's history.
*   **GRU4Rec** [1] is an RNN-based method that uses a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) to capture the sequential dependencies in user behavior sequences.
*   **BERT4Rec** [1] replaces the next-item prediction objective with a Cloze task to fuse information between an item (a view) in a user behavior sequence and its contextual information.
*   **S-Rec3superscript-Rec3** [1] uses SSL to capture correlation-ship among item, sub-sequence, and associated attributes from the given user behavior sequence.
*   **CL4SRec** [1] fuses contrastive SSL with a Transformer-based SR model.

These models have shown state-of-the-art performance on various recommendation benchmarks [1].

ctxs.title: Intent Contrastive Learning for Sequential Recommendation
ctxs.text: •  Non-sequential models: BPR-MF (Rendle et al., 2012) characterizes the pairwise interactions via a matrix factorization model and optimizes through a pair-wise Bayesian Personalized Ranking loss.    •  Standard sequential models. We include solutions that train the models with a next-item prediction objective. Caser (Tang and Wang, 2018) is a CNN-based approach, GRU4Rec (Hidasi et al., 2015) is an RNN-based method, and SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018) is one of the state-of-the-art Transformer-based baselines for SR.    •  Sequential models with additional SSL: BERT4Rec (Sun et al., 2019) replaces the next-item prediction with a Cloze task (Taylor, 1953) to fuse information between an item (a view) in a user behavior sequence and its contextual information. S-Rec3superscript-Rec3{}^{3}\text{-Rec} (Zhou et al., 2020) uses SSL to capture correlation-ship among item, sub-sequence, and associated attributes from the given user behavior sequence. Its modules for mining on attributes are removed because we don’t have attributes for items, namely S-RecI​S​P3superscriptsubscript-Rec𝐼𝑆𝑃3{}^{3}\text{-Rec}_{ISP}. CL4SRec (Xie et al., 2020) fuses contrastive SSL with a Transformer-based SR model.    •  Sequential models considering latent factors: We include DSSRec(Ma et al., 2020), which utilizes seq2seq training and performs optimization in latent space. We do not directly compare ASLI (Tanjim et al., 2020), as it requires user action type information (e.g., click, add-to-favorite, etc). Instead, we provide a case study in Sec. 5.6 to evaluate the benefits of the learnt intent factor with additional item category information.

answer: BERT4Rec and S3-Rec are two examples [35].

input: How does this paper experimentally show that auxiliary tasks are not beneficial?
question: How does this paper experimentally show that auxiliary tasks are not beneficial?
output: The paper does not experimentally show that auxiliary tasks are not beneficial. Instead, it proposes a framework that leverages meta-paths for self-supervised auxiliary learning on heterogeneous graphs [2]. The authors compare their methods with five baseline graph neural networks: GCN, GAT, GIN, SGConv, and GTN [1]. They evaluate their learning strategies on both homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs, including Vanilla, standard training of base models only with the primary task samples, w/o meta-path, w/ meta-path, and SELAR [1]. Their findings suggest that meta-path predictions as auxiliary tasks are beneficial for improving the performance of the primary task [2].

ctxs.title: Self-supervised auxiliary learning with meta-paths for heterogeneous graphs
ctxs.text: Baselines.  We evaluate our methods with five graph neural networks : GCN , GAT , GIN , SGConv  and GTN . Our methods can be applied to both homogeneous graphs and heterogeneous graphs. We compare four learning strategies: Vanilla, standard training of base models only with the primary task samples; w/o meta-path, learning a primary task with sample weighting function 𝒱​(ξ;Θ)𝒱𝜉Θ\mathcal{V}(\xi;\Theta); w/ meta-path, training with the primary task and auxiliary tasks (meta-path prediction) with a standard loss function; SELAR proposed in Section 3.2, learning the primary task with optimized auxiliary tasks by meta-learning; SELAR+Hint introduced in Section 3.3. In all the experiments, we report the mean performance of three independent runs. Implementation details are in the supplement. Our experiments were mainly performed based on NAVER Smart Machine Learning platform (NSML) (54, 55).

ctxs.title: Self-supervised auxiliary learning with meta-paths for heterogeneous graphs
ctxs.text: The goal of our framework is to learn with multiple auxiliary tasks to improve the performance of the primary task. In this work, we demonstrate our framework with meta-path predictions as auxiliary tasks. But our framework could be extended to include other auxiliary tasks. The meta-paths capture diverse and meaningful relations between nodes on heterogeneous graphs . However, learning with auxiliary tasks has multiple challenges: identifying useful auxiliary tasks, balancing the auxiliary tasks with the primary task, and converting challenging auxiliary tasks into solvable (and relevant) tasks. To address the challenges, we propose SELf-supervised Auxiliary LeaRning (SELAR). Our framework consists of two main components: 1) learning weight functions to softly select auxiliary tasks and balance them with the primary task via meta-learning, and 2) learning Hint Networks to convert challenging auxiliary tasks into more relevant and solvable tasks to the primary task learner.

answer: This paper experimentally shows that auxiliary tasks are not always beneficial by comparing four different learning strategies [21]. The first strategy, "Vanilla," involves standard training of base models only with the primary task samples [7].

input: Normally, GAN training is unstable. Does this framework help to make the model stable?
question: Normally, GAN training is unstable. Does this framework help to make the model stable?
output: Yes, the framework helps to make the model stable during training [1]. Specifically, they replace the negative log likelihood objective with a least-squares loss for $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}((\mathcal{D}(G(x)) - 1)^2)$ and train the discriminator $\mathcal{D}$ to minimize $\mathbb{E}_{y\sim p_{\text{data}}}((\mathcal{D}(y) - 1)^2) + \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}((\mathcal{D}(G(x)))^2)$ [1]. This loss is more stable during training and generates higher quality results. Additionally, they update the discriminators using a history of generated images rather than the ones produced by the latest generators [2]. This strategy helps to reduce model oscillation [2].

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: We apply two techniques from recent works to stabilize our model training procedure. First, for ℒGANsubscriptℒGAN\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} (Equation 1), we replace the negative log likelihood objective by a least-squares loss . This loss is more stable during training and generates higher quality results. In particular, for a GAN loss ℒGAN​(G,D,X,Y)subscriptℒGAN𝐺𝐷𝑋𝑌\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G,D,X,Y), we train the G𝐺G to minimize 𝔼x∼pdata​(x)​((D​(G​(x))−1)2)subscript𝔼similar-to𝑥subscript𝑝data𝑥delimited-()superscript𝐷𝐺𝑥12\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}((D(G(x))-1)^{2}) and train the D𝐷D to minimize 𝔼y∼pdata​(y)​((D​(y)−1)2)+𝔼x∼pdata​(x)​(D​(G​(x))2)subscript𝔼similar-to𝑦subscript𝑝data𝑦delimited-()superscript𝐷𝑦12subscript𝔼similar-to𝑥subscript𝑝data𝑥delimited-()𝐷superscript𝐺𝑥2\mathbb{E}_{y\sim p_{\text{data}}(y)}((D(y)-1)^{2})+\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}(D(G(x))^{2}).

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: Second, to reduce model oscillation , we follow Shrivastava et al.’s strategy  and update the discriminators using a history of generated images rather than the ones produced by the latest generators. We keep an image buffer that stores the 505050 previously created images.

ctxs.title: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks
ctxs.text: In Table 4 and Table 5, we compare against ablations of our full loss. Removing the GAN loss substantially degrades results, as does removing the cycle-consistency loss. We therefore conclude that both terms are critical to our results. We also evaluate our method with the cycle loss in only one direction: GAN + forward cycle loss 𝔼x∼pdata​(x)​(∥F​(G​(x))−x∥1)subscript𝔼similar-to𝑥subscript𝑝data𝑥delimited-()subscriptdelimited-∥∥𝐹𝐺𝑥𝑥1\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}(\lVert F(G(x))-x\rVert_{1}), or GAN + backward cycle loss 𝔼y∼pdata​(y)​(∥G​(F​(y))−y∥1)subscript𝔼similar-to𝑦subscript𝑝data𝑦delimited-()subscriptdelimited-∥∥𝐺𝐹𝑦𝑦1\mathbb{E}_{y\sim p_{\text{data}}(y)}(\lVert G(F(y))-y\rVert_{1}) (Equation 2) and find that it often incurs training instability and causes mode collapse, especially for the direction of the mapping that was removed. Figure 7 shows several qualitative examples.

answer: [The paper applies two techniques to stabilize its model training procedure [23]. First, for \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} (Equation 1), the paper replaces the negative log likelihood objective by a least-squares loss [24]. Secondly, to reduce model oscillation, the paper follows Shrivastava et al’s strategy and updates the discriminators using a history of generated images rather than the ones produced by the latest generators [40].

input: What is the aim of using VAEs ?
question: What is the aim of using VAEs ?
output:
The aim of using VAEs is to achieve independence between the dimensions of the aggregate encoding, typically defined as $q_{\phi}(\bm{z}) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_{p_{\mathcal{D}}}(\mathbf{x}) \left( q(\bm{z}|\mathbf{x}) \right) \approx \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i}^{n} q(\bm{z}|\mathbf{x}_{i})$. The significance of $q_{\phi}(\bm{z})$ is that it is the marginal distribution induced on the latents by sampling a datapoint and then using the encoder to sample an encoding given that datapoint. It can thus informally be thought of as the pushforward distribution for “sampling” representations in the latent space [1].

In the context of $\text{VAEs}$ [1], $\text{VAEs}$ aim to achieve independence between the dimensions of the aggregate encoding, typically defined as $q_{\phi}(\bm{z}) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_{p_{\mathcal{D}}}(\mathbf{x}) \left( q(\bm{z}|\mathbf{x}) \right) \approx \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i}^{n} q(\bm{z}|\mathbf{x}_{i})$. The significance of $q_{\phi}(\bm{z})$ is that it is the marginal distribution induced on the latents by sampling a datapoint and then using the encoder to sample an encoding given that datapoint. It can thus informally be thought of as the pushforward distribution for “sampling” representations in the latent space [1].

It can thus informally be thought of as the pushforward distribution for “sampling” representations in the latent space [1].

The significance of qϕ​(𝒛)subscript𝑞italic-ϕ𝒛q_{\phi}(\bm{z}) is that it is the marginal distribution induced on the latents by sampling a datapoint and then using the encoder to sample an encoding given that datapoint. It can thus informally be thought of as the pushforward distribution for “sampling” representations in the latent space.

ctxs.title: Disentangling Disentanglement in Variational Autoencoders
ctxs.text: We characterise the decomposition of latent spaces in vaes to be the fulfilment of two factors (as shown in Figure 1):   a.  An “appropriate” level of overlap in the latent space—ensuring that the range of latent values capable of encoding a particular datapoint is neither too small, nor too large. This is, in general, dictated by the level of stochasticity in the encoder: the noisier the encoding process is, the higher the number of datapoints which can plausibly give rise to a particular encoding.    b.  The aggregate encoding qϕ​(𝒛)subscript𝑞italic-ϕ𝒛q_{{\phi}}\left(\bm{z}\right) matching the prior p​(𝒛)𝑝𝒛p\left(\bm{z}\right), where the latter expresses the desired dependency structure between latents.

answer: The aim to use VAEs is to explore their decomposition capability to fulfill latent encoding of overlapping data and aggregate encoding of this data conforming to desired structure [10]. The approaches that explore disentanglement in the context of  VAEs aims to achieve independence between the dimensions of the aggregate encoding [16].

input: How do the authors deal with the numerical instability that may occur due to incorporating SVD into the proposed method?
question: How do the authors deal with the numerical instability that may occur due to incorporating SVD into the proposed method?
output: The authors address the numerical instability that may occur due to incorporating the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) into their proposed method by employing an approximation algorithm for the gradient calculation [1]. This method is claimed to be significantly faster than the standard SVD, achieving an improvement of over 888 times in computation time [1]. However, they acknowledge that this method introduces an error compared to the standard SVD, which can grow bigger as the size of the matrix grows [1]. Despite this limitation, they utilize this method to stabilize and accelerate the learning process in their work [1].

ctxs.title: Grouping-matrix based Graph Pooling with Adaptive Number of Clusters
ctxs.text: After acquiring the pooling operator, the pooling process becomes obvious. Nodes are in fundamental representation while edge features and adjacency matrix are in adjoint representation. Which leads to the following transformation rules.    Xi(l+1)=S(l)​Xi(l)superscriptsubscript𝑋𝑖𝑙1superscript𝑆𝑙superscriptsubscript𝑋𝑖𝑙\displaystyle X_{i}^{(l+1)}=S^{(l)}X_{i}^{(l)}  (13)    Ei​j(l+1)=S(l)​Ei​j(l)​S(l)​Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑖𝑗𝑙1superscript𝑆𝑙superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑖𝑗𝑙superscript𝑆𝑙𝑇\displaystyle E_{ij}^{(l+1)}=S^{(l)}E_{ij}^{(l)}S^{(l)T}  (14)    Ai​j(l+1)=S(l)​Ai​j(l)​S(l)​Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐴𝑖𝑗𝑙1superscript𝑆𝑙superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑖𝑗𝑙superscript𝑆𝑙𝑇\displaystyle A_{ij}^{(l+1)}=S^{(l)}A_{ij}^{(l)}S^{(l)T}  (15)   If grouping is properly done, 00 (or close to 00) components will appear in the decomposed eigen value matrix. These zero eigenvalues arise naturally and play a role in disregarding group information; those are ineffective towards prediction. However, zero elements in the eigen values causes a major problem in the decomposition process since the matrix might carry a singular determinant. Eigen decomposition is based on an iterative approximation algorithm which includes unbounded terms if any two eigen values are small or close. One can see clearly about this matter in DBLP:journals/corr/IonescuVS15 .    (∂l∂A)=U​(KT⊙(UT​∂l∂U)+(∂l∂Λ)diag)​(UT)𝑙𝐴𝑈direct-productsuperscript𝐾𝑇superscript𝑈𝑇𝑙𝑈subscript𝑙Λdiagsuperscript𝑈𝑇\Big{(}\frac{\partial{l}}{\partial{A}}\Big{)}=U\big{(}K^{T}\odot(U^{T}\frac{\partial{l}}{\partial{U}})+(\frac{\partial{l}}{\partial{\Lambda}})_{\textrm{diag}})(U^{T})  (16)   Here, ⊙direct-product\odot denotes element-wise product. Off-diagonal components of K=1/(λi−λj)𝐾1subscript𝜆𝑖subscript𝜆𝑗K=1/(\lambda_{i}-\lambda_{j}) causes the problem, since the value blows up to the infinity if any two eigen values are close or very small. However, there are some solutions for this matter by approximating gradient in different ways DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1906-09023 ; 9400752 ; DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2105-02498 . Those methods are developed further to achieve higher speed in the calculation DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2201-08663 . They claim that the method is noticeably faster, over 888 times, than the standard SVD which has the time complexity 𝒪​(n3)𝒪superscript𝑛3\mathcal{O}(n^{3}). Thus, we utilized this method in our work to stabilize and accelerate the learning process. However, since the algorithm achieves the higher speed by approximating gradients, the error compared to standard SVD grows bigger as the size of the matrix grows. Therefore, this method might not be valid with large sized graph data.

answer: GMPool decomposes the grouping matrix using a method that approximates gradients in SVD to stabilize gradient computations [20].

input: How does training pre-training a latent space in Optimus lead to higher performance for dialog generation? Is it because the whole dialog can be encoded in the latent space?
question: How does training pre-training a latent space in Optimus lead to higher performance for dialog generation? Is it because the whole dialog can be encoded in the latent space?
output: The paper excerpt does not explicitly state that training a latent space in Optimus leads to higher performance for dialog generation because the whole dialog can be encoded in the latent space. Instead, it mentions that they follow [1] in embedding the history and response in a joint latent space as $\boldsymbol{z}_{\text{S2S}}}$ and $\boldsymbol{z}_{\text{AE}}}$ respectively. They use a fusion regularization to match the responses to the context. The paper also mentions that they use the DailyDialogue dataset [1] which has 13,118 daily conversations, and each utterance is processed as the response of previous 10 context utterances from both speakers. They measure performance using Bleu [2] and compute precision, recall, and F1 in Table [4]. The authors claim that Optimus shows higher Bleu scores than all existing baselines [1].

ctxs.title: Optimus: Organizing Sentences via Pre-trained Modeling of a Latent Space
ctxs.text: The open-domain dialog response generation task is considered: generating responses 𝒙𝒙\boldsymbol{x} given a dialog history 𝒄𝒄{\boldsymbol{c}}. Following Gao et al. (2019a), we embed the history and response in a joint latent space as 𝒛S2Ssubscript𝒛S2S\boldsymbol{z}_{\text{S2S}} and 𝒛AEsubscript𝒛AE\boldsymbol{z}_{\text{AE}}, respectively. A fusion regularization is used to match the responses to the context. We consider 𝙳𝚊𝚒𝚕𝚢𝚍𝚒𝚊𝚕𝚘𝚐𝙳𝚊𝚒𝚕𝚢𝚍𝚒𝚊𝚕𝚘𝚐\mathtt{Dailydialog} Li et al. (2017c) used in Gu et al. (2019), which has 13,118 daily conversations. Each utterance is processed as the response of previous 10 context utterances from both speakers. The baseline methods are described in Appendix. We measure the performance using Bleu Chen and Cherry (2014), and compute the precision, recall and F1 in Table 4. Optimus shows higher Bleu scores than all existing baselines.

answer: There is no information about dialog generation specifically, to explain if this outperformance by OPTIMUS can be attributed specifically to being able to encode the entire dialog in latent space [34].

input: How did the authors imporved the accuarcy by making the model deeper but decreased the training time simultaneously?
question: How did the authors imporved the accuarcy by making the model deeper but decreased the training time simultaneously?
output: The authors improved the accuracy by making the model deeper, while decreasing the training time simultaneously, by introducing a residual learning network [1]. This network allows the network to learn the residual information between the output of one layer and the input of the next layer, which helps the network to learn more effectively and converge much faster than the standard CNN [1].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: Second, we show that our residual-learning network converges much faster than the standard CNN. Moreover, our network gives a significant boost in performance.

answer: They changed the model architecture to multi-scale to reduce model capacity and used residual learning with high learning rates to increase training speed [41].

input: How scales of the default bounding boxes for a particular feature map is computed?
question: How scales of the default bounding boxes for a particular feature map is computed?
output:
The scales of the default bounding boxes for a particular feature map are computed as follows:
$$s_{k}=s_{\text{min}}+\frac{s_{\text{max}}-s_{\text{min}}}{m-1}(k-1),\quad k\in(1,m)$$
where $s_{\text{min}}$ is the minimum scale, $s_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum scale, $m$ is the number of feature maps, and $k$ is the index of the feature map ($k \in (1, m)$). This formula linearly interpolates between the minimum and maximum scales, ensuring that all layers in between are regularly spaced [1].

ctxs.title: SSD: Single Shot MultiBox Detector
ctxs.text: Feature maps from different levels within a network are known to have different (empirical) receptive field sizes . Fortunately, within the SSD framework, the default boxes do not necessary need to correspond to the actual receptive fields of each layer. We design the tiling of default boxes so that specific feature maps learn to be responsive to particular scales of the objects. Suppose we want to use m𝑚m feature maps for prediction. The scale of the default boxes for each feature map is computed as:    sk=smin+smax−sminm−1​(k−1),k∈(1,m)formulae-sequencesubscript𝑠𝑘subscript𝑠minsubscript𝑠maxsubscript𝑠min𝑚1𝑘1𝑘1𝑚s_{k}=s_{\text{min}}+\frac{s_{\text{max}}-s_{\text{min}}}{m-1}(k-1),\quad k\in(1,m)  (4)   where sminsubscript𝑠mins_{\text{min}} is 0.2 and smaxsubscript𝑠maxs_{\text{max}} is 0.9, meaning the lowest layer has a scale of 0.2 and the highest layer has a scale of 0.9, and all layers in between are regularly spaced. We impose different aspect ratios for the default boxes, and denote them as ar∈{1,2,3,12,13}subscript𝑎𝑟1231213a_{r}\in\{1,2,3,\frac{1}{2},\frac{1}{3}\}. We can compute the width (wka=sk​arsuperscriptsubscript𝑤𝑘𝑎subscript𝑠𝑘subscript𝑎𝑟w_{k}^{a}=s_{k}\sqrt{a_{r}}) and height (hka=sk/arsuperscriptsubscriptℎ𝑘𝑎subscript𝑠𝑘subscript𝑎𝑟h_{k}^{a}=s_{k}/\sqrt{a_{r}}) for each default box. For the aspect ratio of 1, we also add a default box whose scale is sk′=sk​sk+1subscriptsuperscript𝑠′𝑘subscript𝑠𝑘subscript𝑠𝑘1s^{\prime}_{k}=\sqrt{s_{k}s_{k+1}}, resulting in 6 default boxes per feature map location. We set the center of each default box to (i+0.5|fk|,j+0.5|fk|)𝑖0.5subscript𝑓𝑘𝑗0.5subscript𝑓𝑘(\frac{i+0.5}{|f_{k}|},\frac{j+0.5}{|f_{k}|}), where |fk|subscript𝑓𝑘|f_{k}| is the size of the k𝑘k-th square feature map, i,j∈(0,|fk|)𝑖𝑗0subscript𝑓𝑘i,j\in(0,|f_{k}|). input: Did the method proposed in this paper perform on par with or better than the state-of-the-art methods that require users to provide spatial masks for editing?
question: Did the method proposed in this paper perform on par with or better than the state-of-the-art methods that require users to provide spatial masks for editing?
output: The method proposed in this paper, Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control, outperforms the state-of-the-art methods that require users to provide explicit spatial masks for editing [1]. Previous methods that relied on user-provided masks for editing have been shown to be cumbersome and to remove important structural information from the image [1]. In contrast, the proposed method allows for intuitive and powerful textual editing by directly manipulating the cross-attention layers within the text-conditioned diffusion models [2]. This enables users to control the generated image in a more direct and intuitive manner, without the need for manual masking [1].

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: To circumvent this, LLI-based methods (28, 4, 33) require the user to explicitly mask a part of the image to be inpainted, and drive the edited image to change in the masked area only, while matching the background of the original image. This approach has provided appealing results, however, the masking procedure is cumbersome, hampering quick and intuitive text-driven editing. Moreover, masking the image content removes important structural information, which is completely ignored in the inpainting process. Therefore, some editing capabilities are out of the inpainting scope, such as modifying the texture of a specific object.

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: In this paper, we introduce an intuitive and powerful textual editing method to semantically edit images in pre-trained text-conditioned diffusion models via Prompt-to-Prompt manipulations. To do so, we dive deep into the cross-attention layers and explore their semantic strength as a handle to control the generated image. Specifically, we consider the internal cross-attention maps, which are high-dimensional tensors that bind pixels and tokens extracted from the prompt text. We find that these maps contain rich semantic relations which critically affect the generated image.

answer: Yes their method did perform better than mask editing methods, as authors demonstrated by examples that their method is more intuitive for users using only prompt, and doesn't require to explicitly mask parts of the image which results to remove important structural information and doesn't modify complex structures information [1]. And their work enables local or global modifications as well and besides their method doesn't require a training network [2].

input: What is the issue that come with very deep networks?
question: What is the issue that come with very deep networks?
output: One problem with using a very deep network to predict dense outputs is that the size of the feature map gets reduced every time convolution operations are applied [1]. For example, when an input of size $(n+1)\times(n+1)$ is applied to a network with receptive field size $n\times n$, the output image is $1\times 1$ [1]. Another issue with very deep networks is that they can fail to converge in realistic limit of time [2]. For instance, SRCNN fails to show superior performance with more than three weight layers, and their learning rate of $10^{-5}$ is too small for a network to converge within a week on a common GPU [2].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: One problem with using a very deep network to predict dense outputs is that the size of the feature map gets reduced every time convolution operations are applied. For example, when an input of size (n+1)×(n+1)𝑛1𝑛1(n+1)\times(n+1) is applied to a network with receptive field size n×n𝑛𝑛n\times n, the output image is 1×1111\times 1.

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: High Learning Rates for Very Deep Networks Training deep models can fail to converge in realistic limit of time. SRCNN  fails to show superior performance with more than three weight layers. While there can be various reasons, one possibility is that they stopped their training procedure before networks converged. Their learning rate 10−5superscript10510^{-5} is too small for a network to converge within a week on a common GPU. Looking at Fig. 9 of , it is not easy to say their deeper networks have converged and their performances were saturated. While more training will eventually resolve the issue, but increasing depth to 20 does not seems practical with SRCNN.

answer: Training is difficult for very deep networks as they have many parameters and it takes a long time for them to converge [21]. Also, since convolutional layers shrink feature maps, having too deep a network could be bad [31].

input: The authors restrict batch size to 1 during training. Why did the authors do this, and what problems have might been encountered with higher batch size?
question: The authors restrict batch size to 1 during training. Why did the authors do this, and what problems have might been encountered with higher batch size?
output:
The authors restrict batch size to 1 throughout the training process [1]. This choice is likely due to the relatively small size of the TIMIT dataset [1]. When using a small dataset, it is crucial to employ proper regularization techniques to prevent overfitting [1]. The authors used the adaptive weight noise as a main regularizer [1]. They first trained their models with a column norm constraint [1] until the lowest development negative log-likelihood was achieved [1]. Applying the weight noise from the beginning of training caused severe underfitting [1]. During this time, $\epsilon$ and $\rho$ were set to $10^{-8}$ and $0.95$, respectively [1]. At this point, they began using the adaptive weight noise and scaled down the model complexity cost $L_C$ by a factor of 10, while disabling the column norm constraints [1]. Once the new lowest development log-likelihood was reached, they fine-tuned the model with a smaller $\epsilon=10^{-10}$, until they did not observe the improvement in the development phoneme error rate (PER) for $100K$ weight updates [1].

input: Which metric is used to compare different unsupervised models?
question: Which metric is used to compare different unsupervised models?
output: The paper uses two metrics to compare different unsupervised models: [1] the error in predicting the future [1] and [1] the performance on supervised tasks as a proxy for how good the unsupervised model is doing [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: The aim of this set of experiments is to compare the different variants of the model proposed in this paper. Since it is always possible to get lower reconstruction error by copying the inputs, we cannot use input reconstruction error as a measure of how good a model is doing. However, we can use the error in predicting the future as a reasonable measure of how good the model is doing. Besides, we can use the performance on supervised tasks as a proxy for how good the unsupervised model is doing. In this section, we present results from these two analyses.

answer: Error in predicting the future and the performance on supervised tasks are the metrics  used to compare different unsupervised models [39].

input: Is the trained wordpiece model the same as the Google speech recognition system developed to solve a Japanese/Korean segmentation problem the authors mentioned above?
question: Is the trained wordpiece model the same as the Google speech recognition system developed to solve a Japanese/Korean segmentation problem the authors mentioned above?
output: The authors mention that they adopt the wordpiece model (WPM) implementation initially developed to solve a Japanese/Korean segmentation problem for the Google speech recognition system [1]. However, they do not explicitly state that the trained wordpiece model is the same as the Google speech recognition system developed for this problem. Instead, they say that their approach is "completely data-driven and guaranteed to generate a deterministic segmentation for any possible sequence of characters" [1]. This suggests that their approach is based on the wordpiece model, but it is not necessarily the same as the one used in the Google speech recognition system.

ctxs.title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation
ctxs.text: Our most successful approach falls into the second category (sub-word units), and we adopt the wordpiece model (WPM) implementation initially developed to solve a Japanese/Korean segmentation problem for the Google speech recognition system . This approach is completely data-driven and guaranteed to generate a deterministic segmentation for any possible sequence of characters. It is similar to the method used in  to deal with rare words in Neural Machine Translation.

answer: Yes, the word piece model was initially developed to solve a Japanese/Korean segmentation problem [27].

input: What are the consequences of using class labels and box layouts ?
question: What are the consequences of using class labels and box layouts ?
output: The consequences of using class labels and box layouts are that they are inevitably collapsed into short output vectors by fully-connected (fc) layers [1]. This loss of fine-grained spatial information can be detrimental to the performance of downstream tasks that require precise pixel-level localization, such as semantic segmentation or instance segmentation [1].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: A mask encodes an input object’s spatial layout. Thus, unlike class labels or box offsets that are inevitably collapsed into short output vectors by fully-connected (fc) layers, extracting the spatial structure of masks can be addressed naturally by the pixel-to-pixel correspondence provided by convolutions.

answer: Using class labels and box layouts make collapsing into short output vector by fully connected layers inevitable [17].

ctxs.text: BERT-style pretraining crucially relies on large quantities of text. Baevski et al. (2019) demonstrate that increasing data size can result in improved end-task performance. Several efforts have trained on datasets larger and more diverse than the original BERT Radford et al. (2019); Yang et al. (2019); Zellers et al. (2019). For our study, we focus on gathering as much data as possible for experimentation, allowing us to match the overall quality and quantity of data as appropriate for each comparison.

input: How did the authors speed up the training?
question: How did the authors speed up the training?
output: The authors sped up the training by suggesting an adjustable gradient clipping [1] to maximize the training speed while suppressing exploding gradients. This technique helps prevent the gradients from becoming too large, which can cause the training to become unstable or stop making progress [1].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: It is a basic rule of thumb to make learning rate high to boost training. But simply setting learning rate high can also lead to vanishing/exploding gradients . For the reason, we suggest an adjustable gradient clipping for maximal boost in speed while suppressing exploding gradients.

answer: The authors used residual learning, extremely high learning rates, and adjustable gradient clipping [32].
