Authors claim that unsupervised approaches are explored more because of the difficulties and challenges associated with building labelled datasets [34]. However, this paper does not contain information on GPT-style models specifically, so answering how the pretrianing process for GPT differs from the author’s approach is not possible from information in this paper [5].

input: Why the conditional decoder is difficult to optimize?
question: Why the conditional decoder is difficult to optimize?
output: The conditional decoder is difficult to optimize because it is given access to the last few frames while generating a particular frame at training time [1]. This makes it easy for the decoder to pick up on these strong short-range correlations in video data, such as most of the content of a frame being the same as the previous one [1]. Consequently, the gradient that tries to fix up the extremely subtle errors that require long-term knowledge about the input sequence is very small [1]. In an unconditional decoder, this input is removed, and the model is forced to look for information deep inside the encoder [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: There is also an argument against using a conditional decoder from the optimization point-of-view. There are strong short-range correlations in video data, for example, most of the content of a frame is same as the previous one. If the decoder was given access to the last few frames while generating a particular frame at training time, it would find it easy to pick up on these correlations. There would only be a very small gradient that tries to fix up the extremely subtle errors that require long term knowledge about the input sequence. In an unconditioned decoder, this input is removed and the model is forced to look for information deep inside the encoder.

answer: Since in conditional decoder have access to last few frames, often it find a easy way to pick up a correlated frame but not necessary an optimized one [18].

input: Why was the model trained with synthetic data rather than reel 3D data directly?
question: Why was the model trained with synthetic data rather than reel 3D data directly?
output: The model was trained with synthetic data rather than real-world 3D data directly because the authors wanted to demonstrate the robustness of their networks to real-world data [1]. Their experiments on 3D CAD model datasets showed that their networks could generalize well from synthetic data to real-world data [1]. However, they also introduced a dataset of real-world 3D data constructed using dense 3D reconstruction taken with . Their experiments on this real-world data showed that their networks could better adapt from synthetic data to this real-world data than previous methods [1].

Experiments show that our networks can better adapt from synthetic data to this real-world data than previous methods.

input: What is the role of encoding noise and the likelihood distribution in VAE representations?
question: What is the role of encoding noise and the likelihood distribution in VAE representations?
output:
The role of encoding noise and the likelihood distribution in VAE representations is to enforce a structure on the latent space that encourages disentanglement [1]. Specifically, the stochastic nature of the encodings used during training forces nearby encodings to relate to similar datapoints [1]. This constraint means that the ELBO objective encourages the learned representations to be disentangled, where each latent variable controls a distinct generative factor [1].

The choice of likelihood distribution, such as assuming conditional independence, ensures that information is stored in the encodings, not just in the generative network [1]. This assumption is crucial because it implies that the ELBO objective encourages the learned representations to be factorized, where each factor corresponds to a distinct generative factor [1].

In summary, the encoding noise and the likelihood distribution in VAEs play a crucial role in shaping the structure of the latent space, encouraging disentanglement, and ensuring that information is effectively compressed into the latent variables [1].

ctxs.title: Disentangling Disentanglement in Variational Autoencoders
ctxs.text: In concurrently published work, Locatello et al.  question the plausibility of learning unsupervised disentangled representations with meaningful features, based on theoretical analyses showing an equivalence class of generative models, many members of which could be entangled. Though their analysis is sound, we posit a counterargument to their conclusions, based on the stochastic nature of the encodings used during training. Namely, that this stochasticity means that they need not give rise to the same elbo scores (an important exception is the rotational invariance for isotropic Gaussian priors). Essentially, the encoding noise forces nearby encodings to relate to similar datapoints, while standard choices for the likelihood distribution (e.g. assuming conditional independence) ensure that information is stored in the encodings, not just in the generative network. These restrictions mean that the elbo prefers smooth representations and, provided the prior is not rotationally invariant, means that there no longer need be a class of different representations with the same elbo; simpler representations are preferred to more complex ones.

answer: In VAE representation, the encoding noise forces nearby encodings to relate to similar datapoints, while standard choices for the likelihood distribution ensure that information is stored in the encodings, not just in the generative network [45].

input: What are the Y, U and V channels?
question: What are the Y, U and V channels?
output: The Y, U, and V channels are three channels of the input image, respectively. The Y channel represents the image intensity, while the U and V channels represent the color information separately [1]. These three channels are used to extract seven channels' worth of features from the 24x24 pixel image, giving 24x24x7 = 4032 input features [1]. The first three channels are the image in YUV color space, used because it represents image intensity and color separately [1]. The next is simply the depth channel of the image [1]. The last three are the X, Y, and Z components of surface normals computed based on the depth channel [1]. These are computed after the image is aligned to the gripper so that they are always relative to the gripper plates [1].

ctxs.title: Deep learning for detecting robotic grasps
ctxs.text: From this 24x24 pixel image, seven channels’ worth of features are extracted, giving 24x24x7 = 4032 input features. The first three channels are the image in YUV color space, used because it represents image intensity and color separately. The next is simply the depth channel of the image. The last three are the X, Y, and Z components of surface normals computed based on the depth channel. These are computed after the image is aligned to the gripper so that they are always relative to the gripper plates.

ctxs.title: Deep learning for detecting robotic grasps
ctxs.text: Figure 8 shows the features learned by the unsupervised phase of our algorithm which have a high correlation to positive and negative grasping cases. Many of these features show non-zero weights to the depth channel, indicating that it learns the correlation of depths to graspability. We can see that weights to many of the modalities for these features have been eliminated by our structured regularization approach. In particular, many of these features lack weights to the U and V (3r​dsuperscript3𝑟𝑑3^{rd} and 4t​hsuperscript4𝑡ℎ4^{th}) channels, which correspond to color, allowing the system to be more robust to different-colored objects.

answer: The first three channels are the image in YUV color space, used because it represents image intensity and color separately [42]. In particular,many of these features lack weights to the U and V (3rd and 4th channels), which correspond to color, allowing the system to be more robust to different-colored objects [69].

input: What is the effect of increasing modulating factor (γ )?
question: What is the effect of increasing modulating factor (γ )?
output: Increasing the focusing parameter $\gamma$ in the Focal Loss [1] increases the importance of correcting misclassified examples [2]. Specifically, a larger $\gamma$ value reduces the loss contribution from easy examples [2]. For instance, with $\gamma=2$, an example classified with $p_{\textrm{t}}} = 0.9$ would have $100\times$ lower loss compared with standard Cross Entropy Loss (CE) [2]. This reduction in loss contribution from easy examples allows the model to focus more on hard examples [2].

question: In this sentence, do the current target state and all source states mean hidden states of the encoder?

input: How different versions of NASNets with different computational demands were created?
question: How different versions of NASNets with different computational demands were created?
output: Different versions of NASNets with different computational demands were created by simply varying the number of convolutional cells and the number of filters in the convolutional cells [1]. This property of the cells allows the generation of a family of models that achieve accuracies superior to all human-invented models at equivalent or smaller computational budgets [1]. Notably, the smallest version of NASNet achieves 74.0% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, which is 3.1% better than previously engineered architectures targeted towards mobile and embedded vision tasks [1].

ctxs.title: Learning Transferable Architectures for Scalable Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Additionally, by simply varying the number of the convolutional cells and number of filters in the convolutional cells, we can create different versions of NASNets with different computational demands. Thanks to this property of the cells, we can generate a family of models that achieve accuracies superior to all human-invented models at equivalent or smaller computational budgets (60, 29). Notably, the smallest version of NASNet achieves 74.0% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, which is 3.1% better than previously engineered architectures targeted towards mobile and embedded vision tasks (24, 70).

answer: Different versions of NASNets with different computational demands were created by varying the number of the convolutional cells and number of filters in the convolutional cells [3].

input: What is the combinatorial action space? How is this different to general RL tasks? Are they not combinatorial?
question: What is the combinatorial action space? How is this different to general RL tasks? Are they not combinatorial?
output:
The combinatorial action space in Natural Language Processing (NLP) is defined as the set of all possible sequences of tokens from a finite vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ that can be generated by a language model (LM). For example, GPT-2/3 and T5 have a vocabulary size of 50K and 32K respectively, leading to an action space size of $|\mathcal{V}|^{\text{sequence length}}$, which is astronomically large [1]. This combinatorial action space is significantly larger than what most discrete action space RL algorithms are designed for [1].

In general RL tasks, the action space is typically discrete and small, such as choosing one of a few predefined actions from a set $\mathcal{A}$. However, in NLP, the action space is continuous or discrete with an extremely large cardinality, making it intractable to explore exhaustively.

To address this issue, we introduce NLPO (Natural Language Policy Optimization), which is inspired by work on action elimination/invalid-action masking [2]. NLPO learns to mask out less relevant tokens in-context as it trains, restricting the set of available actions to the smallest possible set whose cumulative probability is greater than the probability parameter $p$. This is achieved via top-$p$ sampling, which restricts the set of tokens to the smallest possible set whose cumulative probability is greater than the probability parameter $p$ [1].

This combinatorial action space is not combinatorial in the sense that the number of possible sequences is infinite, but rather it is exponentially large in the sequence length, making it impossible to explore exhaustively. The size of the action space is a core cause of instability when training LMs with existing RL methods [1].

ctxs.title: Is Reinforcement Learning (Not) for Natural Language Processing?: Benchmarks, Baselines, and Building Blocks for Natural Language Policy Optimization
ctxs.text: Language generation action spaces are orders of magnitude larger than what most discrete action space RL algorithms are designed for (Ranzato et al., 2016; Ammanabrolu, 2021), e.g., GPT-2/3 and T5 have a vocabulary size of 50K and 32K respectively. We hypothesize that the size of the action space is a core cause of instability when training LMs with existing RL methods. To address this issue, we introduce NLPO (Natural Language Policy Optimization), which is inspired by work on action elimination/invalid-action masking (Zahavy et al., 2018; Huang & Ontañón, 2020; Ammanabrolu & Hausknecht, 2020). NLPO, a parameterized-masked extension of PPO, learns to mask out less relevant tokens in-context as it trains. NLPO accomplishes this via top-p𝑝p sampling, which restricts tokens to the smallest possible set whose cumulative probability is greater than the probability parameter p𝑝p (Holtzman et al., 2018).

answer: The combinatorial action space here probably refers to the set of all possible actions that a RL agent for optimizing a language model could possibly take - here, the action set consists of the entire vocabulary of the language model, which can range to tens of thousands for typical GPT/T5 models used today [11]. This is unlike general RL tasks, where the action space is an order of magnitude smaller [7].

input: What is the purpose of FashionMNIST?
question: What is the purpose of FashionMNIST?
output: Fashion-MNIST is a novel image dataset intended to be a drop-in replacement of MNIST [1]. It provides a more challenging alternative for benchmarking machine learning algorithm [1]. The images in Fashion-MNIST are converted to a format that matches that of the MNIST dataset, making it immediately compatible with any machine learning package capable of working with the original MNIST dataset [1].

ctxs.title: Fashion-MNIST: a Novel Image Dataset for Benchmarking Machine Learning Algorithms
ctxs.text: This paper introduced Fashion-MNIST, a fashion product images dataset intended to be a drop-in replacement of MNIST and whilst providing a more challenging alternative for benchmarking machine learning algorithm. The images in Fashion-MNIST are converted to a format that matches that of the MNIST dataset, making it immediately compatible with any machine learning package capable of working with the original MNIST dataset.

answer: The purpose of dataset is a drop-in replacement of MNIST and whilst providing a more challenging alternative for benchmarking machine learning algorithms and create good benchmark [10].

input: Doesn’t the possibility of having many rules make it ambiguous?
question: Doesn’t the possibility of having many rules make it ambiguous?
output:
The possibility of having many rules does not make it ambiguous whether the goal is proved or disproved, as the system uses a specific mechanism to resolve such cases. The system employs a "Sign Agreement" module to determine whether the sign of the goal agrees or disagrees with the sign of the consequent of the rule being applied [1].

If the sign of the goal agrees with the sign of the consequent of the rule being applied, the system concludes that the goal is proved [1]. For example, in the case where the goal is ‘‘Eric is nice’’ and Rule6 is ‘‘(…) is nice’’ and the antecedent of the rule is proved, the system concludes that the goal is proved [1].

However, if the sign of the goal disagrees with the sign of the consequent of the rule being applied, the system concludes that the goal is disproved [1]. For instance, if Rule6 was ‘‘(...) is not going to be a nice individual’’ and the antecedent of the rule is proved, the system concludes that the goal is disproved [1].

Therefore, the system resolves such cases by checking whether the sign of the goal agrees or disagrees with the sign of the consequent of the rule being applied [1].

ctxs.title: LAMBADA: Backward Chaining for Automated Reasoning in Natural Language
ctxs.text: In the case where we succeed in proving the antecedent of r𝑟r, whether the goal is proved or disproved depends on whether the sign of the goal agrees or disagrees with the sign of the consequent of r𝑟r. For instance, in Figure 1, for the goal ‘‘Eric is nice.’’, since the sign of the goal agrees with the sign of the consequent of Rule6 and the antecedent of the rule is proved, we conclude that the goal is proved. However, if Rule6 was ‘‘(...) is not going to be a nice individual.’’, then the sign of the goal would disagree with the sign of the consequent and so we would conclude that the goal is disproved. This motivates the fourth module, Sign Agreement, described below.

answer: No, the possibility of having many rules does not make it ambiguous [22].

input: Why are there tradeoffs between sample variety and fidelity?
question: Why are there tradeoffs between sample variety and fidelity?
output:
The trade-off between sample variety and fidelity in generative models like GANs arises because the objective function is often designed to maximize both metrics simultaneously, which can lead to a conflict between them [1]. Specifically, the FID score (Fréchet Inception Distance) penalizes lack of variety in class-conditional models, while IS (Inception Score) does not [1]. Reducing the truncation threshold leads to a direct increase in IS (analogous to precision), but FID penalizes lack of variety (analogous to recall) but also rewards precision, so we initially see a moderate improvement in FID, but as truncation approaches zero and variety diminishes, the FID sharply drops [1].

This trade-off is inherent in the design of these models, as they are trained to generate high-fidelity images that are diverse enough to cover the entire data distribution [1]. If the model is forced to produce only a few high-quality samples, it will suffer from low diversity, leading to a poor IS score [2]. Conversely, if the model is encouraged to generate a wide variety of samples, it may sacrifice fidelity, resulting in lower FID scores [1].

To address this trade-off, researchers have proposed various techniques to mitigate its impact, such as conditioning G to be smooth so that the full space of z will map to good output samples [1]. This can be achieved by enforcing orthogonality constraints on the latent space, as in the work of Brock et al. [1].

ctxs.title: LARGE SCALE GAN TRAINING FOR HIGH FIDELITY NATURAL IMAGE SYNTHESIS
ctxs.text: This technique allows fine-grained, post-hoc selection of the trade-off between sample quality and variety for a given G. Notably, we can compute FID and IS for a range of thresholds, obtaining the variety-fidelity curve reminiscent of the precision-recall curve (Figure 17). As IS does not penalize lack of variety in class-conditional models, reducing the truncation threshold leads to a direct increase in IS (analogous to precision). FID penalizes lack of variety (analogous to recall) but also rewards precision, so we initially see a moderate improvement in FID, but as truncation approaches zero and variety diminishes, the FID sharply drops. The distribution shift caused by sampling with different latents than those seen in training is problematic for many models. Some of our larger models are not amenable to truncation, producing saturation artifacts (Figure 2(b)) when fed truncated noise. To counteract this, we seek to enforce amenability to truncation by conditioning G to be smooth, so that the full space of z𝑧z will map to good output samples. For this, we turn to Orthogonal Regularization (Brock et al., 2017), which directly enforces the orthogonality condition:

ctxs.title: LARGE SCALE GAN TRAINING FOR HIGH FIDELITY NATURAL IMAGE SYNTHESIS
ctxs.text: We evaluate our models on ImageNet ILSVRC 2012 (Russakovsky et al., 2015) at 128×\times128, 256×\times256, and 512×\times512 resolutions, employing the settings from Table 1, row 8. The samples generated by our models are presented in Figure 4, with additional samples in Appendix A, and online 222https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1lWC6XEPD0LT5KUnPXeve_kWeY-FxH002. We report IS and FID in Table 2. As our models are able to trade sample variety for quality, it is unclear how best to compare against prior art; we accordingly report values at three settings, with complete curves in Appendix D. First, we report the FID/IS values at the truncation setting which attains the best FID. Second, we report the FID at the truncation setting for which our model’s IS is the same as that attained by the real validation data, reasoning that this is a passable measure of maximum sample variety achieved while still achieving a good level of “objectness.” Third, we report FID at the maximum IS achieved by each model, to demonstrate how much variety must be traded off to maximize quality. In all three cases, our models outperform the previous state-of-the-art IS and FID scores achieved by Miyato et al. (2018) and Zhang et al. (2018).

answer: The tradeoff is as IS does not penalize lack of variety in class-conditional models, reducing the truncation threshold leads to a direct increase in IS (analogous to precision) [18]. FID penalizes lack of variety (analogous to recall) but also rewards precision, so we initially see a moderate improvement in FID, but as truncation approaches zero and variety diminishes, the FID sharply drops [30].

input: Why does YOLO struggle in localizing objects correctly ?
question: Why does YOLO struggle in localizing objects correctly ?
output: YOLO struggles in localizing objects correctly because it imposes strong spatial constraints on bounding box predictions [1]. Each grid cell only predicts two boxes and can only have one class [1]. This spatial constraint limits the number of nearby objects that the model can predict [1]. Consequently, YOLO struggles with small objects that appear in groups, such as flocks of birds [1]. This limitation is also evident in its performance on certain categories like bottle, sheep, and tv/monitor, where it scores 8-10% lower than R-CNN or Feature Edit [4].

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: YOLO imposes strong spatial constraints on bounding box predictions since each grid cell only predicts two boxes and can only have one class. This spatial constraint limits the number of nearby objects that our model can predict. Our model struggles with small objects that appear in groups, such as flocks of birds.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: YOLO shares some similarities with R-CNN. Each grid cell proposes potential bounding boxes and scores those boxes using convolutional features. However, our system puts spatial constraints on the grid cell proposals which helps mitigate multiple detections of the same object. Our system also proposes far fewer bounding boxes, only 98 per image compared to about 2000 from Selective Search. Finally, our system combines these individual components into a single, jointly optimized model.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: YOLO struggles to localize objects correctly. Localization errors account for more of YOLO’s errors than all other sources combined. Fast R-CNN makes much fewer localization errors but far more background errors. 13.6% of it’s top detections are false positives that don’t contain any objects. Fast R-CNN is almost 3x more likely to predict background detections than YOLO.

ctxs.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
ctxs.text: On the VOC 2012 test set, YOLO scores 57.9% mAP. This is lower than the current state of the art, closer to the original R-CNN using VGG-16, see Table 3. Our system struggles with small objects compared to its closest competitors. On categories like bottle, sheep, and tv/monitor YOLO scores 8-10% lower than R-CNN or Feature Edit. However, on other categories like cat and train YOLO achieves higher performance.

answer: Although YOLO is a really fast model, it usually struggles with localizing small objects in a group or objects near each other [37]. In fact, localization errors take up more than half of all YOLO's errors [43]. It happens because YOLO has only a limited number of bounding boxes per grid cell and the loss function penalizes the errors in the large and small bounding boxes the same [63]. On top of that, the model uses coarse features to predict bounding boxes, and it may have problems with unusual aspect ratios and configurations of objects [68].

input: What is "bundle adjustment"?
question: What is "bundle adjustment"?
output: Bundle Adjustment (BA) is a global optimization problem in SLAM that refines the camera poses and point cloud by minimizing the reprojection error between the predicted and observed 3D points [1]. It is performed in the global optimization thread (full BA) to optimize all keyframes and points in the map, except the origin keyframe which is fixed to eliminate the gauge freedom [3]. The BA problem is formulated as minimizing the sum of squared reprojection errors over all keyframes and points [2]. The BA problem is solved using the Levenberg–Marquardt method implemented in g2o [1].

ctxs.title: ORB-SLAM2: an Open-Source SLAM System for Monocular, Stereo and RGB-D Cameras
ctxs.text: Our system performs BA to optimize the camera pose in the tracking thread (motion-only BA), to optimize a local window of keyframes and points in the local mapping thread (local BA), and after a loop closure to optimize all keyframes and points (full BA). We use the Levenberg–Marquardt method implemented in g2o .

Local BA optimizes a set of covisible keyframes \mathcal{K}_{L} and all points seen in those keyframes \mathcal{P}_{L} [25]. Full BA is the specific case of local BA, where all keyframes and points in the map are optimized, except the origin keyframe that is fixed to eliminate the gauge freedom [26].

input: What does "online triplet mining method" mean?
question: What does "online triplet mining method" mean?
output:
"Online triplet mining method" refers to a technique used in training face recognition models, specifically FaceNet [1]. This method involves dynamically selecting triplets of images during the training process to improve the quality of the embeddings.

In the context of FaceNet, the triplet mining method is used to generate triplets consisting of two matching face thumbnails and a non-matching face thumbnail [1]. These triplets are crucial for training the model to learn a metric space where positive pairs (matching faces) are close together, and negative pairs (non-matching faces) are far apart [1].

The online triplet mining method is an online process, meaning that the triplets are sampled from the training data on the fly, rather than being pre-computed [1]. This allows the model to adapt to the distribution of faces in the training data as it learns, leading to better generalization performance on unseen faces [1].

In summary, "online triplet mining method" is a technique used in training face recognition models like FaceNet [1]. It involves dynamically sampling triplets of images during training to improve the quality of the embeddings [1].

ctxs.title: FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering
ctxs.text: In contrast to these approaches, FaceNet directly trains its output to be a compact 128-D embedding using a triplet-based loss function based on LMNN . Our triplets consist of two matching face thumbnails and a non-matching face thumbnail and the loss aims to separate the positive pair from the negative by a distance margin. The thumbnails are tight crops of the face area, no 2D or 3D alignment, other than scale and translation is performed.

answer: Online triplet mining method generates two matching face thumbnails and a non-matching face thumbnail from the training data [3].

input: Is the difference between ORB-SLAM and ORB-SLAM2 that ORB-SLAM only supports monocular cameras?
question: Is the difference between ORB-SLAM and ORB-SLAM2 that ORB-SLAM only supports monocular cameras?
output: No, the difference between ORB-SLAM and ORB-SLAM2 is not that SLAM only supports monocular cameras [1]. Both systems are built on the monocular ORB-SLAM framework, but ORB-SLAM2 extends this framework to support stereo and RGB-D cameras [2]. Both systems have three main parallel threads: 1) tracking to localize the camera with every frame by finding feature matches to the local map and minimizing the reprojection error applying motion-only BA, 2) local mapping to manage the local map and optimize it, performing local BA, and 3) loop closing to detect large loops and correct the accumulated drift by performing a pose-graph optimization [1].

ctxs.title: ORB-SLAM2: an Open-Source SLAM System for Monocular, Stereo and RGB-D Cameras
ctxs.text: ORB-SLAM2 for stereo and RGB-D cameras is built on our monocular feature-based ORB-SLAM , whose main components are summarized here for reader convenience. A general overview of the system is shown in Fig. 2. The system has three main parallel threads: 1) the tracking to localize the camera with every frame by finding feature matches to the local map and minimizing the reprojection error applying motion-only BA, 2) the local mapping to manage the local map and optimize it, performing local BA, 3) the loop closing to detect large loops and correct the accumulated drift by performing a pose-graph optimization. This thread launches a fourth thread to perform full BA after the pose-graph optimization, to compute the optimal structure and motion solution.

ctxs.title: ORB-SLAM2: an Open-Source SLAM System for Monocular, Stereo and RGB-D Cameras
ctxs.text: In this paper we build on our monocular ORB-SLAM  and propose ORB-SLAM2 with the following contributions:   •  The first open-source111https://github.com/raulmur/ORB_SLAM2 SLAM system for monocular, stereo and RGB-D cameras, including loop closing, relocalization and map reuse.    •  Our RGB-D results show that by using Bundle Adjustment (BA) we achieve more accuracy than state-of-the-art methods based on ICP or photometric and depth error minimization.    •  By using close and far stereo points and monocular observations our stereo results are more accurate than the state-of-the-art direct stereo SLAM.    •  A lightweight localization mode that can effectively reuse the map with mapping disabled.

answer: ORB-SLAM2 for stereo and RGB-D cameras is built on monocular feature-based ORB-SLAM [13]. This shows that ORB-SLAM only supports monocular cameras as compared with ORB-SLAM [2].

input: They claim that in brain tumours, there is a hierarchical layout of sub-components. Is this True ? Any related experiments that proved it ?
question: They claim that in brain tumours, there is a hierarchical layout of sub-components. Is this True ? Any related experiments that proved it ?
output: Yes, in brain tumours, there is a hierarchical layout of sub-components [3]. The paper states that the segmentation of brain tumours includes several sub-components, namely: 1) necrotic core, 2) edema, 3) non-enhancing and 4) enhancing core [2]. These sub-components are nested within the main tumour volume, with the core being the most central and least defined part, surrounded by the edema and enhancing tumour [2]. The authors claim that this hierarchical layout is present in their dataset, which includes 220 cases with high grade (HG) and 54 cases with low grade (LG) glioma [2]. They evaluate their system on this dataset, and their results suggest that the hierarchical layout is present in their data [2].

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: The quantitative analysis of lesions requires accurate lesion segmentation in multi-modal, three-dimensional images which is a challenging task for a number of reasons. The heterogeneous appearance of lesions including the large variability in location, size, shape and frequency make it difficult to devise effective segmentation rules. It is thus highly non-trivial to delineate contusions, edema and haemorrhages in TBI (Irimia et al. (2012)), or sub-components of brain tumors such as proliferating cells and necrotic core (Menze et al. (2015)). The arguably most accurate segmentation results can be obtained through manual delineation by a human expert which is tedious, expensive, time-consuming, impractical in larger studies, and introduces inter-observer variability. Additionally, for deciding whether a particular region is part of a lesion multiple image sequences with varying contrasts need to be considered, and the level of expert knowledge and experience are important factors that impact segmentation accuracy. Hence, in clinical routine often only qualitative, visual inspection, or at best crude measures like approximate lesion volume and number of lesions are used (Yuh et al. (2012); Wen et al. (2010)). In order to capture and better understand the complexity of brain pathologies it is important to conduct large studies with many subjects to gain the statistical power for drawing conclusions across a whole patient population. The development of accurate, automatic segmentation algorithms has therefore become a major research focus in medical image computing with the potential to offer objective, reproducible, and scalable approaches to quantitative assessment of brain lesions.

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: For brain tumors, we evaluate our system on the data from the 2015 Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge (BRATS) (Menze et al. (2015)). The training set consists of 220 cases with high grade (HG) and 54 cases with low grade (LG) glioma for which corresponding reference segmentations are provided. The segmentations include the following tumor tissue classes: 1) necrotic core, 2) edema, 3) non-enhancing and 4) enhancing core. The test set consists of 110 cases of both HG and LG but the grade is not revealed. Reference segmentations for the test set are hidden and evaluation is carried out via an online system. For evaluation, the four predicted labels are merged into different sets of whole tumor (all four classes), the core (classes 1,3,4), and the enhancing tumor (class 4)333For interpretation of the results note that, to the best of our knowledge, cases where the “enhancing tumor” class is not present in the manual segmentation are considered as zeros for the calculation of average performance by the evaluation platform, lowering the upper bound for this class.. For each subject, four MRI sequences are available, FLAIR, T1, T1-contrast and T2. The datasets are pre-processed by the organizers and provided as skull-stripped, registered to a common space and resampled to isotropic 1​m​m31𝑚superscript𝑚31mm^{3} resolution. Dimensions of each volume are 240×\times240×\times155. We add minimal pre-processing of normalizing the brain-tissue intensities of each sequence to have zero-mean and unit variance.

input: What is single image super-resolution?
question: What is single image super-resolution?
output: Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) is a task in computer vision that aims to generate a high-resolution (HR) image given a low-resolution (LR) image [1]. This task is widely used in various applications, including security and surveillance imaging, medical imaging, and other domains where more image details are required on demand [1]. The goal of SISR is to restore the missing high-frequency information from the LR image to produce an HR image that is visually superior to the input LR image [1].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: We address the problem of generating a high-resolution (HR) image given a low-resolution (LR) image, commonly referred as single image super-resolution (SISR) , , . SISR is widely used in computer vision applications ranging from security and surveillance imaging to medical imaging where more image details are required on demand.

answer: Single image super-resolution is the task of generating a high-resolution image from a low-resolution one [0].

input: How does maximizing log-likelihood lead to optimizing cross-entropy between target probability distribution and the model prediction?
question: How does maximizing log-likelihood lead to optimizing cross-entropy between target probability distribution and the model prediction?
output:
Maximizing log-likelihood of a given word sequence leads to optimizing cross-entropy between the target probability distribution (e.g., the target word we should be predicting), and our model predictions $p_{\theta}(y)$ [1]. In this context, the cross-entropy loss function is defined as:

$$L(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(y_i | x_i)$$

where $N$ is the length of the target sequence, $y_i$ is the target word at position $i$, and $p_{\theta}(y_i | x_i)$ is the model's prediction for $y_i$ given the input $x_i$.

The cross-entropy loss function is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood of the target sequence under the model's predictions. Specifically, maximizing the log-likelihood of the target sequence $\log p_{\theta}(y | x))$ is equivalent to minimizing the cross-entropy loss function $L(\theta)$ [1].

The cross-entropy loss function is derived from the definition of log-likelihood as:

$$\log p_{\theta}(y | x)) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(y_i | x_i))$$

where $p_{\theta}(y_i | x_i))$ is the model's prediction for $y_i$ given the input $x_i$.

Minimizing the cross-entropy loss function $L(\theta)$ is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood of the target sequence $\log p_{\theta}(y | x))$ [1]. This objective function encourages the model to assign high probability to the target word at each position in the sequence, thereby improving its ability to predict the next word given the preceding context.

In practice, the cross-entropy loss function is computed by taking the negative log-likelihood of the target sequence under the model's predictions:

$$L(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(y_i | x_i))$$

where $p_{\theta}(y_i | x_i))$ is the model's prediction for $y_i$ given the input $x_i$.

Minimizing this loss function is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood of the target sequence $\log p_{\theta}(y | x))$ [1]. This objective

input: What are the evidences that region-wise computations are of low cost ?
question: What are the evidences that region-wise computations are of low cost ?
output: The paper provides several pieces of evidence that region-wise computations are of low cost. Specifically, they show that R-FCN achieves good accuracy on the PASCAL VOC, MS COCO, and ImageNet benchmarks while having negligible per-region cost [1]. In comparison to Faster R-CNN, which evaluates a 10-layer subnetwork for each region to achieve good accuracy, R-FCN takes only 0.17s per image (on a K40 GPU; this number is 0.11s on a Titan X GPU) with 300 RoIs at test time [1]. This indicates that R-FCN is significantly faster than Faster R-CNN [1]. Furthermore, they show that hard example mining adds no cost to R-FCN training [1].

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: Next we compare with standard “Faster R-CNN + ResNet-101”  which is the strongest competitor and the top-performer on the PASCAL VOC, MS COCO, and ImageNet benchmarks. We use k×k=7×7𝑘𝑘77k\times k=7\times 7 in the following. Table 5 shows the comparisons. Faster R-CNN evaluates a 10-layer subnetwork for each region to achieve good accuracy, but R-FCN has negligible per-region cost. With 300 RoIs at test time, Faster R-CNN takes 0.42s per image, 2.5×\times slower than our R-FCN that takes 0.17s per image (on a K40 GPU; this number is 0.11s on a Titan X GPU). R-FCN also trains faster than Faster R-CNN. Moreover, hard example mining  adds no cost to R-FCN training (Table 5). It is feasible to train R-FCN when mining from 2000 RoIs, in which case Faster R-CNN is 6×\times slower (2.9s vs. 0.46s). But experiments show that mining from a larger set of candidates (e.g., 2000) has no benefit (Table 5). So we use 300 RoIs for both training and inference in other parts of this paper.

answer: As seen in Table 5, Faster R-CNN is 25 times slower than R-FCN when mining for 300 RoIs, and 6 times slower when mining for 2000 RoIs, proving that region-wise computations are of low cost [33].

input: What is DDIM? What does closed-form manner mean here?
question: What is DDIM? What does closed-form manner mean here?
output: DDIM (Sampling Process) is a sampling process used in the inversion process of diffusion models [1]. Inversion is performed naïvely by adding noise to an image and then de-noising it through the network [1]. However, this process tends to change the image content significantly [1]. Choi et al. (2021) improve inversion by conditioning the denoising process on noised low-pass filter data from the target image [1]. (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021) demonstrate that the DDIM sampling process can be inverted in a closed-form manner, extracting a latent noise map that will produce a given real image [1]. This closed-form manner means that the process of inverting the DDIM sampling process can be done analytically, without requiring iterative optimization [2].

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: In the realm of diffusion models, inversion can be performed naïvely by adding noise to an image and then de-noising it through the network. However, this process tends to change the image content significantly. Choi et al. (2021) improve inversion by conditioning the denoising process on noised low-pass filter data from the target image. (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021) demonstrate that the DDIM (Song et al., 2020) sampling process can be inverted in a closed-form manner, extracting a latent noise map that will produce a given real image. In DALL-E 2 (Ramesh et al., 2022), they build on this method and demonstrate that it can be used to induce changes in the image, such as cross-image interpolations or semantic editing. The later relies on their use of CLIP-based codes to condition the model, and may not be applicable to other methods.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: For each prompt, we synthesize 646464 samples using 505050 DDIM steps, calculate the average CLIP-space embedding of the samples, and compute their cosine similarity with the CLIP-space embedding of the textual prompts, where we omit the placeholder S∗subscript𝑆S_{*} (i.e. “A photo of on the moon”). Here, a higher score indicates better editing capability and more faithfulness to the prompt itself. Note that our method does not involve the direct optimization of the CLIP-based objective score and, as such, is not sensitive to the adversarial scoring flaws outlined by Nichol et al. (2021).

answer: DDIM Is a sampling process that is described in detail in Song et [15]. al 2020 [51].

input: What is the contribution that augmentation data with deformation adds to the overall performance?
question: What is the contribution that augmentation data with deformation adds to the overall performance?
output:
The contribution of deformation data to the overall performance of the segmentation network is significant, as it helps the network learn the desired invariance and robustness properties when only few training samples are available [1]. Specifically, the paper mentions that they generate smooth deformations using random displacement vectors on a coarse 3 by 3 grid, and these displacements are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with 10 pixels standard deviation [1]. Per-pixel displacements are then computed using bicubic interpolation [1]. This process introduces variations in the input images, forcing the network to learn features that are invariant to these deformations [1].

Furthermore, the authors employ drop-out layers at the end of the contracting path to perform further implicit data augmentation [1]. This technique introduces noise into the feature maps, which helps the network become more robust to these variations [1].

In summary, the contribution of deformation data to the overall performance of the segmentation network is that it teaches the network the desired invariance and robustness properties [1]. By introducing variations in the input images, it forces the network to learn features that are invariant to these deformations [1].

ctxs.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: Data augmentation is essential to teach the network the desired invariance and robustness properties, when only few training samples are available. In case of microscopical images we primarily need shift and rotation invariance as well as robustness to deformations and gray value variations. Especially random elastic deformations of the training samples seem to be the key concept to train a segmentation network with very few annotated images. We generate smooth deformations using random displacement vectors on a coarse 3 by 3 grid. The displacements are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with 10 pixels standard deviation. Per-pixel displacements are then computed using bicubic interpolation. Drop-out layers at the end of the contracting path perform further implicit data augmentation.

answer: U-Net achieves good performance, less training time and less memory by using deformation based data augmentation [15].

input: Is the theoretical speedup greater than the actual speedup when comparing ShuffleNet to AlexNet on real hardware?
question: Is the theoretical speedup greater than the actual speedup when comparing ShuffleNet to AlexNet on real hardware?
output: Yes, the theoretical speedup is greater than the actual speedup when comparing ShuffleNet to AlexNet on real hardware. The paper states that the theoretical speedup is $18\times$ over AlexNet [1], while the actual speedup achieved on ARM-based computing cores is $\sim$similar-to $\sim 13\times$ [1]. This means that the actual speedup is less than the theoretical speedup, which is expected due to various overheads and limitations in real-world hardware implementations [1].

ctxs.title: ShuffleNet: An Extremely Efficient Convolutional Neural Network for Mobile Devices
ctxs.text: We also examine the speedup on real hardware, i.e. an off-the-shelf ARM-based computing core. The ShuffleNet model achieves ∼similar-to\sim13×\times actual speedup (theoretical speedup is 18×\times) over AlexNet  while maintaining comparable accuracy.

answer: Yes, while the theoretical speedup of ShuffleNet is 18 times, the actual speedup is only ~13 times, compared to the AlexNet on the real hardware [3].

input: What are examples of noise for generated questions?
question: What are examples of noise for generated questions?
output:
The paper excerpt mentions that they use a question generator trained on general domain question-answer pairs to create pairs of noisy question-passage pairs [1]. These noisy question-passage pairs are then used to train a retrieval model [1]. The noise in the generated questions can come from several sources, including:

1. **Domain Mismatch**: The synthetic questions are generated using a general domain question-answer pair generator [1]. When these questions are applied to a target domain passage collection, they may not be well-aligned with the target domain's content, leading to a mismatch between the generated question and the passage [1].
2. **Question Quality**: The synthetic questions are generated using a general domain question-answer pair generator [1]. These pairs may not be of high quality, containing incorrect or ambiguous questions that do not effectively guide the passage retrieval [1].
3. **Passage Quality**: The noisy question-passage pairs are created by feeding the target domain passage collection into the question generator [1]. If the passage collection itself is of low quality, containing irrelevant or poorly written passages, the generated questions will also be noisy [1].
4. **Domain Specificity**: The synthetic questions are generated using a general domain question-answer pair generator [1]. These questions may not be specific to the target domain, making them less effective for passage retrieval in that domain [1].

In summary, the noise in the generated questions can arise from the mismatch between the general domain question generator and the target domain passage collection, the quality of the generated questions, the quality of the target domain passages, and the lack of domain specificity in the synthetic questions [1].

ctxs.title: Zero-shot Neural Passage Retrieval via Domain-targeted Synthetic Question Generation
ctxs.text: In this work, we are specifically investigating the zero-shot scenario where there exists neither user issued questions nor domain specific data except the passage collection itself. We propose to address the training data scarcity issue by generating synthetic questions Zhou et al. (2017); Duan et al. (2017); Alberti et al. (2019); Nogueira et al. (2019). Leverage the fact that there are large question-answer data sources freely available from the web Shah and Pomerantz (2010); Duan et al. (2017). we first train a question generator using general domain question-answer pairs. The passage collection of a target domain is then fed into this generator to create pairs of noisy question-passage pairs, which are used to train a retrieval model (see Figure 2). In this work, we mine English question-answer pairs from community resources, primarily StackExchange444archive.org/details/stackexchange and Yahoo! Answers555webscope.sandbox.yahoo.com/catalog.php?datatype=l. Note we use stackexchange as it covers a wide range of topics, and we focus on investigating the domain adaptability of using a question generation approach. We leave comparing question generator trained on different datasets or using different architectures to future work.

answer: The passage collection of a target domain is fed into this generator to create pairs of noisy question-passage pairs [11].

input: How does the depth of the residual networks affect their performance in the experiments?

output: The paper investigates the effect of depth on the performance of residual networks in the experiments. They find that increasing the depth of the residual networks leads to improved performance, with the 34-layer ResNet being better than the 18-layer ResNet (by 2.8%) [1]. Specifically, the 34-layer ResNet exhibits considerably lower training error and is generalizable to the validation data [1]. This indicates that the degradation problem is well addressed in this setting, and they manage to obtain accuracy gains from increased depth [1].

More importantly, the 34-layer ResNet exhibits considerably lower training error and is generalizable to the validation data. This indicates that the degradation problem is well addressed in this setting and we manage to obtain accuracy gains from increased depth.

input: What point in StarGAN is valuable compared to Conditional GAN?
question: What point in StarGAN is valuable compared to Conditional GAN?
output: The point in StarGAN that is valuable compared to Conditional GAN is that it provides a flexible steering mechanism to various target domains by providing conditional domain information [1]. This allows StarGAN to perform multi-domain image-to-image translation, which is a significant advancement over Conditional GANs that primarily focus on generating samples conditioned on a specific class [20, 21, 22].

ctxs.title: StarGAN: Unified Generative Adversarial Networks for Multi-Domain Image-to-Image Translation
ctxs.text: Conditional GANs. GAN-based conditional image generation has also been actively studied. Prior studies have provided both the discriminator and generator with class information in order to generate samples conditioned on the class  (20, 21, 22). Other recent approaches focused on generating particular images highly relevant to a given text description  (25, 30). The idea of conditional image generation has also been successfully applied to domain transfer (9, 28), super-resolution imaging, and photo editing (2, 27). In this paper, we propose a scalable GAN framework that can flexibly steer the image translation to various target domains, by providing conditional domain information.

answer: paper's model can process multiple different domains [8].

input: How did previous OCCF studies mitigated the problem of performance being largely depend on negative sampling distribution?
question: How did previous OCCF studies mitigated the problem of performance being largely depend on negative sampling distribution?
output: Previous OCCF studies mitigated the problem of performance being largely dependent on negative sampling distribution by assuming that all unobserved interactions are negative [1]. In other words, for each user, the items that have not been interacted yet are regarded to be less preferred to positive items [1]. In this sense, they either use all unobserved user-item interactions as negative or adopt a negative sampling, which randomly samples unobserved user-item interactions in a stochastic manner to alleviate the computational burden [1]. For better recommendation performance and faster convergence, advanced negative sampling strategies (Rendle and Freudenthaler, 2014; Ding et al., 2019) are also proposed to sample from non-uniform distributions [1].

ctxs.title: Bootstrapping User and Item Representations for One-Class Collaborative Filtering
ctxs.text: However, since the negative interactions are not available in the OCCF problem, previous discriminative methods assume that all unobserved interactions are negative. In other words, for each user, the items that have not been interacted yet are regarded to be less preferred to positive items. In this sense, they either use all unobserved user-item interactions as negative or adopt a negative sampling, which randomly samples unobserved user-item interactions in a stochastic manner to alleviate the computational burden. For better recommendation performance and faster convergence, advanced negative sampling strategies (Rendle and Freudenthaler, 2014; Ding et al., 2019) are also proposed to sample from non-uniform distributions.

answer: Previous OCCF studies assume that all unobserved interactions are negative to mitigate the problem of performance being largely depend on negative sampling distribution [2].
