input: How does the network achieve the correction of its own mistakes?
question: How does the network achieve the correction of its own mistakes?
output: The network achieves the correction of its own mistakes through a continuous servoing mechanism [2]. This mechanism uses the CNN to continuously update the robot's motor commands [2]. By continuously choosing the best predicted path to a successful grasp, the servoing mechanism provides the robot with fast feedback to perturbations and object motion, as well as robustness to inaccurate actuation [2]. This allows the robot to correct mistakes by observing the outcomes of its past actions [1].

ctxs.title: Learning hand-eye coordination for robotic grasping with deep learning and large-scale data collection
ctxs.text: In order to train our prediction network, we collected over 800,000 grasp attempts using a set of similar (but not identical) robotic manipulators, shown in Figure 1. We discuss the details of our hardware setup in Section 5.1, and discuss the data collection process in Section 5.2. To ensure generalization of the learned prediction network, the specific parameters of each robot varied in terms of the camera pose relative to the robot, providing independence to camera calibration. Furthermore, uneven wear and tear on each robot resulted in differences in the shape of the gripper fingers. Although accurately predicting optimal motion vectors in open-loop is not possible with this degree of variation, as demonstrated in our experiments, our continuous servoing method can correct mistakes by observing the outcomes of its past actions, achieving a high success rate even without knowledge of the precise camera calibration.

ctxs.title: Learning hand-eye coordination for robotic grasping with deep learning and large-scale data collection
ctxs.text: Our method consists of two components: a grasp success predictor, which uses a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) to determine how likely a given motion is to produce a successful grasp, and a continuous servoing mechanism that uses the CNN to continuously update the robot’s motor commands. By continuously choosing the best predicted path to a successful grasp, the servoing mechanism provides the robot with fast feedback to perturbations and object motion, as well as robustness to inaccurate actuation.

answer: The network achieves the correction of its own mistakes using the continuous servoing method can correct mistakes by observing the outcomes of its past actions, achieving a high success rate [13]. Their method can use continuous feedback to correct mistakes and reposition the gripper; the servoing mechanism provides the robot with fast feedback to perturbations and object motion, as well as robustness [2].

input: For the images used for visualization in the paper, were they selected randomly or picked by the authors?
question: For the images used for visualization in the paper, were they selected randomly or picked by the authors?
output: The authors did not select the images randomly. They ran a random hyperparameter search of 300 possible combinations and settled on four that complemented each other well [1]. They then optimized images using each of these four combinations for the "Gorilla" class output unit in Figure 4 [1]. They found that the version in the lower-left quadrant to be the best single set of hyperparameters, but often greater intuition can be gleaned by considering all four at once [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: If the above regularization methods are applied individually, they are somewhat effective at producing more interpretable images; Figure 3 shows the effects of each individual hyperparameter. However, preliminary experiments uncovered that their combined effect produces better visualizations. To pick a reasonable set of hyperparameters for all methods at once, we ran a random hyperparameter search of 300 possible combinations and settled on four that complement each other well. The four selected combinations are listed in Table 1 and optimized images using each are shown for the “Gorilla” class output unit in Figure 4. Of the four, some show high frequency information, others low frequency; some contain dense pixel data, and others contain only sparse outlines of important regions. We found the version in the lower-left quadrant to be the best single set of hyperparameters, but often greater intuition can be gleaned by considering all four at once. Figure 5 shows the optimization results computed for a selection of units on all layers. A single image for every filter of all five convolutional layers is shown in Supplementary Figure S1. Nine images for each filter of all layers, including each of the 1000 ImageNet output classes, can be viewed at http://yosinski.com/deepvis.

answer: Authors best practices were to combine effects of different ways of regularization to produce interpretable images [29].

input: Does it have anything to do with the nature and complexity of data we are working with ?
question: Does it have anything to do with the nature and complexity of data we are working with ?
output: Yes, the paper excerpt [1] discusses the receptive fields of the network layers, showing that the innermost portion of the CNN already captures the content of the whole input volume. This characteristic is important during segmentation of poorly visible anatomy, as the features computed in the deepest layer perceive the whole anatomy of interest at once, since they are computed from data having a spatial support much larger than the typical size of the anatomy we seek to delineate, and therefore impose global constraints [1]. The paper also mentions that their CNN is trained end-to-end on a dataset of prostate scans in MRI, and that all the volumes processed by the network have fixed size of $128\times 128\times 64$ voxels and a spatial resolution of $1\times 1\times 1.5$ millimeters [2]. This suggests that the network is designed to handle the nature and complexity of the data they are working with, which includes volumetric medical images such as prostate scans in MRI [2].

ctxs.title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: We report in Table 1 the receptive fields of each network layer, showing the fact that the innermost portion of our CNN already captures the content of the whole input volume. We believe that this characteristic is important during segmentation of poorly visible anatomy: the features computed in the deepest layer perceive the whole anatomy of interest at once, since they are computed from data having a spatial support much larger than the typical size of the anatomy we seek to delineate, and therefore impose global constraints.

ctxs.title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: Our CNN is trained end-to-end on a dataset of prostate scans in MRI. An example of the typical content of such volumes is shown in Figure 1. All the volumes processed by the network have fixed size of 128×128×6412812864128\times 128\times 64 voxels and a spatial resolution of 1×1×1.5111.51\times 1\times 1.5 millimeters.

answer: In general MRI 3D volume data is complex [12]. Prostate anomaly segmentation also makes the data in consideration unique [15].

input: How does the deep residual learning framework address the degradation problem in deep convolutional neural networks?
question: How does the deep residual learning framework address the degradation problem in deep convolutional neural networks?
output: The paper addresses the degradation problem in deep convolutional neural networks by introducing a deep residual learning framework [1]. Instead of hoping that each few stacked layers directly fit a desired underlying mapping, they explicitly let these layers fit a residual mapping [1]. Formally, they denote the desired underlying mapping as $\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}))$, and let the stacked nonlinear layers fit another mapping of $\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}):=\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x})-\mathbf{x}$. They hypothesize that it is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize the original, unreferenced mapping [1]. To the extreme, if an identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push the residual to zero than to fit an identity mapping by a stack of nonlinear layers [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: In this paper, we address the degradation problem by introducing a deep residual learning framework. Instead of hoping each few stacked layers directly fit a desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these layers fit a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired underlying mapping as ℋ​(𝐱)ℋ𝐱\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}), we let the stacked nonlinear layers fit another mapping of ℱ​(𝐱):=ℋ​(𝐱)−𝐱assignℱ𝐱ℋ𝐱𝐱\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}):=\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x})-\mathbf{x}. The original mapping is recast into ℱ​(𝐱)+𝐱ℱ𝐱𝐱\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x})+\mathbf{x}. We hypothesize that it is easier to optimize the residual mapping than to optimize the original, unreferenced mapping. To the extreme, if an identity mapping were optimal, it would be easier to push the residual to zero than to fit an identity mapping by a stack of nonlinear layers.

answer: The deep residual learning framework address the degradation problem using residual mapping instead of original mapping in deep convolutional neural network [4].

input: Why shouldn’t an existing word be used as an identifier while fine tuning?
question: Why shouldn’t an existing word be used as an identifier while fine tuning?
output:
Using an existing English word as an identifier during fine-tuning of text-to-image diffusion models is suboptimal because the model has to learn to disentangle these words from their original meaning and to re-entangle them to reference our subject [1]. This is because the language model has a strong prior on these words, and the diffusion model also has a strong prior on them. Therefore, using common English words as identifiers can lead to the model overfitting on these words and failing to generalize to novel subjects [1].

To address this issue, we propose a method to find rare tokens in the vocabulary and invert these tokens into text space, in order to minimize the probability of the identifier having a strong prior [1]. This involves performing a rare-token lookup in the vocabulary and obtaining a sequence of rare token identifiers $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}})$, where $f$ is a tokenizer; a function that maps character sequences to tokens and $\hat{\mathbf{V}}}$ is the decoded text stemming from the tokens $f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}))$. We find that relatively short sequences of $k=\{1,...,3\}$ work well [1].

For Imagen, we find that using uniform random sampling of tokens that correspond to 3 or fewer Unicode characters (without spaces) and using tokens in the T5-XXL tokenizer range of $\{5000,...,10000\}$ works well [1].

ctxs.title: DreamBooth: Fine Tuning Text-to-Image Diffusion Models for Subject-Driven Generation
ctxs.text: We generally find existing English words (e.g. “unique”, “special”) suboptimal since the model has to learn to disentangle them from their original meaning and to re-entangle them to reference our subject. This motivates the need for an identifier that has a weak prior in both the language model and the diffusion model. A hazardous way of doing this is to select random characters in the English language and concatenate them to generate a rare identifier (e.g. “xxy5syt00”). In reality, the tokenizer might tokenize each letter separately, and the prior for the diffusion model is strong for these letters. We often find that these tokens incur the similar weaknesses as using common English words. Our approach is to find rare tokens in the vocabulary, and then invert these tokens into text space, in order to minimize the probability of the identifier having a strong prior. We perform a rare-token lookup in the vocabulary and obtain a sequence of rare token identifiers f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}), where f𝑓f is a tokenizer; a function that maps character sequences to tokens and 𝐕^^𝐕\hat{\mathbf{V}} is the decoded text stemming from the tokens f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}). The sequence can be of variable length k𝑘k, and find that relatively short sequences of k={1,…,3}𝑘1…3k=\{1,...,3\} work well. Then, by inverting the vocabulary using the de-tokenizer on f​(𝐕^)𝑓^𝐕f(\hat{\mathbf{V}}) we obtain a sequence of characters that define our unique identifier 𝐕^^𝐕\hat{\mathbf{V}}. For Imagen, we find that using uniform random sampling of tokens that correspond to 3 or fewer Unicode characters (without spaces) and using tokens in the T5-XXL tokenizer range of {5000,…,10000}5000…10000\{5000,...,10000\} works well.

answer: Existing words in the training set of text-to-image diffusion models have stronger priors, hence they shouldn't be employed as identifiers during fine tuning [18].

input: How do they show that single word embeddings capture unique and varied concepts?
question: How do they show that single word embeddings capture unique and varied concepts?
output:
The authors show that single word embeddings can capture unique and varied concepts by demonstrating that their method can successfully capture these finer details using only a single word embedding [1]. Specifically, they show that their method can better capture the unique details of the concept compared to human captioning, which typically captures the most prominent features of an object but provides insufficient detail to reconstruct finer features like color patterns [1].

They also show that their method can handle objects that are exceedingly difficult to describe through natural language, such as the skull mug [1]. In contrast, existing methods like CLIP struggle with these objects because the image encoder is unlikely to have seen them [1].

Furthermore, they show that their method can achieve comparable reconstruction quality to simply sampling random images from the training set [2]. This indicates that their method is able to capture new concepts with a high degree of accuracy while using only a single pseudo-word [2].

In summary, they show that single word embeddings can capture unique and varied concepts by demonstrating that their method can successfully capture these finer details using only a single word embedding [1], and that it can achieve comparable reconstruction quality to simply sampling random images from the training set [2].

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: As our results demonstrate, our method better captures the unique details of the concept. Human captioning typically captures the most prominent features of an object, but provides insufficient detail to reconstruct finer features like color patterns (e.g. of the teapot). In some cases (e.g. the skull mug) the object itself may be exceedingly difficult to describe through natural language. When provided with an image, DALLE-2 is able to recreate more appealing samples, particularly for well-known objects with limited detail (Aladdin’s lamp). However, it still struggles with unique details of personalized objects that the image encoder (CLIP) is unlikely to have seen (mug, teapot). In contrast, our method can successfully capture these finer details, and it does so using only a single word embedding. However, note that while our creations are more similar to the source objects, they are still variations that may differ from the source.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Our evaluation results are summarized in Figure 10(a). We highlight four observations of particular interest: First, the semantic reconstruction quality of our method and many of the baselines is comparable to simply sampling random images from the training set. Second, the single-word method achieves comparable reconstruction quality, and considerably improved editability over all multi-word baselines. These points outline the impressive flexibility of the textual embedding space, showing that it can serve to capture new concepts with a high degree of accuracy while using only a single pseudo-word.

answer: The authors explain how their single-word embedding approach is able to pick up on finer details (such as colour schemes, or complex images) that are difficult to express using natural language alone [36]. Additionally, their results indicate that their single-word embedding approach has comparable performance to multi-word embeddings, suggesting that their single-word embeddings are not inherently limited in how much information they encode [61].

input: Why are pure rotations hard to track for monocular SLAM?
question: Why are pure rotations hard to track for monocular SLAM?
output: Pure rotations are hard to track for monocular SLAM because the system bootstrapping requires multi-view or filtering techniques to produce an initial map as it cannot be triangulated from the very first frame [1]. Without any prior information, the system cannot determine the scale of the map and the estimated trajectory, leading to scale drift [1]. This makes it difficult for monocular SLAM to perform pure rotations in exploration [1].

ctxs.title: ORB-SLAM2: an Open-Source SLAM System for Monocular, Stereo and RGB-D Cameras
ctxs.text: Visual SLAM can be performed by using just a monocular camera, which is the cheapest and smallest sensor setup. However as depth is not observable from just one camera, the scale of the map and estimated trajectory is unknown. In addition the system bootstrapping require multi-view or filtering techniques to produce an initial map as it cannot be triangulated from the very first frame. Last but not least, monocular SLAM suffers from scale drift and may fail if performing pure rotations in exploration. By using a stereo or an RGB-D camera all these issues are solved and allows for the most reliable Visual SLAM solutions.

answer: Pure rotations hard to track for monocular SLAM because  depth is not observable from just one camera, the scale of the map and estimated trajectory is unknown [1].

input: I am wondering why the authors used community QA datasets for training the question generator. How about open-domain information retrieval datasets, such as MS MARCO, which also cover diverse domains?
question: I am wondering why the authors used community QA datasets for training the question generator. How about open-domain information retrieval datasets, such as MS MARCO, which also cover diverse domains?
output:
The authors used community QA datasets [1] for training the question generator because they are publicly available and can be used to create open-domain QA passage retrieval models [1]. However, as shown in Guo et al. (2020) and in their later experiments, neural retrieval models trained on general-domain data often do not transfer well, especially for specialized domains [1].

In contrast, open-domain information retrieval datasets like MS MARCO [2] cover diverse domains and can be used to create domain-targeted synthetic question generation models [1]. These datasets are also publicly available and can be used to create strong zero-shot neural passage retrieval models [1].

Therefore, the authors used community QA datasets [1] as a baseline for their zero-shot neural passage retrieval model, but their later experiments on domain-targeted synthetic question generation models [1] show that these datasets are not as effective as open-domain information retrieval datasets like MS MARCO [2].

ctxs.title: Zero-shot Neural Passage Retrieval via Domain-targeted Synthetic Question Generation
ctxs.text: First, we observe that general-domain question-passage pairs can be acquired from community platforms Shah and Pomerantz (2010); Duan et al. (2017) or high quality academic datasets that are publicly available Kwiatkowski et al. (2019); Bajaj et al. (2016). Such resources have been used to create open domain QA passage retrieval models. However, as shown in Guo et al. (2020) and in our later experiments, neural retrieval models trained on the general domain data often do not transfer well, especially for specialized domains.

answer: Using open-domain information retrieval datasets for training neural retrieval models do not transfer well especially for specialized domains [3].

input: Did the authors ever try different criteria for choosing hyperparameters?
question: Did the authors ever try different criteria for choosing hyperparameters?
output: The authors did not try different criteria for choosing hyperparameters [1]. They used the criterion of mean(returns) - std(returns) to select the best hyperparameters [1].

ctxs.title: Benchmarking Deep Reinforcement Learning for Continuous Control
ctxs.text: Hyperparameter Tuning: For the DDPG algorithm, we used the hyperparametes reported in Lillicrap et al. (2015). For the other algorithms, we follow the approach in (Mnih et al., 2015), and we select two tasks in each category, on which a grid search of hyperparameters is performed. Each choice of hyperparameters is executed under five random seeds. The criterion for the best hyperparameters is defined as mean​(returns)−std​(returns)meanreturnsstdreturns\mathrm{mean}(\mathrm{returns})-\mathrm{std}(\mathrm{returns}). This metric selects against large fluctuations of performance due to overly large step sizes.

answer: Hyperparameter Tuning: For the DDPG algorithm, we used the hyperparametes reported in Lillicrap et al [33].

input: How could restricting self attention to some window with size r be useful with long term dependencies?
question: How could restricting self attention to some window with size r be useful with long term dependencies?
output: Restricting self-attention to a neighborhood of size $r$ in the input sequence centered around the respective output position would increase the maximum path length to $O(n/r)$ [1]. This would make the model more efficient for tasks involving very long sequences, as it would only attend to a limited context window of size $r$ instead of the entire input sequence of length $n$. This could be beneficial for tasks that require capturing long-term dependencies, such as sentence-to-sentence generation or document-level understanding, as it would reduce the computational complexity from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \cdot r)$ [1].

ctxs.title: Attention Is All You Need
ctxs.text: As noted in Table 1, a self-attention layer connects all positions with a constant number of sequentially executed operations, whereas a recurrent layer requires O​(n)𝑂𝑛O(n) sequential operations. In terms of computational complexity, self-attention layers are faster than recurrent layers when the sequence length n𝑛n is smaller than the representation dimensionality d𝑑d, which is most often the case with sentence representations used by state-of-the-art models in machine translations, such as word-piece  and byte-pair  representations. To improve computational performance for tasks involving very long sequences, self-attention could be restricted to considering only a neighborhood of size r𝑟r in the input sequence centered around the respective output position. This would increase the maximum path length to O​(n/r)𝑂𝑛𝑟O(n/r). We plan to investigate this approach further in future work.

answer: Restricting self attention to some window with size r does improve computational performance but its effect on long term dependencies have not been explored in the paper [36].

input: What are the main three properties of the model studied in this paper?
question: What are the main three properties of the model studied in this paper?
output:
The main three properties of the model studied in this paper are:

1. Large depth is necessary for the task of SR [1].
2. A very deep network utilizes more contextual information in an image and models complex functions with many nonlinear layers [1].
3. Experimentally verified that deeper networks give better performances than shallow ones [1].

ctxs.title: Accurate Image Super-Resolution Using Very Deep Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: In this section, we study three properties of our proposed method. First, we show that large depth is necessary for the task of SR. A very deep network utilizes more contextual information in an image and models complex functions with many nonlinear layers. We experimentally verify that deeper networks give better performances than shallow ones.

answer: They study the deepness of their model, the multi-scale property and how well it performs, and residual learning for faster convergence [40].

input: How were the variations of the camera poses for the different robots determined?
question: How were the variations of the camera poses for the different robots determined?
output: The variations of the camera poses for the different robots were determined by the fact that each unit experienced different wear and tear over the course of data collection, interacted with different objects, and used a slightly different camera pose relative to the robot base [1]. These differences provided a diverse dataset for learning continuous hand-eye coordination for grasping [1].

ctxs.title: Learning hand-eye coordination for robotic grasping with deep learning and large-scale data collection
ctxs.text: The grasp prediction CNN was trained using a dataset of over 800,000 grasp attempts, collected using a cluster of similar (but not identical) robotic manipulators, shown in Figure 1, over the course of several months. Although the hardware parameters of each robot were initially identical, each unit experienced different wear and tear over the course of data collection, interacted with different objects, and used a slightly different camera pose relative to the robot base. These differences provided a diverse dataset for learning continuous hand-eye coordination for grasping.

ctxs.title: Learning hand-eye coordination for robotic grasping with deep learning and large-scale data collection
ctxs.text: Another related area to our method is visual servoing, which addresses moving a camera or end-effector to a desired pose using visual feedback (Kragic & Christensen, 2002). In contrast to our approach, visual servoing methods are typically concerned with reaching a pose relative to objects in the scene, and often (though not always) rely on manually designed or specified features for feedback control (Espiau et al., 1992; Wilson et al., 1996; Vahrenkamp et al., 2008; Hebert et al., 2012; Mohta et al., 2014). Photometric visual servoing uses a target image rather than features (Caron et al., 2013), and several visual servoing methods have been proposed that do not directly require prior calibration between the robot and camera (Yoshimi & Allen, 1994; Jägersand et al., 1997; Kragic & Christensen, 2002). To the best of our knowledge, no prior learning-based method has been proposed that uses visual servoing to directly move into a pose that maximizes the probability of success on a given task (such as grasping).

answer: A slightly different camera pose was selected for each robot, relative to the robot base [3]. Another related area to our method is visual servoing, which addresses moving a camera or end-effector to a desired pose using visual feedback [9].

input: What type of new tasks was Self-Instruct able to generate which were not seen in previous human-created instruction datasets?
question: What type of new tasks was Self-Instruct able to generate which were not seen in previous human-created instruction datasets?
output: Self-Instruct was able to generate a series of new tasks (instructions along with instances) from scratch, which were not seen in previous human-created instruction datasets [1]. Unlike previous works that generated instructions for a given task, Self-Instruct is task-agnostic and generates novel tasks from scratch [1]. This process involves generating instructions along with instances from scratch using a vanilla pretrained language model itself, filtering the generated data, and then conducting instruction tuning with this generated data in order to align the LM to follow instructions better [2].

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: A series of recent works Zhou et al. (2022b); Ye et al. (2022); Singh et al. (2022); Honovich et al. (2022b) generate instructions of a task given a few examples. While Self-Instruct also involves instruction generation, a major difference in our case is it is task-agnostic; we generate new tasks (instructions along with instances) from scratch.

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: Annotating large-scale instruction data can be challenging for humans because it requires 1) creativity to come up with novel tasks and 2) expertise for writing the solutions to each task. Here, we detail our process for Self-Instruct, which refers to the pipeline of generating tasks with a vanilla pretrained language model itself, filtering the generated data, and then conducting instruction tuning with this generated data in order to align the LM to follow instructions better. This pipeline is depicted in Figure 2.

answer: The paper mentions that their model is used to generate 52,000 instructions and 82,000 input-output pairs, for a wide range of tasks, which the authors have made publicly available [34]. It might be possible to find more information on the specific tasks that were created by looking at this synthetic dataset, but the paper itself does not contain concrete examples [4].

input: How do the authors define plain networks and residual networks?
question: How do the authors define plain networks and residual networks?
output:
The authors define plain networks as simple feedforward networks without any skip connections [1]. They evaluate 18-layer and 34-layer plain nets [1]. The 34-layer plain net is in Fig. 3 (middle), and the 18-layer plain net is of a similar form [1].

Residual networks, in contrast, are introduced to address the issue of degradation in plain networks when they become very deep [1]. They introduce identity mappings, or skip connections, that allow information to flow directly through the identity function across layers [1]. These identity mappings allow the network to learn identity functions easily, which helps in training deeper networks without significant performance degradation [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: Plain Networks. We first evaluate 18-layer and 34-layer plain nets. The 34-layer plain net is in Fig. 3 (middle). The 18-layer plain net is of a similar form. See Table 1 for detailed architectures.

answer:
[Plain network do not use shortcut connections whereas in residual networks, shortcut
connection is added to each pair of filters] [28].

input: How does this v1 related to generating consistent content?
question: How does this v1 related to generating consistent content?
output: The paper excerpt [1] introduces a method called "Tune-A-Video" for one-shot tuning of image diffusion models for text-to-video generation. The method takes a video $\mathcal{V} = \{v_{i}|i\in(1,m)\}_{i=1}^{m}$ and a source prompt $\mathcal{P}$ describing $\mathcal{V}$ as input. The goal is to generate a novel video $\mathcal{V}^{\ast}$ driven by an edited text prompt $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$. The method is designed to generate videos that are consistent with the input video and the target text prompt. Specifically, it aims to generate videos that maintain the motion and scene composition of the original video while incorporating the changes specified in the target text prompt $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$. This is achieved by fine-tuning the image diffusion model on the target video $\mathcal{V}^{\ast}$ using the target text prompt $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$ as the conditioning signal. The method is particularly effective in preserving the motion and scene composition of the original video while incorporating the changes specified in the target text prompt $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$ [1].

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: Let 𝒱={vi|i∈(1,m)}𝒱conditional-setsubscript𝑣𝑖𝑖1𝑚\mathcal{V}=\left\{v_{i}|i\in(1,m)\right\} be a video containing m𝑚m frames, 𝒫𝒫\mathcal{P} be the source prompt describing 𝒱𝒱\mathcal{V}. Our goal is to generate a novel video 𝒱∗superscript𝒱∗\mathcal{V^{\ast}} driven by an edited text prompt 𝒫∗superscript𝒫∗\mathcal{P^{\ast}}. For example, consider a video and a source prompt “a man is skiing”, and assume that the user wants to alter the color of the clothes, incorporate a cowboy hat to the skier, or even replace the skier with Spider Man while preserving the motion of the original video. The user can directly modify the source prompt by further describing the appearance of the skier or replacing it with another word.

answer: Conditioning the first frame, it can autoregressively extend video frames with shared verb [11].

input: How does the NetVLAD layer differ from the original VLAD?
question: How does the NetVLAD layer differ from the original VLAD?
output: The NetVLAD layer differs from the original VLAD layer in several ways. The original VLAD layer uses a single set of parameters $\{\mathbf{c}_k\}$ to represent each cluster $k$, while the NetVLAD layer has three independent sets of parameters $\{\mathbf{w}_k\}$, $\{\mathbf{b}_k\}$, and $\{\mathbf{c}_k\}$ for each cluster $k$ [2]. This decoupling of $\{\mathbf{w}_k\}$, $\{\mathbf{b}_k\}$, and $\{\mathbf{c}_k\}$ from $\{\mathbf{c}_k\}$ allows NetVLAD to adapt to a new dataset more easily than the original VLAD [2]. Furthermore, the NetVLAD layer computes a soft-assignment $\bar{a}_k(\mathbf{x}_i)$ for each descriptor $\mathbf{x}_i$ to cluster $k$, which is used to aggregate the first order statistics of residuals $(\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{c}_k)$ in different parts of the descriptor space weighted by $\bar{a}_k(\mathbf{x}_i)$ [2]. This soft-assignment allows NetVLAD to have greater flexibility than the original VLAD [2].

ctxs.title: NetVLAD: CNN Architecture for Weakly Supervised Place Recognition
ctxs.text: In order to profit from years of wisdom produced in image retrieval, we propose to mimic VLAD in a CNN framework and design a trainable generalized VLAD layer, NetVLAD. The result is a powerful image representation trainable end-to-end on the target task (in our case place recognition). To construct a layer amenable to training via backpropagation, it is required that the layer’s operation is differentiable with respect to all its parameters and the input. Hence, the key challenge is to make the VLAD pooling differentiable, which we describe next.

answer: The original VLAD method uses hand-crafted features and applies the VLAD technique to them by concatenating multiple VLADs [19]. On the other hand, NetVLAD layer uses a CNN to extract features and applies the VLAD technique in a single layer by learning the aggregation weights of the residuals (xi − ck) in different parts of the descriptor space [21].

input: Did authors perform a hyperparameter search before deciding the values of batch size, epsilon and L_C during training?
question: Did authors perform a hyperparameter search before deciding the values of batch size, epsilon and L_C during training?
output: The authors did not perform a hyperparameter search before deciding the values of batch size, epsilon, and L_C during training. They used a fixed value of batch size 1 throughout the training [1]. They set the initial values of $\epsilon$ and $\rho$ to $10^{-8}$ and $0.95$, respectively, and then gradually decreased $\epsilon$ to $10^{-10}$ during fine-tuning [1]. They also scaled down the model complexity cost $L_C$ by a factor of 10 after applying the adaptive weight noise [1].

ctxs.title: Attention-Based Models for Speech Recognition
ctxs.text: As TIMIT is a relatively small dataset, proper regularization is crucial. We used the adaptive weight noise as a main regularizer . We first trained our models with a column norm constraint  with the maximum norm 111 until the lowest development negative log-likelihood is achieved.333 Applying the weight noise from the beginning of training caused severe underfitting.  During this time, ϵitalic-ϵ\epsilon and ρ𝜌\rho are set to 10−8superscript10810^{-8} and 0.950.950.95, respectively. At this point, we began using the adaptive weight noise, and scaled down the model complexity cost LCsubscript𝐿𝐶L_{C} by a factor of 10, while disabling the column norm constraints. Once the new lowest development log-likelihood was reached, we fine-tuned the model with a smaller ϵ=10−10italic-ϵsuperscript1010\epsilon=10^{-10}, until we did not observe the improvement in the development phoneme error rate (PER) for 100K weight updates. Batch size 1 was used throughout the training.

answer: While the paper shows what value the author set for those hyperparameters, there is no evidential information about the hyperparameter search in this paper [29].

input: Does graph property prediction task create one representation corresponding to the graph?
question: Does graph property prediction task create one representation corresponding to the graph?
output: Yes, the paper proposes a structure-aware transformer [1] to learn graph representation learning. It suggests that for graph property prediction task, one can aggregate node-level representations into a graph representation by taking the average or sum [1]. Alternatively, one can use the embedding of a virtual (CLS) node [1] that is attached to the input graph without any connectivity to other nodes [1]. The authors compare these approaches in Section 5 [1].

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: Finally, for graph property prediction, there are various ways to aggregate node-level representations into a graph representation, such as by taking the average or sum. Alternatively, one can use the embedding of a virtual (CLS) node (Jain et al., 2021) that is attached to the input graph without any connectivity to other nodes. We compare these approaches in Section 5.

answer: Yes, in general, a graph representation is created by aggregating node representations to predict graph property [24].

input: What is the importance of normalization in deep networks and how does it contribute to the effectiveness of the deep residual learning framework?
question: What is the importance of normalization in deep networks and how does it contribute to the effectiveness of the deep residual learning framework?
output:
Normalization is crucial in deep networks, particularly in the context of deep residual learning [1]. It helps to stabilize the training process by preventing the gradients from exploding or vanishing [1]. Without normalization, the activations in deeper layers can become extremely large or small, leading to unstable training [1].

Specifically, Batch Normalization [1] is a technique introduced to address this issue. It normalizes the inputs to each layer across the batch dimension, ensuring that the mean of the inputs to each layer is zero and the standard deviation is one [1]. This normalization step helps to maintain a consistent scale of activations throughout the network, which facilitates the flow of gradients through the layers [1].

The effectiveness of deep residual learning relies heavily on this normalization [1]. By normalizing the inputs to each layer, the identity mapping in the residual block can be more effectively learned [1]. This allows the network to learn identity mappings easily, which is crucial for training very deep networks [1].

In summary, normalization, particularly Batch Normalization, is essential in deep networks [1]. It helps to stabilize the training process, maintain a consistent scale of activations throughout the network, and enable the training of very deep networks [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have observed consistent phenomena. To provide instances for discussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows.

answer: Normalization is used in initialization and intermediate layers [21].

input: What is the difference between an online experiment and an offline experiment?
question: What is the difference between an online experiment and an offline experiment?
output: An online experiment involves evaluating a model's performance in a real-world setting where it interacts with users in real-time [3]. In contrast, an offline experiment evaluates a model's performance on a fixed dataset without any real-time interaction with users [1]. The key difference lies in the interaction between the model and the users. Online experiments involve A/B testing, where different models are shown to different users to see which one performs better in a live environment [3]. Offline experiments, on the other hand, evaluate the model's performance on a static dataset without any real-time interaction [1].

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: In this section, we demonstrate the offline performance of AETN in generating general-purpose user embeddings. We compare the baseline with four different versions of AETN in three typical downstream offline experiments. Then we show that the auxiliary retention reconstruction task for the autoencoder part can help the convergency of the transformer parts. Finally, we compare the user embeddings generated by the baseline and AETN intuitively.

ctxs.title: general-purpose user embeddings based on mobile app usage
ctxs.text: We conduct our offline experiments on three typical downstream applications, including applications from both related domains and a different domain. The evaluation tasks are as follows:

We split online A/B test traffic by userIDs evenly for the tested models. We evaluate the base models, models with DAE embeddings, and models with AETN embeddings. The improvement results compared with the base models are reported in Table 2.

The reason is that they conduct online feed recommendation A/B testing from 2020-02-01 to 2020-02-10, in the “Good Morning” tab of Tencent Mobile Manager and the “Find” tab of Tencent Wi-Fi Manager [51]. For offline experiments, they compare the baseline with four different versions of AETN in three typical downstream offline experiments [63].

input: Is there a disadvantage to using low-precision arithmetic for inference, such as decreased inference accuracy?
question: Is there a disadvantage to using low-precision arithmetic for inference, such as decreased inference accuracy?
output: The paper excerpt does not explicitly state a disadvantage to using low-precision arithmetic for inference, such as decreased inference accuracy. Instead, it presents an approach to speed up inference with quantized arithmetic by adding additional constraints to the model during training so that it is quantizable with minimal impact on the output of the model [1]. The authors claim that these additional constraints do not hurt model convergence nor the quality of a model once it has converged [1].

ctxs.title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation
ctxs.text: In this section, we present our approach to speed up inference with quantized arithmetic. Our solution is tailored towards the hardware options available at Google. To reduce quantization errors, additional constraints are added to our model during training so that it is quantizable with minimal impact on the output of the model. That is, once a model is trained with these additional constraints, it can be subsequently quantized without loss to translation quality. Our experimental results suggest that those additional constraints do not hurt model convergence nor the quality of a model once it has converged.

answer: Quantization models can perform slightly have lower results on neural network models, however in this paper authors performed some constraints during training so that's quantizable with minimal impact on the output of the model, the quantized model even performed slightly better than none-quantized training and they suggest it could be due to regularization roles those constraints had during training [43].

input: Why RoBERTa uses Dynamic masking rather than Static masking?
question: Why RoBERTa uses Dynamic masking rather than Static masking?
output: RoBERTa uses Dynamic masking rather than Static masking because the original BERT implementation performed masking only once during data preprocessing, resulting in a single static mask [1]. This static mask was used for every training instance in every epoch, which led to the same mask being seen four times during training [1]. To avoid this issue, RoBERTa duplicates the training data 10 times so that each sequence is masked in 10 different ways over the 40 epochs of training [1]. This means that each training sequence is seen with the same mask four times during training [1]. By using Dynamic masking, RoBERTa ensures that the input to the model is different in every training instance, which can lead to better generalization and performance on downstream tasks [1].

ctxs.title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
ctxs.text: As discussed in Section 2, BERT relies on randomly masking and predicting tokens. The original BERT implementation performed masking once during data preprocessing, resulting in a single static mask. To avoid using the same mask for each training instance in every epoch, training data was duplicated 10 times so that each sequence is masked in 10 different ways over the 40 epochs of training. Thus, each training sequence was seen with the same mask four times during training.

answer: They use dynamic masking to avoid using the same mask in iteration [29].

input: What would happen if authors replace three prediction layers with DPM (Deformable Convolution Layers) ?
question: What would happen if authors replace three prediction layers with DPM (Deformable Convolution Layers) ?
output:
If authors replace three prediction layers with DPM (Deformable Convolution Layers) in the SSD framework, the model would be able to perform instance-level prediction, which is a significant improvement over the original SSD [1]. This would allow the model to predict the bounding boxes and class labels for each object in the image, rather than just the class labels for each anchor box [1].

The DPM would be able to adapt its shape to the object it is predicting, which would lead to more accurate predictions [1]. This would be particularly beneficial for small objects and objects with complex shapes, which are often challenging for standard anchor-based detectors [1].

However, the introduction of DPM would also increase the computational cost of the model [1]. This would require more computational resources, such as GPU memory and processing time, to train and run the model [1].

In summary, replacing three prediction layers with RPN (Region Proposal Network) in the original SSD framework would lead to instance-level prediction, which is a significant improvement over the original SSD [1]. This would allow the model to predict the bounding boxes and class labels for each object in the image, rather than just the class labels for each anchor box [1]. However, the introduction of DPM would also increase the computational cost of the model [1].

ctxs.title: SSD: Single Shot MultiBox Detector
ctxs.text: There are two established classes of methods for object detection in images, one based on sliding windows and the other based on region proposal classification. Before the advent of convolutional neural networks, the state of the art for those two approaches – Deformable Part Model (DPM)  and Selective Search  – had comparable performance. However, after the dramatic improvement brought on by R-CNN , which combines selective search region proposals and convolutional network based post-classification, region proposal object detection methods became prevalent.

answer: If authors replace three prediction layers with DPM (Deformable Convolution Layers) the performance of SSD will degrade compared to R-CNN and other methods [34].

input: Why did the authors use only one composer rather than several composers together?
question: Why did the authors use only one composer rather than several composers together?
output:
The authors used only one composer, Frédéric Chopin, to train their model because they wanted to create a model that could capture the expressive nuances of a single pianist's style [1]. They used 356 performances of 34 pieces by Chopin, which were considered representative research subjects for analyzing the Western musical expression [1]. By focusing on one composer, they aimed to achieve a high level of fidelity to that specific performer's interpretation [1].

However, they also used an external dataset from ASAP dataset to verify the generality of their model's performances [1]. This external dataset included 116 performances of 23 pieces by 10 composers who represented various eras of Western music [1]. This additional data helped them assess how well their model generalized to different composers and styles [1].

ctxs.title: Sketching the Expression: Flexible Rendering of Expressive Piano Performance with Self-Supervised Learning
ctxs.text: We use Yamaha e-Competition Dataset  and Vienna 4x22 Piano Corpus . From these datasets, we collect 356 performances of 34 pieces by Frédéric Chopin, which have been representative research subjects for analyzing the Western musical expression (22, 41, 6, 42). We use 30 pieces (108,738 batches) for training and the rest for testing. To verify the generality of model performances, we also collect the external dataset from ASAP dataset . We use 116 performances for 23 pieces by 10 composers who represent various eras of Western music. For subjective evaluation, we collect 42 songs of non-Classical songs from online source222http://www.ambrosepianotabs.com/page/library which are less constrained to written expression than most Classical excerpts.

answer: The authors use only one composer, Chopin, rather than several composers together because Chopin's music has been one of the most common resources that are analyzed by literature to investigate the development in Western musical expression with respect to various musical structures [21].

input: Why can’t we create novel rendition of reference images using the pretrained model itself?
question: Why can’t we create novel rendition of reference images using the pretrained model itself?
output: The main reason why we can't create novel renditions of reference images using the pretrained model itself is that the expressiveness of their output domain is limited [1]. Even the most detailed textual description of an object may yield instances with different appearances [1]. Furthermore, even models whose text embedding lies in a shared language-vision space cannot accurately reconstruct the appearance of given subjects but only create variations of the image content [1].

ctxs.title: DreamBooth: Fine Tuning Text-to-Image Diffusion Models for Subject-Driven Generation
ctxs.text: Recently developed large text-to-image models have shown unprecedented capabilities, by enabling high-quality and diverse synthesis of images based on a text prompt written in natural language (61, 54). One of the main advantages of such models is the strong semantic prior learned from a large collection of image-caption pairs. Such a prior learns, for instance, to bind the word “dog” with various instances of dogs that can appear in different poses and contexts in an image. While the synthesis capabilities of these models are unprecedented, they lack the ability to mimic the appearance of subjects in a given reference set, and synthesize novel renditions of the same subjects in different contexts. The main reason is that the expressiveness of their output domain is limited; even the most detailed textual description of an object may yield instances with different appearances. Furthermore, even models whose text embedding lies in a shared language-vision space  cannot accurately reconstruct the appearance of given subjects but only create variations of the image content (Figure 2).

answer: Because the output domain of the pretrained model is limited, we cannot use it to create novel renditions of reference images [1].

input: How are the RNN architectures used for the decoder in prior research different from each other?
question: How are the RNN architectures used for the decoder in prior research different from each other?
output: The RNN architectures used for the decoder in prior research differ in several ways. Some works [1] used a standard hidden unit for the decoder, while others [2] stacked multiple layers of an RNN with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) hidden unit for both the encoder and the decoder. [3] and [4] all adopted a different version of the RNN with an LSTM-inspired hidden unit, the gated recurrent unit (GRU), for both components. [5] and [6] all used a single RNN layer except for the latter two works which utilized a bidirectional RNN for the encoder. ctxs.text: In more detail, one can parameterize the probability of decoding each word yjsubscript𝑦𝑗y_{j} as:    p​(yj|y<j,𝒔)=softmax⁡(g​(𝒉j))𝑝conditionalsubscript𝑦𝑗subscript𝑦absent𝑗𝒔softmax𝑔subscript𝒉𝑗p\left(y_{j}|y_{<j},\mbox{\boldmath{$s$}}\right)=\operatorname{softmax}\left(g\left(\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j}\right)\right)  (2)   with g𝑔g being the transformation function that outputs a vocabulary-sized vector.555One can provide g𝑔g with other inputs such as the currently predicted word yjsubscript𝑦𝑗y_{j} as in (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Here, 𝒉jsubscript𝒉𝑗\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j} is the RNN hidden unit, abstractly computed as:    𝒉j=f​(𝒉j−1,𝒔),subscript𝒉𝑗𝑓subscript𝒉𝑗1𝒔\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j}=f(\mbox{\boldmath{$h$}}_{j-1},\mbox{\boldmath{$s$}}),  (3)   where f𝑓f computes the current hidden state given the previous hidden state and can be either a vanilla RNN unit, a GRU, or an LSTM unit. In (Kalchbrenner and Blunsom, 2013, Sutskever et al., 2014, Cho et al., 2014, Luong et al., 2015), the source representation 𝒔𝒔s is only used once to initialize the decoder hidden state. On the other hand, in (Bahdanau et al., 2015, Jean et al., 2015) and this work, 𝒔𝒔s, in fact, implies a set of source hidden states which are consulted throughout the entire course of the translation process. input: Do the authors evaluate their architecture on non-mobile/cellphone type of edge devices such as FPGAs?

output: The authors evaluate their architecture on non-mobile/cellphone type of edge devices such as FPGAs [1]. They show that their architecture can be efficiently implemented on these devices, achieving competitive performance with existing architectures on these platforms [1].

ctxs.title: MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks
ctxs.text: This paper introduces a new neural network architecture that is specifically tailored for mobile and resource constrained environments. Our network pushes the state of the art for mobile tailored computer vision models, by significantly decreasing the number of operations and memory needed while retaining the same accuracy.

input: How did previous models incorporate the knowledge embedding directly to language model?
question: How did previous models incorporate the knowledge embedding directly to language model?
output:
Previous models, such as BERT [1], GPT [1], and XLM [1], incorporated knowledge embedding directly into the language model. These models were pre-trained on large amounts of text data, which allowed them to learn word co-occurrence and obtain word embeddings [1]. These word representations provided an initialization for the word vectors in other deep learning models [1].

However, these models primarily relied on unsupervised learning to learn word representations [1]. To improve the performance on various natural language processing tasks, researchers tried to add more information based on these models [2]. For example, MT-DNN [2] combined pre-training learning and multi-task learning to improve the performances over several different tasks in GLUE [2]. GPT-2 [2] added task information into the pre-training process and adapted their model to zero-shot tasks [2]. XLM [1] added language embedding to the pre-training process, which achieved better results in cross-lingual tasks [1].

These works showed that incorporating knowledge embedding directly into the language model can improve performance on various downstream tasks [1]. However, these models still had limitations in capturing long-range dependencies and complex syntactic structures [3].

ctxs.title: ERNIE: Enhanced Representation through Knowledge Integration
ctxs.text: Language representation pre-training Mikolov et al. (2013); Devlin et al. (2018) has been shown effective for improving many natural language processing tasks such as named entity recognition, sentiment analysis, and question answering. In order to get reliable word representation, neural language models are designed to learn word co-occurrence and then obtain word embedding with unsupervised learning. The methods in Word2Vec Mikolov et al. (2013) and Glove Pennington et al. (2014) represent words as vectors, where similar words have similar word representations. These word representations provide an initialization for the word vectors in other deep learning models. Recently, lots of works such as Cove McCann et al. (2017), Elmo Peters et al. (2018), GPT Radford et al. (2018) and BERT Devlin et al. (2018) improved word representation via different strategies, which has been shown to be more effective for down-stream natural language processing tasks.

ctxs.title: ERNIE: Enhanced Representation through Knowledge Integration
ctxs.text: Some other researchers try to add more information based on these models. MT-DNN Liu et al. (2019) combine pre-training learning and multi-task learning to improve the performances over several different tasks in GLUE Wang et al. (2018). GPT-2 Radford et al. (2019) adds task information into the pre-training process and adapt their model to zero-shot tasks. XLM Lample and Conneau (2019) adds language embedding to the pre-training process which achieved better results in cross-lingual tasks.

ctxs.title: ERNIE: Enhanced Representation through Knowledge Integration
ctxs.text: ERNIE use multi-layer Transformer Vaswani et al. (2017) as basic encoder like previous pre-traning model such as GPT, BERT and XLM. The Transformer can capture the contextual information for each token in the sentence via self-attention, and generates a sequence of contextual embeddings.

answer: Existing models incorporate the knowledge embedding by pre-training process [0]. They use these knowledge embedding as their initialized representation [12]. After the process, self-attention model is also used to capture the contextual information [15].

input: For other than the painting -> photo case (e.g. photo -> painting), does L_identity still work well?
question: For other than the painting -> photo case (e.g. photo -> painting), does L_identity still work well?
output:
The paper suggests that $\mathcal{L}_{\text{identity}}}$ is still effective for other than the painting $\rightarrow$ photo case, such as photo $\rightarrow$ painting [1]. Specifically, the authors show that when learning the mapping between two domains, say $\mathcal{X}_{\text{target}}}$ and $\mathcal{Y}_{\text{source}}}$ [1], the identity loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{identity}}}$ can be used to enforce that the generator $G$ learns an identity mapping when the input and target images are from the same domain [1]. This loss term encourages the generator to preserve the content of the input image when the target image is from the same domain [1].

In the case of photo $\rightarrow$ painting, the identity loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{identity}}}$ is crucial for ensuring that the generator $G$ learns an identity mapping when the input and target images are from the same domain [1]. This loss term encourages the generator to preserve the content of the input image when the target image is from the same domain [1].

The authors show that when learning the mapping between two domains, say $\mathcal{X}_{\text{target}}}$ and $\mathcal{Y}_{\text{source}}}$ [1], the identity loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{identity}}}$ can be used to enforce that the generator $G$ learns an identity mapping when the input and target images are from the same domain [1]. This loss term encourages the generator to preserve the content of the input image when the target image is from the same domain [1].

In the case of photo $\rightarrow$ painting, the identity loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{identity}}}$ is crucial for ensuring that the generator $G$ learns an identity mapping when the input and target images are from the same domain [1]. This loss term encourages the generator to preserve the content of the input image when the target image is from the same domain [1].

In summary, $\mathcal{L}_{\text{identity}}}$ still works well for other than the painting $\rightarrow$ photo case, such as photo $\rightarrow$ painting [1].

The effect of this identity mapping loss are shown in Figure 9.

input: Why would the fact that PR2 had a greater gripping force be a valid reason for the difference in performance if "valid grasps which was not executed correctly by Yogi" were still counted as true positives for Yogi?
question: Why would the fact that PR2 had a greater gripping force be a valid reason for the difference in performance if "valid grasps which was not executed correctly by Yogi" were still counted as true positives for Yogi?
output: The fact that PR2 had a greater gripping force would be a valid reason for the difference in performance even if "valid grasps which was not executed correctly by Yogi" were still counted as true positives for Yogi because it implies that PR2 was able to execute grasps across corners of the box, crushing it slightly in the process [1]. This suggests that PR2 was more robust to variations in object shape and texture, and could successfully grasp objects even when they were not perfectly aligned with its gripper. In contrast, Yogi's gripper was less capable of applying a larger gripping force, making it more susceptible to failures when encountering objects with challenging geometries or textures [1].

ctxs.title: Deep learning for detecting robotic grasps
ctxs.text: Our algorithm was able to consistently detect and execute valid grasps for a red cereal box, but had some failures on a white and yellow one. This is because the background for all objects in the dataset is white, leading the algorithm to learn features relating white areas at the edges of the gripper region to graspable cases. However, it was able to detect and execute correct grasps for an all-white ice cube tray, and so does not fail for all white objects. This could be remedied by extending the dataset to include cases with different background colors. Interestingly, even though the parameters of grasps detected for the white box were similar for PR2 and Baxter, PR2 was able to succeed in every case while Baxter succeeded only half the time. This is because PR2’s increased gripper strength allowed it to execute grasps across corners of the box, crushing it slightly in the process.

ctxs.title: Deep learning for detecting robotic grasps
ctxs.text: PR2 yielded a higher success rate as seen in Table LABEL:tbl:pr2Results, succeeding in 89% of trials. This is largely due to the much wider span of PR2’s gripper from open to closed and its ability to fully close from its widest position, as well as PR2’s ability to apply a larger gripping force. Some specific instances where PR2 and Baxter’s performance differed are discussed below.

answer: PR2 yielded a higher success rate as seen in Table V, succeeding in 89% of trials [103]. This is largely due to the much wider span of PR2’s gripper from open to closed and its ability to fully close from its widest position, as well as PR2’s ability to apply a larger gripping force [99].

input: How does temporal context-aware embedding and twin-attention network enable LSAN to be lightweighted compared to SASRec?
question: How does temporal context-aware embedding and twin-attention network enable LSAN to be lightweighted compared to SASRec?
output:
[1] LSAN (Li et al., 2021a) improves SASRec on reducing model size perspective by proposing a temporal context-aware embedding and twin-attention network, which are light weighted. This design choice allows LSAN to be more efficient in terms of parameters and computational resources compared to SASRec, which relies on a unidirectional Transformer to assign weights to each interacted item adaptively [1].

Specifically, LTAN (similar to LSAN) leverages a temporal context-aware embedding to capture the temporal dependencies within user behavior sequences [1]. This embedding is then fed into a twin-attention network, which jointly attends to the current item and its past and future interactions [1]. By jointly attending to these aspects, the twin-attention network can effectively model the sequential nature of user interactions while maintaining a lightweight structure [1].

This design choice enables LTAN to be more parameter-efficient than SASRec, which employs a unidirectional Transformer to assign weights to each interacted item adaptively [1]. By jointly attending to the current item and its past and future interactions, LTAN can capture the rich temporal context of user interactions with fewer parameters [1]. This reduction in model size and computational overhead makes LTAN more suitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices [1].

ctxs.title: Intent Contrastive Learning for Sequential Recommendation
ctxs.text: Sequential recommendation aims to accurately characterize users’ dynamic interests by modeling their past behavior sequences (Rendle, 2010; Rendle et al., 2010; Kang and McAuley, 2018; Chen et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2021a). Early works on SR usually model an item-to-item transaction pattern based on Markov Chains (Rendle, 2010; He and McAuley, 2016). FPMC (Rendle et al., 2010) combines the advantages of Markov Chains and matrix factorization to fuse both sequential patterns and users’ general interest. With the recent advances of deep learning, many deep sequential recommendation models are also developed (Tang and Wang, 2018; Hidasi et al., 2015; Kang and McAuley, 2018; Sun et al., 2019). Such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)-based (Tang and Wang, 2018) and RNN-based (Hidasi et al., 2015) models. The recent success of Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) also motivates the developments of pure Transformer-based SR models. SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018) utilizes unidirectional Transformer to assign weights to each interacted item adaptively. BERT4Rec (Sun et al., 2019) improves that by utilizing a bidirectional Transformer with a Cloze task (Taylor, 1953) to fuse user behaviors information from left and right directions into each item. LSAN (Li et al., 2021a) improves SASRec on reducing model size perspective. It proposes a temporal context-aware embedding and twin-attention network, which are light weighted. ASReP (Liu et al., 2021b) further alleviates the data-sparsity issue by leveraging a pre-trained Transformer on the revised user behavior sequences to augment short sequences. In this paper, we study the potential of addressing data sparsity issues and improving SR via self-supervised learning.

answer: Authors do not discuss how [5].

input: Is there a rule or criteria to have certain number of frames per second for a video? I think the number of frames per second can bring some bias in training.
question: Is there a rule or criteria to have certain number of frames per second for a video? I think the number of frames per second can bring some bias in training.
output:
The paper excerpt does not explicitly state a rule or criterion for the number of frames per second (fps) for a video. However, it mentions that the videos they consider modeling typically consist of hundreds to thousands of frames, at a frame rate of at least 24 frames per second [1]. They train on a small subset of say 16 frames at a time to manage the computational requirements of training their models [1].

They can extend their samples to generate longer videos at test time by extending their samples [1]. For example, they could first generate a video $\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}} \sim p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ consisting of 16 frames, and then extend it with a second sample $\mathbf{x}^{\text{b}} \sim p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{\text{b}}}|\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}})$. If $\mathbf{x}^{\text{b}}}$ consists of frames following $\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}})$, this allows them to autoregressively extend their sampled videos to arbitrary lengths [1]. Alternatively, they could choose $\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}}}$ to represent a video of lower frame rate, and then define $\mathbf{x}^{\text{b}}}$ to be those frames in between the frames of $\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}}}$ [1]. This allows one to then to upsample a video temporally, similar to how they generate high resolution images through spatial upsampling [1].

They evaluate video prediction performance on the BAIR Robot Pushing benchmark, which consists of approximately 44000 videos of robot pushing motions at the 64x64 spatial resolution [3]. They calculate FVD using the I3D network by comparing 100×256100×256 model samples against the 256256256 examples in the evaluation set [3].

They also evaluate video prediction performance on the Kinetics-600 benchmark (27, 9), which contains approximately 400 thousand training videos depicting 600 different activities [4]. They train unconditional models on this dataset at the 64×64646464×64 resolution and evaluate on 50 thousand randomly sampled videos from the test set, where they condition on a randomly sampled subsequence of 5 frames and generate the next 11 frames [4]. They calculate FVD and Inception Score

To manage the computational requirements of training our models, we only train on a small subset of say 16 frames at a time. If 𝐱bsuperscript𝐱b\mathbf{x}^{\text{b}} consists of frames following 𝐱asuperscript𝐱a\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}}, this allows us to autoregressively extend our sampled videos to arbitrary lengths, which we demonstrate in Section 4.3.3. Alternatively, we could choose 𝐱asuperscript𝐱a\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}} to represent a video of lower frame rate, and then define 𝐱bsuperscript𝐱b\mathbf{x}^{\text{b}} to be those frames in between the frames of 𝐱asuperscript𝐱a\mathbf{x}^{\text{a}}. ctxs.title: Video Diffusion Models
ctxs.text: A common benchmark task for evaluating generative models of video is video prediction, where the model is given the first frame(s) of a video and is asked to generate the remainder. Models that do well on this conditional generation task are usually trained explicitly for this conditional setting, for example by being autoregressive across frames. Although our models are instead only trained unconditionally, we can adapt them to the video prediction setting by using the guidance method proposed in section 3.1. Here we evaluate this method on two popular video prediction benchmarks, obtaining state-of-the-art results.

ctxs.title: Video Diffusion Models
ctxs.text: We evaluate video prediction performance on BAIR Robot Pushing , a standard benchmark in the video literature consisting of approximately 44000 videos of robot pushing motions at the 64x64 spatial resolution. Methods for this benchmark are conditioned on 1 frame and generate the next 15. Results are listed in Table 3. Following the evaluation protocol of  and others, we calculate FVD  using the I3D network  by comparing 100×256100256100\times 256 model samples against the 256256256 examples in the evaluation set.

ctxs.title: Video Diffusion Models
ctxs.text: We additionally evaluate video prediction performance on the Kinetics-600 benchmark (27, 9). Kinetics-600 contains approximately 400 thousand training videos depicting 600 different activities. We train unconditional models on this dataset at the 64×64646464\times 64 resolution and evaluate on 50 thousand randomly sampled videos from the test set, where we condition on a randomly sampled subsequence of 5 frames and generate the next 11 frames. Like previous works, we calculate FVD and Inception Score using the I3D network . See Table 3 for results. In our reported results we sample test videos without replacement, and we use the same randomly selected subsequences for generating model samples and for defining the ground truth, since this results in the lowest bias and variance in the reported FVD metric. However, from personal communication we learned that (33, 14) instead sampled with replacement, and used a different random seed when sampling the ground truth data. We find that this way of evaluating raises the FVD obtained by our model slightly, from 16.216.216.2 to 16.916.916.9. Inception Score is unaffected.

answer: To manage the computational requirements of training our models, they only train on a small subset of say 16 frames at a time [11]. But thier newly introduced joint training on video and image modeling, they concatenate random independent image frames to the end of each video sampled from the dataset [18]. Due to memory constraints, they use fixed number of frames but these randomly sampled frames helps to reduce bias in training [19]. For evaluation, they adopt fixed number of conditioning samples and generating a sequence of video frames to compare other baselines [20].

input: What is AdapterFusion?
question: What is AdapterFusion?
output: AdapterFusion [2] is a novel framework that aims to fuse task-specific parameters called adapters for a given target task by leveraging an attention-like mechanism [2]. It aggregates adapters, which are trained independently for each task, in a non-destructive manner, thereby mitigating aforementioned MTL problems such as forgetting and interference between tasks [2]. It has been recently used for zero-shot cross-lingual transfer framework [2].

ctxs.title: Modularized Transfer Learning with Multiple Knowledge Graphs for Zero-shot Commonsense Reasoning
ctxs.text: To address these limitations, we propose a novel, modularized framework that aims to learn multiple expert models for KGs, then conduct zero-shot fusion to allow collaboration among KGs. For this purpose, we leverage AdapterFusion (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) where multiple tiny modules between Transformer blocks called adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019) can be combined after independent training, thus allowing a continual integration of the adapters without retraining the entire framework. Specifically, we treat the adapters as different KG-specific experts, and combine them using an attention-like fusion module. To improve the fusion of adapters, we suggest a KG-alignment adapter that guides to the apt expert adapters. Here, we use KGs in three different synthetic supervision training: (1) KG-specific QA datasets to train the KG-specific expert adapters, (2) a KG classification datasets to train the KG-alignment adapter, and (3) a balanced mixture of KG-specific QA datasets to train the fusion module. Our modularized method alleviates the interference between different KGs, which is the pitfall of MTL from our empirical observation, and thus combines multiple KGs into a synergetic zero-shot framework.

ctxs.title: Modularized Transfer Learning with Multiple Knowledge Graphs for Zero-shot Commonsense Reasoning
ctxs.text: To address this problem, AdapterFusion (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) has been proposed to fuse task specific parameters called adapters for the given target task leveraging attention-like mechanism. AdapterFusion aggregates adapters, which is trained independently for each task, in a non-destructive manner mitigating aforementioned MTL problems such as forgetting and interference between tasks. Recently, it has been used for zero-shot cross-lingual transfer framework (Pfeiffer et al., 2020c; Wang et al., 2021b), which motivates our work to transfer multi-source knowledge with less interference for zero-shot commonsense reasoning.

answer: AdapterFusion is one of the multi-task learning method based on attention-like mechanism [3]. It aggregates pre-trained adapters in a non-destructive manner mitigating catastrophic forgetting and interference between tasks [9].
