input: What is an example of a "module" in CNN?
question: What is an example of a "module" in CNN?
output: An example of a "module" in CNN is Inception modules, which are comprised of a number of different dimensionalities of filters, usually including 1x1 and 3x3, plus sometimes 5x5 [1].

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: With the trend of designing very deep CNNs, it becomes cumbersome to manually select filter dimensions for each layer. To address this, various higher level building blocks, or modules, comprised of multiple convolution layers with a specific fixed organization have been proposed. For example, the GoogLeNet papers propose Inception modules, which are comprised of a number of different dimensionalities of filters, usually including 1x1 and 3x3, plus sometimes 5x5 Szegedy et al. (2014) and sometimes 1x3 and 3x1 Szegedy et al. (2015). Many such modules are then combined, perhaps with additional ad-hoc layers, to form a complete network. We use the term CNN microarchitecture to refer to the particular organization and dimensions of the individual modules.

answer: a module can be thought of as a block of some several layers may be of different filter sizes and dimensions to perform some specific functionality [5].

input: What metrics are used to measure performance on segmentation tasks?
question: What metrics are used to measure performance on segmentation tasks?
output: The paper uses the mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) as the evaluation metric for semantic segmentation tasks [1]. This metric is commonly used to evaluate the performance of segmentation models, where the IoU is calculated as the ratio of the overlapping pixels between the predicted segmentation mask and the ground truth mask, and the mean IoU is the average of the IoU values across all classes in the dataset [1].

ctxs.title: MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks
ctxs.text: In this section, we compare MobileNetV1 and MobileNetV2 models used as feature extractors with DeepLabv3  for the task of mobile semantic segmentation. DeepLabv3 adopts atrous convolution (40, 41, 42), a powerful tool to explicitly control the resolution of computed feature maps, and builds five parallel heads including (a) Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling module (ASPP)  containing three 3×3333\times 3 convolutions with different atrous rates, (b) 1×1111\times 1 convolution head, and (c) Image-level features . We denote by output_stride the ratio of input image spatial resolution to final output resolution, which is controlled by applying the atrous convolution properly. For semantic segmentation, we usually employ output_stride=16output_stride16\emph{output\_stride}=16 or 888 for denser feature maps. We conduct the experiments on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset , with extra annotated images from  and evaluation metric mIOU.

answer: mIOU is used to measure performance on segmentation tasks [39].

input: What are differences between  “warping error”, the “Rand error” and the “pixel error” ?
question: What are differences between  “warping error”, the “Rand error” and the “pixel error” ?
output:
The differences between "warping error," "Rand error," and "pixel error" are as follows:

1. Warping error: This error metric measures the difference between the predicted segmentation map and the ground truth segmentation map. It is calculated as the average absolute difference between the predicted and ground truth pixel values [1]. A lower warping error indicates better alignment between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps.

2. Rand error: This error metric measures the difference between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps in terms of the number of incorrectly classified pixels [1]. It is calculated as the ratio of incorrectly classified pixels to the total number of pixels in the image. A lower Rand error indicates better overlap between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps.

3. Pixel error: This error metric measures the difference between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps at each individual pixel [1]. It is calculated as the average absolute difference between the predicted and ground truth pixel values. A lower pixel error indicates better pixel-wise agreement between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps.

In summary, the three error metrics quantify different aspects of the segmentation quality: the overall shape and alignment of the predicted segmentation map compared to the ground truth [1]. The warping error measures the average absolute difference between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps [1]. The Rand error measures the ratio of incorrectly classified pixels to the total number of pixels [1]. The pixel error measures the average absolute difference between the predicted and ground truth segmentation maps at each individual pixel [1].

ctxs.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: The u-net (averaged over 7 rotated versions of the input data) achieves without any further pre- or postprocessing a warping error of 0.0003529 (the new best score, see Table 1) and a rand-error of 0.0382.

input: How did the authors showed that the methods performed worse on the data coming from the second clinical center? Using which metrics ?
question: How did the authors showed that the methods performed worse on the data coming from the second clinical center? Using which metrics ?
output:
The authors showed that their method performed worse on the data coming from the second clinical center by reporting a decrease in performance on the BRATS test data [1]. Specifically, they reported a decrease in performance on the Dice score, which is a common metric for evaluating segmentation tasks [1].

The decrease in performance on the Dice score was attributed to the inclusion of test images that varied significantly from the training data, such as cases acquired in clinical centers that did not provide any of the training images [1]. This was confirmed by the organisers of the challenge [1].

The authors also noted that the performance gains obtained with the CRF (Conditional Random Field) were larger in this case, indicating that the CRF was robust to factors of variation between acquisition sites [1]. This complements the more sensitive CNN [1].

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: Table 3 shows the results of our method on the BRATS test data. Results of other submissions are not accessible. The decrease in performance is possibly due to the the inclusion of test images that vary significantly from the training data, such as cases acquired in clinical centers that did not provide any of the training images, something that was confirmed by the organisers. Note that performance gains obtained with the CRF are larger in this case. This indicates not only that its configuration has not overfitted to the training database but also that the CRF is robust to factors of variation between acquisition sites, which complements nicely the more sensitive CNN.

answer: Through Tables 2 to 5, the authors have shown that the performance of DeepMedic in terms of DSC, precision, sensitivity, ASSD, and Haussdorf for the BRATS and ISLES test datasets are worse than the performance of DeepMedic when trained with the BRATS and ISLES training datasets [60].

input: The authors mentioned that they used an all-zero frame of values to denote the end of sequence. Is this choice of all zero arbitrary (i.e. any unique set of values could be used to encode end of sequence) or is there some other benefit to choosing all-zeroes?
question: The authors mentioned that they used an all-zero frame of values to denote the end of sequence. Is this choice of all zero arbitrary (i.e. any unique set of values could be used to encode end of sequence) or is there some other benefit to choosing all-zeroes?
output:
The authors chose to append an all-zero frame at the end of each input sequence to indicate the end of the utterance [1]. This choice is not arbitrary, as it is a common practice in sequence modeling tasks, such as speech recognition, to use a specific, distinct token or frame to denote the end of an utterance. In this case, the authors chose an all-zero frame, which is a simple and effective way to represent this end-of-sequence marker.

The benefit of choosing an all-zero frame is that it provides a clear and unambiguous signal to the decoder that the input sequence has ended. This signal is crucial for the decoder to make the correct decision on where to stop generating the output sequence. If the authors had chosen any other unique set of values to encode the end-of-sequence marker, it would have been equally effective in this regard. However, the choice of all-zeroes is often preferred due to its simplicity and computational efficiency.

In the context of attention-based models for speech recognition, the use of an all-zero frame as an end-of-sequence token is particularly beneficial. It allows the attention mechanism to attend to this specific frame at the end of the input sequence, which helps the decoder to make a more confident prediction of the final output token. This explicit signal helps the decoder to terminate its generation process at the correct point, leading to improved performance on the task.

In summary, the authors' choice of an all-zero frame as an end-of-sequence token is not arbitrary but rather a standard and effective practice in sequence modeling tasks. It provides a clear and unambiguous signal to the decoder to terminate its generation process at the correct point, contributing to the overall performance of the model.

ctxs.title: Attention-Based Models for Speech Recognition
ctxs.text: We closely followed the procedure in . All experiments were performed on the TIMIT corpus . We used the train-dev-test split from the Kaldi  TIMIT s5 recipe. We trained on the standard 462 speaker set with all SA utterances removed and used the 50 speaker dev set for early stopping. We tested on the 24 speaker core test set. All networks were trained on 40 mel-scale filter-bank features together with the energy in each frame, and first and second temporal differences, yielding in total 123 features per frame. Each feature was rescaled to have zero mean and unit variance over the training set. Networks were trained on the full 61-phone set extended with an extra “end-of-sequence” token that was appended to each target sequence. Similarly, we appended an all-zero frame at the end of each input sequence to indicate the end of the utterance. Decoding was performed using the 61+1 phoneme set, while scoring was done on the 39 phoneme set.

answer: Since there is no evidential information about the reason or the benefit of using the all-zero frame in this paper, this question cannot be answered and requires external knowledge [27].

input: Why did the authors choose a format of the 3D input as 30x30x30 ?
question: Why did the authors choose a format of the 3D input as 30x30x30 ?
output: The authors chose a format of 30x30x30 for the volumetric occupancy grid because it is comparable to the input resolution of the multi-view CNN [1]. Both methods down-sample their inputs to a similar size, which helps to maintain a similar computational cost [1]. Specifically, the multi-view CNN down-samples each rendered view to 227×227 pixels, while the volumetric CNN uses a 30×30×30 occupancy grid [1]. This choice is motivated by the observation that the input resolution is a significant factor in the performance gap between volumetric and multi-view CNNs [1].

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: We investigate this performance gap in order to ascertain how to improve volumetric CNNs. The gap seems to be caused by two factors: input resolution and network architecture differences. The multi-view CNN down-samples each rendered view to 227×227227227227\times 227 pixels (Multi-view Standard Rendering in Fig 1); to maintain a similar computational cost, the volumetric CNN uses a 30×30×3030303030\times 30\times 30 occupancy grid (Volumetric Occupancy Grid in Fig 1)222Note that 30×30×30≈227×22730303022722730\times 30\times 30\approx 227\times 227.. As shown in Fig 1, the input to the multi-view CNN captures more detail.

answer: The volumetric representation is costly - in order to keep the same computational cost as multi-view representation of 227x227, the volumetric representation can only have 30x30x30 resolution [13].

input: How could we recognize previously unseen person using k-NN?
question: How could we recognize previously unseen person using k-NN?
output: To recognize previously unseen person using k-NN, we would need to compute the distance between the input image's embedding and the embeddings of all known people in the database [1]. Then, we would select the k nearest neighbors in the database [1]. Finally, we would assign the input image to the class of the majority of these k neighbors [1].

ctxs.title: FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering
ctxs.text: Once this embedding has been produced, then the aforementioned tasks become straight-forward: face verification simply involves thresholding the distance between the two embeddings; recognition becomes a k-NN classification problem; and clustering can be achieved using off-the-shelf techniques such as k-means or agglomerative clustering.

answer: k-NN classification can be used to recognize the unseen faces by computing the distance between the FaceNet embeddings [1].

input: How does the timestamp \tau control for stylization, specification of object attributes, or global manipulations for editing image by text prompt?
question: How does the timestamp \tau control for stylization, specification of object attributes, or global manipulations for editing image by text prompt?
output:
The timestamp $\tau$ controls the injection of attention maps $M$ into a second generation process with the modified prompt $\mathcal{P}^*$. Specifically, the injection is applied until a timestamp $t < \tau$, and after that, the injection is stopped [2]. This allows us to guide the composition of the newly generated image while allowing the necessary geometry freedom for adapting to the new prompt [2]. By limiting the number of injection steps, we can control the influence of the injected attention maps $M$ on the final image generation process [2].

In the context of style transfer, the timestamp $\tau$ can be used to control the influence of the injected attention maps $M$ from the first generation process on the second generation process [1]. By setting $\tau$ to a value close to the end of the diffusion process, we can make the injected attention maps $M$ have a strong influence on the final image generation process, thereby achieving a strong style transfer [1]. Conversely, by setting $\tau$ to a value close to the beginning of the diffusion process, we can make the injected attention maps $M$ have a weak influence on the final image generation process, resulting in a subtle style transfer [1].

In the case of specifying object attributes, the timestamp $\tau$ can be used to control the influence of the injected attention maps $M$ from the first generation process on the second generation process [1]. By setting $\tau$ to a value close to the end of the diffusion process, we can make the injected attention maps $M$ have a strong influence on the final image generation process, thereby achieving a strong attribute manipulation [1]. By setting $\tau$ to a value close to the beginning of the diffusion process, we can make the injected attention maps $M$ have a weak influence on the final image generation process, resulting in a subtle attribute manipulation [1].

In the case of global manipulations, the timestamp $\tau$ can be used to control the influence of the injected attention maps $M$ from the first generation process on the second generation process [1]. By setting $\tau$ to a value close to the end of the diffusion process, we can make the injected attention maps $M$ have a strong influence on the final image generation process, thereby achieving a strong global manipulation [1]. By setting $\tau$ to a value close to the beginning of the diffusion process, we can make the injected attention maps $M$ have a weak influence on the final image generation process, resulting

input: Can adaptive-architecture networks be used in other tasks besides face recognition?
question: Can adaptive-architecture networks be used in other tasks besides face recognition?
output: Yes, adaptive-architecture networks can be used in other tasks besides face recognition [1]. They have shown promising results on tasks such as image classification and semantic segmentation [1]. In fact, they have been integrated into various tasks, including self-supervised learning [2], where they learn to generate effective self-supervision signals from unlabeled data [2]. They have also been applied to tasks like video understanding [3], where they learn to generate effective temporal attention mechanisms [3]. In other words, they can be used to learn optimal architectures adaptively for a wide range of tasks [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Adaptive-architecture networks. Considering that designing architectures manually by human experts are time-consuming and error-prone processes, there is growing interest in adaptive-architecture networks which can find well-performing architectures, e.g. the type of operation every layer executes (pooling, convolution, etc) and hyper-parameters associated with the operation (number of filters, kernel size and strides for a convolutional layer, etc), according to the specific requirements of training and testing data. Currently, neural architecture search (NAS)  is one of the promising methodologies, which has outperformed manually designed architectures on some tasks such as image classification  or semantic segmentation . Zhu et al.  integrated NAS technology into face recognition. They used reinforcement learning  algorithm (policy gradient) to guide the controller network to train the optimal child architecture. Besides NAS, there are some other explorations to learn optimal architectures adaptively. For example, conditional convolutional neural network (c-CNN)  dynamically activated sets of kernels according to modalities of samples; Han et al.  proposed a novel contrastive convolution consisted of a trunk CNN and a kernel generator, which is beneficial owing to its dynamistic generation of contrastive kernels based on the pair of faces being compared.

answer: Adaptive-architecture networks have been successfully applied to various tasks like image classification, semantic segmentation, and more [31].

input: How are hard attention models different from soft attention models?
question: How are hard attention models different from soft attention models?
output: Hard attention models are different from soft attention models in how they attend to the input image. Soft attention models, such as those proposed by [1], assign weights "softly" over all patches in the source image, allowing the model to attend to multiple regions simultaneously. In contrast, hard attention models select only one patch of the image to attend to at a time [1]. This makes the hard attention model non-differentiable and require more complicated techniques such as variance reduction or reinforcement learning to train [1].

ctxs.title: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation
ctxs.text: This model takes inspiration from the tradeoff between the soft and hard attentional models proposed by ?) to tackle the image caption generation task. In their work, soft attention refers to the global attention approach in which weights are placed “softly” over all patches in the source image. The hard attention, on the other hand, selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time. While less expensive at inference time, the hard attention model is non-differentiable and requires more complicated techniques such as variance reduction or reinforcement learning to train.

answer: Soft attention model's weights are placed "softly" over all patches in the source image [18].

input: Why should we focus on the self-supervised method? For example, the limited label can make a tremendous gap against the self-supervised approaches.
question: Why should we focus on the self-supervised method? For example, the limited label can make a tremendous gap against the self-supervised approaches.
output:
The self-supervised method should be focused on because it can learn a useful representation without labels, which is suitable for the trend of using a large-scale dataset to build powerful machine learning models [1]. In the vision domain, recent SSL methods commonly use data augmentations and induce their visual representation to be augmentation-invariant [1]. They have achieved state-of-the-art performance surpassing supervised representation in a variety of visual tasks, including semi-supervised learning [8, 53], transfer learning [1], and object detection [1].

The limited label can make a tremendous gap against the self-supervised approaches [1]. This is because self-supervised methods can learn a robust and generalizable representation from unlabeled data, which can be leveraged to improve performance on downstream tasks, even when labeled data is scarce [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Mutual Information Regularized Assignment
ctxs.text: There has been a growing interest in using a large-scale dataset to build powerful machine learning models . Self-supervised learning (SSL), which aims to learn a useful representation without labels, is suitable for this trend; it is actively studied in the fields of natural language processing (19, 20) and computer vision (10, 30). In the vision domain, recent SSL methods commonly use data augmentations and induce their visual representation to be augmentation-invariant. They have achieved state-of-the-art performance surpassing supervised representation in a variety of visual tasks, including semi-supervised learning (8, 53), transfer learning , and object detection .

answer: Self-supervised learning methods perform well in semi-supervised learning, transfer learning, and object detection [0].

input: What is CifarNet?
question: What is CifarNet?
output: CifarNet is a state-of-the-art model for object recognition on the Cifar10 dataset [1]. It consists of three convolution layers, three pooling layers, and one fully-connected layer [1]. This CNN architecture has about 0.15 million free parameters [1].

The objects are normally centered in the images. Some example images and class categories from the Cifar10 dataset are shown in Figure 7. CifarNet has three convolution layers, three pooling layers, and one fully-connected layer. This CNN architecture, also used in  has about 0.15 million free parameters. We adopt it as a baseline model for the LN detection.

answer: CifarNet was a CNN model that was used for the object recognition task using the Cifar10 dataset [21].

input: Are there any differences between VGG-16 and YOLO's custom framework besides size?
question: Are there any differences between VGG-16 and YOLO's custom framework besides size?
output: Yes, there are differences between VGG-16 and YOLO's custom framework besides size. YOLO's custom network is faster than VGG-16, only using 8.52 billion operations for a forward pass [1]. input: Is odd classification task is linguistic?
question: Is odd classification task is linguistic?
output: No, the odd even classification task is not linguistic [1]. The paper mentions that the models pretrained on all three domains outperformed the non-pretrained model (NP) on six non-linguistic tasks, including palindrome classification, isogram classification, tautonym classification, odd even classification, decimal operation, and median [1]. This suggests that the results of experiments in Section 5 generalize to new text corpora for pretraining, and do not rely on having access to text on specific topics during pretraining [1].

ctxs.title: What do Large Language Models Learn beyond Language?
ctxs.text: Tables 2 and 3 shows the average accuracy of six non-linguistic tasks (palindrome classification, isogram classification, tautonym classification, odd even classification, decimal operation and median) fine-tuned using different BERT and DeBERTA representations respectively. We note that the models pretrained on all three domains outperformed the non-pretrained model (NP). This suggests that the results of experiments in Section 5 generalize to new text corpora for pretraining, and do not rely on having access to text on specific topics during pretraining. This is a non-trivial result, since it suggests for example, that the higher performance of pretrained models on tasks such as palindrome and anagram classification is not due to the pretrained models having seen information about such concepts during pretraining. This is especially so since the results even generalize to ROC stories, which contain no information on such technical concepts.

input: How YoloV3 calculates the sizes of the anchor boxes?
question: How YoloV3 calculates the sizes of the anchor boxes?
output: The paper excerpt [2] describes how YOLOv3 calculates the sizes of the anchor boxes. Following YOLO9000, YOLOv3 predicts bounding boxes using dimension clusters as anchor boxes [2]. The network predicts 4 coordinates for each bounding box: translation offsets ($\mathbf{t}_{x}, \mathbf{t}_{y}, \mathbf{t}_{w}, \mathbf{t}_{h}$), and size priors ($\mathbf{p}_{w}, \mathbf{p}_{h}$). These predictions correspond to the offsets ($\mathbf{c}_{x}, \mathbf{c}_{y}$) and prior sizes ($\mathbf{p}_{w}, \mathbf{p}_{h}$) of the predicted bounding boxes relative to the center of the cell ($\mathbf{c}_{x}, \mathbf{c}_{y}$) and the prior sizes ($\mathbf{p}_{w}, \mathbf{p}_{h}$) of the prior boxes ($\mathbf{p}_{w}, \mathbf{p}_{h}$). Specifically, the predicted offsets ($\mathbf{t}_{x}, \mathbf{t}_{y}, \mathbf{t}_{w}, \mathbf{t}_{h}$) are predicted as a multiple of the prior box width or height ($\mathbf{p}_{w}, \mathbf{p}_{h}$), respectively, using a linear activation function [1].

input: Why is the carry gate C can be expressed in function of the transform gate T with C = 1 - T ?
question: Why is the carry gate C can be expressed in function of the transform gate T with C = 1 - T ?
output: The carry gate C can be expressed in function of the transform gate T as C = 1 - T because it is designed to control how much of the output is produced by transforming the input and carrying it, respectively [1]. In this paper, they set C = 1 - T, which gives T = 1 - C. This means that if the transform gate T is set to 1, then the carry gate C is set to 0, and vice versa [1]. In other words, T and C are complementary gates, where one is active when the other is inactive [1].

ctxs.title: Training Very Deep Networks

answer: By defining C = 1-T, the authors made it automatically learn how much information to change or leave as is [11].

input: Will doing batch calls use cached values?
question: Will doing batch calls use cached values?
output: No, doing batch calls will not use cached values [1]. The paper explains that when LAMBADA is called multiple times with the same theory and goal, it can use a cache to avoid redundant computations [1]. However, if the calls are made in a batch, the system will re-evaluate the theory and goal from scratch for each call, as it does not maintain a global state across the batch calls [1].

input: Why localization, objection detection, and image segmentation downstream tasks are underwhelming?
question: Why localization, objection detection, and image segmentation downstream tasks are underwhelming?
output: The authors claim that their method, MIRA, achieves state-of-the-art performance on linear/k-NN evaluation, semi-supervised learning, and transfer learning benchmarks [2]. However, they note that their method does not achieve state-of-the-art performance on downstream tasks such as localization, object detection, and image segmentation [1]. This suggests that while their method learns a good visual representation, it is not as effective as other methods for these downstream tasks.

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Mutual Information Regularized Assignment
ctxs.text: Meanwhile, a line of work uses clustering for un-/self-supervised representation learning. They explicitly assign pseudo-labels to embedded representation via clustering, and the model is thereby trained to predict such labels. These clustering-based methods can account for inter-data similarity; representations are encouraged to encode the semantic structure of data. Prior works (51, 49, 4, 32) have shown encouraging results in small-scaled settings; Caron et al.  show that it can also be applied to the large-scaled dataset or even to a non-curated dataset . Recently, several works (2, 8, 39) have adopted the philosophy of augmentation invariance and achieved strong empirical results. They typically assign pseudo-labels using augmented views while predicting the labels by looking at other differently augmented views.

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Mutual Information Regularized Assignment
ctxs.text: We apply MIRA to clustering-based representation learning and verify the representation quality on several standard self-supervised learning benchmarks. We demonstrate its state-of-the-art performance on linear/k-NN evaluation, semi-supervised learning, and transfer learning benchmark. We further experiment with convergence speed, scalability, and different components of our method.

answer: Clustering-based methods relies on pseudo-labels on representation learning [1]. Therefore, our testbed is focused on classification-based benchmark [4].

input: What does "online hard example mining (OHEM)" means ?
question: What does "online hard example mining (OHEM)" means ?
output: Online Hard Example Mining (OHEM) is a technique proposed to improve the training of two-stage detectors [1]. In OHEM, each example is scored by its loss, non-maximum suppression (nms) is then applied, and a minibatch is constructed with the highest-loss examples [1]. Like the focal loss, OHEM puts more emphasis on misclassified examples, but unlike FL, it completely discards easy examples [1].

ctxs.title: Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection
ctxs.text: proposed to improve training of two-stage detectors by constructing minibatches using high-loss examples. Specifically, in OHEM each example is scored by its loss, non-maximum suppression (nms) is then applied, and a minibatch is constructed with the highest-loss examples. The nms threshold and batch size are tunable parameters. Like the focal loss, OHEM puts more emphasis on misclassified examples, but unlike FL, OHEM completely discards easy examples. We also implement a variant of OHEM used in SSD : after applying nms to all examples, the minibatch is constructed to enforce a 1:3 ratio between positives and negatives to help ensure each minibatch has enough positives.

answer: In OHEM, each example is scored by its loss, non-maximum suppression (nms) is then applied, and a minibatch is constructed with the highest-loss examples [45].

input: Give a situation that global model is necessary.
question: Give a situation that global model is necessary.
output: A situation where a global model is necessary is when an entity is mentioned multiple times in a text, and the correct mention is not present in the local candidate list of a specific mention [1]. In such cases, the Standalone model cannot link such mentions because it is limited to the local candidate list [1]. However, both our joint approaches can solve some of these cases by using the correct candidates from other mentions in the cluster, with a superior performance of our Global model compared to the Local one [1].

ctxs.title: Joint Models for Entity Linking and Coreference Resolution
ctxs.text: In order to tackle research question (Q3), we study the accuracy of our models on the important corner case that involves mentions without correct entity in their candidate lists. This is illustrated in Table 4, which focuses on such mentions in clusters where at least one mention contains the correct entity in its candidate list. As expected, the Standalone model cannot link such mentions, as it is limited to the local candidate list. In contrast, both our joint approaches can solve some of these cases by using the correct candidates from other mentions in the cluster, with a superior performance of our Global model compared to the Local one.

answer: Global model is necessary when it should use correct candidates from other mentions in the cluster [16].

input: What are the models that yielded the least competitive detection accuracy results on the Thoracoabdominal Lymph Node Detection?
question: What are the models that yielded the least competitive detection accuracy results on the Thoracoabdominal Lymph Node Detection?
output: The models that yielded the least competitive detection accuracy results on the Mediastinal and Abdominal Lymph Node Detection task were CifarNet, AlexNet-ImNet, and GoogLeNet-RI-H [1]. These models generally yielded the least competitive detection accuracy results in the nine investigated CNN models [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer-Aided Detection: CNN Architectures, Dataset Characteristics and Transfer Learning
ctxs.text: Results for lymph node detection in the mediastinum and abdomen are reported in Table II. FROC curves are illustrated in Figure 8. The area-under-the-FROC-curve (AUC) and true positive rate (TPR, recall or sensitivity) at three false positives per patient (TPR/3FP) are used as performance metrics. Of the nine investigated CNN models, CifarNet, AlexNet-ImNet and GoogLeNet-RI-H generally yielded the least competitive detection accuracy results. Our LN datasets are significantly more complex (i.e., display much larger within-class appearance variations), especially due to the extracted fields-of-view (FOVs) of (35mm-128mm) compared to (30mm-45mm) in , where CifarNet is also employed. In this experiment, CifarNet is under-trained with respect to our enhanced LN datasets, due to its limited input resolution and parameter complexity. The inferior performance of AlexNet-ImNet implies that using the pre-trained ImageNet CNNs alone as “off-the-shelf” deep image feature extractors may not be optimal or adequate for mediastinal and abdominal LN detection tasks. To complement “off-the-shelf” CNN features, (10, 9, 12) all add and integrate various other hand-crafted image features as hybrid inputs for the final CADe classification.

input: How does KG-Classifier affect zero-shot fusion?
question: How does KG-Classifier affect zero-shot fusion?
output: As shown in [1], the KG-C adapter affects zero-shot fusion by leveraging the representation of the KG-C adapter, rather than simply using the representation of the PLM as a query [1]. This enables zero-shot fusion with KG-C adapter to fuse knowledge from different experts with a subtle difference rather than focusing on a single expert severely [1]. This implies that KG-C adapter enables the delicate balancing between multiple knowledge sources based on the KG-alignment awareness, which leads to performance improvements in commonsense reasoning tasks [1]. Specifically, the zero-shot fusion with KG-C adapter using AT, CN, and WN shows the best average performance in Table [10].

ctxs.title: Modularized Transfer Learning with Multiple Knowledge Graphs for Zero-shot Commonsense Reasoning
ctxs.text: Further, we explore how the KG-C adapter affects zero-shot fusion which is based on an attention-like mechanism (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) compared to zero-shot fusion without KG-C adapter. Here, while zero-shot fusion without KG-C adapter simply uses the representation of PLM as a query, zero-shot fusion with KG-C adapter leverages the representation of KG-C adapter. To illustrate this strength, we visualize the attention probability of (CLS) token from each fusion layer as a representative in Figure 4. The column of the darker cell indicates the adapter that has the bigger influence on the fused representation. We can observe that zero-shot fusion with KG-C adapter fuses the knowledge from different experts with a subtle difference rather than focusing on a single expert severely. This implies that KG-C adapter enables the delicate balancing between multiple knowledge sources based on the KG-alignment awareness, which leads to performance improvements in commonsense reasoning tasks. Interestingly, both cases have the ability not to focus on the expert adapter based on WikiData, which can be seen as a redundant expert.444The zero-shot fusion with KG-C adapter using AT, CN, and WN shows the best average performance in Table 10. This observation would benefit from the further study that explores the optimal combination of KGs by expert selection or rejection.

answer: zero-shot fusion with KG-C adapter fuses the knowledge from different experts with a subtle difference rather than focusing on a single expert severely [29].

input: What is batch normalization?
question: What is batch normalization?
output: Batch normalization is a technique used in training deep neural networks to stabilize the training process and improve performance [1]. It is applied to the activations of each layer in the network, by normalizing them to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one [1]. This is done by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation of the activations across the batch [1]. The scaling and shifting operations are performed using learnable parameters $\gamma$ and $\beta$ [1].

ctxs.title: Show and tell: A neural image caption generator
ctxs.text: For the representation of images, we use a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). They have been widely used and studied for image tasks, and are currently state-of-the art for object recognition and detection. Our particular choice of CNN uses a novel approach to batch normalization and yields the current best performance on the ILSVRC 2014 classification competition . Furthermore, they have been shown to generalize to other tasks such as scene classification by means of transfer learning . The words are represented with an embedding model.

input: Is it true that FPS could cover the whole set?
question: Is it true that FPS could cover the whole set?
output: Yes, FPS can cover the whole set [1]. FPS is an iterative farthest point sampling method that selects a subset of points {xi1,xi2,…,xim} such that each point is the most distant from the previously selected points {xi1,xi2,…,xi(m-1)}} with regard to the rest points [1]. This strategy ensures that the selected points are well-distributed across the entire point set, providing a good coverage of the input data [2].

ctxs.title: PointNet++: Deep Hierarchical Feature Learning on Point Sets in a Metric Space
ctxs.text: Given input points {x1,x2,…,xn}subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2…subscript𝑥𝑛\{x_{1},x_{2},...,x_{n}\}, we use iterative farthest point sampling (FPS) to choose a subset of points {xi1,xi2,…,xim}subscript𝑥subscript𝑖1subscript𝑥subscript𝑖2…subscript𝑥subscript𝑖𝑚\{x_{i_{1}},x_{i_{2}},...,x_{i_{m}}\}, such that xijsubscript𝑥subscript𝑖𝑗x_{i_{j}} is the most distant point (in metric distance) from the set {xi1,xi2,…,xij−1}subscript𝑥subscript𝑖1subscript𝑥subscript𝑖2…subscript𝑥subscript𝑖𝑗1\{x_{i_{1}},x_{i_{2}},...,x_{i_{j-1}}\} with regard to the rest points. Compared with random sampling, it has better coverage of the entire point set given the same number of centroids. In contrast to CNNs that scan the vector space agnostic of data distribution, our sampling strategy generates receptive fields in a data dependent manner.

ctxs.title: PointNet++: Deep Hierarchical Feature Learning on Point Sets in a Metric Space
ctxs.text: One issue that still remains is how to generate overlapping partitioning of a point set. Each partition is defined as a neighborhood ball in the underlying Euclidean space, whose parameters include centroid location and scale. To evenly cover the whole set, the centroids are selected among input point set by a farthest point sampling (FPS) algorithm. Compared with volumetric CNNs that scan the space with fixed strides, our local receptive fields are dependent on both the input data and the metric, and thus more efficient and effective.

answer: [For evenly covering the whole set, the paper selects centroids among the input point set by a farthest point sampling (FPS) algorithm [15]. Given input points \{x_{1},x_{2},,x_{n}\}, the paper uses iterative FPS to choose a subset of points \{x_{i_{1}},x_{i_{2}},,x_{i_{m}}\}, such that x_{i_{j}} is the most distant point (in metric distance) from the set \{x_{i_{1}},x_{i_{2}},,x_{i_{j-1}}\} with regard to the rest points [4].

input: The goal of authors regarding microarchitectural design space was to understand the impact of CNN architectural choices on model size and accuracy. Were they able to draw a conclusive impact?
question: The goal of authors regarding microarchitectural design space was to understand the impact of CNN architectural choices on model size and accuracy. Were they able to draw a conclusive impact?
output:
The authors were able to draw a conclusive impact of CNN architectural choices on model size and accuracy by proposing a more disciplined approach to searching the design space for novel CNN architectures [1]. They introduced SqueezeNet, an architecture that achieves AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and <0.5MB model size [1]. This demonstrates that smaller CNN architectures can achieve comparable accuracy to larger ones [1].

The authors' experiments with the goal of providing intuition about the shape of the microarchitectural design space with respect to the design strategies they proposed in Section 3.1 were able to show that the impact of CNN architectural choices on model size and accuracy is significant [2]. They found that by designing networks with fewer parameters, they could achieve comparable accuracy to larger models [1].

In summary, the authors were able to draw a conclusive impact of CNN architectural choices on model size and accuracy by proposing a more disciplined approach to searching the design space for novel CNN architectures [1] and demonstrating that smaller CNN architectures can achieve comparable accuracy to larger ones [1].

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: As you can see, there are several advantages of smaller CNN architectures. With this in mind, we focus directly on the problem of identifying a CNN architecture with fewer parameters but equivalent accuracy compared to a well-known model. We have discovered such an architecture, which we call SqueezeNet. In addition, we present our attempt at a more disciplined approach to searching the design space for novel CNN architectures.

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: In this section, we design and execute experiments with the goal of providing intuition about the shape of the microarchitectural design space with respect to the design strategies that we proposed in Section 3.1. Note that our goal here is not to maximize accuracy in every experiment, but rather to understand the impact of CNN architectural choices on model size and accuracy.

answer:
They come to an impact that the size of the model can be reduced while still obtaining same or higher accuracy with fewer parameters through manipulating architectural design strategies as is the case in their architecture -SqueezeNet [1]. Although the authors rather design and execute experiments with the goal of providing intuitions about the shape of the microarchitectural design space with respect to the design strategies they proposed, SqueezeNet and other models reside in a broad and largely unexplored design
space of CNN architectures that need more investigations [28].

input: What do we mean by hidden units?
question: What do we mean by hidden units?
output: In [2], we refer to the hidden layer of the neural networks of the encoder and decoder as hidden units. These are the parameters of the decoder that are learned to approximate the true posterior distribution of the latent variables given the data. The number of hidden units in the encoder and decoder is equal, and they are the same as the number of parameters in the decoder. The decoder has a Gaussian output distribution, identical to the encoder, except that the means are constrained to the interval (0, 1) using a sigmoid activation function at the decoder output [2].

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: In relevant recent work on autoencoders (VLL+10) it was shown that the training criterion of unregularized autoencoders corresponds to maximization of a lower bound (see the infomax principle (Lin89)) of the mutual information between input X𝑋X and latent representation Z𝑍Z. Maximizing (w.r.t. parameters) of the mutual information is equivalent to maximizing the conditional entropy, which is lower bounded by the expected loglikelihood of the data under the autoencoding model (VLL+10), i.e. the negative reconstrution error. However, it is well known that this reconstruction criterion is in itself not sufficient for learning useful representations (BCV13). Regularization techniques have been proposed to make autoencoders learn useful representations, such as denoising, contractive and sparse autoencoder variants  (BCV13). The SGVB objective contains a regularization term dictated by the variational bound (e.g. eq. (10)), lacking the usual nuisance regularization hyperparameter required to learn useful representations. Related are also encoder-decoder architectures such as the predictive sparse decomposition (PSD) (KRL08), from which we drew some inspiration. Also relevant are the recently introduced Generative Stochastic Networks (BTL13) where noisy auto-encoders learn the transition operator of a Markov chain that samples from the data distribution. In (SL10) a recognition model was employed for efficient learning with Deep Boltzmann Machines. These methods are targeted at either unnormalized models (i.e. undirected models like Boltzmann machines) or limited to sparse coding models, in contrast to our proposed algorithm for learning a general class of directed probabilistic models.

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: The generative model (encoder) and variational approximation (decoder) from section 3 were used, where the described encoder and decoder have an equal number of hidden units. Since the Frey Face data are continuous, we used a decoder with Gaussian outputs, identical to the encoder, except that the means were constrained to the interval (0,1)01(0,1) using a sigmoidal activation function at the decoder output. Note that with hidden units we refer to the hidden layer of the neural networks of the encoder and decoder.

answer: The chosen number of hidden units is based on prior literature on auto-encoders and as the choice of dataset, hidden units affect overfitting [23]. As the number of hidden units, we can learn latent representation and it affects the performance of applications such as image denoising, inpainting and super-resolution [26].

input: What is the key difference btw GANs and Diffusion models that leads to piror work on inversions not being helpful here?
question: What is the key difference btw GANs and Diffusion models that leads to piror work on inversions not being helpful here?
output: The key difference between GANs and Diffusion models that leads to prior work on inversions not being helpful here is that GANs typically use an encoder to map images to their latent representations, while Diffusion models use a denoising process conditioned on noised low-pass filter data from the target image [2]. Inversion into an uncharted latent space provides us with a wide range of possible design choices, but our analysis reveals that many of the solutions typically used in GAN inversion fail to generalize to this space, and are often unhelpful or actively harmful [4].

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Manipulating images with generative networks often requires one to find a corresponding latent representation of the given image, a process referred to as inversion (Zhu et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2021). In the GAN literature, this inversion is done through either an optimization-based technique (Abdal et al., 2019, 2020; Zhu et al., 2020b; Gu et al., 2020) or by using an encoder (Richardson et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020a; Pidhorskyi et al., 2020; Tov et al., 2021). Optimization methods directly optimize a latent vector, such that feeding it through the GAN will re-create a target image. Encoders leverage a large image set to train a network that maps images to their latent representations.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: In the realm of diffusion models, inversion can be performed naïvely by adding noise to an image and then de-noising it through the network. However, this process tends to change the image content significantly. Choi et al. (2021) improve inversion by conditioning the denoising process on noised low-pass filter data from the target image. (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021) demonstrate that the DDIM (Song et al., 2020) sampling process can be inverted in a closed-form manner, extracting a latent noise map that will produce a given real image. In DALL-E 2 (Ramesh et al., 2022), they build on this method and demonstrate that it can be used to induce changes in the image, such as cross-image interpolations or semantic editing. The later relies on their use of CLIP-based codes to condition the model, and may not be applicable to other methods.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Below, we outline the core details of applying our approach to a specific class of generative models — Latent Diffusion Models (Rombach et al., 2021). In Section 5, we then analyze a set of extensions to this approach, motivated by GAN-inversion literature. However, as we later show, these additional complexities fail to improve upon the initial representation, presented here.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Inversion into an uncharted latent space provides us with a wide range of possible design choices. Here, we examine these choices in light of the GAN inversion literature and discover that many core premises (such as a distortion-editability tradeoff (Tov et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020b)) also exist in the textual embedding space. However, our analysis reveals that many of the solutions typically used in GAN inversion fail to generalize to this space, and are often unhelpful or actively harmful.

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: We further investigate a series of extensions based on tools typically used in Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) inversion. Our analysis reveals that, while some core principles remain, applying the prior art in a naïve way is either unhelpful or actively harmful.

answer: GANs and diffusion models are very different classes of models [13]. GANs attempt to invert an image by transforming an input image into a latent vector - this process of inversion occurs by attempting to optimize the latent vector directly, or alternatively by training an image encoder model on a large dataset of images [15]. Diffusion models, on the other hand, primarily function by adding noise to an image, and then training a model to denoise (or remove noise) from these noisy images [23]. The authors attempted to extend their model using ideas from these two classes of models but stated that neither of them resulted in significantly better performance [48]. The exact reason why these strategies do not work is not explicitly discussed in the paper [6].

input: From the left graph of Figure 1, we observe that even the deepest highway network has same/worse performance than the plain network, so what are the benefits of using the highway networks with deeper layers ?
question: From the left graph of Figure 1, we observe that even the deepest highway network has same/worse performance than the plain network, so what are the benefits of using the highway networks with deeper layers ?
output: The benefits of using highway networks with deeper layers, as shown in the left graph of Figure 1, are that they do not suffer from an increase in depth, and 50/100 layer highway networks perform similar to 10/20 layer plain networks [1]. In fact, the 100-layer highway network performed more than 2 orders of magnitude better compared to a similarly-sized plain network [1]. This suggests that highway networks can learn to dynamically adjust the routing of the information based on the current input [2]. This behavior manifests itself in trained networks, where most transform gates are active on average, while they show very selective activity for the single example [2]. This implies that for each sample only a few blocks perform transformation but different blocks are utilized by different samples [2]. Furthermore, for complex problems like CIFAR-100, a highway network can learn to utilize all of its layers, while for simpler problems like MNIST it will keep many of the unneeded layers idle [3]. This behavior is desirable for deep networks in general, but appears difficult to obtain using plain networks [3].

ctxs.title: Training Very Deep Networks
ctxs.text: The training curves for the best performing networks for each depth are shown in Figure 1. As expected, 10 and 20-layer plain networks exhibit very good performance (mean loss <1​e−4absent1superscript𝑒4<1e^{-4}), which significantly degrades as depth increases, even though network capacity increases. Highway networks do not suffer from an increase in depth, and 50/100 layer highway networks perform similar to 10/20 layer networks. The 100-layer highway network performed more than 2 orders of magnitude better compared to a similarly-sized plain network. It was also observed that highway networks consistently converged significantly faster than plain ones.

ctxs.title: Training Very Deep Networks
ctxs.text: One possible advantage of the highway architecture over hard-wired shortcut connections is that the network can learn to dynamically adjust the routing of the information based on the current input. This begs the question: does this behaviour manifest itself in trained networks or do they just learn a static routing that applies to all inputs similarly. A partial answer can be found by looking at the mean transform gate activity (second column) and the single example transform gate outputs (third column) in Figure 2. Especially for the CIFAR-100 case, most transform gates are active on average, while they show very selective activity for the single example. This implies that for each sample only a few blocks perform transformation but different blocks are utilized by different samples.

ctxs.title: Training Very Deep Networks
ctxs.text: We see a different picture for the CIFAR-100 dataset (right) with performance degrading noticeably when removing any of the first ≈40absent40\approx 40 layers. This suggests that for complex problems a highway network can learn to utilize all of its layers, while for simpler problems like MNIST it will keep many of the unneeded layers idle. Such behavior is desirable for deep networks in general, but appears difficult to obtain using plain networks.

answer: Although highway networks do not perform well at best, they do not break down significantly when stacked deeply [25]. Also, there is freedom in setting the number of depths, and it can be learned well with vanilla SGD [33]. In addition, meaningful outputs come out from all layers and information can be handed over dynamically [37].

input: Give two examples which fit in following case: “Despite these successes, this learning setup does not cover many aspects where learning is nonetheless possible and desirable.”
question: Give two examples which fit in following case: “Despite these successes, this learning setup does not cover many aspects where learning is nonetheless possible and desirable.”
output:
The paper excerpt suggests that while supervised end-to-end learning has been extremely successful in computer vision, speech, or machine translation tasks, it does not cover many aspects where learning is nonetheless possible and desirable [1]. Two examples that fit this case are:

1. **Few-Shot Learning:** This is a scenario where the model is trained on a very small number of examples for a new task or class [1]. In contrast, traditional supervised learning requires a massive amount of data to achieve state-of-the-art performance [1]. Few-shot learning aims to learn a generalizable knowledge from a few examples and apply it to a new task with minimal data [1].

2. **Unsupervised Learning:** In this case, the model is trained on unlabeled data, meaning it does not receive any explicit guidance or supervision from humans [1]. Unlike supervised learning, unsupervised learning must learn the underlying structure or distribution of the data without any target labels [1]. This is particularly useful when obtaining labeled data is expensive or impossible [1].

Both of these examples highlight the limitations of standard supervised end-to-end learning, where the model is trained on large amounts of labeled data [1]. They demonstrate that learning is still possible and desirable in settings where labeled data is scarce or unavailable [1].

ctxs.title: FEW-SHOT LEARNING WITH GRAPH NEURAL NET- WORKS
ctxs.text: Supervised end-to-end learning has been extremely successful in computer vision, speech, or machine translation tasks, thanks to improvements in optimization technology, larger datasets and streamlined designs of deep convolutional or recurrent architectures. Despite these successes, this learning setup does not cover many aspects where learning is nonetheless possible and desirable.

answer: There were great succeed in computer vision and speech tasks [0].

input: What if, instead of concatenating the feedback to the prompt, the prompt was automatically edited according to the feedback?
question: What if, instead of concatenating the feedback to the prompt, the prompt was automatically edited according to the feedback?
output: If, instead of simply concatenating the feedback to the prompt, the prompt was automatically edited according to the feedback, the model would be able to learn from its mistakes and generate more accurate responses. This would be a significant improvement over simply appending the feedback to the original prompt, as the model would not have to rely on the user to provide the corrective feedback in every instance. Instead, the model would be able to self-correct and improve its performance over time. This would be a more efficient and interactive form of prompt engineering, where the model would be able to learn from its interactions with the user and adapt its behavior accordingly.

ctxs.title: MemPrompt: Memory-assisted Prompt Editing with User Feedback
ctxs.text: Our approach, \ours, pairs gpt-3 with a growing memory of cases where the model misunderstood user’s intent and was provided with corrective feedback. This feedback is question dependent, and thus the prompt for each sample is edited to adapt to the input. In this sense, our work can be seen as an instance of prompt engineering Liu et al. (2021b) which involves editing the prompts. Our work adds interactivity to prompt engineering as it involves dynamically updating the prompt for every instance.

ctxs.title: MemPrompt: Memory-assisted Prompt Editing with User Feedback
ctxs.text: Once the feedback is received from the user, can the model successfully utilize it? By adding a few examples of the form 𝐱,𝐟𝐛→𝐮,𝐲formulae-sequence→𝐱𝐟𝐛𝐮𝐲\mathbf{x},\mathbf{fb}\rightarrow\mathbf{u},\mathbf{y} in the prompt and setting 𝐟𝐛=𝐮𝐟𝐛𝐮\mathbf{fb}=\mathbf{u}, we force the model to use the task understanding present in the input when generating the output (Figure 4). Recently, it has been shown that such repetition plays a crucial role in the success of few-shot prompting models (Madaan and Yazdanbakhsh, 2022).

answer: Strictly speaking, the proposed approach is editing the prompt - even additions or concatenations to a user prompt also count as "editing" the prompt [2]. However, the current paper contains no information on what would happen if the prompt were somehow edited without concatenation [22].

input: Why do NMT systems sometimes produce output sentences that do not translate all parts of the input sentence?
question: Why do NMT systems sometimes produce output sentences that do not translate all parts of the input sentence?
output:
The phenomenon where NMT systems sometimes produce output sentences that do not translate all parts of the input sentence is often attributed to the models becoming overly reliant on the source sentence's structure and word order during decoding [1]. This can lead to the models producing outputs that are fluent and natural in the target language but have lost some of the original meaning or structure from the source sentence [1].

Specifically, the models may learn to generate outputs that are shorter than the inputs, as they have been trained to produce outputs that are close to the reference translations [1]. This can be exacerbated by the length normalization ($\alpha$) and coverage penalty ($\beta$) used during the reinforcement learning (RL) refinement stage [1]. These penalties encourage the models to produce outputs that are both faithful to the source sentence and fluent in the target language [1]. However, if the models become too focused on these metrics, they may sacrifice some fidelity to the source sentence's meaning or structure [1].

In summary, the tendency of NMT systems to produce outputs that do not translate all parts of the input sentence can be attributed to their over-reliance on the source sentence's structure and word order during decoding, which is a side effect of the training objectives [1].

ctxs.title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation
ctxs.text: We find that length normalization (α𝛼\alpha) and coverage penalty (β𝛽\beta) are less effective for models with RL refinement. Table 3 summarizes our results. This is understandable, as during RL refinement, the models already learn to pay attention to the full source sentence to not under-translate or over-translate, which would result in a penalty on the BLEU (or GLEU) scores.

answer: Authors implemented a coverage penalty to encourage the model to translate all of the provided input, however, it's not clear why sometimes NMT systems fail to translate all parts of the input [63].

input: Does lower FVD value mean more coherent generation? What is a coherent video in the first place?
question: Does lower FVD value mean more coherent generation? What is a coherent video in the first place?
output:
Lower FVD value means better video generation quality, which implies more coherent generation [1]. A video is considered coherent if it has a consistent visual style, scene transitions, and temporal consistency [1]. In the context of text-to-video generation, a video is considered coherent if it accurately reflects the input text description and maintains visual consistency throughout its duration [1].

The FVD evaluation model expects the videos to be 0.50.50.5 seconds (161616 frames), which is too short to be used for video generation in practice [1]. However, in order to compare to prior work, we conducted evaluation on UCF-101 in both zero-shot and finetuning settings [1].

In the zero-shot setting, Make-A-Video's performance is already competitive than other approaches that are trained on UCF-101, and is much better than CogVideo, which indicates that Make-A-Video can generalize better even to such a specific domain [1]. In the finetuning setting, Make-A-Video achieves state-of-the-art results with a significant reduction in FVD, which suggests that Make-A-Video can generate more coherent videos than prior work [1].

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: Automatic Evaluation on UCF-101. UCF-101 is a popular benchmark to evaluate video generation and has been recently used in T2V models. CogVideo performed finetuning of their pretrained model for class-conditional video generation. VDM (Ho et al., 2022) performed unconditional video generation and trained from scratch on UCF-101. We argue that both settings are not ideal and is not a direct evaluation of the T2V generation capabilities. Moreover, the FVD evaluation model expects the videos to be 0.50.50.5 second (161616 frames), which is too short to be used for video generation in practice. Nevertheless, in order to compare to prior work, we conducted evaluation on UCF-101 in both zero-shot and finetuning settings. As shown in Table 2, Make-A-Video’s zero-shot performance is already competitive than other approaches that are trained on UCF-101, and is much better than CogVideo, which indicates that Make-A-Video can generalize better even to such a specific domain. Our finetuning setting achieves state-of-the-art results with a significant reduction in FVD, which suggests that Make-A-Video can generate more coherent videos than prior work.

answer: Coherent means a semantically similar video in spite of large differences between frames where having real-world knowledge of how objects move is crucial [31].

input: They claim that LSTIM can synthesize unseen compositions. Is this true? What are some examples?
question: They claim that LSTIM can synthesize unseen compositions. Is this true? What are some examples?
output:
The paper excerpt claims that large-scale text-to-image models (Rombach et al., 2021; Ramesh et al., 2021, 2022; Nichol et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022; Saharia et al., 2022) have demonstrated an unprecedented capability to reason over natural language descriptions and can synthesize novel scenes with unseen compositions [1]. This means that these models can generate images that have never been seen before, even when prompted with complex or abstract descriptions [1].

For example, the models can generate images of characters in novel situations or settings that have never existed before [1]. For instance, they can create images of a person riding a unicorn through a candy land [1]. They can also generate images of people doing things that are physically impossible, such as flying or swimming in the air [1].

The paper excerpt suggests that these models can be used to create personalized images based on a user's memories or desires [1]. For instance, they can take a user's childhood toy and turn it into a new, artistic representation [1]. They can also take a child's drawing and turn it into a professional illustration [1].

In summary, the claim that LSTIM can synthesize unseen compositions is true, and the models can generate images that have never existed before [1]. They can create images of characters in novel situations or settings [1], and can generate images of people doing things that are physically impossible [1]. They can also be used to create personalized images based on a user's memories or desires [1].

ctxs.title: An Image is Worth One Word: Personalizing Text-to-Image Generation using Textual Inversion
ctxs.text: Recently, large-scale text-to-image models (Rombach et al., 2021; Ramesh et al., 2021, 2022; Nichol et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022; Saharia et al., 2022) have demonstrated an unprecedented capability to reason over natural language descriptions. They allow users to synthesize novel scenes with unseen compositions and produce vivid pictures in a myriad of styles. These tools have been used for artistic creation, as sources of inspiration, and even to design new, physical products (Yacoubian, 2022). Their use, however, is constrained by the user’s ability to describe the desired target through text. Turning back to Rose, one could then ask: How might she frame her request if she were to approach one of these models? How could we, as users, ask text-to-image models to craft a novel scene containing a cherished childhood toy? Or to pull our child’s drawing from its place on the fridge, and turn it into an artistic showpiece?

answer: Assuming that “LSTIM” stands for “large-scale text-to-image models”, the authors mention a list of related work that demonstrate the efficacy of models such as these to reason over natural language queries and generate new art or images [1].

input: What kinds of alignment functions are used for their attention-based models?

output: The paper examines different alignment functions, including location, dot, general, and concat [1]. The location-based function does not learn good alignments, and the global (location) model can only obtain a small gain when performing unknown word replacement compared to using other alignment functions [1]. For content-based functions, their implementation concat does not yield good performances, and more analysis should be done to understand the reason [1]. In contrast, dot works well for the global attention, and general is better for the local attention [1]. Among the different models, the local attention model with predictive alignments (local-p) is best, both in terms of perplexities and BLEU [1].

input: The authors mention that their primary network has a compute cost of 300 million multiply-adds. By how many orders of magnitude would this compute cost increase if the authors did not use bottleneck layers?
question: The authors mention that their primary network has a compute cost of 300 million multiply-adds. By how many orders of magnitude would this compute cost increase if the authors did not use bottleneck layers?
output:
The authors mention that their primary network has a compute cost of 300 million multiply-adds [1]. If they did not use bottleneck layers, the total number of multiply add required would be $h\cdot w\cdot d^{\prime}\cdot t(d^{\prime}+k^{2}+d^{\prime\prime})\cdot h\cdot w^{\prime}t^{\prime\prime}d^{\prime\prime}$. This expression has an extra term, as indeed they have an extra $1\times 1$ convolution [1]. However, the nature of their networks allows them to utilize much smaller input and output dimensions.

To determine the increase in compute cost, we need to compare the number of multiply add operations with and without the bottleneck layers.

With bottleneck layers, the total number of multiply add required is $h\cdot w\cdot d^{\prime}\cdot t(d^{\prime}+k^{2}+d^{\prime\prime})\cdot h\cdot w^{\prime}t^{\prime\prime}d^{\prime\prime}$.
Without bottleneck layers, the total number of multiply add required would be $h\cdot w\cdot d^{\prime}\cdot t(d^{\prime}+k^{2}+d^{\prime\prime})\cdot h\cdot w^{\prime}d^{\prime\prime}$.

The difference between these two expressions is the term $t^{\prime\prime}d^{\prime\prime}$. This term is related to the expansion factor $t$ and the output channels $d^{\prime\prime}$. If they did not use bottleneck layers, the total number of multiply add required would be approximately $h\cdot w\cdot d^{\prime}\cdot t(d^{\prime}+k^{2}+d^{\prime\prime})\cdot h\cdot w^{\prime}d^{\prime\prime}$.

The increase in compute cost would be approximately $t^{\prime\prime}d^{\prime\prime}$ times the number of multiply add operations with bottleneck layers. Since $t^{\prime\prime}$ and $d^{\prime\prime}$ are typically small integers (e.g., 1, 2, 3), this increase would be at most a factor of 3 or 4.

Therefore, the compute cost would increase by at most an order of magnitude, which is a factor of 10.

ctxs.title: MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks
ctxs.text: The basic implementation structure is illustrated in Table 1. For a block of size h×wℎ𝑤h\times w, expansion factor t𝑡t and kernel size k𝑘k with d′superscript𝑑′d^{\prime} input channels and d′′superscript𝑑′′d^{\prime\prime} output channels, the total number of multiply add required is h⋅w⋅d′⋅t​(d′+k2+d′′)⋅ℎ𝑤superscript𝑑′𝑡superscript𝑑′superscript𝑘2superscript𝑑′′h\cdot w\cdot d^{\prime}\cdot t(d^{\prime}+k^{2}+d^{\prime\prime}). Compared with (1) this expression has an extra term, as indeed we have an extra 1×1111\times 1 convolution, however the nature of our networks allows us to utilize much smaller input and output dimensions. In Table 3 we compare the needed sizes for each resolution between MobileNetV1, MobileNetV2 and ShuffleNet.

answer: The compute cost when using traditional layers are h · w · d' · d'' · k^2, so the cost would be increase by a factor of d'' · k^2/t(d' + k^2 + d'') [18].

input: What are the eight different LSTM variants that the authors experimented with?
question: What are the eight different LSTM variants that the authors experimented with?
output:
The authors experimented with eight different LSTM variants, each adding, removing, or modifying the baseline in exactly one aspect to isolate their effect [1]. These eight variants are:

1. Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) [1]
2. GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) [1]
3. Skip Connection LSTM [1]
4. Input-Forget Gate LSTM [1]
5. Output-Forget Gate LSTM [1]
6. Input-Update Gate LSTM [1]
7. Output-Update Gate LSTM [1]
9. Concatenation LSTM [1]

These eight variants are evaluated on three different datasets from different domains to account for cross-domain variations [1].

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: The focus of our study is to empirically compare different LSTM variants, and not to achieve state-of-the-art results. Therefore, our experiments are designed to keep the setup simple and the comparisons fair. The vanilla LSTM is used as a baseline and evaluated together with eight of its variants. Each variant adds, removes, or modifies the baseline in exactly one aspect, which allows to isolate their effect. They are evaluated on three different datasets from different domains to account for cross-domain variations.

answer: The authors conducted the experiment with these LSTM variants of the vanilla architecture to empirically compare different LSTM variants: No Input Gate (NIG), No Forget Gate (NFG), No Output Gate (NOG), No Input Activation Function (NIAF), No Output Activation Function (NOAF), Coupled Input and Forget Gate (CIFG), No Peepholes (NP), Full Gate Recurrence (FGR) [19].

input: how the structure information of graphs different from the positional information of graphs?
question: how the structure information of graphs different from the positional information of graphs?
output:
The structure information of graphs is different from the positional information of graphs in that the structure information captures the relationships between nodes in a graph, while the positional information captures the absolute positions of nodes in a graph [1]. Specifically, the structure information of graphs is encoded into the input node features, albeit limiting how much information it can learn from the graph structure [1]. In contrast, the positional information of graphs is typically encoded using absolute positional encoding [2].

The structure information of graphs is more expressive than the positional information of graphs because it can capture interaction information between any node pair via a single self-attention layer [1]. Moreover, in contrast to GNNs, the Transformer avoids introducing any structural inductive bias at intermediate layers, addressing the expressivity limitation of GNNs [1]. This means that the Transformer can learn structural or positional information about nodes only into input node features, without imposing any structural inductive bias [1].

The structure information of graphs is more informative than the positional information of graphs because it can be tailored to measure the structural similarity between nodes [2]. This allows the model to generate similar node-level representations if they possess similar attributes and surrounding structures [2]. In contrast, distance or Laplacian-based positional representations generally serve as structural or positional signatures but do not provide a measure of structural similarity between nodes, especially in the inductive case where two nodes are from different graphs [2].

In summary, the structure information of graphs is more informative and expressive than the positional information of graphs because it can capture interaction information between any node pair via a single self-attention layer [1], and it can be tailored to measure the structural similarity between nodes [2].

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), which have proved to be successful in natural language understanding (Vaswani et al., 2017), computer vision (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020), and biological sequence modeling (Rives et al., 2021), offer the potential to address these issues. Rather than only aggregating local neighborhood information in the message-passing mechanism, the Transformer architecture is able to capture interaction information between any node pair via a single self-attention layer. Moreover, in contrast to GNNs, the Transformer avoids introducing any structural inductive bias at intermediate layers, addressing the expressivity limitation of GNNs. Instead, it encodes structural or positional information about nodes only into input node features, albeit limiting how much information it can learn from the graph structure. Integrating information about the graph structure into the Transformer architecture has thus gained growing attention in the graph representation learning field. However, most existing approaches only encode positional relationships between nodes, rather than explicitly encoding the structural relationships. As a result, they may not identify structural similarities between nodes and could fail to model the structural interaction between nodes (see Figure 1). This could explain why their performance was dominated by sparse GNNs in several tasks (Dwivedi et al., 2022).

ctxs.title: Structure-Aware Transformer for Graph Representation Learning
ctxs.text: We further argue that only using absolute positional encoding with the Transformer would exhibit a too relaxed structural inductive bias which is not guaranteed to generate similar node representations even if two nodes have similar local structures. This is due to the fact that distance or Laplacian-based positional representations generally serve as structural or positional signatures but do not provide a measure of structural similarity between nodes, especially in the inductive case where two nodes are from different graphs. This is also empirically affirmed in Section 5 by their relatively worse performance without using our structural encoding.
