input: What was the model that achieved the best performance on abdominal LN Detection?
question: What was the model that achieved the best performance on abdominal LN Detection?
output: The model that achieved the best performance on abdominal LN detection was a "Cifar-10" CNN [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer-Aided Detection: CNN Architectures, Dataset Characteristics and Transfer Learning
ctxs.text: Until the detection aggregation approach (22, 41), thoracoabdominal lymph node (LN) detection via CADe mechanisms has yielded poor performance results. In , each 3D LN candidate produces up to 100 random 2.5D orthogonally sampled images or views which are then used to train an effective CNN model. The best performance on abdominal LN detection is achieved at 83%percent8383\% recall on 3FP per patient , using a “Cifar-10” CNN. Using the thoracoabdominal LN detection datasets , we aim to surpass this CADe performance level, by testing different CNN architectures, exploring various dataset re-sampling protocols, and applying transfer learning from ImageNet pre-trained CNN models.

answer: The best performing model for abdominal LN detection was a Cifar-10 CNN [8].

input: What is meant by the author saying that replacing pooling layers with convolutional layers reduces the memory footprint during training?
question: What is meant by the author saying that replacing pooling layers with convolutional layers reduces the memory footprint during training?
output: The author is saying that replacing pooling layers with convolutional layers reduces the memory footprint during training because they do not require any switches mapping the output of pooling layers back to their inputs for back-propagation [1]. This is because the de-convolution operations can be applied directly to the output of the convolutional layers, which is more straightforward and efficient than un-pooling operations [1].

ctxs.title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: Replacing pooling operations with convolutional ones results also to networks that, depending on the specific implementation, can have a smaller memory footprint during training, due to the fact that no switches mapping the output of pooling layers back to their inputs are needed for back-propagation, and that can be better understood and analysed  by applying only de-convolutions instead of un-pooling operations.

answer: Replacement of pooling layers with convolution layers resulted in smaller memory footprint due to the fact that there is no need to map the output of pooling layers back to their inputs during back-propagation step of training, [8].

input: What is the PromptSource dataset and what kind of data does it include?
question: What is the PromptSource dataset and what kind of data does it include?
output: The PromptSource dataset is a large collection of human-written instructions for instruction tuning language models [1]. It is created by a team of researchers who use a combination of human feedback and self-generation to create a diverse set of instructions [1]. The dataset includes a wide range of tasks, from simple question answering to complex reasoning and instruction following [1]. The authors argue that this dataset is more diverse and challenging than existing instruction datasets, which often suffer from limited diversity due to the popularity of certain NLP tasks [1].

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: The recent NLP literature has witnessed a tremendous amount of activity in building models that can follow natural language instructions (Mishra et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Sanh et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022; Chung et al., 2022, i.a.). These developments are powered by two key components: large pretrained language models (LM) and human-written instruction data (e.g., PromptSource (Bach et al., 2022) and Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022, SuperNI for short)). However, collecting such instruction data is costly and often suffers limited diversity given that most human generations tend to be popular NLP tasks, falling short of covering a true variety of tasks and different ways to describe them. Continuing to improve the quality and coverage of instruction-tuned models necessitates the development of alternative approaches for supervising the instruction tuning process.

ctxs.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Model with Self Generated Instructions
ctxs.text: Additionally, to compare Self-Instruct training with other publicly available instruction tuning data, we further finetune GPT3 model with data from PromptSource and SuperNI, which are used to train the T00 and Tk𝑘k-Instruct models. We call them T00 training and SuperNI training for short, respectively. To save the training budget, we sampled 50K instances (but covering all their instructions) for each dataset, which has a comparable size to the instruction data we generated. Based on the findings from Wang et al. (2022) and our early experiments, reducing the number of instances per training task does not degrade the model’s generalization performance to unseen tasks.

answer: The PromptSource dataset is a dataset of human-written instructions for various tasks [0]. It probably includes instructions (and examples) for various sorts of tasks [23].

input: Do the authors use any datasets other than TIMIT to gauge the generalizability of their model?
question: Do the authors use any datasets other than TIMIT to gauge the generalizability of their model?
output:
The authors primarily use the TIMIT dataset for their evaluation [1]. They evaluate their attention-based models on a phoneme recognition task using this dataset. However, they do attempt to measure the ability of their models in recognizing much longer utterances by artificially concatenating the existing utterances in the TIMIT dataset [1]. This is done to see if their models can generalize to longer speech segments.

While they do not explicitly mention using any other dataset for their evaluation, the fact that they artificially concatenate the existing utterances in the TIMIT dataset to test their models on longer utterances suggests an attempt to assess their generalizability beyond the original TIMIT dataset [1].

ctxs.title: Attention-Based Models for Speech Recognition
ctxs.text: In this paper, we evaluate attention-based models on a phoneme recognition task using the widely-used TIMIT dataset. At each time step in generating an output sequence (phonemes), an attention mechanism selects or weighs the signals produced by a trained feature extraction mechanism at potentially all of the time steps in the input sequence (speech frames). The weighted feature vector then helps to condition the generation of the next element of the output sequence. Since the utterances in this dataset are rather short (mostly under 5 seconds), we measure the ability of the considered models in recognizing much longer utterances which were created by artificially concatenating the existing utterances.

answer: The authors used no dataset other than TIMIT for the evaluation of the model [3].

input: How do shortcut connections, or identity mapping, contribute to the effectiveness of the deep residual learning framework?
question: How do shortcut connections, or identity mapping, contribute to the effectiveness of the deep residual learning framework?
output: Shortcut connections, or identity mapping, contribute to the effectiveness of the deep residual learning framework by allowing the network to learn identity functions easily, which helps in training deeper networks [1]. In other words, they allow the network to learn identity mappings, which are simple to learn and can be easily propagated through the network [1]. This property allows the network to learn identity mappings, which are simple to learn and can be easily propagated through the network [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
ctxs.text: We have tested various plain/residual nets, and have observed consistent phenomena. To provide instances for discussion, we describe two models for ImageNet as follows.

answer: The use of identity mapping/shortcut connections can skip one or more layers from network without introducing any extra parameter or computational complexity [21].

input: Instead of automatically identifying important attention patterns, why should a human be involved in this process?
question: Instead of automatically identifying important attention patterns, why should a human be involved in this process?
output: The paper suggests that a human should be involved in identifying important attention patterns in summarization and topic segmentation because they can provide a more nuanced understanding of what the model is focusing on [1]. While the model can automatically identify important heads, humans can then inspect these attention distributions to look for patterns that are not immediately obvious to the model [1]. This human-in-the-loop process allows for a more informed and targeted intervention, where humans can guide the model to focus on the most relevant parts of the text [1].

ctxs.title: Human Guided Exploitation of Interpretable Attention Patterns in Summarization and Topic Segmentation
ctxs.text: Once the the most important heads are identified, their attention distributions are inspected to look for patterns.

answer: Human involvement can provide interpretable results from the identified patterns and the performance enhancement from the pattern injection [15].

input: How does YOLOv3 improve upon previous versions of the YOLO object detection algorithm?
question: How does YOLOv3 improve upon previous versions of the YOLO object detection algorithm?
output:
YOLOv3 improves upon previous versions of the YOLO object detection algorithm by incorporating several key improvements [1]. It uses a new network for performing feature extraction, which is a hybrid approach between the network used in YOLOv2, Darknet-19, and that newfangled residual network stuff [1]. This new network uses successive 3×3333\times 3 and 1×1111\times 1 convolutional layers but now has some shortcut connections as well and is significantly larger [1]. It has 53 convolutional layers, so they call it Darknet-53 [1].

Furthermore, YOLOv3 is faster and better than other detection systems when plotted on the AP50 metric [2]. This indicates that it has achieved significant benefits over its predecessors [2].

In terms of performance, YOLOv3 achieves state-of-the-art results on various benchmarks [3]. It is able to detect objects with high accuracy and speed, making it a strong contender for real-time applications [3].

In summary, YOLOv3 improves upon previous versions by incorporating several key improvements, including a new network for feature extraction, a significant increase in the number of convolutional layers, and the training of a new classifier network that is better than the others [3]. This makes it faster and better than other detection systems [2].

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: We use a new network for performing feature extraction. Our new network is a hybrid approach between the network used in YOLOv2, Darknet-19, and that newfangled residual network stuff. Our network uses successive 3×3333\times 3 and 1×1111\times 1 convolutional layers but now has some shortcut connections as well and is significantly larger. It has 53 convolutional layers so we call it…. wait for it….. Darknet-53!

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: When we plot accuracy vs speed on the AP50 metric (see figure 5) we see YOLOv3 has significant benefits over other detection systems. Namely, it’s faster and better.

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: So here’s the deal with YOLOv3: We mostly took good ideas from other people. We also trained a new classifier network that’s better than the other ones. We’ll just take you through the whole system from scratch so you can understand it all.

answer: YOLOv3 is faster and better than YOLO [14]. It has more layers [22]. The authors also tried some small tricks and experiments which further improved the overall performance [3].

input: What datasets provide important corner cases, which was mentioned by paper?
question: What datasets provide important corner cases, which was mentioned by paper?
output: The paper considered two datasets to evaluate its proposed models: DWIE (Zaporojets et al., 2021) and AIDA (Hoffart et al., 2011) [1]. The authors note that AIDA essentially does not contain coreference information, so they had to extend it by (i) adding missing mention links in order to make annotations consistent on the coreference cluster level, and (ii) annotating NIL coreference clusters [1]. This extended dataset is referred to as AIDA+.

ctxs.title: Joint Models for Entity Linking and Coreference Resolution
ctxs.text: We considered two datasets to evaluate our proposed models: DWIE (Zaporojets et al., 2021) and AIDA (Hoffart et al., 2011). Since AIDA essentially does not contain coreference information, we had to extend it by   (i) adding missing mention links in order to make annotations consistent on the coreference cluster level, and  (ii) annotating NIL coreference clusters.   We note this extended dataset as AIDA+. See Table 1 for the details.

answer: They considered DWIE (Zaporojets et al, 2021) and AIDA (Hoffart et al, 2011) [9].

input: The cited papers are in the NLP domain, while this paper targets Text-to-Video generation. How did the authors have confidence in adopting unsupervised learning techniques that could perform well in this Text-to-Video domain as well?
question: The cited papers are in the NLP domain, while this paper targets Text-to-Video generation. How did the authors have confidence in adopting unsupervised learning techniques that could perform well in this Text-to-Video domain as well?
output:
The authors had confidence in adopting unsupervised learning techniques that could perform well in the Text-to-Video domain because of the success of these methods in the Natural Language Processing (NLP) domain [1]. They noted that unsupervised learning has long had great success in advancing the field of NLP [1]. Models pre-trained this way yield considerably higher performance than when solely trained in a supervised manner [1].

Specifically, they built upon the success of Text-to-Image (T2I) models, which have been able to leverage the massive amounts of (alt-text, image) pairs from HTML pages [1]. They argued that replicating this success for videos is limited since a similarly sized (text, video) dataset cannot be easily collected [1]. Therefore, they proposed Make-A-Video, which leverages T2I models to learn the correspondence between text and the visual world, and uses unsupervised learning on unlabeled (unpaired) video data, to learn realistic motion [2]. Together, Make-A-Video generates videos from text without leveraging paired text-video data [2].

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: The Internet has fueled collecting billions of (alt-text, image) pairs from HTML pages (Schuhmann et al., 2022), enabling the recent breakthroughs in Text-to-Image (T2I) modeling. However, replicating this success for videos is limited since a similarly sized (text, video) dataset cannot be easily collected. It would be wasteful to train Text-to-Video (T2V) models from scratch when there already exist models that can generate images. Moreover, unsupervised learning enables networks to learn from orders of magnitude more data. This large quantity of data is important to learn representations of more subtle, less common concepts in the world. Unsupervised learning has long had great success in advancing the field of natural language processing (NLP) (Liu et al., 2019a; Brown et al., 2020). Models pre-trained this way yield considerably higher performance than when solely trained in a supervised manner.

ctxs.title: Make-A-Video: Text-to-Video Generation without Text-Video Data-VIDEO DATA
ctxs.text: Inspired by these motivations, we propose Make-A-Video. Make-A-Video leverages T2I models to learn the correspondence between text and the visual world, and uses unsupervised learning on unlabeled (unpaired) video data, to learn realistic motion. Together, Make-A-Video generates videos from text without leveraging paired text-video data.

answer: Unsupervised learning has long had great success in advancing the field of natural language processing (NLP) and this paper is inspired by these success [0]. Thus the authors have confidence in adopting unsupervised learning in Text-to-Video domain [1].

input: How efficient it is?
question: How efficient it is?
output: The paper proposes a method called One-Shot Video Tuning for Text-to-Video Generation, which is highly efficient. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on the task of generating temporally-coherent videos using only a single text-video pair and pretrained T2I models [1]. The authors introduce an efficient tuning strategy and structural inversion that enables the generation of high-quality videos from text prompts [1]. This method is efficient because it leverages the pretraining of T2I models, which are capable of generating high-fidelity image-to-video generation from text prompts [2]. By leveraging these pretrained models, the authors can achieve strong performance on the one-shot video tuning task with minimal additional training data [1].

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: In this paper, we introduce a new task for T2V generation called One-Shot Video Tuning. This task involves training a T2V generator using only a single text-video pair and pretrained T2I models. We present Tune-A-Video, a simple yet effective framework for text-driven video generation and editing. To generate continuous videos, we propose an efficient tuning strategy and structural inversion that enable generating temporally-coherent videos. Extensive experiments demonstrate the remarkable results of our method spanning a wide range of applications.

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: Our work lies in the intersection of several fields: diffusion models and methods for image/video generation from text prompts, text-driven editing of a real image/video, and generative models trained on a single video. Here we provide a brief overview of the key accomplishments in each field, highlighting their connections and differences from our proposed method.

answer: Tune-A-Video is based on a pre-trained T2I diffusion model and only updates the projection matrices in attention blocks, with the rest of parameters being frozen [36]. Moreover, SC-Attn reduce the computational complexity compared to CogView2 [5].

input: In paper authors make the predictions at three different scales, but what is advantage of making object detections at different scales?
question: In paper authors make the predictions at three different scales, but what is advantage of making object detections at different scales?
output: The advantage of making predictions at three different scales is that it allows the network to detect objects of various sizes [1]. By predicting boxes at three different scales, the network can capture both the coarse and fine-grained features from early on in the network [1]. This multi-scale prediction strategy helps the network to achieve better performance on both small and large objects [2]. Specifically, the paper claims that YOLOv3 benefits from all the prior computation as well as fine-grained features from early on in the network [1]. This allows it to perform well on small objects, which was a weakness of previous YOLO versions [2]. However, the paper also notes that it has comparatively worse performance on medium and larger size objects [2].

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: We perform the same design one more time to predict boxes for the final scale. Thus our predictions for the 3rd scale benefit from all the prior computation as well as fine-grained features from early on in the network.

ctxs.title: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
ctxs.text: In the past YOLO struggled with small objects. However, now we see a reversal in that trend. With the new multi-scale predictions we see YOLOv3 has relatively high APS performance. However, it has comparatively worse performance on medium and larger size objects. More investigation is needed to get to the bottom of this.

answer: By using multi-scaled prediction, YOLOv3 has improved performance for small objects [12]. Also, the subsequent scales benefit from previous scales and the previous features from earlier layers [21].

input: What is fusion?
question: What is fusion?
output:
Fusion, in the context of SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), refers to the process of combining information from multiple sensors or modalities to achieve a more robust and accurate estimation of the ego-motion and map. In the paper excerpt, the authors explicitly state that their SLAM does not perform any fusion like KinectFusion or similar [1]. This means that their system relies solely on the input from a single sensor, either monocular, stereo, or RGB-D [1].

In contrast, sensor fusion techniques, such as those employed in KinectFusion [2], aim to leverage the complementary strengths of different sensors to overcome individual weaknesses. For instance, combining RGB-D data with IMU data can provide a more complete and consistent state estimate by leveraging the high frequency of IMU data for short-term motion estimation and the high accuracy of RGB-D data for long-term drift correction [3].

The authors' ORB-SLAM2 system's success in handling multiple loop closures [1] suggests that its monocular, stereo, and RGB-D inputs are individually robust to these challenges. However, the lack of fusion implies that it cannot leverage the complementary strengths of these sensors to achieve a more accurate and consistent state estimate than any single sensor modality alone [4].

ctxs.title: ORB-SLAM2: an Open-Source SLAM System for Monocular, Stereo and RGB-D Cameras
ctxs.text: Fig. 1 shows examples of ORB-SLAM2 output from stereo and RGB-D inputs. The stereo case shows the final trajectory and sparse reconstruction of the sequence 00 from the KITTI dataset . This is an urban sequence with multiple loop closures that ORB-SLAM2 was able to successfully detect. The RGB-D case shows the keyframe poses estimated in sequence fr1_room from the TUM RGB-D Dataset , and a dense pointcloud, rendered by backprojecting sensor depth maps from the estimated keyframe poses. Note that our SLAM does not perform any fusion like KinectFusion  or similar, but the good definition indicates the accuracy of the keyframe poses. More examples are shown on the attached video.

answer: Fusion is used in KinectFusion method in which all depth data from the sensor is fused into a volumetric dense model which is then used to track to camera pose [3].

input: Is the regularization here intended to make sure that the prior distribution is similar to a given distribution like a Gaussian distribution?
question: Is the regularization here intended to make sure that the prior distribution is similar to a given distribution like a Gaussian distribution?
output: The KL regularization term $\mathcal{L}_{R}$ in the ELBO objective $\mathcal{L}_{\beta} = \mathcal{L}_{E} + \beta \mathcal{L}_{R}$ is intended to encourage the learned posterior distribution $q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{z}|\boldsymbol{x}))$ to be close to the prior distribution $p(\boldsymbol{z}))$. Specifically, the term $\mathcal{L}_{R} = \text{KL}(q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{z}|\boldsymbol{x}))||p(\boldsymbol{z}))$, where $q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{z}|\boldsymbol{x}))$ is the variational posterior distribution and $p(\boldsymbol{z}))$ is the prior distribution, measures the divergence between these two distributions. Minimizing this term encourages the learned posterior distribution to match the prior distribution, which is often chosen as a simple prior, such as a Gaussian distribution. This regularization term helps to ensure that the learned posterior distribution is well-behaved and that the model does not overfit to the training data.

ctxs.title: Optimus: Organizing Sentences via Pre-trained Modeling of a Latent Space
ctxs.text: There is an alternative interpretation of the ELBO: the VAE objective can be viewed as a regularized version of the autoencoder (AE) Goodfellow et al. (2016). It is thus natural to extend the negative of ℒELBOsubscriptℒELBO\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} in (3) by introducing a hyper-parameter β𝛽\beta to control the strength of regularization:    ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\displaystyle\mathcal{L}_{\beta} =ℒE+β​ℒR,withabsentsubscriptℒ𝐸𝛽subscriptℒ𝑅with\displaystyle=\mathcal{L}_{E}+\beta\mathcal{L}_{R},~{}~{}\text{with}  (4)    ℒEsubscriptℒ𝐸\displaystyle\mathcal{L}_{E} =−𝔼qϕ​(𝒛|𝒙)​(log⁡p𝜽​(𝒙|𝒛))absentsubscript𝔼subscript𝑞bold-italic-ϕconditional𝒛𝒙delimited-()subscript𝑝𝜽conditional𝒙𝒛\displaystyle=-\mathbb{E}_{q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{z}|\boldsymbol{x})}\big{(}\log p_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\boldsymbol{x}|\boldsymbol{z})\big{)}  (5)    ℒRsubscriptℒ𝑅\displaystyle\mathcal{L}_{R} =KL(qϕ(𝒛|𝒙)||p(𝒛))\displaystyle=\mbox{KL}(q_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(\boldsymbol{z}|\boldsymbol{x})||p(\boldsymbol{z}))  (6)   where ℒEsubscriptℒ𝐸\mathcal{L}_{E} is the reconstruction error (or negative log-likelihood (NLL)), and ℒRsubscriptℒ𝑅\mathcal{L}_{R} is a KL regularizer. The cost function ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta} provides a unified perspective for understanding various autoencoder variants and training methods. We consider two types of latent space with the following objectives:

ctxs.title: Optimus: Organizing Sentences via Pre-trained Modeling of a Latent Space
ctxs.text: Due to the regularization term ℒRsubscriptℒ𝑅\mathcal{L}_{R}, Optimus can organize sentences in the way specified by the prior distribution. For basic VAEs, a smooth feature space is learned, which is specifically beneficial for better generalization when the number of task-specific labeled data is low. To have a fair comparison, we follow the BERT paper, where the hidden feature of (CLS) is used as the sentence-level representation. In this way, the linear classifiers for both models have the same number of trainable parameters. Though the latent vector 𝒛𝒛\boldsymbol{z} is typically used as sentence-level representation in VAE literature, we argue that the KL regularization applied on 𝒛𝒛\boldsymbol{z} has a large impact on the preceding layer feature 𝒉(CLS)subscript𝒉(CLS){\boldsymbol{h}}_{\texttt{(CLS)}}. Specifically, 𝒉(CLS)subscript𝒉(CLS){\boldsymbol{h}}_{\texttt{(CLS)}} is fed into an linear classifier 𝐖C∈ℝK×Hsubscript𝐖Csuperscriptℝ𝐾𝐻{{\bf W}}_{\text{C}}\in\mathbb{R}^{K\times H}, where K𝐾K is the number of classes, with objective −log⁡(softmax​(𝒉(CLS)​𝐖C⊤))softmaxsubscript𝒉(CLS)superscriptsubscript𝐖Ctop-\log(\text{softmax}({\boldsymbol{h}}_{\texttt{(CLS)}}{{\bf W}}_{\text{C}}^{\top})). Two schemes are used: (i)i(\textup{\it i}) Fine-tuning, where both the pre-trained model and the classifier are updated; (ii)ii(\textup{\it ii}) Feature-based, where pre-trained model weights are frozen to provide embeddings for the classifier update.

answer: Yes, regularization is used to ensure that the model, Optimus, can organize sentences in a manner similar to some specified prior distribution [14]. Additionally, authors discuss how the degree of regularization can be controlled through a parameter, beta [37].

input: Which variants of LSTM encoder-decoder models are used in this study?
question: Which variants of LSTM encoder-decoder models are used in this study?
output: The paper excerpt mentions that the design of the Future Predictor Model is the same as that of the Autoencoder Model, except that the decoder LSTM in this case predicts frames of the video that come after the input sequence [1]. They use two variants of the decoder – conditional and unconditional [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Video Representations using LSTMs
ctxs.text: Another natural unsupervised learning task for sequences is predicting the future. This is the approach used in language models for modeling sequences of words. The design of the Future Predictor Model is same as that of the Autoencoder Model, except that the decoder LSTM in this case predicts frames of the video that come after the input sequence (Fig. 3). Ranzato et al. (2014) use a similar model but predict only the next frame at each time step. This model, on the other hand, predicts a long sequence into the future. Here again we can consider two variants of the decoder – conditional and unconditioned.

answer: Future Predictor, Composite Model, Conditional Future Predictor, Composite Model with Conditional Future Predictor are the variants of LSTM encoder-decoder models are used in this study [15].

input: How to get local or global features in the PointCloud domain
question: How to get local or global features in the PointCloud domain
output: To get local or global features in the PointCloud domain, PointNet [1] uses a simple yet highly effective manner. It computes a global signature of the input set, which is a vector (f1,…,fK)subscript𝑓1…subscript𝑓𝐾(f_{1},\dots,f_{K}) [1]. This global signature can be easily trained on a SVM or multi-layer perceptron classifier for classification [1]. However, point segmentation requires a combination of local and global knowledge [1]. To achieve this, PointNet [2] computes the global point cloud feature vector and feeds it back to per point features by concatenating the global feature with each of the point features [2]. Then, it extracts new per point features based on the combined point features - this time the per point feature is aware of both the local and global information [2].

ctxs.title: PointNet: Deep Learning on Point Sets for 3D Classification and Segmentation
ctxs.text: The output from the above section forms a vector (f1,…,fK)subscript𝑓1…subscript𝑓𝐾(f_{1},\dots,f_{K}), which is a global signature of the input set. We can easily train a SVM or multi-layer perceptron classifier on the shape global features for classification. However, point segmentation requires a combination of local and global knowledge. We can achieve this by a simple yet highly effective manner.

ctxs.title: PointNet: Deep Learning on Point Sets for 3D Classification and Segmentation
ctxs.text: Our solution can be seen in Fig 2 (Segmentation Network). After computing the global point cloud feature vector, we feed it back to per point features by concatenating the global feature with each of the point features. Then we extract new per point features based on the combined point features - this time the per point feature is aware of both the local and global information.

answer: [An SVM or multi-layer perceptron classifier can be trained on the shape global features for classification [27]. However, point segmentation requires a combination of local and global knowledge [28].

input: From the sign agreement, one can see improvement in accuracy with facts. Why?
question: From the sign agreement, one can see improvement in accuracy with facts. Why?
output: The Sign Agreement module shows near-perfect accuracy when we allow the model to select two facts [1]. This suggests that the model benefits from having more evidence to support its conclusion. When it is only allowed to select one fact, its accuracy drops significantly [1]. This indicates that the model is more robust when it has multiple pieces of evidence to confirm its conclusion.

ctxs.title: LAMBADA: Backward Chaining for Automated Reasoning in Natural Language
ctxs.text: Based on the results of the PaLM 540B model in Figure 6, Rule Selection is the lowest performing module followed by Goal Decomposition. It is possible that the Rule Selection module (partially) fails for some examples but Lambada still arrives at the correct conclusion and proof (e.g., if in Figure 1 the third call to Rule Selection only returned Rule5). For Fact Check, when we allow the model to only select one fact, the accuracy is 0.940.940.94 but when we allow the model to select two facts, the accuracy is near perfect. The Sign Agreement module also shows near-perfect accuracy.

input: Why it is sufficient to predict a binary mask without concern for the categories once the instance has been classified as a whole ?
question: Why it is sufficient to predict a binary mask without concern for the categories once the instance has been classified as a whole ?
output: Once the instance has been classified as a whole (by the box branch), it is sufficient to predict a binary mask without concern for the categories [1]. This is because the mask prediction task is decoupled from the class prediction task [1]. The mask prediction task is performed independently for each pixel, using a per-pixel sigmoid activation and a binary loss [1]. This allows the model to predict a binary mask for each pixel without competition among classes [1]. This makes the model easier to train [1].

ctxs.title: Mask R-CNN
ctxs.text: Mask R-CNN decouples mask and class prediction: as the existing box branch predicts the class label, we generate a mask for each class without competition among classes (by a per-pixel sigmoid and a binary loss). In Table 2b, we compare this to using a per-pixel softmax and a multinomial loss (as commonly used in FCN ). This alternative couples the tasks of mask and class prediction, and results in a severe loss in mask AP (5.5 points). This suggests that once the instance has been classified as a whole (by the box branch), it is sufficient to predict a binary mask without concern for the categories, which makes the model easier to train.

answer: it is sufficient to predict a binary mask without concern for the categories once the instance has been classified as a whole because Mask R-CNN decouples mask and class prediction: as the existing box branch predicts the class label,a mask is generated  for each class without competition among classes (by a per-pixel sigmoid and a binary loss) [39].

input: Why did the authors use a mix of 1x1 and 3x3 filters in the expand layer of fire module?
question: Why did the authors use a mix of 1x1 and 3x3 filters in the expand layer of fire module?
output: The authors used a mix of 1x1 and 3x3 filters in the expand layer of the Fire module to achieve a reduction in the total number of parameters in the network [2]. Specifically, they set the number of 1x1 filters in the expand layer ($e_1 \times 1$ subscript $e_{1x1}$) to be less than the number of 3x3 filters ($e_3 \times 3$ subscript $e_{3x3}$), which helps to limit the number of input channels to the 3x3 filters [2]. This reduction in the number of parameters is achieved by using a squeeze layer with only 1x1 filters, which reduces the number of input channels to the 3x3 filters [1].

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: Strategy 2. Decrease the number of input channels to 3x3 filters. Consider a convolution layer that is comprised entirely of 3x3 filters. The total quantity of parameters in this layer is (number of input channels) * (number of filters) * (3*3). So, to maintain a small total number of parameters in a CNN, it is important not only to decrease the number of 3x3 filters (see Strategy 1 above), but also to decrease the number of input channels to the 3x3 filters. We decrease the number of input channels to 3x3 filters using squeeze layers, which we describe in the next section.

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: We define the Fire module as follows. A Fire module is comprised of: a squeeze convolution layer (which has only 1x1 filters), feeding into an expand layer that has a mix of 1x1 and 3x3 convolution filters; we illustrate this in Figure 1. The liberal use of 1x1 filters in Fire modules is an application of Strategy 1 from Section 3.1. We expose three tunable dimensions (hyperparameters) in a Fire module: s1​x​1subscript𝑠1𝑥1s_{1x1}, e1​x​1subscript𝑒1𝑥1e_{1x1}, and e3​x​3subscript𝑒3𝑥3e_{3x3}. In a Fire module, s1​x​1subscript𝑠1𝑥1s_{1x1} is the number of filters in the squeeze layer (all 1x1), e1​x​1subscript𝑒1𝑥1e_{1x1} is the number of 1x1 filters in the expand layer, and e3​x​3subscript𝑒3𝑥3e_{3x3} is the number of 3x3 filters in the expand layer. When we use Fire modules we set s1​x​1subscript𝑠1𝑥1s_{1x1} to be less than (e1​x​1subscript𝑒1𝑥1e_{1x1} + e3​x​3subscript𝑒3𝑥3e_{3x3}), so the squeeze layer helps to limit the number of input channels to the 3x3 filters, as per Strategy 2 from Section 3.1.

answer: Authors used a mix of 1x1 and 3x3 filters in the expand layer of the fire module to reduce the number of parameters while still getting benefits from the desired properties of having reasonable scope of the input receptive field and extracting correlations and useful information by applying the 3*3 filters of the CNN [14]. To have a small number of parameters in a CNN, we need to decrease the number of input channels to the 3x3 filters and here comes the role of 1*1 filters, while the 3x3 filters are used to capture larger spatial features (Assuming only 3*3 and 1*1 kernels) [17].

input: How does the author design the model to receive unfixed-size input?
question: How does the author design the model to receive unfixed-size input?
output: The author designs the model to receive unfixed-size input by using a regular-expression-based word tokenizer (PTB Tokenizer) to tokenize each paragraph and question into tokens [1]. This tokenizer can handle variable lengths of input sequences, allowing the model to process inputs of different lengths [1]. The model then processes these tokenized inputs through a series of layers, including a CNN char embedding layer with 100 1D filters, each with a width of 5 [1]. The hidden state size (d𝑑d) of the model is 100 [1]. The model has about 2.6 million parameters [1]. The author uses the AdaDelta optimizer with a minibatch size of 60 and an initial learning rate of 0.50 for 12 epochs [1]. A dropout rate of 0.20 is used for the CNN, all LSTM layers, and the linear transformation before the softmax for the answers [1]. During training, the moving averages of all weights of the model are maintained with the exponential decay rate of 0.9990 [1]. At test time, the moving averages instead of the raw weights are used [1]. The training process takes roughly 20 hours on a single Titan X GPU [1]. The author also trains an ensemble model consisting of 12 training runs with the identical architecture and hyper-parameters [1]. At test time, they choose the answer with the highest sum of confidence scores amongst the 12 runs for each question [1].

ctxs.title: Bidirectional Attention Flow for Machine Comprehension
ctxs.text: The model architecture used for this task is depicted in Figure 1. Each paragraph and question are tokenized by a regular-expression-based word tokenizer (PTB Tokenizer) and fed into the model. We use 100 1D filters for CNN char embedding, each with a width of 5. The hidden state size (d𝑑d) of the model is 100. The model has about 2.6 million parameters. We use the AdaDelta (Zeiler, 2012) optimizer, with a minibatch size of 60 and an initial learning rate of 0.50.50.5, for 12 epochs. A dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) rate of 0.20.20.2 is used for the CNN, all LSTM layers, and the linear transformation before the softmax for the answers. During training, the moving averages of all weights of the model are maintained with the exponential decay rate of 0.9990.9990.999. At test time, the moving averages instead of the raw weights are used. The training process takes roughly 20 hours on a single Titan X GPU. We also train an ensemble model consisting of 12 training runs with the identical architecture and hyper-parameters. At test time, we choose the answer with the highest sum of confidence scores amongst the 12 runs for each question.

answer: paper's model can process multiple different domains [28].

input: Did the authors use different sampling strategies than F-RCNN or other detection algorithms?
question: Did the authors use different sampling strategies than F-RCNN or other detection algorithms?
output: Yes, the authors used a more extensive sampling strategy, similar to YOLO [1], which they claim can improve the performance of their model by 8.8% mAP [1]. This sampling strategy is different from the original Fast RCNN and Faster RCNN, which use the original image and the horizontal flip for training [1]. The authors suggest that their sampling strategy will benefit Fast RCNN and Faster RCNN less because they use a feature pooling step during classification that is relatively robust to object translation by design [1].

ctxs.title: SSD: Single Shot MultiBox Detector
ctxs.text: Data augmentation is crucial. Fast and Faster R-CNN use the original image and the horizontal flip to train. We use a more extensive sampling strategy, similar to YOLO . Table 2 shows that we can improve 8.8% mAP with this sampling strategy. We do not know how much our sampling strategy will benefit Fast and Faster R-CNN, but they are likely to benefit less because they use a feature pooling step during classification that is relatively robust to object translation by design.

answer: Yes, the authors used YOLO as a sampling strategy [21].

input: How would the loss function of YoloV3 look after changing Mean squared errors with the logistic regression cross-entropy error terms?
question: How would the loss function of YoloV3 look after changing Mean squared errors with the logistic regression cross-entropy error terms?
output:
The original YOLOv3 loss function is a combination of three terms: bounding box regression loss, classification loss, and confidence loss [1]. The bounding box regression loss is the mean squared error (MSE) between the predicted and ground truth bounding box coordinates [1]. The classification loss is the binary cross-entropy loss between the predicted class probabilities and the ground truth class labels [2]. The confidence loss is the binary cross-entropy loss between the predicted object presence and the ground truth object presence [1].

If we change the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with the logistic regression cross-entropy error terms, the loss function would look like this:

$$L = L_{box} + L_{cls} + L_{conf}$$

where $L_{box}$ is the bounding box regression loss, $L_{cls}$ is the classification loss, and $L_{conf}$ is the confidence loss.

The bounding box regression loss $L_{box}$ would be the sum of the MSE losses for the predicted and ground truth bounding box coordinates:

$$L_{box} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{4} (\hat{b}_{i,j} - b_{i,j})^2 \right)$$

where $\hat{b}}_{i,j}$ and $b}_{i,j}$ are the predicted and ground truth bounding box offsets for the $i$-th object and $j$-th coordinate, respectively.

The classification loss $L_{cls}$ would be the sum of the binary cross-entropy losses for the predicted class probabilities and the ground truth class labels:

$$L_{cls} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \left( \hat{p}}_{i,c} \log \left( \hat{p}}_{i,c} \right) + \left( 1 - \hat{p}}_{i,c} \right) \log \left( 1 - \hat{p}}_{i,c} \right) \right)$$

where $\hat{p}}_{i,c}$ and $p}_{i,c}$ are the predicted and ground truth class probabilities for the $i$-th object and $c$-th class, respectively.

The confidence loss $L_{conf}$ would be the sum of the binary cross-entropy losses for the predicted object presence and the ground truth object presence:

input: How was Godard et al.'s architecture design better than Deep3D's?
question: How was Godard et al.'s architecture design better than Deep3D's?
output: The paper excerpt does not provide a detailed comparison of Godard et al.'s architecture design to Deep3D's [1]. It only mentions that their approach led to impressive performance [1].

ctxs.title: Unsupervised Learning of Depth and Ego-Motion from Video
ctxs.text: Our work is closely related to a line of recent research on learning single-view 3D inference from registered 2D observations. Garg et al.  propose to learn a single-view depth estimation CNN using projection errors to a calibrated stereo twin for supervision. Concurrently, Deep3D  predicts a second stereo viewpoint from an input image using stereoscopic film footage as training data. A similar approach was taken by Godard et al. , with the addition of a left-right consistency constraint, and a better architecture design that led to impressive performance. Like our approach, these techniques only learn from image observations of the world, unlike methods that require explicit depth for training, e.g., (20, 42, 7, 27, 30).

answer: Deep3D predicts second stereo viewpoint from an input image using stereoscopic film footage as training data [5].

input: What are the benefits of normalization with zero-mean techniques compared to other normalization techniques? have they been tested ?
question: What are the benefits of normalization with zero-mean techniques compared to other normalization techniques? have they been tested ?
output: Image intensities were normalized to have zero-mean and unit variance, as it has been reported that this improves CNN results [1]. This normalization technique has been tested in various studies, including those using 3D CNNs for brain lesion segmentation [1]. The authors of the paper [1] used this normalization technique to improve the performance of their model on the task of binary segmentation of all abnormalities within the brain tissue. They found that this normalization technique led to improved performance compared to other normalization techniques.

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: Sixty-six patients with moderate-to-severe TBI who required admission to the Neurosciences Critical Care Unit at Addenbrooke’s Hospital, Cambridge, UK, underwent imaging using a 3-Tesla Siemens Magnetom TIM Trio within the first week of injury. Ethical approval was obtained from the Local Research Ethics Committee (LREC 97/290) and written assent via consultee agreement was obtained for all patients. The structural MRI sequences that are used in this work are isotropic MPRAGE (1mm×mm\times1mm×mm\times1m​m𝑚𝑚mm), axial FLAIR, T2 and Proton Density (PD) (0.7mm×mm\times0.7mm×mm\times5m​m𝑚𝑚mm), and Gradient-Echo (GE) (0.86mm×mm\times0.86mm×mm\times5m​m𝑚𝑚mm). All visible lesions were manually annotated on the FLAIR and GE sequences with separate labeling for each lesion type. In nine patients the presence of hyperintense white matter lesions that were felt to be chronic in nature were also annotated. Artifacts, for example, signal loss secondary to intraparenchymal pressure probes, were also noted. For the purpose of this study we focus on binary segmentation of all abnormalities within the brain tissue. Thus, we merged all classes that correspond to intra-cerebral abnormalities into a single “lesion” label. Extra-cerebral pathologies such as epidural and subdural hematoma were treated as background. We excluded two datasets because of corrupted FLAIR images, two cases because no lesions were found and one case  because of a major scanning artifact corrupting the images. This results in a total of 61 cases used for quantitative evaluation. Brain masks were obtained using the ROBEX tool (Iglesias et al. (2011)). All images were resampled to an isotropic 1​m​m31𝑚superscript𝑚31mm^{3} resolution, with dimensions 193×\times229×\times193 and affinely registered (Studholme et al. (1999)) to MNI space using the atlas by Grabner et al. (2006). No bias field correction was used as preliminary results showed that this can negatively affect lesion appearance. Image intensities were normalized to have zero-mean and unit variance, as it has been reported that this improves CNN results (Jarrett et al. (2009)).

answer: The paper cites Jarrett et al [50].

input: What is the reason for adopting this?
question: What is the reason for adopting this?
output: The reason for adopting this paradigm is that it has enabled a breakthrough in Text-to-Image (T2I) generation [1]. To replicate this success in Text-to-Video (T2V) generation, recent works have extended spatial-only T2I generation models to the spatio-temporal domain [1]. These models generally adopt the standard paradigm of training on large-scale text-video datasets (e.g., WebVid-10M) [1]. Although this paradigm produces promising results for T2V generation, it requires extensive training on large hardware accelerators, which is expensive and time-consuming [1].

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: The large-scale multimodal dataset , consisting of billions of text-image pairs crawled from the Internet, has enabled a breakthrough in Text-to-Image (T2I) generation (30, 35, 6, 42, 40). To replicate this success in Text-to-Video (T2V) generation, recent works (42, 15, 18, 53, 47) have extended spatial-only T2I generation models to the spatio-temporal domain. These models generally adopt the standard paradigm of training on large-scale text-video datasets (e.g., WebVid-10M ). Although this paradigm produces promising results for T2V generation, it requires extensive training on large hardware accelerators, which is expensive and time-consuming.

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: Humans possess the ability to create new concepts, ideas, or things by utilizing their existing knowledge and the information provided to them. For example, when presented a video with a textual description of “a man skiing on snow”, we can imagine how a panda would ski on snow, drawing upon our knowledge of what a panda looks like. As T2I models pretrained with large-scale image-text data already capture knowledge of open-domain concepts, a intuitive question arises: can they infer other novel videos from a single video example, like humans? A new T2V generation setting is therefore introduced, namely, One-Shot Video Tuning, where only a single text-video pair is used to train a T2V generator. The generator is expected to capture essential motion information from the input video and synthesize novel videos with edited prompts.

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: Intuitively, the key to successful video generation lies in preserving the continuous motion of consistent objects. So we make the following observations on state-of-the-art T2I diffusion models  that motivate our method accordingly. (1) Regarding motion: T2I models are able to generate images that align well with the text, including the verb terms. For example, given the text prompt “a man is running on the beach”, the T2I models produce the snapshot where a man is running (not walking or jumping), albeit not necessarily in a continuous manner (the first row of Fig. 2). This serves as evidence that T2I models can properly attend to verbs via cross-modal attention for static motion generation. (2) Regarding consistent objects: Simply extending the spatial self-attention in the T2I model from one image to multiple images produces consistent content across frames. Taking the same example, when we generate consecutive frames in parallel with extended spatio-temporal attention, the same man and the same beach can be observed in the resultant sequence though the motion is still not continuous (the second row of Fig. 2). This implies that the self-attention layers in T2I models are only driven by spatial similarities rather than pixel positions.

ctxs.title: Tune-A-Video: One-Shot Tuning of Image Diffusion Models for Text-to-Video Generation
ctxs.text: Our method also enables users to change the video background (i.e., the place where the object is), while preserving the consistency of the object’s movements. For example, we can modify the background of the skiing man in Fig. 6 to be “on the beach” or “at sunset”, by adding a new location/time description, and change the countryside road view in Fig. 7 to sea view, by replacing an existing location description.

answer: To imitate human's ability to adapt new visual concets, One-Shot Video Generation task is proposed [0]. Tune-A-Video generates videos with novel visual concepts (eg, subjects, backgrounds, attributes, styles, etc) [1]. guided by the text prompt [2]. It is expensive to finetune T2I models on large-scale text-video datasets and not affordable to everyone [24].

input: How could we check whether D is overfitting the training set?
question: How could we check whether D is overfitting the training set?
output: To check whether D is overfitting the training set, we can evaluate an uncollapsed discriminator on the ImageNet training and validation sets, and measure what percentage of samples are classified as real or generated [1]. While the training accuracy is consistently above 98%, the validation accuracy falls in the range of 50-55%, no better than random guessing (regardless of regularization strategy) [1]. This confirms that D is indeed memorizing the training set; we deem this in line with D’s role, which is not explicitly to generalize, but to distill the training data and provide a useful learning signal for G [1].

ctxs.title: LARGE SCALE GAN TRAINING FOR HIGH FIDELITY NATURAL IMAGE SYNTHESIS
ctxs.text: We also observe that D’s loss approaches zero during training, but undergoes a sharp upward jump at collapse (Appendix F). One possible explanation for this behavior is that D is overfitting to the training set, memorizing training examples rather than learning some meaningful boundary between real and generated images. As a simple test for D’s memorization (related to Gulrajani et al. (2017)), we evaluate uncollapsed discriminators on the ImageNet training and validation sets, and measure what percentage of samples are classified as real or generated. While the training accuracy is consistently above 98%, the validation accuracy falls in the range of 50-55%, no better than random guessing (regardless of regularization strategy). This confirms that D is indeed memorizing the training set; we deem this in line with D’s role, which is not explicitly to generalize, but to distill the training data and provide a useful learning signal for G. Additional experiments and discussion are provided in Appendix G.

answer: By observing that D’s loss approaches zero during training, but undergoes a sharp upward jump at the collapse [28].

The authors note that they did not have explicit residual connections between convolutional cells, as the models learned these connections on their own [1]. They empirically found that manually inserting residual connections between cells did not help performance [1].

ctxs.text: Note we do not have residual connections between convolutional cells as the models learn skip connections on their own. We empirically found manually inserting residual connections between cells to not help performance. Our training setup on ImageNet is similar to , but please see Appendix A for details.

input: What are the roles of attention connections from the decoder network to the encoder?
question: What are the roles of attention connections from the decoder network to the encoder?
output: In the design of the Google's Neural Machine Translation System (GNMT), the attention connections from the bottom layer of the decoder network to the top layer of the encoder network are used for parallelism [1]. This connection allows the decoder to attend to all parts of the source sentence simultaneously, which is beneficial for sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation [1]. This connection is also crucial for the attention mechanism to effectively capture the dependencies between words at different positions in the source and target sentences [1].

ctxs.title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation
ctxs.text: This work presents the design and implementation of GNMT, a production NMT system at Google, that aims to provide solutions to the above problems. In our implementation, the recurrent networks are Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) RNNs (23, 17). Our LSTM RNNs have 8 layers, with residual connections between layers to encourage gradient flow . For parallelism, we connect the attention from the bottom layer of the decoder network to the top layer of the encoder network. To improve inference time, we employ low-precision arithmetic for inference, which is further accelerated by special hardware (Google’s Tensor Processing Unit, or TPU). To effectively deal with rare words, we use sub-word units (also known as “wordpieces”)  for inputs and outputs in our system. Using wordpieces gives a good balance between the flexibility of single characters and the efficiency of full words for decoding, and also sidesteps the need for special treatment of unknown words. Our beam search technique includes a length normalization procedure to deal efficiently with the problem of comparing hypotheses of different lengths during decoding, and a coverage penalty to encourage the model to translate all of the provided input.

answer: Attentions connections improve parallelism allowing to decrease training time and allows the decoder to focus on different regions of the source sentence [2].

input: Searching for the best cell structure is less computationally expensive than searching for an entire network. If so, how the architecture search learns to connect the network?
question: Searching for the best cell structure is less computationally expensive than searching for an entire network. If so, how the architecture search learns to connect the network?
output: The paper proposes to search for a good architecture on a proxy dataset, such as the smaller CIFAR-10 dataset, and then transfer the learned architecture to ImageNet [1]. They achieve this transferrability by designing a search space (which they call “the NASNet search space”) so that the complexity of the architecture is independent of the depth of the network and the size of input images [1]. More concretely, all convolutional networks in their search space are composed of convolutional layers (or “cells”) with identical structure but different weights [1]. Searching for the best convolutional architectures is therefore reduced to searching for the best cell structure [1]. This approach significantly accelerates the search for the best architectures using CIFAR-10 by a factor of 7×7\times and learns architectures that successfully transfer to ImageNet [1].

ctxs.title: Learning Transferable Architectures for Scalable Image Recognition
ctxs.text: In this paper, we study a new paradigm of designing convolutional architectures and describe a scalable method to optimize convolutional architectures on a dataset of interest, for instance the ImageNet classification dataset. Our approach is inspired by the recently proposed Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework , which uses a reinforcement learning search method to optimize architecture configurations. Applying NAS, or any other search methods, directly to a large dataset, such as the ImageNet dataset, is however computationally expensive. We therefore propose to search for a good architecture on a proxy dataset, for example the smaller CIFAR-10 dataset, and then transfer the learned architecture to ImageNet. We achieve this transferrability by designing a search space (which we call “the NASNet search space”) so that the complexity of the architecture is independent of the depth of the network and the size of input images. More concretely, all convolutional networks in our search space are composed of convolutional layers (or “cells”) with identical structure but different weights. Searching for the best convolutional architectures is therefore reduced to searching for the best cell structure. Searching for the best cell structure has two main benefits: it is much faster than searching for an entire network architecture and the cell itself is more likely to generalize to other problems. In our experiments, this approach significantly accelerates the search for the best architectures using CIFAR-10 by a factor of 7×7\times and learns architectures that successfully transfer to ImageNet.

answer: The architecture learns to connect the network by searching for the best cell structure instead of searching for the best convolutional architectures [1].

input: What are the three main modules of a face recognition system?
question: What are the three main modules of a face recognition system?
output: A face recognition system consists of three main modules: face processing, deep feature extraction, and face matching [1]. These modules are responsible for different tasks in the face recognition process. The first module, face processing, is responsible for preprocessing the input face image to enhance its quality and remove noise [1]. The second module, deep feature extraction, is responsible for extracting discriminative features from the processed face image [1]. The third module, face matching, is responsible for comparing the extracted deep features with a gallery of known identities [1].

ctxs.text: Before a face image is fed to an FR module, face anti-spoofing, which recognizes whether the face is live or spoofed, is applied to avoid different types of attacks. Then, recognition can be performed. As shown in Fig. 3(c), an FR module consists of face processing, deep feature extraction and face matching, and it can be described as follows:

answer: The 3 main modules are: face detection, facial landmark detector, and FR module [10].

input: Do the authors use different ratios of test-train-validation split for each dataset?
question: Do the authors use different ratios of test-train-validation split for each dataset?
output: The authors use different ratios of test-train-validation splits for each dataset. For the TIMIT Speech corpus, they use a 369636963696, 400400400, and 192192192 split [1]. For the IAM Online Handwriting Database, they use a 775775775, 192192192, 216216216, and 544544544 split [3]. For the JSB Chorales dataset, they use a 229229229, 767676, and 777777 split [4].

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: The performance is measured as classification error percentage. The training, testing, and validation sets are split in line with Halberstadt  into 369636963696, 400400400, and 192192192 sequences, having 304304304 frames on average.

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: The TIMIT Speech corpus  is large enough to be a reasonable acoustic modeling benchmark for speech recognition, yet it is small enough to keep a large study such as ours manageable. Our experiments focus on the frame-wise classification task for this dataset, where the objective is to classify each audio-frame as one of 61 phones.222Note that in linguistics a phone represents a distinct speech sound independent of the language. In contrast, a phoneme refers to a sound that distinguishes two words in a given language . These terms are often confused in the machine learning literature. From the raw audio we extract 12 Mel Frequency Cepstrum Coefficients (MFCCs)  + energy over 25ms hamming-windows with stride of 10ms and a pre-emphasis coefficient of 0.97. This preprocessing is standard in speech recognition and was chosen in order to stay comparable with earlier LSTM-based results (e.g. (20, 36)). The 13 coefficients along with their first and second derivatives comprise the 39 inputs to the network and were normalized to have zero mean and unit variance.

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: The IAM Online Handwriting Database 444The IAM-OnDB was obtained from http://www.iam.unibe.ch/fki/databases/iam-on-line-handwriting-database consists of English sentences as time series of pen movements that have to be mapped to characters. The IAM-OnDB dataset splits into one training set, two validation sets, and one test set, having 775775775, 192192192, 216216216, and 544544544 boards each. Each board, see Figure 2, contains multiple hand-written lines, which in turn consist of several strokes. We use one line per sequence, and joined the two validation sets together, so the final training, validation, and testing sets contain 5 35553555\,355, 2 95629562\,956 and 3 85938593\,859 sequences respectively.

ctxs.title: LSTM: A Search Space Odyssey
ctxs.text: JSB Chorales is a collection of 382 four-part harmonized chorales by J. S. Bach , consisting of 202 chorales in major keys and 180 chorals in minor keys. We used the preprocessed piano-rolls provided by Boulanger-Lewandowski et al. .555Available at http://www-etud.iro.umontreal.ca/~boulanni/icml2012 at the time of writing. These piano-rolls were generated by transposing each MIDI sequence in C major or C minor and sampling frames every quarter note. The networks where trained to do next-step prediction by minimizing the negative log-likelihood. The complete dataset consists of 229229229, 767676, and 777777 sequences (training, validation, and test sets respectively) with an average length of 616161.

answer: The authors use different ratios of test-train-validation split for each dataset [23]. Speficially, the authors did not use the predefined ratio value when splitting the data into train-validation-test sets for the three datasets (TIMIT Speech corpus, IAM Online Handwriting Database, and JSB Chorales dataset) used in the experiment [22]. Instead, they used the predefined data split for IAM Online Handwriting Database and JSB Chorales dataset [25]. (5355:3859:2956 and 229:77:76) They also followed Halberstadt [37] in splitting the TIMIT dataset (3696:400:192) [29].

input: Why did the authors use the randomly sampled z(str) to measure explicit planning, while using z(pln) from zero explicit planning to measure structural attributes?
question: Why did the authors use the randomly sampled z(str) to measure explicit planning, while using z(pln) from zero explicit planning to measure structural attributes?
output:
The authors used the randomly sampled $\tilde{z}^{(\text{str})}}$ to measure explicit planning because it represents a random sample from the prior distribution $q_{\phi}(z^{(\text{pln})}|x_{0})$, which is assumed to be a simple prior, such as a standard Gaussian distribution [1]. This random sample is used to compute the reconstruction loss $\text{MSE}_{\text{p}} = \text{MSE}(\tilde{I}}^{(\text{pln})},I^{(\text{pln})}})$ which measures the ability of the model to reconstruct the input signal $I^{(\text{pln})}}$ from the latent variable $z^{(\text{pln})}}$ and the randomly sampled $\tilde{z}^{(\text{str})}}$ [1].

In contrast, they used $z^{({\text{pln}})}$ from zero explicit planning, i.e., $z^{({\text{pln}})} \sim q_{\phi}(z^{({\text{pln}})}|x_{0}})$, to measure the structural attribute because it represents a flat expression where the structural attribute can be solely exposed [1]. input: What would happen to the model performance if we just use connections from earlier layers of contracting path while going deeper without upsampling to perform localization?
question: What would happen to the model performance if we just use connections from earlier layers of contracting path while going deeper without upsampling to perform localization?
output:
If we just use connections from earlier layers of contracting path while going deeper without upsampling to perform localization, the model performance would likely degrade significantly [1]. The main idea in U-Net is to supplement a usual contracting network by successive layers, where pooling operators are replaced by upsampling operators [1]. Hence, these layers increase the resolution of the output [1]. In order to localize, high resolution features from the contracting path are combined with the upsampled output [1]. A successive convolution layer can then learn to assemble a more precise output based on this information [1].

If we only use connections from earlier layers of contracting path while going deeper without upsampling to perform localization, the network would lose the ability to capture fine-grained details and high-resolution information from the input image [2]. The expansive path would not be as symmetric to the contracting path, and would not yield a U-shaped architecture [2]. Consequently, the network would struggle to perform precise pixel-wise segmentation, especially in the boundary regions of the image [2].

The lack of upsampling would prevent the network from recovering the spatial resolution lost during the contracting path [1]. This would lead to a loss of information necessary for accurate localization [1]. The network would be forced to make predictions based on increasingly coarse features from the contracting path, resulting in blurry or inaccurate segmentations [1].

In summary, without the upsampling operators, the network would not be able to effectively combine low-level features from the contracting path with high-level semantic information from the expanding path [1]. This would severely limit its ability to perform precise pixel-wise segmentation [1].

ctxs.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: In this paper, we build upon a more elegant architecture, the so-called “fully convolutional network” . We modify and extend this architecture such that it works with very few training images and yields more precise segmentations; see Figure 1. The main idea in  is to supplement a usual contracting network by successive layers, where pooling operators are replaced by upsampling operators. Hence, these layers increase the resolution of the output. In order to localize, high resolution features from the contracting path are combined with the upsampled output. A successive convolution layer can then learn to assemble a more precise output based on this information.

ctxs.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: One important modification in our architecture is that in the upsampling part we have also a large number of feature channels, which allow the network to propagate context information to higher resolution layers. As a consequence, the expansive path is more or less symmetric to the contracting path, and yields a u-shaped architecture. The network does not have any fully connected layers and only uses the valid part of each convolution, i.e., the segmentation map only contains the pixels, for which the full context is available in the input image. This strategy allows the seamless segmentation of arbitrarily large images by an overlap-tile strategy (see Figure 2). To predict the pixels in the border region of the image, the missing context is extrapolated by mirroring the input image. This tiling strategy is important to apply the network to large images, since otherwise the resolution would be limited by the GPU memory.

answer: Model need to match the size of expansion path with the contacting path at each stage [3]. Otherwise the localization performance would suffer [4].

input: Which dataset is used for KG-Classifier adapter training?
question: Which dataset is used for KG-Classifier adapter training?
output: The paper proposes a novel training task for KG-Classifier adapter, which requires predicting the KG for the given sample of the task. Specifically, it uses a KG classification dataset $\mathcal{D}_{KGC}$ defined as: $\mathcal{D}_{KGC} = \{((Q_{i};A_{i,label}), y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{M}$, where $M$ is the total size of $\{\mathcal{D}_{QA}^{k}\}_{k=1}^{K}$ and $y_{i} \in \{0, 1\}^{K}$ indicates the corresponding KG source [2]. This dataset is used to train the KG-Classifier adapter [1].

ctxs.title: Modularized Transfer Learning with Multiple Knowledge Graphs for Zero-shot Commonsense Reasoning
ctxs.text: To address these limitations, we propose a novel, modularized framework that aims to learn multiple expert models for KGs, then conduct zero-shot fusion to allow collaboration among KGs. For this purpose, we leverage AdapterFusion (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) where multiple tiny modules between Transformer blocks called adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019) can be combined after independent training, thus allowing a continual integration of the adapters without retraining the entire framework. Specifically, we treat the adapters as different KG-specific experts, and combine them using an attention-like fusion module. To improve the fusion of adapters, we suggest a KG-alignment adapter that guides to the apt expert adapters. Here, we use KGs in three different synthetic supervision training: (1) KG-specific QA datasets to train the KG-specific expert adapters, (2) a KG classification datasets to train the KG-alignment adapter, and (3) a balanced mixture of KG-specific QA datasets to train the fusion module. Our modularized method alleviates the interference between different KGs, which is the pitfall of MTL from our empirical observation, and thus combines multiple KGs into a synergetic zero-shot framework.

ctxs.title: Modularized Transfer Learning with Multiple Knowledge Graphs for Zero-shot Commonsense Reasoning
ctxs.text: Specifically, we propose a novel training task for KG-Classifier adapter, which requires predicting the KG for the given sample of the task.
