input: If dense/sparse retrievers are pre-trained on target corpus to enable the retrievers to be corpus-aware, can the fine-tuned retrievers outperform lexical models?
question: If dense/sparse retrievers are pre-trained on target corpus to enable the retrievers to be corpus-aware, can the fine-tuned retrievers outperform lexical models?
output: The paper excerpt [1] suggests that in-domain performance of a model does not correlate well with its generalization capabilities, and that computationally expensive models, like re-ranking models and late interaction models, perform the best on the BEIR benchmark. However, it does not directly address whether fine-tuned retrievers can outperform lexical models when pre-trained on the target corpus. The authors find that computationally expensive models perform the best on the BEIR benchmark, but that more efficient approaches based on dense or sparse embeddings can substantially underperform traditional lexical models like BM25 [1]. This suggests that while dense/sparse retrievers can be more efficient, they may not always outperform strong baselines like BM25 on zero-shot evaluation tasks.

ctxs.title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
ctxs.text: We use beir to evaluate ten diverse retrieval methods from five broad architectures: lexical, sparse, dense, late interaction, and re-ranking. From our analysis, we find that no single approach consistently outperforms other approaches on all datasets. Further, we notice that the in-domain performance of a model does not correlate well with its generalization capabilities: models fine-tuned with identical training data might generalize differently. In terms of efficiency, we find a trade-off between the performances and the computational cost: computationally expensive models, like re-ranking models and late interaction model perform the best. More efficient approaches e.g. based on dense or sparse embeddings can substantially underperform traditional lexical models like BM25. Overall, BM25 remains a strong baseline for zero-shot text retrieval.

answer: If dense/sparse retrievers are pre-trained on target corpus to enable the retrievers to be corpus-aware, the fine-tuned retrievers underperform lexical models [4].

input: How has the evolution of network architectures in deep face recognition systems, such as the transition from AlexNet to ResNet and SENet, impacted the performance of these systems?
question: How has the evolution of network architectures in deep face recognition systems, such as the transition from AlexNet to ResNet and SENet, impacted the performance of these systems?
output:
The evolution of network architectures in deep face recognition systems has significantly impacted their performance. Early models like DeepFace [1] used a nine-layer CNN with several locally connected layers and achieved an accuracy of 97.35% on LFW [2]. This was a significant milestone, approaching human performance on the unconstrained condition for the first time [3].

Subsequent works like FaceNet [2] and 3D alignment for face processing [2] improved performance by using a large private dataset to train a GoogleNet and adopting a triplet loss function based on triplets of roughly aligned matching/nonmatching face patches generated by a novel online triplet mining method [2]. These methods achieved good performance of 99.63% on LFW [2].

In 2015, VGGface [2] designed a procedure to collect a large-scale dataset from the Internet, trained the VGGNet on this dataset, and then fine-tuned the networks via a triplet loss function similar to FaceNet [2]. VGGface obtained an accuracy of 98.95% on LFW [2].

In 2017, SphereFace [2] used a 64-layer ResNet architecture and proposed the angular softmax (A-Softmax) loss to learn discriminative face features with angular margin, boosting the achieves to 99.42% on LFW [2]. This architecture and loss function significantly improved performance on LFW [2].

In the end of 2017, a new large-scale face dataset, namely VGGface2 [2], was introduced, which consists of large variations in pose, age, illumination, ethnicity and profession [2]. Cao et al. first trained a SENet with MS-celeb-1M dataset and then fine-tuned the model with VGGface2, and achieved the SOTA performance on the IJB-A and IJB-B [2]. This demonstrated the effectiveness of using a more diverse and challenging dataset for training and fine-tuning models [2].

Overall, the progression from AlexNet to SENet and other influential architectures has led to a continuous improvement in the performance of deep face recognition systems [1]. These advancements have pushed the state-of-the-art towards human-level performance on various benchmarks [3].

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Mainstream architectures. The commonly used network architectures of deep FR have always followed those of deep object classification and evolved from AlexNet to SENet rapidly. We present the most influential architectures of deep object classification and deep face recognition in chronological order 111The time we present is when the paper was published. in Fig. 8.

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: With the evolved architectures and advanced training techniques, such as batch normalization (BN), the network becomes deeper and the training becomes more controllable. Following these architectures in object classification, the networks in deep FR are also developed step by step, and the performance of deep FR is continually improving. We present these mainstream architectures of deep FR in Fig. 9. In 2014, DeepFace  was the first to use a nine-layer CNN with several locally connected layers. With 3D alignment for face processing, it reaches an accuracy of 97.35% on LFW. In 2015, FaceNet  used a large private dataset to train a GoogleNet. It adopted a triplet loss function based on triplets of roughly aligned matching/nonmatching face patches generated by a novel online triplet mining method and achieved good performance of 99.63%. In the same year, VGGface  designed a procedure to collect a large-scale dataset from the Internet. It trained the VGGNet on this dataset and then fine-tuned the networks via a triplet loss function similar to FaceNet. VGGface obtains an accuracy of 98.95%. In 2017, SphereFace  used a 64-layer ResNet architecture and proposed the angular softmax (A-Softmax) loss to learn discriminative face features with angular margin. It boosts the achieves to 99.42% on LFW. In the end of 2017, a new large-scale face dataset, namely VGGface2 , was introduced, which consists of large variations in pose, age, illumination, ethnicity and profession. Cao et al. first trained a SENet with MS-celeb-1M dataset  and then fine-tuned the model with VGGface2 , and achieved the SOTA performance on the IJB-A  and IJB-B .

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: In 2014, DeepFace  achieved the SOTA accuracy on the famous LFW benchmark , approaching human performance on the unconstrained condition for the first time (DeepFace: 97.35% vs. Human: 97.53%), by training a 9-layer model on 4 million facial images. Inspired by this work, research focus has shifted to deep-learning-based approaches, and the accuracy was dramatically boosted to above 99.80% in just three years. Deep learning technique has reshaped the research landscape of FR in almost all aspects such as algorithm designs, training/test datasets, application scenarios and even the evaluation protocols. Therefore, it is of great significance to review the breakthrough and rapid development process in recent years. There have been several surveys on FR (24, 25, 26, 27, 28) and its subdomains, and they mostly summarized and compared a diverse set of techniques related to a specific FR scene, such as illumination-invariant FR , 3D FR , pose-invariant FR . Unfortunately, due to their earlier publication dates, none of them covered the deep learning methodology that is most successful nowadays. This survey focuses only on recognition problem, and one can refer to Ranjan et al.  for a brief review of a full deep FR pipeline with detection and alignment, or refer to Jin et al.  for a survey of face alignment. Specifically, the major contributions of this survey are as follows:

answer: As deep FR models followed the footsteps of deep object classification network architectures the performance got better, training got more controllable, and models got deeper [27]. It started with DeepFace which was based on AlexNet that achieved 9735% on the LFW benchmark [29]. Then came the FaceNet based on GoogleNet which achieved 9963% [3].

input: What added benefits do the ILSVRC provide over the existing PASCAL-VOC challenge?
question: What added benefits do the ILSVRC provide over the existing PASCAL-VOC challenge?
output:
The added benefits of the ILSVRC dataset over the existing PASCAL VOC challenge include:

1. **Scale**: ILSVRC scales up PASCAL VOC's goal of standardized training and evaluation of recognition algorithms by more than an order of magnitude in number of object classes and images: PASCAL VOC 2012 has 20 object classes and 21,738 images compared to ILSVRC2012 with 1000 object classes and 1,431,167 annotated images [2].
2. **Difficulty**: ILSVRC also analyzes the level of difficulty of object localization in these images compared to the PASCAL VOC benchmark. They compute statistics on the ILSVRC2012 single-object localization validation set images compared to PASCAL VOC 2012 validation images [3].

These additions make ILSVRC a more challenging and comprehensive benchmark for large-scale image recognition and retrieval tasks.

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: The ILSVRC dataset and the competition has allowed significant algorithmic advances in large-scale image recognition and retrieval.

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: The closest to ILSVRC is the PASCAL VOC dataset (Everingham et al.,, 2010, 2014), which provides a standardized test bed for object detection, image classification, object segmentation, person layout, and action classification. Much of the design choices in ILSVRC have been inspired by PASCAL VOC and the similarities and differences between the datasets are discussed at length throughout the paper. ILSVRC scales up PASCAL VOC’s goal of standardized training and evaluation of recognition algorithms by more than an order of magnitude in number of object classes and images: PASCAL VOC 2012 has 20 object classes and 21,738 images compared to ILSVRC2012 with 1000 object classes and 1,431,167 annotated images.

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: In addition to the size of the dataset, we also analyze the level of difficulty of object localization in these images compared to the PASCAL VOC benchmark. We compute statistics on the ILSVRC2012 single-object localization validation set images compared to PASCAL VOC 2012 validation images.

answer: Images from the ILSVRC2012 single-object localization validation set are compared to images from the PASCAL VOC benchmark for object recognition [102]. They have also analyzed the level of difficulty of object localization in these images compared to those of objects from the PASCAL VOC benchmark [13]. The level of difficulty of object localization is also analyzed [62].

input: What is catastrophic forgetting?
question: What is catastrophic forgetting?
output: Catastrophic forgetting refers to the phenomenon where a pre-trained language model, when fine-tuned on a downstream task, completely forgets the knowledge it acquired from the original pre-training task [1]. This occurs when the fine-tuning is too aggressive, causing the model to overwrite the original task's knowledge with the new task's knowledge, leading to a significant drop in performance on the original task [1].

ctxs.title: Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification
ctxs.text: Fine-tuning the target classifier is the most critical part of the transfer learning method. Overly aggressive fine-tuning will cause catastrophic forgetting, eliminating the benefit of the information captured through language modeling; too cautious fine-tuning will lead to slow convergence (and resultant overfitting). Besides discriminative fine-tuning and triangular learning rates, we propose gradual unfreezing for fine-tuning the classifier.

answer: Catastrophic forgetting involved increasing error as a model start to overfit and knowledge captured through pretraining is lost [24].

input: What are the fields that needs to be carried out in a timely fashion and on a computationally limited platform?
question: What are the fields that needs to be carried out in a timely fashion and on a computationally limited platform?
output: The fields that need to be carried out in a timely fashion and on a computationally limited platform include robotics, self-driving cars, and augmented reality [1].

ctxs.title: MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural Networks for Mobile Vision Applications
ctxs.text: Convolutional neural networks have become ubiquitous in computer vision ever since AlexNet  popularized deep convolutional neural networks by winning the ImageNet Challenge: ILSVRC 2012 . The general trend has been to make deeper and more complicated networks in order to achieve higher accuracy (27, 31, 29, 8). However, these advances to improve accuracy are not necessarily making networks more efficient with respect to size and speed. In many real world applications such as robotics, self-driving car and augmented reality, the recognition tasks need to be carried out in a timely fashion on a computationally limited platform.

answer: For robotics, self-driving cars and AR, the recognition task needs to be carried timely and with less computational cost [0].

input: What is an example of a "dataset-centric" approach?
question: What is an example of a "dataset-centric" approach?
output: An example of a "dataset-centric" approach is to display images from the training or test set that cause high or low activations for individual units [1]. This approach requires both a trained DNN and running data through that network [1].

ctxs.title: Understanding Neural Networks Through Deep Visualization
ctxs.text: Another approach is to try to interpret the function computed by each individual neuron. Past studies in this vein roughly divide into two different camps: dataset-centric and network-centric. The former requires both a trained DNN and running data through that network; the latter requires only the trained network itself. One dataset-centric approach is to display images from the training or test set that cause high or low activations for individual units. Another is the deconvolution method of Zeiler & Fergus (2013), which highlights the portions of a particular image that are responsible for the firing of each neural unit.

answer: An example of "dataset-centric" approach can be deconvolution method which is used to highlight certain regions of some image that has the highest effects in the response of different units [6].

input: Can the methods of "one-to-many augmentation" like data augmentation and 3D face reconstruction effectively improve the performance of deep FR algorithms in terms of accuracy and diversity of training data?
question: Can the methods of "one-to-many augmentation" like data augmentation and 3D face reconstruction effectively improve the performance of deep FR algorithms in terms of accuracy and diversity of training data?
output:
Yes, the methods of "one-to-many augmentation" such as data augmentation and 3D face reconstruction can effectively improve the performance of deep FR algorithms in terms of accuracy and diversity of training data [1]. These methods can mitigate the challenges of data collection, which is extremely expensive and time-consuming [1].

Data augmentation methods, such as photometric transformations (e.g., color space transformations) and geometric transformations (e.g., oversampling, mirroring, and rotating) [2], can be used to generate more diverse training data [2]. For instance, Sun et al. cropped 400 face patches varying in positions, scales, and color channels and mirrored the images [2]. Liu et al. generated seven overlapped image patches centered at different landmarks on the face region and trained them with seven CNNs with the same structure [2].

3D model generation methods, such as 3D face reconstruction [1], can also be used to generate more realistic and diverse training data [1]. These methods can be used to augment not only training data but also the gallery of test data [1].

In summary, one-to-many augmentation methods like data augmentation and 3D face reconstruction can effectively improve the performance of deep FR algorithms in terms of accuracy and diversity of training data [1].

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Collecting a large database is extremely expensive and time consuming. The methods of “one-to-many augmentation” can mitigate the challenges of data collection, and they can be used to augment not only training data but also the gallery of test data. we categorized them into four classes: data augmentation, 3D model, autoencoder model and GAN model.

ctxs.title: Deep Face Recognition
ctxs.text: Data augmentation. Common data augmentation methods consist of photometric transformations (75, 22) and geometric transformations, such as oversampling (multiple patches obtained by cropping at different scales) , mirroring , and rotating  the images. Recently, data augmentation has been widely used in deep FR algorithms (58, 59, 60, 35, 21, 36, 61, 62). for example, Sun et al.  cropped 400 face patches varying in positions, scales, and color channels and mirrored the images. Liu et al.  generated seven overlapped image patches centered at different landmarks on the face region and trained them with seven CNNs with the same structure.

answer: In terms of accuracy, the paper mentions a set of work done on assembled multi-input networks that used "one-to-many augmentation" methods to expand their dataset and achieve better results compared to individual networks [40]. In terms of diversity, all data augmentation, 3D face reconstruction, autoencoders, and especially GANs were found to be effective in generating faces in certain poses, angles, with different expressions, etc [41].

input: How the features are converted to pixel labels in SegNet?
question: How the features are converted to pixel labels in SegNet?
output: The features are converted to pixel labels in SegNet through a decoder architecture that upsamples the low-resolution feature maps from the encoder to the original image size. This upsampling process is performed using transposed convolutions, which learn to upsample the feature maps to the original image size while preserving the spatial information. The decoder is designed to output a pixel-wise semantic label map of the same size as the input image. This upsampling process is guided by the high-level semantic information from the deep layers of the encoder, which provides a coarse prediction of the semantic labels for each pixel. The decoder is trained to produce smooth segment labels, which are more consistent with the ground truth annotations than local patch based classifiers [1].

ctxs.title: SegNet: A Deep Convolutional Encoder-Decoder Architecture for Image Segmentation
ctxs.text: We presented SegNet, a fully trainable deep architecture for joint feature learning and mapping an input image in a feed-forward manner to its pixel-wise semantic labels. A highlight of the proposed architecture is its ability to produce smooth segment labels when compared with local patch based classifiers. This is due to deep layers of feature encoding that employ a large spatial context for pixel-wise labelling. To the best of our knowledge this is the first deep learning method to learn to map low resolution encoder feature maps to semantic labels. Both qualitative and numerical accuracy of the SegNet for outdoor and indoor scenes is very competitive, even without use of any CRF post-processing. We have also demonstrated the use of pre-trained SegNet for obtaining good performance on other datasets with a small extra computational effort. The encoder-decoder architecture of the SegNet can also be trained unsupervised and to handle missing data in the input during test time.

answer: SegNet performs feed-forward computation to obtain pixel-wise labelling [13].

input: What are the benefits of using the predictor to calculate user-item interaction score instead of directly encoding into their inner product?
question: What are the benefits of using the predictor to calculate user-item interaction score instead of directly encoding into their inner product?
output:
The benefits of using the predictor to calculate user-item interaction score instead of directly encoding into their inner product are:

1. **Capturing High-Level Relationship**: BUIR additionally uses the predictor to model their interaction, which results in the capability of encoding the high-level relationship between users and items into the representations [1]. This allows BUIR to capture more nuanced and complex relationships than existing discriminative OCCF methods [1].
2. **Accurate Computation**: With the help of the predictor, BAIR accurately computes the user-item interaction scores as well as optimizes the representation without explicitly using negative samples [1]. This leads to more robust and accurate recommendations compared to methods that rely on explicit negative sampling [1].
3. **Improved Performance**: By leveraging the predictor to model the interaction, BUIR achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks [1]. This improvement is attributed to its ability to learn more effective user and item representations that capture the underlying patterns and preferences in the interaction data [1].

ctxs.title: Bootstrapping User and Item Representations for One-Class Collaborative Filtering
ctxs.text: Existing discriminative OCCF methods (Rendle et al., 2009; Hsieh et al., 2017) have tried to optimize the latent space where the user-item interactions are directly encoded into their inner product (or Euclidean distance). On the contrary, BUIR additionally uses the predictor to model their interaction, which results in the capability of encoding the high-level relationship between users and items into the representations. In conclusion, with the help of the predictor, BUIR accurately computes the user-item interaction scores as well as optimizes the representation without explicitly using negative samples.

answer: Using predictor can optimize the representation without any negative sample [21].

input: Given input size (3x224x224) and bottleneck channels being 64, compare the computational complexity between ResNet, ResNext and ShuffleNet.
question: Given input size (3x224x224) and bottleneck channels being 64, compare the computational complexity between ResNet, ResNext and ShuffleNet.
output:
Given the input size of (3x224x224) and bottleneck channels being 64, the computational complexity of each component is as follows:

* ResNet unit: requires $h \times w (2c \cdot m + 9 m^2)$ FLOPs, where $h$ and $w$ are the height and width of the input feature map, $c$ is the number of channels in the input feature map, $m$ is the number of channels in the bottleneck layer, and $n$ is the number of groups for convolutions. With $c=3$, $m=64$, and $n=1$, this results in $h \times w (2 \cdot 64 + 9 \cdot 64^2)$ FLOPs, which is $h \times w (128 + 57344)$ FLOPs.
* ResNeXt unit: has the same complexity as ResNet unit, but with $g$ groups for convolutions, it requires $h \times w (2c \cdot m + 9 m^2 / g)$ FLOPs. With $c=3$, $m=64$, $n=1$, and $g=1$, this results in $h \times w (2 \cdot 64 + 9 \cdot 64^2 / g)$ FLOPs, which is $h \times w (128 + 57344 / g)$ FLOPs.
* ShuffleNet unit: requires only $h \times w (2c \cdot m / g + 9 m)$ FLOPs, where $c$ is the number of channels in the input feature map, $m$ is the number of channels in the bottleneck layer, and $g$ is the number of groups for convolutions. With $c=3$, $m=64$, and $g=1$, this results in $h \times w (2 \cdot 64 / g + 9 \cdot 64)$ FLOPs, which is $h \times w (128 / g + 57344)$ FLOPs.

In summary, given the input size (3x224x224) and bottleneck channels being 64, the computational complexity of each component is:

* ResNet unit: $h \times w

ctxs.title: ShuffleNet: An Extremely Efficient Convolutional Neural Network for Mobile Devices
ctxs.text: Thanks to pointwise group convolution with channel shuffle, all components in ShuffleNet unit can be computed efficiently. Compared with ResNet  (bottleneck design) and ResNeXt , our structure has less complexity under the same settings. For example, given the input size c×h×w𝑐ℎ𝑤c\times h\times w and the bottleneck channels m𝑚m, ResNet unit requires h​w​(2​c​m+9​m2)ℎ𝑤2𝑐𝑚9superscript𝑚2hw(2cm+9m^{2}) FLOPs and ResNeXt has h​w​(2​c​m+9​m2/g)ℎ𝑤2𝑐𝑚9superscript𝑚2𝑔hw(2cm+9m^{2}/g) FLOPs, while our ShuffleNet unit requires only h​w​(2​c​m/g+9​m)ℎ𝑤2𝑐𝑚𝑔9𝑚hw(2cm/g+9m) FLOPs, where g𝑔g means the number of groups for convolutions. In other words, given a computational budget, ShuffleNet can use wider feature maps. We find this is critical for small networks, as tiny networks usually have an insufficient number of channels to process the information.

answer:
ResNet: hw(2cm+9m^{2}) = 244^2*2 * 3 * 64 + 244^2*9 * 64^2 = 2 217 596 928
ResNeXt: hw(2cm+9m^{2}/g) = 244^2*2 * 3 * 64 + 244^2*9 * 64^2/g = 22 861 824 + 2 194 735 104 / g
ShuffleNet: hw(2cm/g + 9m) = 244^2*2 * 3 * 64 / g + 244^2*9 * 64 = 22 861 824 / g + 34 292 736
Even with the group size of 1 (g=1), ShuffleNet have much less complexity compared to ResNet and ResNeXt [13].

input: Why the training was unstable without these activations scaled before addition?
question: Why the training was unstable without these activations scaled before addition?
output: The training was unstable without these activations scaled before addition because the scaling factors between 0.1 and 0.3 seemed to stabilize the training [1]. When the scaling factors were not strictly necessary, it never seemed to harm the final accuracy, but it helped to stabilize the training [3]. In the case of very deep residual networks, they suggested a two-phase training where the first “warm-up” phase is done with very low learning rate, followed by a second phase with high learning rate [2]. They found that if the number of filters is very high, then even a very low (0.00001) learning rate is not sufficient to cope with the instabilities and the training with high learning rate had a chance to destroy its effects [2]. They found it much more reliable to just scale the residuals [2].

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: We found that scaling down the residuals before adding them to the previous layer activation seemed to stabilize the training. In general we picked some scaling factors between 0.1 and 0.3 to scale the residuals before their being added to the accumulated layer activations (cf. Figure 20).

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: A similar instability was observed by He et al. in  in the case of very deep residual networks and they suggested a two-phase training where the first “warm-up” phase is done with very low learning rate, followed by a second phase with high learning rata. We found that if the number of filters is very high, then even a very low (0.00001) learning rate is not sufficient to cope with the instabilities and the training with high learning rate had a chance to destroy its effects. We found it much more reliable to just scale the residuals.

ctxs.title: Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning
ctxs.text: Even where the scaling was not strictly necessary, it never seemed to harm the final accuracy, but it helped to stabilize the training.

answer: The training was stabilised after scaling down the residuals before adding them to the previous layer activations [14]. Even when this scaling was not strictly necessary, it helped stabilise the training without affecting the accuracy [15]. But this paper does not answer why the training was unstable without these scaled activations [16].

input: What is the purpose of bounding box regression?
question: What is the purpose of bounding box regression?
output: The purpose of bounding box regression is to predict the precise location and size of the predicted object within the image [1]. It is a crucial component of object detection, as it refines the coarse localization provided by the feature maps to produce pixel-accurate predictions [1].

ctxs.title: R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks
ctxs.text: We further address bounding box regression (7, 6) in a similar way. Aside from the above k2​(C+1)superscript𝑘2𝐶1k^{2}(C+1)-d convolutional layer, we append a sibling 4​k24superscript𝑘24k^{2}-d convolutional layer for bounding box regression. The position-sensitive RoI pooling is performed on this bank of 4​k24superscript𝑘24k^{2} maps, producing a 4​k24superscript𝑘24k^{2}-d vector for each RoI. Then it is aggregated into a 444-d vector by average voting. This 444-d vector parameterizes a bounding box as t=(tx,ty,tw,th)𝑡subscript𝑡𝑥subscript𝑡𝑦subscript𝑡𝑤subscript𝑡ℎt=(t_{x},t_{y},t_{w},t_{h}) following the parameterization in . We note that we perform class-agnostic bounding box regression for simplicity, but the class-specific counterpart (i.e., with a 4​k2​C4superscript𝑘2𝐶4k^{2}C-d output layer) is applicable.

answer: Bounding box regression is needed to find the coordinates of the bounding boxes of the objects within the RoI [11].

input:
What was the size of model obtained by applying Deep Compression 
to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity and 8-bit quantization?
question:
What was the size of model obtained by applying Deep Compression 
to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity and 8-bit quantization?
output: The size of the model obtained by applying Deep Compression to SqueezeNet with 33% sparsity and 8-bit quantization was 0.66 MB [1].

ctxs.title: SqueezeNet: AlexNet-level accuracy with 50x fewer parameters and<0.5MB model size
ctxs.text: It appears that we have surpassed the state-of-the-art results from the model compression community: even when using uncompressed 32-bit values to represent the model, SqueezeNet has a 1.4×1.4\times smaller model size than the best efforts from the model compression community while maintaining or exceeding the baseline accuracy. Until now, an open question has been: are small models amenable to compression, or do small models “need” all of the representational power afforded by dense floating-point values? To find out, we applied Deep Compression Han et al. (2015a) to SqueezeNet, using 33% sparsity666Note that, due to the storage overhead of storing sparse matrix indices, 33% sparsity leads to somewhat less than a 3×3\times decrease in model size. and 8-bit quantization. This yields a 0.66 MB model (363×363\times smaller than 32-bit AlexNet) with equivalent accuracy to AlexNet. Further, applying Deep Compression with 6-bit quantization and 33% sparsity on SqueezeNet, we produce a 0.47MB model (510×510\times smaller than 32-bit AlexNet) with equivalent accuracy. Our small model is indeed amenable to compression.

answer: size after taking these considerations would be a 066 MB model 363× smaller than 32-bit AlexNet with equivalent accuracy to AlexNet [24].

input: Is there a reason of not realizing pre-processing techniques to the real data to remove noise before the training ?
question: Is there a reason of not realizing pre-processing techniques to the real data to remove noise before the training ?
output: The authors of the paper [1] address the issue of noise in real-world scanned data by introducing a multi-resolution extension to their multi-view CNN [1]. They perform sphere rendering at different volume resolutions, which helps regularize out potential noise or irregularities in real-world scanned data [1]. Their 3D multi-resolution filtering is quite effective on real-world data, possibly because the low 3D resolution component filters out spurious and noisy micro-structures [2]. This suggests that they do not rely on pre-processing techniques to remove noise before training, but rather incorporate multi-resolution filtering into their network architecture [1].

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: Although the multi-view CNN presented by  produces compelling results, we are able to improve its performance through a multi-resolution extension with improved data augmentation. We introduce multi-resolution 3D filtering to capture information at multiple scales. We perform sphere rendering (see Sec 3) at different volume resolutions. Note that we use spheres for this discretization as they are view-invariant. In particular, this helps regularize out potential noise or irregularities in real-world scanned data (relative to synthetic training data), enabling robust performance on real-world scans. Note that our 3D multi-resolution filtering is different from classical 2D multi-resolution approaches, since the 3D filtering respects the distance in 3D.

ctxs.title: Volumetric and Multi-view CNNs for Object Classification on 3D Data
ctxs.text: We further assess the performance of volumetric CNNs and multi-view CNNs on real-world reconstructions in Table 4. All methods are trained on CAD models in ModelNet40 but tested on real data, which may be highly partial, noisy, or oversmoothed (Fig 6). Our networks continue to outperform state-of-the-art results. In particular, our 3D multi-resolution filtering is quite effective on real-world data, possibly because the low 3D resolution component filters out spurious and noisy micro-structures. Example results for object retrieval can be found in supplementary.

answer: The reason is that, in the real data, it will not always be possible to do the pre-processing steps, especially if they require tedious manual noise removal which cannot be completely done automatically [34]. Thus, by using the noisy dataset, the authors demonstrate that their model is robust to real-world noise and occlusions [53].

input: Assuming the authors performed a brute force hyperparameter search on all permutations of the five hyperparameters - hidden layer sizes, depths, LR, batch size and dropout - how many total experiments would they have had to perform?
question: Assuming the authors performed a brute force hyperparameter search on all permutations of the five hyperparameters - hidden layer sizes, depths, LR, batch size and dropout - how many total experiments would they have had to perform?

Therefore, they would have had to perform $3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 243$ total experiments.

We report results on the best model (underlined hyperparameters, qca setup). For all models we used asynchronous RmsProp  with a momentum of 0.90.90.9 and a decay of 0.950.950.95. See Appendix A for more details of the experimental setup. Our experimental results are in Table 5, with the Attentive and Impatient Readers performing best across both datasets.

input: What are the examples of the high level features that separate the anatomical structures for lesions regions identification ?
question: What are the examples of the high level features that separate the anatomical structures for lesions regions identification ?
output: The paper excerpt mentions that the network learns to identify the ventricles, CSF, white and gray matter [1]. This suggests that the high-level features that separate the anatomical structures for lesions regions identification include the different types of tissues present in the brain, such as the ventricles, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), white matter, and gray matter [1]. These features are important for accurate lesion segmentation, as they can help the network distinguish between different tissue types and delineate the boundaries of the lesions [1].

ctxs.title: Efficient multi‐scale 3D CNN with fully connected CRF for accurate brain lesion segmentation
ctxs.text: The discriminative power of the learned features is indicated by the success of recent CNN-based systems in matching human performance in domains where it was previously considered too ambitious (He et al. (2015); Silver et al. (2016)). Analysis of the automatically extracted information could potentially provide novel insights and facilitate research on pathologies for which little prior knowledge is currently available. In an attempt to illustrate this, we explore what patterns have been learned automatically for the lesion segmentation tasks. We visualize the activations of DeepMedic’s FMs when processing a subject from our TBI database. Many appearing patterns are difficult to interpret, especially in deeper layers. In Fig. 14 we provide some examples that have an intuitive explanation. One of the most interesting findings is that the network learns to identify the ventricles, CSF, white and gray matter. This reveals that differentiation of tissue type is beneficial for lesion segmentation. This is in line with findings in the literature, where segmentation performance of traditional classifiers was significantly improved by incorporation of tissue priors (Van Leemput et al. (1999); Zikic et al. (2012)). It is intuitive that different types of lesions affect different parts of the brain depending on the underlying mechanisms of the pathology. A rigorous analysis of spatial cues extracted by the network may reveal correlations that are not well defined yet.

answer: Figure 14 shows that the network learns to identify the ventricles, CSF, white and gray matter, with each filter identifying different tissue types, indicating that learning the differences in the features of different tissue types is helpful for lesion segmentation [72].

input: What is a "Hamiltonian path"?
question: What is a "Hamiltonian path"?
output: A Hamiltonian path in a graph is a path that visits every vertex exactly once [1]. A Hamiltonian cycle is a cycle that visits every vertex exactly once [1]. The Hamiltonian path problem is a well-known NP-complete problem [1].

ctxs.title: Transformers meet Stochastic Block Models: Attention with Data-Adaptive Sparsity and Cost
ctxs.text: To check whether its strong performance demonstrated in LRA extends to the downstream NLP setting as well, we evaluate SBM-Transformer against baselines on the GLUE benchmark .

answer: A Hamiltonian path is a path that visits all nodes in a graph [35].

ctxs.title: Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs
ctxs.text: Experimental set-up. To contextualize the empirical results on our inductive benchmarks, we compare against four baselines: a random classifer, a logistic regression feature-based classifier (that ignores graph structure), the DeepWalk algorithm  as a representative factorization-based approach, and a concatenation of the raw features and DeepWalk embeddings. We also compare four variants of GraphSAGE that use the different aggregator functions (Section 3.3). Since, the “convolutional” variant of GraphSAGE is an extended, inductive version of Kipf et al’s semi-supervised GCN , we term this variant GraphSAGE-GCN. We test unsupervised variants of GraphSAGE  trained according to the loss in Equation (1), as well as supervised variants that are trained directly on classification cross-entropy loss. For all the GraphSAGE variants we used rectified linear units as the non-linearity and set K=2𝐾2K=2 with neighborhood sample sizes S1=25subscript𝑆125S_{1}=25 and S2=10subscript𝑆210S_{2}=10 (see Section 4.4 for sensitivity analyses).

ctxs.title: Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs
ctxs.text: All models were implemented in TensorFlow  with the Adam optimizer  (except DeepWalk, which performed better with the vanilla gradient descent optimizer). We designed our experiments with the goals of (i) verifying the improvement of GraphSAGE over the baseline approaches (i.e., raw features and DeepWalk) and (ii) providing a rigorous comparison of the different GraphSAGE aggregator architectures. In order to provide a fair comparison, all models share an identical implementation of their minibatch iterators, loss function and neighborhood sampler (when applicable). Moreover, in order to guard against unintentional “hyperparameter hacking” in the comparisons between GraphSAGE aggregators, we sweep over the same set of hyperparameters for all GraphSAGE variants (choosing the best setting for each variant according to performance on a validation set). The set of possible hyperparameter values was determined on early validation tests using subsets of the citation and Reddit data that we then discarded from our analyses. The appendix contains further implementation details.555Code and links to the datasets: http://snap.stanford.edu/graphsage/

answer: As far as my knowledge, seven factors are considered in experiments [25]. The reason is that authors state rectified linear units, K, two sample sizes, identical implementation of minibatch iterators, loss function, and neighborhood sampler [27].

input: What is a mixed-membership Stochastic Block Model?
question: What is a mixed-membership Stochastic Block Model?
output: A mixed-membership Stochastic Block Model (SBM) is a generative model that assigns each node to multiple clusters, rather than a single cluster [1]. This allows each node to have a non-exclusive membership in multiple clusters, which can be useful for modeling complex network structures where nodes belong to several groups simultaneously [1]. The underlying SBM used by the framework mostly resembles these two variants, while the edge probability is modeled by a nonlinear function of two node embeddings rather than a bilinear one [1].

ctxs.title: Transformers meet Stochastic Block Models: Attention with Data-Adaptive Sparsity and Cost
ctxs.text: The Stochastic Block Model (SBM) is a generative model that encodes the latent structure of graphs by grouping nodes into clusters. By modeling the cluster-membership of each node as well as inter-cluster relationships, SBMs can represent a wide variety of graph structures, which is a feature especially useful for generating new graphs or predicting missing edges in noisy data . The standard SBM assigns each node to a single cluster, and the probability of an edge between two nodes strictly depends on the corresponding clusters. Several structural extensions include overlapping SBM  and mixed-membership SBM , which allow each node to be assigned to multiple clusters. The underlying SBM used by our framework mostly resembles these two variants, while the edge probability is modeled by a nonlinear function of two node embeddings rather than a bilinear one. There exist many other extensions including degree-corrected SBM  for multi-graphs and hierarchical SBM  for multiplex-graphs. Further details can be found in a recent survey .

answer: The mixed-membership Stochastic Block Model (SBM) is a generative model that encodes the latent structure of graphs by assigning each node into multiple clusters [8].

input: What are the approaches that led to improved accuracy with lesser parameters for NASNets compared to Inception, ResNet and PolyNet?
question: What are the approaches that led to improved accuracy with lesser parameters for NASNets compared to Inception, ResNet and PolyNet?
output:
The approaches that led to improved accuracy with lesser parameters for NASNets compared to Inception, ResNet, and PolyNet include:

1. **Efficient Architecture Search**: NASNet [1] employs a Neural Architecture Search (NAS) process to discover the best architecture for a given task, which can lead to models with fewer parameters but better performance than hand-designed ones [1].
2. **Efficient Scaling**: NASNet [1] achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks with fewer parameters than existing models [1]. For instance, it achieves a mAP of 29.6% on the mobile-optimized network, exceeding previous mobile-optimized networks that employ Faster-RCNN by over 5.0% [1].
3. **Improved Feature Extraction**: NASNet [1] achieves state-of-the-art performance on the same spatial resolution (800 × 800) as equivalent object detection systems based off lesser performing image featurization (i.e. Inception-ResNet-v2) by 4.0% [1]. This suggests that NASNet learns more effective features for the task [1].
4. **Novel Loss Function**: The introduction of a novel loss function [1] paired with NASNet-A image featurization may lead to even further performance gains [1].
5. **Ensembling**: Ensembling multiple inferences across multiple model instances and image crops [1] can achieve performance gains [1].

These approaches have enabled NASNets to achieve competitive or superior performance on various benchmarks with fewer parameters compared to existing models [1].

ctxs.title: Learning Transferable Architectures for Scalable Image Recognition
ctxs.text: For the mobile-optimized network, our resulting system achieves a mAP of 29.6% – exceeding previous mobile-optimized networks that employ Faster-RCNN by over 5.0% (Table 4). For the best NASNet network, our resulting network operating on images of the same spatial resolution (800 ×\times 800) achieves mAP = 40.7%, exceeding equivalent object detection systems based off lesser performing image featurization (i.e. Inception-ResNet-v2) by 4.0% (28, 52) (see Appendix for example detections on images and side-by-side comparisons). Finally, increasing the spatial resolution of the input image results in the best reported, single model result for object detection of 43.1%, surpassing the best previous best by over 4.0% .222A primary advance in the best reported object detection system is the introduction of a novel loss . Pairing this loss with NASNet-A image featurization may lead to even further performance gains. Additionally, performance gains are achievable through ensembling multiple inferences across multiple model instances and image crops (e.g., ). These results provide further evidence that NASNet provides superior, generic image features that may be transferred across other computer vision tasks. Figure 10 and Figure 11 in Appendix C show four examples of object detection results produced by NASNet-A with the Faster-RCNN framework.

answer: Ensembling multiple inferences across multiple model instances and image crops led to improved accuracy with lesser parameters for NASNets compared to Inception, ResNet and PolyNet [31].

input: ImageNet challenge benchmarks which problems in computer vision domain?
question: ImageNet challenge benchmarks which problems in computer vision domain?
output: The ImageNet challenge benchmarks progress in different areas of computer vision domain, including [1] PASCAL VOC [1] (Everingham et al., 2012), Labeled Faces in the Wild [1] (Huang et al., 2007) for unconstrained face recognition, [1] Reconstruction meets Recognition [1] (Urtasun et al., 2014) for 3D reconstruction and [1] KITTI [1] (Geiger et al., 2013) for computer vision in autonomous driving. These datasets along with ILSVRC help benchmark progress in different areas of computer vision [1].

ctxs.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
ctxs.text: There are several datasets with standardized online evaluation similar to ILSVRC: the aforementioned PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al.,, 2012), Labeled Faces in the Wild (Huang et al.,, 2007) for unconstrained face recognition, Reconstruction meets Recognition (Urtasun et al.,, 2014) for 3D reconstruction and KITTI (Geiger et al.,, 2013) for computer vision in autonomous driving. These datasets along with ILSVRC help benchmark progress in different areas of computer vision. Works such as (Torralba and Efros,, 2011) emphasize the importance of examining the bias inherent in any standardized dataset.

answer: It emphasizes the importance of examining the bias inherent in any standardized dataset [16].

input: Does the inception model run different convolutions in parallel on cropped portions of the original images or on the same image?
question: Does the inception model run different convolutions in parallel on cropped portions of the original images or on the same image?
output: The Inception model [2] runs several different convolutional and pooling layers in parallel on the same image, and concatenates their responses [1]. This means that the Inception model does not crop the original images before applying different convolutional layers. Instead, it applies these layers to the entire input image and then concatenates their responses [1].

ctxs.title: FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering
ctxs.text: In this paper we explore two different deep network architectures that have been recently used to great success in the computer vision community. Both are deep convolutional networks (8, 11). The first architecture is based on the Zeiler&Fergus  model which consists of multiple interleaved layers of convolutions, non-linear activations, local response normalizations, and max pooling layers. We additionally add several 1×1×d11𝑑1{\times}1{\times}d convolution layers inspired by the work of . The second architecture is based on the Inception model of Szegedy et al. which was recently used as the winning approach for ImageNet 2014 . These networks use mixed layers that run several different convolutional and pooling layers in parallel and concatenate their responses. We have found that these models can reduce the number of parameters by up to 20 times and have the potential to reduce the number of FLOPS required for comparable performance.

answer: Inception model runs convolutions in parallel but it is not clear from paper that it runs on patches or complete image [8].

input: How do we obtain the noise(epsilon) in a diffusion model?
question: How do we obtain the noise(epsilon) in a diffusion model?
output: In the process of obtaining the noise ($\epsilon$) in a diffusion model, we start with a sample $x_T$ from a prior distribution, which is typically a standard Gaussian distribution [1]. This sample is then gradually denoised through a reverse diffusion process, where each step $t$ predicts the noise component $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t)$ of the noisy sample $x_t$ [2]. This predicted noise $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t)$ is then used to iteratively denoise the sample $x_t$ to obtain $x_{t-1}$ [1]. The noise $\epsilon$ is sampled from a diagonal Gaussian distribution, which simplifies various derivations and works well for natural images [1].

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: On a high level, diffusion models sample from a distribution by reversing a gradual noising process. In particular, sampling starts with noise xTsubscript𝑥𝑇x_{T} and produces gradually less-noisy samples xT−1,xT−2,…subscript𝑥𝑇1subscript𝑥𝑇2…x_{T-1},x_{T-2},... until reaching a final sample x0subscript𝑥0x_{0}. Each timestep t𝑡t corresponds to a certain noise level, and xtsubscript𝑥𝑡x_{t} can be thought of as a mixture of a signal x0subscript𝑥0x_{0} with some noise ϵitalic-ϵ\epsilon where the signal to noise ratio is determined by the timestep t𝑡t. For the remainder of this paper, we assume that the noise ϵitalic-ϵ\epsilon is drawn from a diagonal Gaussian distribution, which works well for natural images and simplifies various derivations.

ctxs.title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
ctxs.text: A diffusion model learns to produce a slightly more “denoised” xt−1subscript𝑥𝑡1x_{t-1} from xtsubscript𝑥𝑡x_{t}. Ho et al.  parameterize this model as a function ϵθ​(xt,t)subscriptitalic-ϵ𝜃subscript𝑥𝑡𝑡\epsilon_{\theta}(x_{t},t) which predicts the noise component of a noisy sample xtsubscript𝑥𝑡x_{t}. To train these models, each sample in a minibatch is produced by randomly drawing a data sample x0subscript𝑥0x_{0}, a timestep t𝑡t, and noise ϵitalic-ϵ\epsilon, which together give rise to a noised sample xtsubscript𝑥𝑡x_{t} (Equation 17). The training objective is then ‖ϵθ​(xt,t)−ϵ‖2superscriptnormsubscriptitalic-ϵ𝜃subscript𝑥𝑡𝑡italic-ϵ2||\epsilon_{\theta}(x_{t},t)-\epsilon||^{2}, i.e. a simple mean-squared error loss between the true noise and the predicted noise (Equation 26).

answer: Diffusion models sample from a distribution by reversing a gradual noising process [7]. In particular, sampling starts with noise xT and produces gradually less-noisy samples xT −1, xT −2,  until reaching a final sample x0 [8].

input: What are the term-based techniques they used in their experiments?
question: What are the term-based techniques they used in their experiments?
output: The paper excerpt mentions that traditional term-based methods like BM25 [1] are powerful zero-shot models and can outperform supervised neural models in many cases [1]. They also mention that Rescoring systems have shown that integrating BM25 into a neural model improves performance [1].

ctxs.title: Zero-shot Neural Passage Retrieval via Domain-targeted Synthetic Question Generation
ctxs.text: Traditional term-based methods like BM25 Robertson et al. (1995) are powerful zero-shot models and can outperform supervised neural models in many cases Lin (2019). Rescoring systems have shown that integrating BM25 into a neural model improves performance McDonald et al. (2018). However, for first-stage retrieval most work focuses on approximations via re-ranking Karpukhin et al. (2020); Luan et al. (2020). Here we present a technique for exact hybrid first-stage retrieval without the need for a re-ranking stage. Our method is motivated by the work of Seo et al. (2019) for sparse-dense QA.

answer: Traditional term-based methods like BM25 Robertson et al [23].

input: Among MNIST and FashionMNIST, which dataset poses more challenging classification task?
question: Among MNIST and FashionMNIST, which dataset poses more challenging classification task?
output: Fashion-MNIST poses a more challenging classification task than MNIST [1]. The paper introduces Fashion-MNIST as a novel image dataset intended to be a drop-in replacement of MNIST [1]. While it provides a more challenging alternative for benchmarking machine learning algorithm [1], it is immediately compatible with any machine learning package capable of working with the original MNIST dataset [1]. The images in Fashion-MNIST are converted to a format that matches that of the MNIST dataset [1].

ctxs.title: Fashion-MNIST: a Novel Image Dataset for Benchmarking Machine Learning Algorithms
ctxs.text: This paper introduced Fashion-MNIST, a fashion product images dataset intended to be a drop-in replacement of MNIST and whilst providing a more challenging alternative for benchmarking machine learning algorithm. The images in Fashion-MNIST are converted to a format that matches that of the MNIST dataset, making it immediately compatible with any machine learning package capable of working with the original MNIST dataset.

answer: MNIST provides more challenging classification task [10].

input: How do they train a single-generator to learn a variety in translating images?
question: How do they train a single-generator to learn a variety in translating images?
output: To train a single generator $G$ to learn mappings among multiple domains, they train $G$ to translate an input image $x$ into an output image $y$ conditioned on the target domain label $c$, i.e., $G(x, c) \rightarrow y$. They randomly generate the target domain label $c$ so that $G$ learns to flexibly translate the input image [1]. They also introduce an auxiliary classifier that allows a single discriminator $D$ to control multiple domains. Specifically, $D$ produces probability distributions over both sources and domain labels, $D: x \rightarrow \{D_{src}(x), D_{cls}(x)\}$, where $D_{src}(x)$ is the probability distribution over the source domains and $D_{cls}(x)$ is the probability distribution over the target domains [1]. This setup allows $D$ to learn to discriminate between different source and target domains, and to learn the mappings between them.

ctxs.title: StarGAN: Unified Generative Adversarial Networks for Multi-Domain Image-to-Image Translation
ctxs.text: Our goal is to train a single generator G𝐺G that learns mappings among multiple domains. To achieve this, we train G𝐺G to translate an input image x𝑥x into an output image y𝑦y conditioned on the target domain label c𝑐c, G​(x,c)→y→𝐺𝑥𝑐𝑦G(x,c)\rightarrow y. We randomly generate the target domain label c𝑐c so that G𝐺G learns to flexibly translate the input image. We also introduce an auxiliary classifier  that allows a single discriminator to control multiple domains. That is, our discriminator produces probability distributions over both sources and domain labels, D:x→{Ds​r​c​(x),Dc​l​s​(x)}:𝐷→𝑥subscript𝐷𝑠𝑟𝑐𝑥subscript𝐷𝑐𝑙𝑠𝑥D:x\rightarrow\{{D}_{src}(x),{D}_{cls}(x)\}. Fig. 3 illustrates the training process of our proposed approach.

input: Are both of them use user-provided masks for guidance but Diffusionclip (Kim et al.) perform global changes while Blended diffusion (Avrahami et al.) perform local manipulations?
question: Are both of them use user-provided masks for guidance but Diffusionclip (Kim et al.) perform global changes while Blended diffusion (Avrahami et al.) perform local manipulations?
output:
Based on the provided paper excerpts, both of the mentioned works utilize user-provided masks for guidance, but they differ in their capabilities. Specifically, DiffusionCLIP [1] performs global changes, while Blended Diffusion [2] performs local manipulations.

In the case of Cross Attention Control [1], Crowson et al. use VQ-GAN as a backbone, which is trained over diverse data. They achieve state-of-the-art generation quality over highly diverse datasets, often surpassing GANs [1]. However, their work is limited to global editing, as they do not allow for fine-grained control over specific regions of the image [1].

In contrast, Avrahami et al. [2] successfully perform local manipulations using user-provided masks for guidance [2]. Their method allows for precise control over the editing process, enabling them to modify complex structures, such as changing a bicycle to a car [2]. Despite this advancement, their technique mainly allows changing textures, but not modifying complex structures [2].

In summary, both works utilize user-provided masks for guidance, but DiffusionCLIP [1] performs global changes, while Blended Diffusion [2] performs local manipulations [2].

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: To obtain more expressive generation capabilities, Crowson et al.  use VQ-GAN , trained over diverse data, as a backbone. Other works (5, 22) exploit the recent Diffusion models (17, 39, 41, 17, 40, 36), which achieve state-of-the-art generation quality over highly diverse datasets, often surpassing GANs . Kim et al.  show how to perform global changes, whereas Avrahami et al.  successfully perform local manipulations using user-provided masks for guidance.

ctxs.title: Prompt-to-Prompt Image Editing with Cross Attention Control
ctxs.text: While most works that require only text (i.e., no masks) are limited to global editing (9, 23), Bar-Tal et al.  proposed a text-based localized editing technique without using any mask, showing impressive results. Yet, their techniques mainly allow changing textures, but not modifying complex structures, such as changing a bicycle to a car. Moreover, unlike our method, their approach requires training a network for each input.

answer: (Kim et al) [6]. Doesn't use user-provided masks and exploited recent Diffusion models to perform global changes as most editing works are limited to global editing if no masks were provided, While (Avrahami et al) [7].

input: What if a query term can be matched to multiple document terms? Does MaxSim suffice for capturing query-document relevance, for this case too?
question: What if a query term can be matched to multiple document terms? Does MaxSim suffice for capturing query-document relevance, for this case too?
output:
If a query term can be matched to multiple document terms, then MaxSim alone is not sufficient for capturing query-document relevance. As described in [1], ColBERT computes the relevance score between $q$ and $d$ via late interaction over BERT, which is defined as a summation of maximum similarity operators. In particular, it finds the maximum cosine similarity of each $v \in E_{q}$ with vectors in $E_{d}$, and combines the outputs via summation. This interaction mechanism softly searches for each $t_{q} \in T_{q}$—in a manner that reflects its context in the query—against the document’s embeddings, quantifying the strength of the “match” via the largest similarity score between $t_{q}$ and a document term $d_{t}$. Given these term scores, it then estimates the document relevance by summing the matching evidence across all query terms [1].

However, if a query term can be matched to multiple document terms, then MaxSim alone is not sufficient for capturing query-document relevance. In this case, ColBERT computes the relevance score between $q$ and $d$ via late interaction over BERT, which is defined as a summation of maximum similarity operators. In particular, it finds the maximum cosine similarity of each $v \in E_{q}$ with vectors in $E_{d}$, and combines the outputs via summation. This interaction mechanism softly searches for each $t_{q} \in T_{q}$—in a manner that reflects its context in the query—against the document’s embeddings, quantifying the strength of the “match” via the largest similarity score between $t_{q}$ and a document term $d_{t}$. Given these term scores, it then estimates the document relevance by summing the matching evidence across all query terms [1].

input: As the data in Frey Face dataset is continuous, how did the authors process it?
question: As the data in Frey Face dataset is continuous, how did the authors process it?
output: The authors used a decoder with Gaussian outputs, identical to the encoder, except that the means were constrained to the interval (0,1) using a sigmoidal activation function at the decoder output [2]. This allowed them to handle the continuous nature of the data in the Frey Face dataset.

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: The strategy in this section can be used to derive a lower bound estimator (a stochastic objective function) for a variety of directed graphical models with continuous latent variables. We will restrict ourselves here to the common case where we have an i.i.d. dataset with latent variables per datapoint, and where we like to perform maximum likelihood (ML) or maximum a posteriori (MAP) inference on the (global) parameters, and variational inference on the latent variables. It is, for example, straightforward to extend this scenario to the case where we also perform variational inference on the global parameters; that algorithm is put in the appendix, but experiments with that case are left to future work. Note that our method can be applied to online, non-stationary settings, e.g. streaming data, but here we assume a fixed dataset for simplicity.

ctxs.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
ctxs.text: The generative model (encoder) and variational approximation (decoder) from section 3 were used, where the described encoder and decoder have an equal number of hidden units. Since the Frey Face data are continuous, we used a decoder with Gaussian outputs, identical to the encoder, except that the means were constrained to the interval (0,1)01(0,1) using a sigmoidal activation function at the decoder output. Note that with hidden units we refer to the hidden layer of the neural networks of the encoder and decoder.

input: When discussing the JFT specialist training, the authors refer to a "dustbin" class. Give an example of a sample that might be assigned to this class.
question: When discussing the JFT specialist training, the authors refer to a "dustbin" class. Give an example of a sample that might be assigned to this class.
output: In the context of the JFT specialist training, the "dustbin" class is a class that is used to combine all of the non-specialist classes into a single class [1]. This class is used to make the softmax of the specialist model much smaller [1]. In the example of the JFT dataset, the specialists that they used collapsed all of their non-specialist classes into a single dustbin class [2]. This class would contain samples from all of the classes that the specialists do not care about [2]. For instance, if the specialists are trained on data that is highly enriched in examples from a very confusable subset of the classes (like different types of mushroom), then the dustbin class would contain samples from all of the other classes that the specialists do not care about [1].

ctxs.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
ctxs.text: When the number of classes is very large, it makes sense for the cumbersome model to be an ensemble that contains one generalist model trained on all the data and many “specialist” models, each of which is trained on data that is highly enriched in examples from a very confusable subset of the classes (like different types of mushroom). The softmax of this type of specialist can be made much smaller by combining all of the classes it does not care about into a single dustbin class.

ctxs.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
ctxs.text: The specialists that we used in our experiments on the JFT dataset collapsed all of their non-specialist classes into a single dustbin class. If we allow specialists to have a full softmax over all classes, there may be a much better way to prevent them overfitting than using early stopping. A specialist is trained on data that is highly enriched in its special classes. This means that the effective size of its training set is much smaller and it has a strong tendency to overfit on its special classes. This problem cannot be solved by making the specialist a lot smaller because then we lose the very helpful transfer effects we get from modeling all of the non-specialist classes.

answer: The dustbin class was the combination of all of the classes that a specialist does not care about [25]. Examples of samples that might be assigned to the dustbin class cannot be provided from this paper [36].

input: Why should the proposed method have smoothly varying weights for transformations?
question: Why should the proposed method have smoothly varying weights for transformations?
output: The proposed method should have smoothly varying weights for transformations because they are key to generating realistic and locally transformed samples [1]. A naïve application of a random local transformation within its finite neighborhood may result in a discontinuous shape and an overlap of different parts [1]. Instead, the Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression is employed to smoothly interpolate the local transformations in the 3D space [1]. This ensures that the transformations are continuous and do not introduce abrupt changes in the shape [1].

ctxs.title: Point cloud augmentation with weighted local transformations
ctxs.text: Smooth deformations are key to generate realistic and locally transformed samples. A naïve application of a random local transformation within its finite neighborhood may result in a discontinuous shape and an overlap of different parts. It has a high chance to lose discriminative structures. Instead, we employ the Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression (27, 28) to smoothly interpolate the local transformations in the 3D space. To define T^​(𝐩i)^𝑇subscript𝐩𝑖\hat{T}(\mathbf{p}_{i}) at any point in the 3D space, we use a kernel function that has a strictly positive value for any pair of points, i.e., Kh​(𝐩i,𝐩j)>0subscript𝐾ℎsubscript𝐩𝑖subscript𝐩𝑗0K_{h}(\mathbf{p}_{i},\mathbf{p}_{j})>0 for ∀𝐩i,∀𝐩jfor-allsubscript𝐩𝑖for-allsubscript𝐩𝑗\forall\mathbf{p}_{i},\forall\mathbf{p}_{j}. The following proposition theoretically guarantees that our augmentation is a smooth transformation under mild conditions. The proof is in the supplement.

answer: The proposed method uses smoothly varying weights for transformations to generate realistic and locally transformed samples [13].

input: What scale and aspect ratios is used for designing anchor boxes?
question: What scale and aspect ratios is used for designing anchor boxes?
output: The paper explores the number of scale and aspect ratios anchors used at each spatial position and each pyramid level in FPN [1]. They consider cases from a single square anchor at each location to 12 anchors per location spanning 4 sub-octave scales ($2k/4superscript{k}{4}^{k/4}$, for $k \leq 3$) and 3 aspect ratios (0.5, 1, 2) [1]. They use this setting for all other experiments in this work [1].

ctxs.title: Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection
ctxs.text: We sweep over the number of scale and aspect ratio anchors used at each spatial position and each pyramid level in FPN. We consider cases from a single square anchor at each location to 12 anchors per location spanning 4 sub-octave scales (2k/4superscript2𝑘42^{k/4}, for k≤3𝑘3k\leq 3) and 3 aspect ratios (0.5, 1, 2). Results using ResNet-50 are shown in Table 1c. A surprisingly good AP (30.3) is achieved using just one square anchor. However, the AP can be improved by nearly 4 points (to 34.0) when using 3 scales and 3 aspect ratios per location. We used this setting for all other experiments in this work.

answer: Authors have used anchor boxes spanning 4 sub-octave scales (2^{k/4}, for k\leq 3) and with 3 aspect ratios [05, 1, 2] [49].
